topics.title: FAT12
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FAT12

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FAT12 (12-bit File Allocation Table)** is a legacy file system architecture introduced with **MS-DOS 1.0 (1981)** and used primarily on **floppy diskettes** (5.25" and 3.5") and early hard disk volumes. It is the simplest variant of the FAT family where each cluster address in the File Allocation Table is encoded using **12 bits (1.5 bytes)**. The maximum addressable cluster count in pure FAT12 is **4084 clusters** ($2^{12} - 12$ reserved values).

The file system consists of four primary structural regions placed sequentially on the disk:
1. **Reserved Region** (Boot Sector / Volume Boot Record — VBR)
2. **FAT Region** (typically two copies for redundancy)
3. **Root Directory Region** (fixed size, located immediately after FAT)
4. **Data Region** (clusters holding actual file content)

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a floppy disk as a giant hotel with numbered rooms (clusters).**
> - The **front desk register (FAT)** tells you, for each occupied room, which **next room** the hotel guest (file) is staying in. Because it's a 12-bit register, the room numbers range from 0 to 4095.
> - The **reservation book (Root Directory)** is a fixed ledger that lists the guest's name, check-in details, the **first room** they were given, and how long they are staying.
> - When a forensic investigator walks in, they read the register to **trace** a guest's entire path through the hotel, even if some rooms are messy or empty (slack space).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Cluster addressing width:** **12 bits** (3 hexadecimal digits)
- **Reserved FAT12 values:** $0\times 000$ (free), $0\times FF7$ (bad), $0\times FF8$–$0\times FFF$ (EOF)
- **Maximum clusters (data + reserved):** **4096** ($2^{12}$)
- **Maximum usable data clusters:** **4084**
- **Maximum volume size:** Approximately **16 MB** (for 4 KB clusters)
- **Root directory entries:** Fixed at 512 (typical), 16 bytes/file minimum

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Logical Regions of a FAT12 Volume (Sequential Layout)

A FAT12 formatted media is laid out as a **contiguous sequence of sectors**, addressed from Sector 0 upward. Below is the breakdown of the regions:

#### Region 1 — Reserved Region (Sector 0 onwards)
- Contains the **Volume Boot Record (VBR)**, also called the **Boot Sector**.
- Critical for forensic acquisition because it contains the **BIOS Parameter Block (BPB)**.
- The BPB stores geometry such as bytes-per-sector, sectors-per-cluster, total sectors, and FAT size.

#### Region 2 — FAT Region
- The FAT is a **linked-list** array.
- Each entry occupies **12 bits** (1.5 bytes).
- Entries are stored in **little-endian byte order**, with a clever byte-sharing trick (explained in §3).
- Two copies are typically maintained: **FAT1** and **FAT2** (for redundancy against corruption).

#### Region 3 — Root Directory Region
- **Fixed in size** (cannot grow dynamically, unlike FAT32).
- Composed of **32-byte directory entries**.
- Located **immediately after** the FAT(s) — its position can be calculated.
- Each entry contains the file name, attributes, timestamps, **starting cluster number**, and file size.

#### Region 4 — Data Region
- Begins at the first sector after the Root Directory.
- Allocated in **clusters** (each cluster = 1 to 64 sectors).
- Cluster numbering starts at **2** (clusters 0 and 1 are reserved for media descriptors and EOF marker).
- The space between the end of a file's data and the end of its last cluster is called **Slack Space** — a goldmine in forensics.

### 2.2 BPB (BIOS Parameter Block) — Key Fields for Forensics

| Field Name | Offset (bytes) | Size (bytes) | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| `OEM Name` | 3 | 8 | Identifies formatting OS |
| `Bytes per Sector` | 11 | 2 | Typically **512** |
| `Sectors per Cluster` | 13 | 1 | Power of 2: **1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64** |
| `Reserved Sector Count` | 14 | 2 | Usually 1 |
| `Number of FATs` | 16 | 1 | Typically 2 |
| `Root Entry Count` | 17 | 2 | Usually **512** (root dir entries) |
| `Total Sectors (16-bit)` | 19 | 2 | If 0, see 32-bit field |
| `Sectors per FAT` | 22 | 2 | Size of each FAT in sectors |
| `Media Descriptor` | 21 | 1 | $0\times F0$ (HDD), $0\times F8$ |
| `Sectors per Track` | 24 | 2 | CHS geometry |
| `Number of Heads` | 26 | 2 | CHS geometry |
| `Hidden Sectors` | 28 | 4 | Partition offset before this volume |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All cluster arithmetic, slack space calculation, and root-directory-offset problems on the KTU exam are solved using the formulas below. Memorize them.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cluster Size** | $\text{ClusterSize} = \text{BytesPerSector} \times \text{SectorsPerCluster}$ | Bytes |
| 2 | **Root Directory Size (bytes)** | $\text{RootDirSize} = \text{RootEntryCount} \times 32$ | Bytes |
| 3 | **Root Directory Size (sectors)** | $\text{RootDirSectors} = \lceil \text{RootDirSize} \div \text{BytesPerSector} \rceil$ | Sectors |
| 4 | **FAT Region Size (sectors)** | $\text{FATRegionSectors} = \text{NumberOfFATs} \times \text{SectorsPerFAT}$ | Sectors |
| 5 | **First Data Sector** | $\text{FirstDataSector} = \text{ReservedSectors} + \text{FATRegionSectors} + \text{RootDirSectors}$ | Sector index |
| 6 | **Data Region Size (sectors)** | $\text{DataSectors} = \text{TotalSectors} - \text{FirstDataSector}$ | Sectors |
| 7 | **Total Data Clusters** | $\text{TotalClusters} = \lfloor \text{DataSectors} \div \text{SectorsPerCluster} \rfloor$ | Clusters |
| 8 | **Sector Address of Cluster N** | $\text{FirstSector}(N) = \text{FirstDataSector} + (N - 2) \times \text{SectorsPerCluster}$ | Sector index |
| 9 | **FAT Entry Byte Offset** | $\text{Offset} = N + \lfloor N \div 2 \rfloor$ | Bytes (for entry N) |
| 10 | **Slack Space per File** | $\text{Slack} = \text{ClusterSize} - (\text{FileSize} \bmod \text{ClusterSize})$ | Bytes |
| 11 | **Max FAT12 Clusters** | $2^{12} - 12 = 4084$ | Clusters (data region) |
| 12 | **Max FAT12 Volume** | $\approx 4084 \times 32\,\text{KB} \approx 128\,\text{MB}$ (theoretical) | Megabytes |
| 13 | **Max File Size (FAT12)** | $\text{FileSize}_{\max} = 2^{32} - 1$ (due to 32-bit size field) | Bytes (~4 GB) |
| 14 | **Unused (Free) Sectors** | $\text{FreeSectors} = \text{FreeClusters} \times \text{SectorsPerCluster}$ | Sectors |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORENSIC NOTE:** In the FAT12 byte-offset formula, the term $N + \lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ arises because each entry is 12 bits = 1.5 bytes. Two FAT entries share three consecutive bytes. The **even-indexed** entry uses the lower 8 bits of the 16-bit little-endian word, while the **odd-indexed** entry uses the upper 8 bits. The decryption is non-trivial and is the most-tested derivation on the KTU exam.

### 2.4 Real-World Forensic Utility

FAT12 volumes are routinely encountered in:
- **Legacy floppy disk evidence** (3.5" disks from old office systems)
- **Embedded device firmware** (older routers, industrial controllers)
- **Bootable recovery media** (Windows ME, MS-DOS rescue disks)
- **Small USB tokens and memory cards** with old FAT formatting

Forensic investigators use FAT12 analysis to:
- **Recover deleted files** (by scanning unallocated clusters and reconstructing FAT chains).
- **Detect data hiding in slack space** (information appended past the logical EOF).
- **Timestamp analysis** (file creation, modification, and access times).
- **Timeline reconstruction** of events on legacy systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 12-bit FAT Entry Decoding Algorithm

The most important derivation in FAT12 forensics is converting a **cluster number $N$** into the **physical byte offset** in the FAT and then decoding the **12-bit value**.

#### Step 1 — Compute the Byte Offset of FAT Entry $N$

Each FAT entry is 12 bits (1.5 bytes). For $N$ entries, total bits = $12N$. Converting to bytes:

$$\text{TotalBytes} = \frac{12N}{8} = 1.5N = N + \frac{N}{2}$$

Since the byte offset must be an integer, the actual integer offset is:

$$\text{Offset}(N) = N + \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor \quad \text{(bytes from start of FAT)}$$

> **Logic:** For every two 12-bit entries (totaling 24 bits = 3 bytes), we consume 3 bytes. The floor handles the half-byte boundary.

#### Step 2 — Read Two Bytes from the FAT

Read two consecutive bytes at the computed offset:

$$B_1 = \text{FAT}[\text{Offset}(N)]$$
$$B_2 = \text{FAT}[\text{Offset}(N) + 1]$$

These are little-endian, so the 16-bit combined word is:

$$W = B_1 + 256 \times B_2 \quad \text{(value range: } 0 \text{ to } 65535\text{)}$$

#### Step 3 — Extract the 12-bit FAT Value

- **If $N$ is EVEN** (including $N=0$): use the **low 12 bits** of $W$.

$$F(N) = W \;\text{AND}\; 0x0FFF$$

- **If $N$ is ODD**: use the **high 12 bits** of $W$, which means shift right by 4.

$$F(N) = (W \;\text{AND}\; 0xFFF0) \gg 4$$

#### Worked Example

> **Problem:** Given FAT bytes at offset 5 and 6 as: `0x23, 0x56`. Determine the FAT12 entries for cluster $N=3$ and $N=4$.

**Step A — Compute offsets:**

$$\text{Offset}(3) = 3 + \lfloor 1.5 \rfloor = 3 + 1 = 4$$
$$\text{Offset}(4) = 4 + \lfloor 2.0 \rfloor = 4 + 2 = 6$$

**Step B — Read bytes for $N=3$ (offset 4 and 5):**

$$B_1 = \text{FAT}[4] = 0x23, \quad B_2 = \text{FAT}[5] = 0x56$$
$$W = 0x23 + 256 \times 0x56 = 0x23 + 0x5600 = 0x5623$$

**Step C — Since $N=3$ is ODD, take the high 12 bits:**

$$F(3) = (0x5623 \;\text{AND}\; 0xFFF0) \gg 4 = 0x5620 \gg 4 = 0x0562$$

> **Interpretation:** $F(3) = 0x562 = 1378$. Cluster 3 points to cluster 1378 (or 1378 is the EOF marker if $\ge 0xFF8$).

**Step D — For $N=4$ (offset 6 and 7), read next two bytes:**

Assume `FAT[6] = 0xAB, FAT[7] = 0xCD`:

$$W = 0xAB + 256 \times 0xCD = 0xCDAB$$
$$F(4) = 0xCDAB \;\text{AND}\; 0x0FFF = 0x0DAB = 3499 \quad (\text{since } N=4 \text{ is even})$$

### 3.2 Derivation: First Data Sector Location

The location where user data begins is critical for any forensic tool to map logical cluster numbers to physical sector addresses.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FirstDataSector} &= \text{ReservedSectors} \\
&\quad + (\text{NumberOfFATs} \times \text{SectorsPerFAT}) \\
&\quad + \left\lceil \frac{\text{RootEntryCount} \times 32}{\text{BytesPerSector}} \right\rceil
\end{aligned}$$

**Reasoning (line by line):**
1. Start at sector 0. The first sectors are reserved for the boot code → add `ReservedSectors`.
2. After reserved, we find the FAT copies. There are `NumberOfFATs` copies, each consuming `SectorsPerFAT` sectors → add their product.
3. After the FAT region comes the Root Directory. Its size in bytes is `RootEntryCount × 32`. Convert to sectors using ceiling division → add that count.

### 3.3 Derivation: Slack Space Calculation

When a file is written, it occupies whole clusters. If a file's size is not a multiple of the cluster size, the last cluster has unused space called **slack space**.

$$\text{SlackSpace} = \text{ClusterSize} - (\text{FileSize} \bmod \text{ClusterSize})$$

**Special case:** If the file size is an exact multiple of the cluster size, the slack is `0` (no slack) — but in practice, the OS may write one extra full cluster of slack (RAM slack or file-system slack).

#### Worked Example

> A file of size **1730 bytes** is stored on a FAT12 volume with **Bytes per Sector = 512** and **Sectors per Cluster = 2** (cluster size = 1024 bytes).
>
> $$\text{Slack} = 1024 - (1730 \bmod 1024) = 1024 - 706 = 318 \text{ bytes}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Special FAT12 Values Decoded

| FAT12 Value (Hex) | FAT12 Value (Dec) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `0x000` | 0 | **Free cluster** (available for allocation) |
| `0x001` | 1 | **Reserved** (do not use) |
| `0x002` to `0xFEF` | 2 to 4079 | **Next cluster** pointer in chain |
| `0xFF0` to `0xFF6` | 4080 to 4086 | **Reserved values** |
| `0xFF7` | 4087 | **Bad cluster** (defective, avoided) |
| `0xFF8` to `0xFFF` | 4088 to 4095 | **End-of-File (EOF)** marker |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination tip:** KTU often asks students to identify the special values of FAT12 entries. Always report them in **both hex and decimal** to score full marks.

### 3.5 Cluster-to-Sector Mapping (Analytical Verification)

To verify whether a given cluster number $N$ exists within a FAT12 volume:

$$\text{Conditions for valid } N: \quad 2 \leq N \leq (\text{TotalClusters} + 1)$$

The physical sector address of cluster $N$:

$$\text{Sector}(N) = \text{FirstDataSector} + (N - 2) \times \text{SectorsPerCluster}$$

**Validation example:** If `FirstDataSector = 33`, `SectorsPerCluster = 2`, then `Sector(2) = 33 + 0 = 33` (cluster 2 starts at sector 33, the very first data cluster). `Sector(3) = 33 + 2 = 35` (cluster 3 starts at sector 35). This matches the standard convention.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FAT12 Volume — High-Level Block Architecture

The diagram below maps the **physical layout** of a FAT12 formatted media. Each block represents a contiguous region, and arrows indicate the on-disk flow (sector addresses increase left-to-right and top-to-bottom).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    boot["Boot Sector (VBR + BPB)\nSector 0\nSize: 1 sector (typically)"]:::reserved
    reservedExtra["Other Reserved Sectors\n(ReservedSectors - 1 sectors)"]:::reserved
    fat1["FAT1\nFile Allocation Table Copy 1\nSize: SectorsPerFAT"]:::fat
    fat2["FAT2\nFile Allocation Table Copy 2\nSize: SectorsPerFAT"]:::fat
    rootdir["Root Directory Region\nFixed Size: RootEntryCount x 32 bytes\nMax 512 entries, 32 bytes each"]:::rootdir
    cluster2["Cluster 2\n(First Data Cluster)"]:::data
    cluster3["Cluster 3"]:::data
    cluster4["Cluster 4"]:::data
    clusterN["Cluster N\n(Last Data Cluster)"]:::data
    free["Free / Unallocated Clusters\n(Marked 0x000 in FAT)"]:::free
    bad["Bad Clusters\n(Marked 0xFF7 in FAT)"]:::bad

    boot --> reservedExtra
    reservedExtra --> fat1
    fat1 --> fat2
    fat2 --> rootdir
    rootdir --> cluster2
    cluster2 --> cluster3
    cluster3 --> cluster4
    cluster4 --> clusterN
    clusterN --> free
    free --> bad

    classDef reserved fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef fat fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef rootdir fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#616161,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
```

### 4.2 32-Byte Root Directory Entry — Field Map

This schematic shows the byte-level layout of a single directory entry, which is the **fundamental forensic record** for file metadata in FAT12.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Entry["Directory Entry (32 bytes total)"]
        A0["0-7\nFilename (8 bytes)"]:::field
        A1["8-10\nExtension (3 bytes)"]:::field
        A2["11\nAttribute Byte\nRead-Only, Hidden,\nSystem, Volume Label,\nDirectory, Archive"]:::attr
        A3["12\nReserved (NT)\nor Lowercase (VFAT)"]:::field
        A4["13\nCreate Time Tenths"]:::time
        A5["14-15\nCreate Time"]:::time
        A6["16-17\nCreate Date"]:::time
        A7["18-19\nLast Access Date"]:::time
        A8["20-21\nFirst Cluster HIGH WORD"]:::field
        A9["22-23\nWrite Time"]:::time
        AA["24-25\nWrite Date"]:::time
        AB["26-27\nFirst Cluster LOW WORD"]:::field
        AC["28-31\nFile Size (32-bit)"]:::size
    end

    classDef field fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef attr fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef time fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef size fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
```

> **Key Forensic Insight:** A deleted file's directory entry is marked with the byte `0xE5` at offset 0. The remaining bytes of the entry persist in unallocated directory space, allowing recovery tools to reconstruct metadata.

### 4.3 FAT12 Cluster Chain — Linked-List Walk

This diagram shows how a fragmented file's clusters are linked through the FAT.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Root Directory Entry\nFirst Cluster: 5]:::root
    B[Cluster 5\nFAT[5] = 12]:::data
    C[Cluster 12\nFAT[12] = 7]:::data
    D[Cluster 7\nFAT[7] = 0xFFF\nEOF]:::eof

    A -- next --> B
    B -- next --> C
    C -- next --> D

    classDef root fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef eof fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

### 4.4 12-bit FAT Entry Packing — Byte Sharing Visualization

The following block diagram illustrates how **two 12-bit entries are packed into 3 bytes**, the core trick of FAT12 encoding.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Byte1["Byte 0 (Offset 0)"]
        B0["Bits 0-7: Entry 0 (even), Low 8 bits"]
    end
    subgraph Byte2["Byte 1 (Offset 1)"]
        B1["Bits 0-3: Entry 0 (even), High 4 bits\nBits 4-7: Entry 1 (odd), Low 4 bits"]
    end
    subgraph Byte3["Byte 2 (Offset 2)"]
        B2["Bits 0-7: Entry 1 (odd), High 8 bits"]
    end

    Byte1 --> Byte2
    Byte2 --> Byte3

    style Byte1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style Byte2 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B
    style Byte3 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a)** What is the maximum number of data clusters supported by the FAT12 file system? Justify your answer with a calculation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The FAT12 file system uses **12-bit cluster addresses**. The total number of distinct 12-bit values is:

$$2^{12} = 4096 \text{ values}$$

Out of these, 12 values are reserved for special purposes (1 free-marker, 1 reserved, 6 reserved values, 1 bad-cluster marker, 4 EOF markers). Therefore, the maximum number of **data clusters** is:

$$\text{MaxClusters}_{\text{FAT12}} = 4096 - 12 = 4084 \text{ clusters}$$

This is the theoretical upper bound used by forensic tools when validating cluster numbers during cluster-chain reconstruction.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(b)** Explain the significance of the value `0xFF7` in a FAT12 entry. What does the value `0x000` indicate?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Value `0xFF7` (decimal 4087):** This is the **bad-cluster marker**. It indicates that the corresponding cluster on the physical media is defective and should not be used for data storage. The operating system (and forensic tools) skip such clusters when allocating files or when reconstructing cluster chains.

- **Value `0x000` (decimal 0):** This indicates a **free/unallocated cluster**. The cluster is available for use by the operating system when new files are written or when existing files are extended. In forensic analysis, clusters marked `0x000` are candidates for deleted-file recovery (if their data area is not yet overwritten).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "0xFF7 means deleted" — this is **incorrect**. The `0xE5` byte at directory offset 0 marks a deleted file entry, not the FAT entry. FAT entries marked `0x000` indicate the **cluster itself** is free, not that its data is deleted.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. A)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]` — **14 Marks**

A FAT12 volume has the following BIOS Parameter Block (BPB) values:
- Bytes per Sector = 512
- Sectors per Cluster = 2
- Reserved Sectors = 1
- Number of FATs = 2
- Root Entry Count = 512
- Sectors per FAT = 12
- Total Sectors = 5760

### **Part (a)** — Calculate the following for the given volume: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Cluster size in bytes
   (ii) Root Directory size in sectors
   (iii) First Data Sector number
   (iv) Total number of data clusters

### **Part (b)** — Given a file starting at cluster 4, with cluster size 1024 bytes and logical file size of 1740 bytes, calculate the following: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Slack space in the last cluster
   (ii) Physical sector address of the first byte of cluster 4
   (iii) If the FAT entries (in hex) for clusters 4, 5, 6, 7 are: `0x005`, `0x006`, `0xFF8`, `0x000` — interpret the cluster chain and state whether the file is fragmented or contiguous.

---

### **Model Solution**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**(i) Cluster Size (1 mark):**

$$\text{ClusterSize} = 512 \times 2 = 1024 \text{ bytes}$$

**(ii) Root Directory Size in Sectors (2 marks):**

$$\text{RootDirBytes} = 512 \times 32 = 16384 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{RootDirSectors} = \lceil 16384 \div 512 \rceil = 32 \text{ sectors}$$

**(iii) First Data Sector (2 marks):**

$$\text{FirstDataSector} = 1 + (2 \times 12) + 32 = 1 + 24 + 32 = 57$$

**(iv) Total Data Clusters (2 marks):**

$$\text{DataSectors} = 5760 - 57 = 5703 \text{ sectors}$$

$$\text{TotalClusters} = \lfloor 5703 \div 2 \rfloor = 2851 \text{ data clusters}$$

(Valid for FAT12 since $2851 < 4084$.)

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Slack Space (2 marks):**

$$\text{Slack} = 1024 - (1740 \bmod 1024) = 1024 - 716 = 308 \text{ bytes}$$

**(ii) Physical Sector of Cluster 4 (2 marks):**

$$\text{Sector}(4) = 57 + (4 - 2) \times 2 = 57 + 4 = \text{Sector } 61$$

**(iii) Cluster Chain Interpretation (3 marks):**

| Cluster | FAT Entry | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | `0x005` | Next cluster is **5** |
| 5 | `0x006` | Next cluster is **6** |
| 6 | `0xFF8` | **End-of-File (EOF)** reached |

Cluster 7 (`0x000`) is **not** part of this file's chain (it's free/unallocated).

Since the chain $4 \to 5 \to 6$ uses **consecutive cluster numbers**, the file is **CONTIGUOUS (not fragmented)**. The file occupies 3 clusters = $3 \times 1024 = 3072$ bytes of disk space, but only 1740 bytes of logical data, producing 308 bytes of slack in the last cluster (cluster 6).

---

### **Alternative Question for Internal Choice**

**Q3. B)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]` — **14 Marks**

### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

A FAT12 disk has the following two bytes stored in the FAT at byte offsets **5** and **6** (hex): `0x34` and `0x12`.

(i) Determine the byte offset of FAT entry $N=3$ in the FAT.
(ii) Decode the 12-bit FAT12 entry value for $N=3$ (entry index 3 is **odd**).
(iii) State what this value means (free, next cluster pointer, bad cluster, or EOF).

### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

Describe the **boot process** of a FAT12 volume:
(i) What is the role of the BIOS Parameter Block (BPB) at the start of the boot sector? (3 marks)
(ii) Explain the difference between the **reserved region**, **FAT region**, **root directory region**, and **data region** of a FAT12 volume. (4 marks)

---

### **Model Solution for Q3.B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**(i) Byte offset of FAT entry N=3 (2 marks):**

$$\text{Offset}(3) = 3 + \lfloor 3 \div 2 \rfloor = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ bytes}$$

**(ii) Decode 12-bit value (3 marks):**

Read two bytes at offset 4 and 5 (note: the problem gave bytes at offset 5 and 6, but for entry $N=3$ we need bytes at offset 4 and 5). Let us assume `FAT[4] = 0x34, FAT[5] = 0x12`:

$$W = 0x34 + 256 \times 0x12 = 0x34 + 0x1200 = 0x1234$$

Since $N=3$ is ODD, take the high 12 bits:

$$F(3) = (0x1234 \;\text{AND}\; 0xFFF0) \gg 4 = 0x1230 \gg 4 = 0x0123$$

$$F(3) = 0x123 = 291 \text{ (decimal)}$$

**(iii) Interpretation (2 marks):**

The value $0x123$ lies in the range `0x002` to `0xFEF`, hence it is a **valid next-cluster pointer**. Cluster 3 points to cluster 291 as the next cluster in the file's chain.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Role of BPB (3 marks):**

The BIOS Parameter Block is located within the **Volume Boot Record (VBR)** at the very beginning of the FAT12 volume (sector 0). It contains the **media geometry** and **layout parameters** required by the operating system to:
- Determine the cluster size (Bytes per Sector × Sectors per Cluster).
- Locate the FAT region, root directory, and data region on disk.
- Compute the total number of sectors and clusters in the volume.

For forensic investigators, the BPB is the **first forensic artifact** to read, as it allows calculation of all subsequent region offsets without scanning.

**(ii) Region differences (4 marks):**

| Region | Location | Size | Content |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reserved** | Sector 0 onwards | `ReservedSectors` sectors | Boot code + BPB |
| **FAT Region** | After Reserved | `NumberOfFATs × SectorsPerFAT` sectors | File Allocation Table (12-bit entries) |
| **Root Directory** | After FAT | `RootEntryCount × 32` bytes (fixed) | 32-byte directory entries (file metadata) |
| **Data Region** | After Root Directory | Rest of the volume | Actual file content in clusters |

- The **Reserved region** contains the boot code and BPB, which is read by the system BIOS to identify the file system.
- The **FAT region** is a linked-list index mapping clusters to their next cluster (or EOF marker).
- The **Root Directory region** is a **fixed-size** table of 32-byte entries listing files in the root. Unlike FAT32, this region cannot grow dynamically.
- The **Data region** contains the actual file content, organized in clusters numbered from 2 upward. All forensic recovery of deleted content focuses on unallocated clusters in this region.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for FAT12 Questions**
> 1. **Confusing FAT entry indexing with byte indexing:** Students often treat 12-bit entries as 2 bytes each, leading to a wrong offset formula. Use the $N + \lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ rule.
> 2. **Endianness errors:** Always read FAT bytes in little-endian order: $W = B_1 + 256 \times B_2$, not the other way around.
> 3. **Cluster numbering starts at 2:** Cluster 0 and Cluster 1 are reserved; data clusters begin from 2. The formula $\text{Sector}(N) = \text{FirstDataSector} + (N-2) \times \text{SectorsPerCluster}$ reflects this.
> 4. **Slack space in FAT12 is up to one cluster minus one byte:** Forgetting the modular arithmetic in the slack formula is a frequent mistake. If `FileSize` is exactly a multiple of `ClusterSize`, slack is `0` (no partial cluster).
> 5. **Confusing special FAT values:** `0xFF7` = bad cluster; `0xFF8`–`0xFFF` = EOF. Some students mistakenly mark `0x000` as "bad" — it is actually **free**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for FAT12 (Module 1)**

- [x] **FAT12 = 12-bit File Allocation Table**, the simplest FAT variant, used on floppy disks and small legacy media.
- [x] **Maximum data clusters = 4084** (12 reserved values out of 4096 total).
- [x] **Maximum volume size ≈ 16 MB** (for 4 KB clusters, practical); up to ~128 MB theoretical.
- [x] **Four regions:** Reserved (Boot), FAT, Root Directory, Data.
- [x] **Root Directory is FIXED in size** (typically 512 entries × 32 bytes = 16 KB).
- [x] **BPB fields to memorize:** Bytes per Sector, Sectors per Cluster, Reserved Sectors, Number of FATs, Root Entry Count, Sectors per FAT, Total Sectors.
- [x] **Cluster numbering starts at 2** (clusters 0 and 1 are reserved).
- [x] **FAT entry offset formula:** $\text{Offset}(N) = N + \lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ bytes.
- [x] **Decoding rule:** Even $N$ → lower 12 bits; Odd $N$ → upper 12 bits (shift right by 4).
- [x] **Special values:** `0x000` = free, `0xFF7` = bad, `0xFF8`–`0xFFF` = EOF.
- [x] **Slack space formula:** $\text{ClusterSize} - (\text{FileSize} \bmod \text{ClusterSize})$.
- [x] **First Data Sector formula:** $\text{Reserved} + (\text{FATs} \times \text{SectorsPerFAT}) + \text{RootDirSectors}$.
- [x] **Deleted file marker in directory entry:** byte `0xE5` at offset 0 of the 32-byte entry.
- [x] **Directory entry key fields:** Name, Attribute byte, Time/Date stamps, First Cluster (high + low 16-bit words), File Size (32-bit).
- [x] **Little-endian byte order** is used throughout FAT12 (BPB, FAT entries, directory fields).
- [x] **Forensic applications:** deleted file recovery, slack space analysis, timeline reconstruction, hidden data detection in unallocated clusters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FAT16
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FAT16

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FAT16 (File Allocation Table 16-bit)** is a legacy file system architecture introduced by Microsoft (originally for MS-DOS) that organizes and manages files on a storage volume using a **16-bit cluster addressing scheme**. It maintains a linked-list based allocation table in which each entry occupies exactly **16 bits (2 bytes)**, allowing a maximum of **65,536** addressable clusters per volume.

In the context of **Digital Forensics (PECST754)**, FAT16 is critically studied because:
- It is one of the earliest widely deployed file systems and is still found in USB drives, memory cards, embedded devices, and legacy bootable media.
- Its **simple, predictable, and well-documented structure** makes it a primary teaching case for understanding data recovery, slack space analysis, and evidence extraction.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **library with numbered bookshelves**:
- The **FAT table** is like the librarian's master register — it tells you, for every book (file), which bookshelf (cluster) it sits on, and where to look next if the book spans multiple shelves.
- Each **shelf** is a *cluster* — a fixed-size box that can hold fragments of files.
- The **Root Directory** is the front-desk catalog listing every book's title (filename) and the first shelf number where it begins.

When the librarian (OS) wants to read a book, it consults the register (FAT) and follows the chain of shelf numbers (cluster chain) until the book ends.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Forensic Insight:** Because FAT16 writes its allocation table at a *fixed, predictable location* on the disk and rarely overwrites deleted metadata, forensic examiners can reliably reconstruct deleted files by parsing the FAT chain directly — even when the directory entry has been wiped.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundaries (Bold)

- **Maximum cluster count:** **65,536** clusters (2¹⁶)
- **Maximum cluster size:** **32 KB** (64 sectors × 512 bytes)
- **Maximum volume size:** **2 GB** practical / **4 GB** theoretical
- **Root directory entries:** **512** entries (fixed)
- **Root directory size:** **16,384 bytes** (512 × 32)
- **Directory entry size:** **32 bytes**
- **FAT entry size:** **2 bytes (16 bits)**
- **Reserved sector default:** **1 sector** (Boot Sector)
- **Typical FAT copies:** **2**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of FAT16 Architecture

The FAT16 volume is partitioned into **four distinct regions**, read sequentially from sector 0:

1. **Reserved Region (Boot Sector + FSInfo + Backup)**
   - Sector 0 = **Boot Sector (BPB – BIOS Parameter Block)**. Contains geometry and structural parameters.
   - Sector 1 = FSInfo sector (optional but standard).
   - Additional reserved sectors may exist for boot code.

2. **FAT Region**
   - Contains **two identical copies** of the File Allocation Table (FAT1 and FAT2).
   - Each entry is 16 bits wide.
   - Special values encode cluster status:
     - `0x0000` = Free cluster
     - `0x0001` = Reserved
     - `0x0002 → 0xFFEF` = Pointer to next cluster in chain
     - `0xFFF0 → 0xFFF6` = Reserved
     - `0xFFF7` = Bad cluster
     - `0xFFF8 → 0xFFFF` = End-of-chain marker

3. **Root Directory Region**
   - **Fixed size**, located immediately after the FATs.
   - Contains **512 entries** of 32 bytes each.
   - Each entry holds 8.3 filename, attributes, timestamps, starting cluster, and file size.
   - Subdirectories are stored in the data region but point back here for the master listing.

4. **Data Region**
   - Allocated in **clusters** (sector multiples).
   - This is where actual file content and subdirectory contents live.
   - Cluster 2 is conventionally the *first usable cluster* in the data region.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step "Why & How" Logic

| Step | Action | Why It Matters Forensically |
|------|--------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Read Boot Sector at LBA 0 | Extract BPB to compute partition geometry |
| 2 | Locate FAT Region using BPB fields | Identify allocation map |
| 3 | Parse Root Directory entries | Recover filenames & metadata |
| 4 | Walk cluster chains via FAT | Reassemble file contents |
| 5 | Examine unallocated clusters | Recover deleted/residual data |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All FAT16 calculations depend on the BIOS Parameter Block (BPB) fields stored at Sector 0.**

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units |
|-----------|----------------------|-------|
| **Bytes Per Sector (BPS)** | Read from BPB offset 0x0B | bytes |
| **Sectors Per Cluster (SPC)** | Read from BPB offset 0x0D | sectors |
| **Cluster Size (CS)** | $CS = SPC \times BPS$ | bytes |
| **Reserved Sector Count (RSC)** | BPB offset 0x0E | sectors |
| **Number of FATs (NF)** | BPB offset 0x10 (typically 2) | count |
| **Root Entry Count (REC)** | BPB offset 0x11 (fixed = 512) | entries |
| **Total Sectors (TS)** | BPB offset 0x13 (16-bit) | sectors |
| **Sectors Per FAT (SPF)** | BPB offset 0x16 | sectors |
| **Root Directory Size (RDS)** | $RDS = \lceil REC \times 32 \;/\; BPS \rceil$ | sectors |
| **Data Region Start (DRS)** | $DRS = RSC + (NF \times SPF) + RDS$ | sectors |
| **Data Region Size (DRSize)** | $DRSize = TS - DRS$ | sectors |
| **Total Clusters (TC)** | $TC = DRSize \;/\; SPC$ | clusters |
| **Maximum Volume Size (MVS)** | $MVS = TC \times CS$ | bytes |
| **Maximum Cluster Size Rule** | $CS \le 32{,}768$ bytes (32 KB) | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems & IoT Forensics:** FAT16 remains standard in SD cards (<2GB) and older industrial controllers — examiners must parse it on drones, automotive ECUs, and surveillance DVRs.
- **Bootable Media Analysis:** Recovery CDs, BIOS flash drives, and legacy forensic toolkits (e.g., WinPE, Helix) use FAT16 for compatibility — analysts trace execution chains through FAT metadata.
- **Anti-Forensics Detection:** File wiping tools that do not erase FAT chains leave recoverable artifacts; investigators exploit FAT16's predictable metadata to defeat such tools.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Compute Cluster Size from BPB Values

Given:
- Bytes Per Sector (BPS) = **512 bytes**
- Sectors Per Cluster (SPC) = **8 sectors**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS &= SPC \times BPS \\
   &= 8 \times 512 \\
   &= 4{,}096 \text{ bytes} \\
   &= 4 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each cluster groups 8 contiguous sectors (each sector holding 512 bytes). Multiplying yields total cluster capacity.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Compute Number of Clusters and Maximum Volume Size

Given (typical FAT16 BPB values):
- Total Sectors (TS) = **32,256 sectors**
- Reserved Sectors (RSC) = **1 sector**
- Sectors Per FAT (SPF) = **64 sectors**
- Number of FATs (NF) = **2**
- Sectors Per Cluster (SPC) = **8**

**Step 1 — Root Directory Size in sectors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
REC &= 512 \text{ entries} \\
RDS &= \lceil (512 \times 32) \;/\; 512 \rceil \\
    &= \lceil 16{,}384 \;/\; 512 \rceil \\
    &= 32 \text{ sectors}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each directory entry is 32 bytes. 512 entries × 32 bytes = 16,384 bytes of catalog data. Dividing by 512-byte sectors gives 32 sectors needed.

**Step 2 — Data Region Start sector:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRS &= RSC + (NF \times SPF) + RDS \\
    &= 1 + (2 \times 64) + 32 \\
    &= 1 + 128 + 32 \\
    &= 161 \text{ sectors}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The data region begins after the boot sector, the two FAT copies, and the root directory catalog.

**Step 3 — Data Region Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRSize &= TS - DRS \\
       &= 32{,}256 - 161 \\
       &= 32{,}095 \text{ sectors}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Subtract all overhead sectors from total to find sectors available for actual file content.

**Step 4 — Total Clusters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= DRSize \;/\; SPC \\
   &= 32{,}095 \;/\; 8 \\
   &= 4{,}011 \text{ clusters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Data region is divided into equal-sized clusters. Each cluster holds 8 sectors of data.

**Step 5 — Maximum Volume Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MVS &= TC \times CS \\
    &= 4{,}011 \times 4{,}096 \\
    &= 16{,}429{,}056 \text{ bytes} \\
    &\approx 15.67 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Total capacity equals the number of clusters multiplied by per-cluster capacity.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Forensic Cluster Chain Reconstruction

Suppose a directory entry indicates a file starts at **Cluster 14**, and reading the FAT yields:

- FAT[14] = 15
- FAT[15] = 22
- FAT[22] = 16
- FAT[16] = 0xFFFF (End-of-chain)

**Reconstructed chain:** 14 → 15 → 22 → 16 → (end)

**Recovered file size** (assuming each cluster holds 4,096 bytes and no slack):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Size &= (\text{number of clusters in chain}) \times CS \\
     &= 4 \times 4{,}096 \\
     &= 16{,}384 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Caveat:** The *directory entry* stores the official file size in bytes. If the stored size < (clusters × cluster size), the trailing bytes form **File Slack** — residual data from prior files, a critical forensic artifact.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical FAT16 Cluster Sizes vs Volume Size

| Volume Size | Sectors/Cluster | Cluster Size |
|-------------|-----------------|--------------|
| 0 – 32 MB | 1 | 512 bytes |
| 33 MB – 64 MB | 2 | 1 KB |
| 65 MB – 128 MB | 4 | 2 KB |
| 129 MB – 256 MB | 8 | 4 KB |
| 257 MB – 512 MB | 16 | 8 KB |
| 513 MB – 1 GB | 32 | 16 KB |
| 1 GB – 2 GB | 64 | 32 KB |

This mapping is memorized by forensic tool vendors (e.g., Sleuth Kit, FTK Imager) and is frequently tested.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FAT16 Volume Layout (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Boot Sector LBA 0 BPB and Boot Code] --> B[Reserved Region Sectors 0 to RSC-1]
    B --> C[FAT Region FAT1 NF copies]
    B --> D[FAT Region FAT2 Backup copy]
    C --> E[Root Directory Region 512 entries of 32 bytes]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Data Region Cluster 2 to Cluster N]
    F --> G[File A Content Clusters]
    F --> H[File B Content Clusters]
    F --> I[Subdirectory Clusters]
    F --> J[Unallocated Clusters Free Space]
    F --> K[Slack Space Residual Data]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The volume is read **left to right** in sequential sector order.
- The **FAT Region** appears *before* the root directory because the OS needs the FAT to navigate subdirectories stored in the data region.
- **Slack Space** and **Unallocated Clusters** are the primary forensic interest zones.

### 4.2 Directory Entry Structure (32 Bytes Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DE[Directory Entry 32 Bytes] --> F1[Offset 0x00 Filename 8 bytes]
    DE --> F2[Offset 0x08 Extension 3 bytes]
    DE --> F3[Offset 0x0B Attribute Byte 1 byte]
    DE --> F4[Offset 0x0C Reserved 1 byte]
    DE --> F5[Offset 0x0D Creation Time Tenths 1 byte]
    DE --> F6[Offset 0x0E Creation Time 2 bytes]
    DE --> F7[Offset 0x10 Creation Date 2 bytes]
    DE --> F8[Offset 0x12 Last Access Date 2 bytes]
    DE --> F9[Offset 0x14 High Cluster Number 2 bytes]
    DE --> F10[Offset 0x16 Write Time 2 bytes]
    DE --> F11[Offset 0x18 Write Date 2 bytes]
    DE --> F12[Offset 0x1A Low Cluster Number 2 bytes]
    DE --> F13[Offset 0x1C File Size 4 bytes]
```

### 4.3 FAT Cluster Chain Walk (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start at Root Directory Entry] --> S1[Read Starting Cluster Number from Entry]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Cluster Offset using DRS and SPC]
    S2 --> S3[Read Cluster Content into Buffer]
    S3 --> S4[Lookup FAT Entry for Current Cluster]
    S4 --> S5{Entry Value}
    S5 -->|0x0000| S6[Cluster Free Anomaly]
    S5 -->|0x0002 to 0xFFEF| S7[Move to Next Cluster in Chain]
    S7 --> S3
    S5 -->|0xFFF7| S8[Mark Cluster as Bad]
    S5 -->|0xFFF8 to 0xFFFF| S9[End of File Reconstruction Complete]
```

### 4.4 File Slack & Unallocated Space Forensic Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Cluster N Used for File] --> C2[Logical File End per Directory Size]
    C2 --> C3[File Slack Zone From Logical End to Cluster Boundary]
    C3 --> C4[Residual Data Previous File Wiped]
    C1 --> C5[Cluster N+1 Next Cluster in Chain or New File]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Insight:** Even after a file is "deleted" (directory entry overwritten with 0xE5), the data and FAT chain remain intact until reused. This is the cornerstone of *carving* techniques in FAT16 investigations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define the FAT16 file system. List its four main regions and state the size of each directory entry.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

FAT16 is a 16-bit File Allocation Table file system used in legacy DOS/Windows and embedded storage. It organizes data using fixed-size clusters addressed by 16-bit entries in the File Allocation Table.

The four main regions are:
1. **Reserved Region** — Contains the Boot Sector (BPB) at LBA 0.
2. **FAT Region** — Holds two copies of the allocation table with 16-bit entries.
3. **Root Directory Region** — Fixed catalog of 512 entries.
4. **Data Region** — Contains clusters holding actual file content.

Each directory entry occupies **32 bytes** and stores the 8.3 filename, attribute byte, timestamps, starting cluster, and file size.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
*What is File Slack? Why is it significant in FAT16 forensics?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

File Slack is the unused space between the **logical end of a file** (as recorded in the directory entry's size field) and the **physical end of the last cluster** allocated to that file.

It is significant in FAT16 forensics because:
- It often retains **residual data from previously stored files** that were deleted or overwritten only partially.
- Since FAT16 uses fixed cluster sizes (up to 32 KB), slack space can be substantial.
- Investigators extract slack to recover historical data, prior versions, and evidence of prior system use, making it a primary artifact in incident response.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*(a)* Explain the complete **structural layout of a FAT16 volume** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the Boot Sector (BPB) and the FAT entries. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A FAT16 volume has the following BPB parameters:
- Bytes Per Sector = 512
- Sectors Per Cluster = 4
- Reserved Sectors = 1
- Number of FATs = 2
- Sectors Per FAT = 128
- Total Sectors = 8,000

Calculate: (i) Cluster Size, (ii) Root Directory Size (in sectors), (iii) Data Region Start, (iv) Total Clusters, (v) Maximum Volume Size. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FAT16 Volume Layout (7 Marks):**

The FAT16 volume is divided into four sequential regions:

1. **Reserved Region:** Sector 0 is the Boot Sector containing the BIOS Parameter Block (BPB), which stores critical geometry parameters such as bytes per sector, sectors per cluster, reserved sector count, number of FATs, sectors per FAT, and total sector count. The BPB is the forensic "master key" — every calculation begins here.

2. **FAT Region:** Contains two identical copies of the 16-bit File Allocation Table. Each entry describes the state of one cluster: free, used (with pointer to next cluster), reserved, bad, or end-of-chain. The FAT implements a singly linked list across the data region.

3. **Root Directory Region:** Fixed-size catalog of 512 entries × 32 bytes = 16,384 bytes (32 sectors). Each entry maps an 8.3 filename to its starting cluster and size.

4. **Data Region:** The bulk of the volume, divided into clusters (cluster 2 is first usable). Stores all file content, subdirectory contents, and free space.

*(Block diagram to be drawn as per Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

The **Boot Sector's BPB** is essential because it tells the OS — and the forensic tool — where every region begins and how large it is. Without it, the volume is unreadable.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

*(i) Cluster Size:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS &= SPC \times BPS \\
   &= 4 \times 512 \\
   &= 2{,}048 \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(ii) Root Directory Size in sectors:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
RDS &= (512 \times 32) \;/\; 512 \\
    &= 32 \text{ sectors}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(iii) Data Region Start:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRS &= RSC + (NF \times SPF) + RDS \\
    &= 1 + (2 \times 128) + 32 \\
    &= 1 + 256 + 32 \\
    &= 289 \text{ sectors}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(iv) Total Clusters:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= (TS - DRS) \;/\; SPC \\
   &= (8{,}000 - 289) \;/\; 4 \\
   &= 7{,}711 \;/\; 4 \\
   &= 1{,}927 \text{ clusters (integer division)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(v) Maximum Volume Size:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
MVS &= TC \times CS \\
    &= 1{,}927 \times 2{,}048 \\
    &= 3{,}946{,}496 \text{ bytes} \\
    &\approx 3.76 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*(a)* Describe the **directory entry structure** of FAT16. Explain how timestamps (Creation, Modification, Last Access) are encoded and their forensic value. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Explain the concepts of **File Slack, RAM Slack, and Unallocated Space** in FAT16. Discuss how each is exploited during forensic investigation of a deleted file. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Directory Entry Structure & Timestamps (7 Marks):**

A FAT16 directory entry is a **32-byte** record with the following layout:

| Offset | Size | Field |
|--------|------|-------|
| 0x00 | 8 bytes | Filename (8.3 format) |
| 0x08 | 3 bytes | Extension |
| 0x0B | 1 byte | Attribute byte (Hidden, System, Directory, Archive, Read-only) |
| 0x0C | 1 byte | Reserved |
| 0x0D | 1 byte | Creation time (tenths of second) |
| 0x0E | 2 bytes | Creation time |
| 0x10 | 2 bytes | Creation date |
| 0x12 | 2 bytes | Last access date |
| 0x14 | 2 bytes | High cluster word (FAT16/FAT32 marker) |
| 0x16 | 2 bytes | Write time |
| 0x18 | 2 bytes | Write date |
| 0x1A | 2 bytes | Low cluster word (starting cluster) |
| 0x1C | 4 bytes | File size in bytes |

**Timestamp Encoding (FAT16 uses a 2-second resolution for time):**

- **Time field (2 bytes):**
  - Bits 15–11: Hours (0–23)
  - Bits 10–5: Minutes (0–59)
  - Bits 4–0: Seconds/2 (0–29)

- **Date field (2 bytes):**
  - Bits 15–9: Year (offset from 1980)
  - Bits 8–5: Month (1–12)
  - Bits 4–0: Day (1–31)

**Forensic Value:**
- Establishes **timeline of activity** on the system.
- Discrepancies between MAC times and log entries reveal tampering.
- **Last Access Date** is updated on read — useful to prove a file was opened.
- **Creation time** is preserved even after deletion (until directory entry is reused), aiding timeline reconstruction.

**(b) Slack Space Concepts & Forensic Exploitation (7 Marks):**

**File Slack:**
The space between the **logical end of file** (per directory size field) and the **physical end of the last cluster** allocated. Example: a 10 KB file on an 8 KB-cluster volume occupies 2 clusters (16 KB), leaving **6 KB of slack**.

**RAM Slack (OS-dependent):**
A subset of file slack specifically filled with data from the **OS's memory buffers** when the file was last written. May contain passwords, fragments of prior processes, or memory-resident cryptographic keys.

**Unallocated Space:**
Clusters that are marked as free (FAT entry = 0x0000) but may still contain data from **previously deleted files**. The FAT entry is cleared, but the actual cluster contents persist until overwritten.

**Forensic Exploitation of Deleted Files:**

1. **Carving the Directory Entry:** When a file is deleted, the first byte of the filename is replaced with `0xE5`. By scanning for `0xE5` followed by valid filename patterns, the original directory entry can be recovered.
2. **Walking the FAT Chain:** Even after deletion, the FAT chain often remains intact. Investigators reconstruct the file by following cluster pointers from the starting cluster (preserved in the directory entry).
3. **Extracting Slack Data:** The recovered file's slack is examined for evidence of prior content, prior system users, and residual credential material.
4. **Unallocated Cluster Mining:** Tools like *The Sleuth Kit* (`fls`, `icat`) and *Autopsy* carve unallocated space for hidden, embedded, or wiped files.
5. **Cross-Referencing Timestamps:** MAC times from directory entries are correlated with file system journals and application logs to validate the recovery.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> - Students frequently confuse **Root Directory Region** with **Subdirectory storage**. Subdirectories are stored in the **Data Region**, *not* in the fixed Root Directory Region. Listing this incorrectly costs 1–2 marks.
> - In BPB calculations, students often forget that **Root Directory size is fixed** (512 entries) in FAT16 — it is *not* stored in the data region.
> - FAT entry values `0xFFF8–0xFFFF` denote **End-of-Chain**, not "Reserved" (which is `0xFFF0–0xFFF6`). Conflating these loses the marker interpretation marks.
> - For timestamp decoding, students forget the **2-second resolution** (Seconds/2 in bits 4–0 of time field). Reporting seconds as raw 0–59 instead of 0–29 loses accuracy marks.
> - When calculating "Total Clusters," use **integer division** — partial trailing clusters are simply unused. Writing fractional answers loses full credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — FAT16 Forensic Essentials**

- FAT16 uses **16-bit cluster entries**, supporting up to **65,536 clusters**.
- Volume is partitioned into **4 regions**: Reserved → FAT → Root Directory → Data.
- **Boot Sector (Sector 0)** holds the BPB — the forensic master key for all geometry.
- BPB critical fields: BPS (offset 0x0B), SPC (0x0D), RSC (0x0E), NF (0x10), REC (0x11), TS (0x13), SPF (0x16).
- **Root Directory** is fixed at **512 entries × 32 bytes = 16,384 bytes = 32 sectors**.
- **Cluster size** = SPC × BPS, ranging from **512 bytes to 32 KB**.
- **Two FAT copies** are maintained for redundancy; examiners cross-validate.
- Special FAT entry values: `0x0000` (free), `0xFFF7` (bad), `0xFFF8–0xFFFF` (EOF).
- **Directory entry = 32 bytes**: filename, attribute, timestamps (2-sec resolution), starting cluster, file size.
- **Deletion behavior:** First byte of filename replaced with `0xE5`, but FAT chain and data persist.
- **File Slack** = Logical end to cluster boundary; **RAM Slack** = OS buffer residue within file slack.
- **Unallocated Space** = Free clusters still containing prior file data.
- Forensic tools: *The Sleuth Kit*, *Autopsy*, *FTK Imager*, *WinHex*, *X-Ways*.
- FAT16 is still encountered in **USB drives <2 GB, SD cards, embedded systems, bootable media**.
- Timestamps in FAT16: **DOS format** — date from 1980, time with 2-second resolution.
- **Long File Names (LFN)** in FAT16 use additional directory entries with attribute `0x0F`.
- Maximum theoretical volume size = **2 GB** (with 32 KB clusters); practical limit ≈ **2 GB**.
- Cluster 2 is the **first usable cluster** in the data region.
- For deleted file recovery: parse directory → walk FAT chain → assemble clusters → extract slack.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FAT32
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FAT32

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# FAT32 - Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**FAT32** (File Allocation Table, 32-bit) is a derivative of the File Allocation Table file system family, employing a **32-bit addressing scheme** for cluster allocation entries. It was introduced by **Microsoft** in 1996 (with Windows 95 OSR2) and remains a dominant file system on removable media (USB drives, SD cards, flash drives) due to its simplicity, cross-platform compatibility, and minimal overhead.

In digital forensics, FAT32 is classified as a **contiguous/linked-list-based file system** where each file is represented as a chain of clusters tracked through entries in the File Allocation Table. The deletion behavior, slack space, and timestamp granularity (2-second resolution for last-modified time) make FAT32 a critical artifact in evidence recovery and timeline analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** FAT32 is studied under Module 1 to understand how legacy file systems allocate, store, and recover data. Examiners frequently expect candidates to interpret **Boot Sector fields**, **FAT entry values**, and **slack space calculations**.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a massive **library warehouse** with thousands of identical storage boxes (clusters). Instead of putting a book's chapters in one giant room (which would waste space), the librarian splits each book into pieces and scatters them across multiple boxes.

To find any chapter, the librarian maintains a **master ledger** (the FAT) where each entry tells: *"After box #42, go look at box #17, then box #103..."* This linked-list approach is FAT32.

When you "delete" a file, the librarian doesn't shred the boxes — it just **erases the ledger entries** (replacing them with `0x00000000`). The actual data sits untouched until overwritten. **This is the foundation of forensic recovery from FAT32.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Cluster address space:** **32 bits** (4 bytes per FAT entry)
> - **Max partition size:** **2 TiB** (with 512-byte sectors) — practical limit due to FAT mirroring constraints
> - **Max single file size:** **4 GiB minus 1 byte** (exactly **4,294,967,295 bytes**)
> - **Root directory:** Moved into the data region (unlike FAT16) → it's just another cluster chain
> - **Cluster size range:** **512 bytes to 32 KiB** (sectors per cluster: 1 to 64)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Architectural Breakdown of FAT32

The FAT32 volume is logically partitioned into **four sequential regions**, addressed linearly from sector 0:

### 1. Reserved Region (Boot Sector)
- Occupies sectors 0 to (BPB\_BPB\_ReservedSectorsCount - 1)
- Contains the **BIOS Parameter Block (BPB)** at sector 0
- Includes backup boot sector (typically at sector 6)
- Stores critical geometry metadata: bytes per sector, sectors per cluster, total sectors, FAT size, root cluster number, FSInfo sector pointer

### 2. FAT Region
- Contains **two identical copies** of the File Allocation Table (FAT #1 and FAT #2) for redundancy
- Each FAT entry is **4 bytes (32 bits)** — but only the lower **28 bits** are used (upper 4 bits are reserved/zero in standard implementations)
- Total FAT sectors = BPB\_BPB\_FATSz32 × sectors-per-FAT

### 3. Data Region
- Begins at the **first cluster (cluster 2)** — clusters 0 and 1 are pseudo-clusters used as media descriptors
- Contains the **Root Directory** as cluster N (stored in BPB\_BPB\_RootClus), plus all subdirectories and file data
- Each directory entry is **32 bytes**

### 4. The 'Why' Behind Each Region
- **Why two FATs?** → Crash recovery. If FAT #1 is corrupted, OS can rebuild from FAT #2.
- **Why move root directory into data region?** → In FAT16, root directory was fixed-size at the top of the data region (limiting entries). FAT32 makes it scalable.
- **Why reserved bits in FAT entries?** → Future-proofing for FAT64 and to avoid false EOF detection at `0xFFFFFFFF`.

## FAT32 Entry Value Legend (Critical for Forensics)

| FAT Entry Value (Hex) | Meaning | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `0x00000000` | Free cluster | Was never used OR file deleted from this point |
| `0x00000001` | Reserved cluster | Should never appear in chain |
| `0x00000002` | Reserved (allocation start) | Pseudo-cluster, marks chain origin |
| `0x00000003` to `0x0FFFFFEF` | Next cluster pointer | Valid chain link — points to next cluster |
| `0x0FFFFFF0` to `0x0FFFFFF6` | Reserved values | System-reserved range |
| `0x0FFFFFF7` | Bad cluster marker | Marks physically damaged sector |
| `0x0FFFFFF8` to `0x0FFFFFFFF` | End-Of-Chain (EOC) | Last cluster of the file |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** A "deleted" file shows FAT entries replaced with `0x00000000`, but the **directory entry's first character is replaced with `0xE5`** (lowercase sigma). This is the textbook signature for unlinked files in FAT32.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partition Size | $S_{\text{partition}} = BPB\_TotSec32 \times S_{\text{bytes/sector}}$ | bytes | Must be $\leq 2^{32} \times S_{\text{cluster}}$ |
| Cluster Size | $S_{\text{cluster}} = S_{\text{bytes/sector}} \times BPB\_SecPerClus$ | bytes | Range: 512 to 32,768 |
| Data Region Start Sector | $S_{\text{data}} = R + (N_{\text{FATs}} \times F)$ | sectors | $R$ = reserved, $N_{\text{FATs}}$ = 2, $F$ = FAT size in sectors |
| First Data Sector | $S_{\text{firstdata}} = R + (N_{\text{FATs}} \times F)$ | sectors | Same as data region start |
| Total Data Clusters | $C_{\text{data}} = \lfloor (T - S_{\text{firstdata}}) / BPB\_SecPerClus \rfloor$ | clusters | Max $2^{28} - 2$ usable clusters |
| File Slack | $F_{\text{slack}} = S_{\text{cluster}} - L_{\text{logical}}$ | bytes | $L_{\text{logical}}$ = actual file size |
| RAM Slack | $R_{\text{slack}} = S_{\text{sector}} - (L_{\text{logical}} \mod S_{\text{sector}})$ | bytes | Range: 0 to 511 |
| Max File Size | $\leq 2^{32} - 1 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}295$ | bytes | Theoretically $4{,}294{,}967{,}295$ |
| Directory Entry Count | $N_{\text{entries}} = \lfloor S_{\text{cluster}} / 32 \rfloor$ | entries/cluster | 32 bytes per entry |
| FAT Entries per Sector | $E_{\text{per-sect}} = \lfloor S_{\text{sector}} / 4 \rfloor$ | entries/sector | 128 for 512-byte sectors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** FAT32 forensic analysis is used in **incident response** (USB key analysis), **criminal investigations** (recovering deleted evidence from SD cards), **data recovery** (commercial tools like R-Studio, Autopsy), and **e-discovery** for legal proceedings.

## Differences: FAT12 vs FAT16 vs FAT32 (High-Yield Comparison)

| Feature | FAT12 | FAT16 | FAT32 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Entry size | 12 bits | 16 bits | **32 bits** |
| Max partition | 32 MiB | 2 GiB | **2 TiB** |
| Max file | 32 MiB | 2 GiB | **4 GiB - 1** |
| Root dir location | Fixed table | Fixed table | **Cluster chain (movable)** |
| Cluster size range | 512 B – 4 KiB | 512 B – 32 KiB | **512 B – 32 KiB** |
| Timestamp resolution | 2 sec (date) | 2 sec | 2 sec (modification only) |
| Backup boot sector | No | No | **Yes (sector 6)** |
| FSInfo sector | No | No | **Yes (sector 1)** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Computing the Data Cluster Count

**Problem Statement:** Given a FAT32 volume with the following Boot Sector (BPB) parameters:
- `BPB_BytsPerSec = 512`
- `BPB_SecPerClus = 8`
- `BPB_RsvdSecCnt = 32`
- `BPB_NumFATs = 2`
- `BPB_FATSz32 = 2048` (sectors per FAT)
- `BPB_TotSec32 = 8,388,608` (total sectors)

**Find:** The total number of usable data clusters.

### Step 1: Identify the First Data Sector

The first data sector is calculated as the sum of reserved sectors and all FAT sectors:

$$
S_{\text{firstdata}} = BPB\_RsvdSecCnt + (BPB\_NumFATs \times BPB\_FATSz32)
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
S_{\text{firstdata}} = 32 + (2 \times 2048)
$$

$$
S_{\text{firstdata}} = 32 + 4096 = 4128 \text{ sectors}
$$

> **Logic:** Reserved sectors come first, then both copies of the FAT. After these, the data region begins.

### Step 2: Compute Total Data Sectors

$$
S_{\text{data}} = BPB\_TotSec32 - S_{\text{firstdata}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{data}} = 8{,}388{,}608 - 4128 = 8{,}384{,}480 \text{ sectors}
$$

### Step 3: Convert Sectors to Clusters

Divide total data sectors by sectors per cluster:

$$
C_{\text{data}} = \lfloor S_{\text{data}} / BPB\_SecPerClus \rfloor
$$

$$
C_{\text{data}} = \lfloor 8{,}384{,}480 / 8 \rfloor = 1{,}048{,}060 \text{ clusters}
$$

### Step 4: Verify FAT32 Limits

Maximum theoretical clusters = $2^{28} - 2 = 268{,}435{,}454$.

Since $1{,}048{,}060 < 268{,}435{,}454$, this is **a valid FAT32 volume**. ✓

---

## Derivation 2: Complete Slack Space Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A FAT32 file has the following properties:
- Cluster size: **4096 bytes** (8 sectors × 512 bytes)
- Logical file size: **8195 bytes**
- Sector size: 512 bytes

**Find:** (a) File Slack, (b) RAM Slack, (c) Total slack evidence.

### Part (a): File Slack

File slack is the difference between the last allocated cluster and the actual file end:

$$
F_{\text{slack}} = S_{\text{cluster}} - (L_{\text{logical}} \mod S_{\text{cluster}})
$$

First, compute $L_{\text{logical}} \mod S_{\text{cluster}}$:

$$
8195 \mod 4096 = 8195 - 4096 = 4099
$$

Then:

$$
F_{\text{slack}} = 4096 - 4099
$$

Wait — this yields a negative number. **Correction:** When the logical size exceeds a multiple of cluster size, file slack is the difference between the cluster size and the *remainder within the current cluster*.

Correct interpretation:

$$
F_{\text{slack}} = S_{\text{cluster}} - (L_{\text{logical}} \mod S_{\text{cluster}})
$$

If $L_{\text{logical}} = 8195$ and $S_{\text{cluster}} = 4096$:

$$
8195 \mod 4096 = 4099 \quad \text{(but } 4099 > 4096 \text{, so this means the file occupies 2 full clusters with remainder 3)}
$$

Actually, $8195 = 2 \times 4096 + 3$, so the file uses **2 clusters** and the last cluster has $4096 - 3 = 4093$ bytes of slack.

$$
F_{\text{slack}} = 4096 - 3 = 4093 \text{ bytes}
$$

> **Logic:** The file occupies two clusters (8192 bytes) plus 3 bytes in the third cluster. The unused portion of the third cluster is file slack.

### Part (b): RAM Slack

RAM slack is the portion of the last sector (within the file's logical range) that was never written by the OS:

$$
R_{\text{slack}} = S_{\text{sector}} - (L_{\text{logical}} \mod S_{\text{sector}})
$$

$$
8195 \mod 512 = 8195 - 16 \times 512 = 8195 - 8192 = 3
$$

$$
R_{\text{slack}} = 512 - 3 = 509 \text{ bytes}
$$

> **Logic:** The OS writes in sector-aligned blocks. The last sector is only partially used (3 bytes), so 509 bytes of RAM-style data remain.

### Part (c): Verification — Relationship Between Slacks

$$
F_{\text{slack}} = R_{\text{slack}} + \text{Unused Trailing Sectors in Last Cluster}
$$

Unused trailing sectors = $4096 - 3 - 509 = 3584$ bytes (i.e., 7 sectors × 512).

$$
F_{\text{slack}} = 509 + 3584 = 4093 \text{ bytes} \quad ✓
$$

---

## Derivation 3: Total Directory Entry Capacity

**Problem Statement:** Given cluster size = 8192 bytes and 32 bytes per directory entry.

**Find:** Maximum directory entries per cluster.

$$
N_{\text{entries}} = S_{\text{cluster}} / S_{\text{entry}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{entries}} = 8192 / 32 = 256 \text{ entries per cluster}
$$

For a directory spanning 10 clusters:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 256 \times 10 = 2560 \text{ entries}
$$

> **Forensic Insight:** When a directory needs more entries, FAT32 automatically allocates additional clusters (cluster chain) — unlike FAT16's fixed root directory table.

---

## Analytical Matrix: Forensic Interpretation of Boot Sector Fields

| BPB Field | Offset (hex) | Size | Forensic Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| `BS_jmpBoot` | `0x00` | 3 bytes | Jump instruction; if corrupted, signature of malware/virus overwrite |
| `BS_OEMName` | `0x03` | 8 bytes | OEM identifier — e.g., "MSDOS5.0", "mkfs.fat" (Linux mkdosfs) |
| `BPB_BytsPerSec` | `0x0B` | 2 bytes | Must be 512, 1024, 2048, or 4096 |
| `BPB_SecPerClus` | `0x0D` | 1 byte | Must be power of 2; common = 8 (4 KiB clusters) |
| `BPB_RsvdSecCnt` | `0x0E` | 2 bytes | Typically 32 for FAT32; non-standard = possible hiding technique |
| `BPB_NumFATs` | `0x10` | 1 byte | Must be 2; value of 1 indicates manual tampering |
| `BPB_TotSec32` | `0x20` | 4 bytes | Total sectors — used for partition size calculation |
| `BPB_FATSz32` | `0x24` | 4 bytes | Sectors per FAT — critical for locating data region |
| `BPB_RootClus` | `0x2C` | 4 bytes | First cluster of root directory; often cluster 2 |
| `BS_VolLab` | `0x47` | 11 bytes | Volume label — may contain user-applied names |
| `BS_FilSysType` | `0x52` | 8 bytes | Always "FAT32   " — if absent, suspect non-FAT32 volume |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Block-Level Functional Architecture of FAT32 Volume

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BootSect["Boot Sector (Sector 0)\nContains BPB\nJMP, OEM Name, BytsPerSec, SecPerClus"]
    FSInfo["FSInfo Sector (Sector 1)\nFree Cluster Count\nNext Free Cluster Hint"]
    BackupBoot["Backup Boot Sector (Sector 6)\nCopy of BPB for recovery"]
    RsvdRest["Reserved Region Remainder\nSectors 2-5, 7-31"]
    FAT1["FAT #1 (Primary)\nCluster Allocation Table\n32-bit entries"]
    FAT2["FAT #2 (Mirror)\nIdentical copy of FAT #1"]
    RootDir["Root Directory Cluster\nLocated at BPB_RootClus\n(e.g., Cluster 2)"]
    SubDir["Subdirectories\nTreated as files\nCluster chains"]
    DataFiles["User Data Files\nText, images, executables"]
    SlackArea["Slack Space\nFile Slack, RAM Slack\nResidual data zones"]
    DeletedMeta["Deleted File Residue\nE5h marker in directory\nZeroed FAT entries"]

    BootSect --> FSInfo
    FSInfo --> BackupBoot
    BackupBoot --> RsvdRest
    RsvdRest --> FAT1
    FAT1 --> FAT2
    FAT2 --> RootDir
    RootDir --> SubDir
    SubDir --> DataFiles
    DataFiles --> SlackArea
    DataFiles --> DeletedMeta
    RootDir --> DeletedMeta
    SubDir --> DeletedMeta
```

## FAT32 Cluster Chain State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FreeCluster: Initial state
    FreeCluster --> Allocated: File write begins
    Allocated --> Allocated: Next FAT entry points to next cluster
    Allocated --> EndOfChain: Final cluster (0x0FFFFFF8 to 0x0FFFFFFFF)
    Allocated --> BadCluster: I/O error detected (0x0FFFFFF7)
    EndOfChain --> FreeCluster: File deleted (entry zeroed)
    Allocated --> FreeCluster: File deleted (all chain entries zeroed)
    BadCluster --> BadCluster: Permanently excluded
    FreeCluster --> [*]: Format
    EndOfChain --> [*]: Truncate
```

## Forensic Recovery Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Acquire Disk Image\nbit-for-bit copy] --> B[Parse Boot Sector BPB]
    B --> C[Locate FAT Region]
    C --> D[Scan FAT for Orphan Clusters]
    D --> E{E0xh signature found?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Extract Directory Entry Metadata]
    E -- No --> G[File Carving by Signature]
    F --> H[Reconstruct File Chain]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Recover Data from Data Region]
    I --> J[Validate File Integrity]
    J --> K[Generate Forensic Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** The **mirror FAT** (FAT #2) is a built-in redundancy feature. In some anti-forensic attacks, attackers may modify FAT #1 but forget to update FAT #2 — examiners can detect inconsistencies by comparing both copies.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define FAT32 file system. List any four key differences between FAT16 and FAT32.**

**Model Answer:**

FAT32 (File Allocation Table, 32-bit) is a Microsoft file system introduced in 1996 that uses 32-bit entries in the File Allocation Table to manage cluster allocation on a disk volume.

**Four key differences (FAT16 vs FAT32):**

1. **Entry size:** FAT16 uses 16-bit entries; FAT32 uses 32-bit entries (with 28 bits usable).
2. **Maximum partition size:** FAT16 supports up to 2 GiB; FAT32 supports up to 2 TiB.
3. **Maximum file size:** FAT16 limits files to 2 GiB; FAT32 supports files up to 4 GiB minus 1 byte.
4. **Root directory:** In FAT16, the root directory is a fixed-size table in a reserved region; in FAT32, the root directory is a movable cluster chain located within the data region.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the forensic significance of slack space in FAT32 with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Slack space** in FAT32 is the unused portion of the last cluster allocated to a file. It contains residual data from previous file operations or RAM content, making it valuable for forensic investigators.

**Types of Slack Space:**
- **RAM Slack:** Bytes between the end of logical file data and the end of the last sector written by the OS (often contains memory dumps).
- **File Slack:** The entire unused remainder of the last cluster allocated to the file.

**Example:** If cluster size = 4096 bytes and file size = 1500 bytes, the file occupies 1 cluster but uses only 1500 bytes. The remaining **2596 bytes** of file slack may contain data from a previously deleted file, which can be recovered as evidence.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**Q.A.(a)** Describe the complete architecture of FAT32 file system with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the BPB (BIOS Parameter Block) and FSInfo sector. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**FAT32 Architecture (Block Diagram):**

A FAT32 volume is divided into four logical regions:

1. **Reserved Region** (sector 0 to BPB\_RsvdSecCnt - 1)
2. **FAT Region** (two copies of the File Allocation Table)
3. **Data Region** (contains root directory and all files, starting from cluster 2)

**Role of BPB:** The BPB is located in the boot sector (sector 0) and contains **critical metadata** describing the geometry and structure of the FAT32 volume. Key fields include:

- `BPB_BytsPerSec` (bytes per sector, typically 512)
- `BPB_SecPerClus` (sectors per cluster)
- `BPB_RsvdSecCnt` (number of reserved sectors, usually 32)
- `BPB_NumFATs` (number of FAT copies, must be 2)
- `BPB_TotSec32` (total sectors in the volume)
- `BPB_FATSz32` (sectors per FAT)
- `BPB_RootClus` (first cluster of the root directory)

The BPB is essential for any forensic tool to **parse and interpret** the volume correctly. Without it, locating files and directories is impossible.

**Role of FSInfo Sector:** Located at sector 1 by default, the FSInfo sector stores **dynamic filesystem information**:
- `FSI_Free_Count` — Number of free clusters (optimization hint)
- `FSI_Nxt_Free` — Hint for the next free cluster (speeds up file allocation)
- `FSI_LeadSig` and `FSI_TrailSig` — Signature values to validate sector integrity

Forensically, the FSInfo sector helps examiners **quickly identify free space** for carving operations and validates volume consistency.

---

**Q.A.(b)** A FAT32 volume has `BPB_BytsPerSec = 512`, `BPB_SecPerClus = 8`, `BPB_RsvdSecCnt = 32`, `BPB_NumFATs = 2`, `BPB_FATSz32 = 1024`, `BPB_TotSec32 = 4,194,304`. Calculate:
(i) Total partition size
(ii) Cluster size
(iii) First data sector
(iv) Total number of usable data clusters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total Partition Size:**

$$
S_{\text{partition}} = BPB\_TotSec32 \times BPB\_BytsPerSec
$$

$$
S_{\text{partition}} = 4{,}194{,}304 \times 512 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648 \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ GiB}
$$

**(ii) Cluster Size:**

$$
S_{\text{cluster}} = BPB\_BytsPerSec \times BPB\_SecPerClus
$$

$$
S_{\text{cluster}} = 512 \times 8 = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KiB}
$$

**(iii) First Data Sector:**

$$
S_{\text{firstdata}} = BPB\_RsvdSecCnt + (BPB\_NumFATs \times BPB\_FATSz32)
$$

$$
S_{\text{firstdata}} = 32 + (2 \times 1024) = 32 + 2048 = 2080
$$

**(iv) Total Usable Data Clusters:**

Total data sectors:

$$
S_{\text{data}} = BPB\_TotSec32 - S_{\text{firstdata}} = 4{,}194{,}304 - 2080 = 4{,}192{,}224
$$

Total clusters:

$$
C_{\text{data}} = \lfloor 4{,}192{,}224 / 8 \rfloor = 524{,}028 \text{ clusters}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q.B.(a)** Explain file deletion and data recovery in FAT32 file system. What are the FAT entry values that signify free, reserved, bad, and end-of-chain clusters? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**File Deletion Process in FAT32:**

When a user "deletes" a file in FAT32, the actual file data remains intact on disk. The OS performs only two modifications:

1. **Directory Entry Modification:** The first character of the file's 8.3 filename is replaced with `0xE5` (the lowercase sigma character), signaling the entry is deleted.
2. **FAT Chain Zeroing:** All FAT entries corresponding to the file's cluster chain are replaced with `0x00000000`, marking those clusters as free.

**Recovery Process:** Forensic tools scan the directory for entries starting with `0xE5` and search for non-zero data in clusters that are now marked free. By reading residual directory entries (or by file carving based on file signatures like JPEG headers `0xFFD8FF`), investigators can reconstruct deleted files.

**FAT Entry Values:**

| Value (Hex) | Significance |
|---|---|
| `0x00000000` | Free cluster |
| `0x00000001` | Reserved (cluster 1 — pseudo) |
| `0x00000002` | Reserved (cluster 2 — pseudo, allocation start) |
| `0x00000003` to `0x0FFFFFEF` | Valid next-cluster pointer |
| `0x0FFFFFF7` | Bad cluster |
| `0x0FFFFFF8` to `0x0FFFFFFFF` | End-of-chain (EOC) marker |

---

**Q.B.(b)** A forensic examiner encounters a FAT32 image with `BPB_BytsPerSec = 512`, `BPB_SecPerClus = 4`, and a deleted file with logical size 3500 bytes. Calculate:
(i) Cluster size
(ii) File slack
(iii) RAM slack
(iv) Total recoverable slack space. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Cluster Size:**

$$
S_{\text{cluster}} = 512 \times 4 = 2048 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(ii) File Slack:**

$$
F_{\text{slack}} = S_{\text{cluster}} - (L_{\text{logical}} \mod S_{\text{cluster}})
$$

$$
3500 \mod 2048 = 3500 - 2048 = 1452
$$

$$
F_{\text{slack}} = 2048 - 1452 = 596 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(iii) RAM Slack:**

$$
R_{\text{slack}} = S_{\text{sector}} - (L_{\text{logical}} \mod S_{\text{sector}})
$$

$$
3500 \mod 512 = 3500 - 6 \times 512 = 3500 - 3072 = 428
$$

$$
R_{\text{slack}} = 512 - 428 = 84 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(iv) Total Recoverable Slack Space:**

$$
\text{Total Slack} = F_{\text{slack}} + \text{Unused Sectors in Last Cluster}
$$

Unused trailing sectors = $2048 - 1452 - 84 = 512$ bytes (1 full sector).

$$
\text{Total Slack} = 596 \text{ bytes (entire file slack, including RAM slack within)}
$$

Alternatively:

$$
\text{Unused trailing bytes} = 512, \quad \text{RAM slack} = 84, \quad \text{Total} = 596 \text{ bytes} \quad ✓
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing cluster number with cluster size** — A cluster number (e.g., cluster 100) is an index; cluster size is in bytes.
> 2. **Forgetting that clusters 0 and 1 are reserved** — Data region starts at cluster 2.
> 3. **Miscalculating file slack** — Always use `cluster_size - (logical_size mod cluster_size)`; never subtract logical size directly from cluster size unless logical size < cluster size.
> 4. **Treating FAT32 root directory like FAT16** — In FAT32, the root directory can grow dynamically by allocating new clusters; it is not fixed.
> 5. **Ignoring the 4-byte FAT entry size** — Some students mistakenly calculate 2 entries per 512-byte sector; correct is 128 entries per 512-byte sector.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ FAT32 uses **32-bit (4-byte) entries** in the File Allocation Table; upper 4 bits are reserved.
- ✅ **Maximum partition size** is **2 TiB**; **maximum file size** is **4 GiB - 1 byte**.
- ✅ The volume structure has **four regions**: Reserved, FAT (2 copies), Data Region, and the data clusters themselves.
- ✅ Cluster sizes range from **512 B to 32 KiB**, always a power of 2 multiple of sector size.
- ✅ The **BPB** in the boot sector contains all geometry parameters required to parse the volume.
- ✅ **FSInfo sector** (sector 1) stores free cluster count and next-free hint.
- ✅ **Backup boot sector** at sector 6 enables crash recovery.
- ✅ Deletion replaces directory entry's first character with `0xE5` and zeroes FAT chain entries — **data persists**.
- ✅ **End-of-chain values** are `0x0FFFFFF8` to `0x0FFFFFFFF`; **bad cluster** is `0x0FFFFFF7`; **free cluster** is `0x00000000`.
- ✅ **File slack = cluster size - (logical size mod cluster size)**; **RAM slack = sector size - (logical size mod sector size)**.
- ✅ The **root directory** in FAT32 is a movable cluster chain (typically cluster 2), unlike FAT16's fixed table.
- ✅ For forensics: always verify both FAT copies are identical — mismatches may indicate tampering or incomplete writes.
- ✅ Each directory entry is **32 bytes**; one cluster of 4096 bytes holds **128 entries**.
- ✅ Timestamp resolution for last-modified time is **2 seconds** (FAT32), unlike NTFS's 100-nanosecond resolution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NTFS (New Technology File System)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** NTFS (New Technology File System)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**NTFS (New Technology File System)** is a proprietary, journaled, hierarchical file system developed by Microsoft, introduced in 1993 with the **Windows NT 3.1** operating system. It is the default and primary file system for the Microsoft Windows NT family of operating systems (NT, 2000, XP, Vista, 7, 8, 10, 11, and Windows Server variants).

From a KTU 2024 Digital Forensics perspective, NTFS is of paramount importance because it is the file system on which the vast majority of forensic examinations of personal computers and enterprise servers are conducted. Its journaling, metadata-rich architecture, and advanced features (like Alternate Data Streams, encryption, and access control) leave extensive forensic artifacts that an investigator must understand, parse, and interpret.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Forensic Mandate (KTU 2024)**
> A digital forensics investigator must master NTFS internals because:
> 1. It stores rich metadata (timestamps, MACB times) for every file.
> 2. It maintains journals (USN Journal, $LogFile) that record file system changes.
> 3. It supports Alternate Data Streams (ADS) which can hide data.
> 4. It employs a Master File Table (MFT) — the equivalent of a "DNA database" of the file system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hyper-Organized Library"

Imagine NTFS as a **massive, hyper-organized public library** that has just opened after renovation:

- **The Library Building (Volume)** is the entire hard disk partition.
