Since `0x4C45 ≠ 0x0001`, the trailing bytes of sector 0 **do not match** the USN value. This is **expected** because the trailing bytes have been *replaced* with the USN value during write — but here the value `0x4C45` is what we *read*, and it must equal the USN.

### Step 3: Re-evaluate — the standard fixup flow

Let me restate the canonical NTFS fixup algorithm correctly:

1. During **write**, the OS takes 2 bytes from each sector (offsets 510 and 1022), saves them in the USN array, and writes the USN value to those positions.
2. During **read**, the OS reads the last 2 bytes of each sector and checks if they equal the USN value. If yes → entry is consistent. Then it restores the original bytes from the USN array.

So with USN = `0x0001` and the trailing 2 bytes = `0x4C45`, we have a **mismatch**. The entry is **CORRUPT / TORN WRITE DETECTED**.

### Step 4: Final verdict

$$
\text{Sector0\_Check: } 0x4C45 \stackrel{?}{=} 0x0001 \Rightarrow \text{FAIL}
$$

$$
\text{Sector1\_Check: } 0x4C45 \stackrel{?}{=} 0x0001 \Rightarrow \text{FAIL}
$$

**Result**: Both sectors fail the fixup check. The OS will report this MFT entry as **corrupt**. Forensic tools like Sleuth Kit will mark the file as "Orphan" and may attempt recovery using the backup copy in `$MFTMirr`.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cluster Size Effect on Maximum Resident File Size

**Problem**: What is the maximum file size that can be stored as a **resident** attribute within a single 1024-byte MFT entry, assuming a 4096-byte cluster and 100 bytes of other attributes (header + fixup + non-data attributes)?

### Step 1: Resident attribute overhead

The `$DATA` resident attribute has its own attribute header:

- Attribute type: 4 bytes
- Attribute length: 4 bytes
- Non-resident flag: 1 byte (= `0x00` for resident)
- Name length: 1 byte
- Name offset: 2 bytes
- Flags: 2 bytes
- Attribute ID: 2 bytes
- Content size: 4 bytes
- Content offset: 2 bytes
- Indexed flag: 2 bytes
- **Total resident attribute header overhead = 24 bytes**

### Step 2: MFT entry components

$$
\text{MFT\_Entry\_Size} = 1024 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Header\_Size} = 48 \text{ bytes (file record header + padding)}
$$

$$
\text{Fixup\_Size} = 2 \times 2 + 2 = 6 \text{ bytes (USN + 2 USN array entries + USN word count) }
$$

$$
\text{Other\_Attributes} = 100 \text{ bytes (} \text{\$STANDARD\_INFORMATION} + \text{\$FILE\_NAME} \text{)}
$$

$$
\text{Resident\_Attribute\_Header} = 24 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 3: Available space for resident data

$$
\text{Available} = 1024 - 48 - 6 - 100 - 24 - 4 \text{ (end-of-attrs marker)}
$$

$$
\text{Available} = 1024 - 182 = 842 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **General rule of thumb** (widely cited in KTU study materials): the practical maximum resident file size is **about 700–800 bytes** for a default NTFS volume. Any file larger than this must be stored in a **non-resident** `$DATA` attribute with a data run list pointing to disk clusters.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common MFT Attributes Cheat Sheet

| Attribute Type (Hex) | Attribute Name | Resident? | Forensic Value |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| `0x10` | `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` | Always Resident | MACB timestamps, owner ID, security flags |
| `0x20` | `$ATTRIBUTE_LIST` | Can be non-resident | Lists all attributes if they exceed one MFT entry |
| `0x30` | `$FILE_NAME` | Always Resident | Filename, parent directory ref, creation time |
| `0x40` | `$OBJECT_ID` | Resident | 16-byte GUID; tracks file identity across renames |
| `0x50` | `$SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR` | Can be non-resident | ACLs, owner SID |
| `0x60` | `$VOLUME_NAME` | Resident | Volume label |
| `0x70` | `$VOLUME_INFORMATION` | Resident | NTFS version, dirty flag |
| `0x80` | `$DATA` | Both | Actual file content (resident or non-resident) |
| `0x90` | `$INDEX_ROOT` | Resident | Root node of B-tree index for small directories |
| `0xA0` | `$INDEX_ALLOCATION` | Non-resident | B-tree nodes for large directories |
| `0xB0` | `$BITMAP` | Resident | Cluster allocation bitmap for directory index |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Logical Architecture of NTFS MFT and File Record

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the overall functional architecture of how the MFT file (`$MFT`) is laid out on disk and how individual file records are organized.

```mermaid
graph TD
    boot[Boot Sector sector 0] -->|BPB points to| mftStart[MFT Start Cluster]
    mftStart --> mftFile[File $MFT Record 0]
    mftFile --> entry0[Record 0 $MFT self]
    mftFile --> entry1[Record 1 $MFTMirr]
    mftFile --> entry2[Record 2 $LogFile]
    mftFile --> entry15[Records 3 to 15 system files]
    mftFile --> entryUser[Record 16 onwards user files]
    entry0 --> hdrA[File Record Header 48 bytes]
    entry0 --> fixA[Fixup Array variable]
    entry0 --> attrA[Attributes variable list]
    attrA --> stdInfo[Type 0x10 $STANDARD_INFORMATION]
    attrA --> fileName[Type 0x30 $FILE_NAME]
    attrA --> dataAttr[Type 0x80 $DATA]
    attrA --> endMark[Type 0xFFFFFFFF End Marker]
    dataAttr -->|Resident less than 700 bytes| resData[Data inside MFT entry]
    dataAttr -->|Non-resident large files| dataRuns[Data run list cluster addresses]
    dataRuns --> clusters[Disk Clusters on volume]
    fixA -->|verify on read| tornCheck[Torn write detection sector 510 and 1022]
    tornCheck -->|pass| validRec[Record valid]
    tornCheck -->|fail| corruptRec[Record corrupt use $MFTMirr]
    mftFile --> logFileSys[LogFile journaling $LogFile Record 2]
    logFileSys --> recover[NTFS recovery on mount]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: MFT Entry Byte Layout (1024 bytes)

The following schematic shows the *byte-level layout* of a single MFT entry with relative offsets.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph hdr["File Record Header 0 to 47"]
        sig[Offset 0x00 Signature FILE 4 bytes]
        usnOff[Offset 0x04 USN offset 2 bytes]
        usnSz[Offset 0x06 USN size words 2 bytes]
        lsn[Offset 0x08 LSN 8 bytes]
        seqNo[Offset 0x10 Sequence number 2 bytes]
        hlink[Offset 0x12 Hard link count 2 bytes]
        attrOff[Offset 0x14 First attribute offset 2 bytes]
        flags[Offset 0x16 Flags in use directory 2 bytes]
        usedSz[Offset 0x18 Used size 4 bytes]
        allocSz[Offset 0x1C Allocated size 4 bytes]
        baseRef[Offset 0x20 Base record ref 8 bytes]
        nextID[Offset 0x28 Next attribute ID 2 bytes]
    end
    subgraph fix["Fixup Array variable"]
        usnVal[USN value 2 bytes]
        usnA1[USN array entry sector 0 2 bytes]
        usnA2[USN array entry sector 1 2 bytes]
    end
    subgraph attrs["Attribute Region variable"]
        a1[Attribute 0x10 $STANDARD_INFORMATION]
        a2[Attribute 0x30 $FILE_NAME]
        a3[Attribute 0x80 $DATA]
        aEnd[End marker 0xFFFFFFFF]
    end
    subgraph unused["Trailing Unused Space"]
        pad[Padding to 1024 bytes]
    end
    hdr --> fix
    fix --> attrs
    attrs --> unused
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Forensic Workflow When Analyzing an MFT Entry

This diagram shows the *flow of forensic decision-making* when an investigator opens an MFT entry during a case.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Investigator opens MFT entry] --> sigCheck{Signature equals FILE}
    sigCheck -->|No| invalid[Entry invalid skip]
    sigCheck -->|Yes| fixCheck{Fixup array verifies}
    fixCheck -->|No| corrupt[Use $MFTMirr backup or partial recovery]
    fixCheck -->|Yes| flagCheck{Flags bit 0 in use equals 1}
    flagCheck -->|No| deleted[Marked as deleted recover metadata]
    flagCheck -->|Yes| active[Active file extract data]
    deleted --> parseAttr[Parse attributes for forensics]
    active --> parseAttr
    corrupt --> parseAttr
    parseAttr --> readSI[Read $STANDARD_INFORMATION 0x10 for MACB]
    parseAttr --> readFN[Read $FILE_NAME 0x30 for name and parent]
    parseAttr --> readData[Read $DATA 0x80 for data runs]
    readSI --> timeline[Build event timeline]
    readFN --> ownership[Map directory hierarchy]
    readData --> content[Extract or carve file content]
    timeline --> report[Generate forensic report]
    ownership --> report
    content --> report
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: NTFS Boot → MFT Resolution Path

```mermaid
graph TD
    a[Volume boot sector PBR] --> b[Parse BPB]
    b --> c{Bytes per sector}
    b --> d{Sectors per cluster}
    b --> e[Clusters per MFT record]
    b --> f[MFT start LCN]
    f --> g[Compute MFT byte offset = LCN x cluster size]
    g --> h[Open $MFT record 0]
    h --> i[Verify FILE signature]
    i --> j[Apply fixup array to restore trailing bytes]
    j --> k[Walk attribute list]
    k --> l[Identify file record entry 0 = MFT itself]
    k --> m[Identify file record entry 5 = root directory]
    k --> n[All other entries are file or directory metadata]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked to draw a diagram of MFT or NTFS boot process, the *Boot → BPB → MFT resolution path* (Figure 4.4) and the *Entry byte layout* (Figure 4.2) are the two most frequently asked. Practice redrawing them from memory.

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Distribution Note**: Part A questions test *Remember* and *Understand* cognitive levels per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be 4–6 lines long, with definitions, key terms, and one example.

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Master File Table (MFT) in NTFS. List any three reserved system files stored in the first 16 MFT entries.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Master File Table (MFT)** is a hidden system file (`$MFT`, MFT entry 0) in the NTFS file system that acts as a central index database. It contains one or more **file records (MFT entries)** of size 1024 bytes each, where every file, directory, and metadata file on the NTFS volume is described. Each MFT record stores attributes such as timestamps, filename, security descriptors, and pointers to the file's data.

Three reserved system files in the first 16 MFT entries:

1. **$MFT (entry 0)** — the Master File Table itself.
2. **$MFTMirr (entry 1)** — a mirror copy of the first 4 MFT entries used for disaster recovery.
3. **$LogFile (entry 2)** — a transactional journal that ensures NTFS recoverability after a crash.

*(Any three from the system table in Section 2.1.1 are acceptable.)*

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a resident attribute in an MFT entry? Give an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **resident attribute** is an MFT attribute whose entire data content is stored *inside the MFT entry itself*, rather than in separate disk clusters. The attribute header contains a content offset and a content size that point to the actual data within the 1024-byte record.

**Example**: A small text file of fewer than ~700 bytes (such as a `readme.txt` containing "Hello World") would have its `$DATA` attribute stored as a *resident* attribute. The `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` and `$FILE_NAME` attributes are *always* resident regardless of file size because they store only metadata (timestamps, filenames, security flags). Resident attributes are valuable in forensics because they survive even when the file's data clusters are wiped.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B as an internal choice. Full model answers are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Set A]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the structure of an MFT file record in NTFS. Discuss the role of the fixup array in maintaining sector-level integrity.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: MFT File Record Structure

**Model Answer:**

A single **MFT file record (entry)** is a 1024-byte structure divided into four logical regions:

1. **File Record Header (bytes 0–47)**: Contains the magic signature "FILE" (4 bytes) at offset `0x00`, the LogFile Sequence Number (8 bytes), sequence number, hard-link count, offset to the first attribute, flags, used and allocated sizes, base-record reference, and next attribute ID.

2. **Update Sequence Header / Fixup Array (variable)**: Located at the offset specified in the header (typically around `0x30`). Contains the *USN value* (Update Sequence Number) and an array of original 2-byte values that were overwritten at the end of each sector.

3. **Attribute Region (variable)**: An ordered list of attribute records describing the file. Each attribute starts with a 4-byte type code (e.g., `0x10` for `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`), followed by its length, a resident/non-resident flag, optional name, and then the attribute's data. The attribute list ends with the 4-byte marker `0xFFFFFFFF`.

4. **Trailing Unused Space**: Padding up to 1024 bytes.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph region1["File Record Header 48 bytes"]
        h1[Signature FILE]
        h2[USN offset and size]
        h3[LSN 8 bytes]
        h4[Sequence number 2 bytes]
        h5[Flags 2 bytes]
        h6[Used and Allocated size]
        h7[Base record ref]
    end
    subgraph region2["Fixup Array variable"]
        f1[USN value]
        f2[USN array for sector 0]
        f3[USN array for sector 1]
    end
    subgraph region3["Attribute Region variable"]
        a1[Attribute 0x10 $STANDARD_INFORMATION]
        a2[Attribute 0x30 $FILE_NAME]
        a3[Attribute 0x80 $DATA]
        a4[End marker 0xFFFFFFFF]
    end
    subgraph region4["Unused space"]
        u1[Padding to 1024 bytes]
    end
    region1 --> region2
    region2 --> region3
    region3 --> region4
```

Forensic significance: The header tells investigators whether the entry is **in use, deleted, or corrupt**. The attribute region contains **MACB timestamps, filenames, parent directory reference, and data run lists** — the four most commonly examined data points in any investigation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Role of the Fixup Array

**Model Answer:**

The **fixup array (update sequence)** is a small integrity-check mechanism that protects MFT records from *torn writes* — a situation where a power failure or system crash occurs midway through writing a 1024-byte entry spanning two 512-byte sectors.

**Mechanism**:

1. **During write**: NTFS saves the *last 2 bytes* of each 512-byte sector (offsets 510 and 1022) into the fixup array. It then **overwrites** these 2 bytes with a 2-byte *Update Sequence Number (USN)* that is incremented on every MFT modification.

2. **During read**: NTFS reads the last 2 bytes of each sector. If they **match** the stored USN value, the entry is considered consistent. NTFS then **restores** the original 2 bytes from the fixup array, presenting the user with the correct data.

3. **On mismatch**: If the trailing bytes do not equal the USN, NTFS concludes that a torn write occurred. The entry is marked **dirty** and a CHKDSK or auto-recovery from `$MFTMirr` is triggered.

**Worked Example (from Section 3.2)**: If the USN = `0x0001` and the last 2 bytes of both sectors read `0x4C45`, the fixup check fails — the OS will mark the entry as corrupt and attempt recovery.

**Forensic Importance**: Forensic tools can use the fixup array to detect tampering. If an investigator manually edits MFT entries on a forensic image, the fixup values will not match and the file system will treat the entry as corrupt. This is why forensic writes are made to *copies* of the image, not the original.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Set B — Alternative Choice]`

**Q: Explain the time-stamp attributes stored in the MFT. How does the MFT aid in forensic timeline reconstruction and recovery of deleted files?**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Time-Stamp Attributes in the MFT

**Model Answer:**

NTFS stores time-stamp information in two attributes of every MFT entry:

1. **`$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (attribute type `0x10`)** — *always resident*. Contains four 64-bit FILETIME values (representing 100-nanosecond intervals since 1 January 1601):
   - **Creation Time** — when the file was first created.
   - **Modification Time (M-time)** — when the file's *data* was last written.
   - **MFT Entry Modification Time (MFT Change / MFT-modify)** — when any attribute of the MFT entry itself was last changed (e.g., rename, permission change).
   - **Last Access Time (A-time)** — when the file was last read or executed (with a 1-hour precision granularity by default to reduce filesystem update overhead).

2. **`$FILE_NAME` (attribute type `0x30`)** — *always resident*. Contains a secondary set of timestamps that are updated only when the file is renamed or moved. Crucially, `$FILE_NAME` timestamps are **not** updated by user-mode applications; they are managed by the NTFS driver itself, making them **more resistant to timestomping** than `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` values.

**Forensic Tool Workflow**: Tools like *Sleuth Kit's `istat`* or *FTK* automatically extract both sets of timestamps. Investigators compare the two sets — if `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` says a file was created in 2010 but `$FILE_NAME` says 2015, this discrepancy is a strong indicator of **timestamp manipulation (timestomping)**, often a hallmark of anti-forensic activity by attackers.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: MFT in Forensic Timeline Reconstruction and Deleted-File Recovery

**Model Answer:**

**Timeline Reconstruction**:
By parsing every MFT entry on a volume, forensic tools can extract the MACB timestamps of *every* file and directory. The investigator then sorts these chronologically to build a complete activity timeline. This is used to:

- Identify when a malicious executable was first dropped on the system.
- Track user activity in the hours before/during a breach.
- Correlate file creation events with login events (from the Security Event Log) and registry modifications.

**Deleted-File Recovery**:
When a file is "deleted" in Windows, the OS does **not** erase the MFT entry. Instead:

1. The *in-use* flag (offset `0x16`, bit 0) in the header is cleared to `0x00`.
2. The cluster bitmap (`$Bitmap`) marks the data clusters as free.
3. The directory entry pointing to the file is removed.

The actual MFT entry persists in its slot until the slot is **recycled** for a new file. Until then, forensic tools can still read:
- The **original filename** from `$FILE_NAME`.
- The **original timestamps** from `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`.
- The **data run list** from `$DATA` (allowing partial data recovery if clusters are not yet overwritten).
- The **parent directory reference** from `$FILE_NAME` (showing where the file used to live).

**Tools Used**: Sleuth Kit's `fls` (list files including deleted), `icat` (extract file content by inode), and `istat` (display MFT entry statistics). Commercial tools like X-Ways and Autopsy also support MFT-resident deleted-file carving.

**Limitation**: If the MFT slot has been recycled, the original MFT entry is overwritten. Recovery then depends on file-system journaling (`$LogFile`) and data carving techniques.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in MFT Questions** (based on KTU valuation patterns):
>
> 1. **Confusing MFT entry size (1024 bytes) with MFT file size (variable, often MBs/GBs)**. Many students incorrectly write "MFT is 1024 bytes". The MFT is a *file* whose size grows with usage; each *entry* inside it is 1024 bytes.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that the first 16 entries are reserved for system files**. If asked to "list the first 5 system files" and you list user files starting from record 16, you will lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Omitting the "FILE" magic signature** when describing the MFT entry header. Examiners specifically look for `0x46494C45` (little-endian "FILE") at offset 0x00.
>
> 4. **Mixing up resident vs. non-resident attributes**. Students often claim "small files are non-resident" — the correct statement is that files with very small data content (and certain metadata attributes) are *resident* (stored inside the MFT entry).
>
> 5. **Not explaining the fixup array's *purpose* in the torn-write detection** — merely listing its location loses marks. Always explain: *overwrite trailing bytes during write → compare on read → restore originals*.
>
> 6. **Forgetting $FILE_NAME timestamps** when discussing timestomping. Examiners reward students who mention that `$FILE_NAME` (managed by the kernel) is harder to forge than `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (writable by user-mode code).
>
> 7. **In calculation problems, not showing unit conversion** (bytes vs. clusters vs. sectors). Always state the cluster size and sector size explicitly in your solution.
>
> 8. **Writing "deleted files cannot be recovered"** — this is **wrong** for NTFS. Always clarify: *the MFT entry persists until recycled; metadata is recoverable; data recovery depends on cluster reuse status*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for MFT** (read this 5 minutes before the exam):

- **MFT = central index file (`$MFT`, entry 0) of NTFS; each entry is 1024 bytes** by default.
- **First 16 entries (records 0–15) are reserved system files**: `$MFT`, `$MFTMirr`, `$LogFile`, `$Volume`, `$AttrDef`, root dir, `$Bitmap`, `$Boot`, `$BadClus`, `$Secure`, `$UpCase`, `$Extend`, plus a few extension system files.
- **MFT entry layout**: Header (48 B) → Fixup Array (variable) → Attributes (variable) → End marker `0xFFFFFFFF` → Padding to 1024 B.
- **Header signature**: ASCII "FILE" (`0x46494C45`) at offset `0x00`.
- **Flags** at offset `0x16`: bit 0 = in-use, bit 1 = directory, bit 2 = extension record.
- **Fixup array purpose**: detect torn writes by overwriting last 2 bytes of each sector with a USN; mismatch on read → corrupt entry.
- **Resident attribute** = data stored inside the MFT entry; **Non-resident** = data in disk clusters, described by data run list (cluster_start, length pairs).
- **Key attributes for forensics**: `0x10` (`$STANDARD_INFORMATION` — MACB times), `0x30` (`$FILE_NAME` — name + parent ref + extra timestamps), `0x80` (`$DATA` — file content or data runs), `0x20` (`$ATTRIBUTE_LIST` — pointer to extension records).
- **MACB timestamps**: Modification, Access, Created, MFT-change — all 64-bit FILETIME values (100-ns intervals since 1601-01-01).
- **Timestomping detection**: compare `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` vs. `$FILE_NAME` timestamps; mismatch = suspicious.
- **Deleted file recovery**: MFT entry's in-use flag cleared, but entry persists until slot is reused; recover filename, timestamps, parent ref, and data runs.
- **Resident file size limit**: ~700–800 bytes (depends on attribute overhead).
- **Cluster math formula**: `MFT_entry_byte_offset = MFT_start_cluster × bytes_per_cluster + (entry_index × 1024)`.
- **Cluster math for sector**: `Physical_sector = byte_offset / 512`.
- **Tools**: Sleuth Kit (`fls`, `istat`, `icat`), Autopsy, FTK, X-Ways Forensics, EnCase.
- **Forensic integrity**: never edit MFT on the original disk; always work on forensic image copies.
- **Bonus fact**: `$MFTMirr` (entry 1) is a partial backup of the first 4 MFT entries, located in the middle of the volume for redundancy.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Advice**: For any KTU question on MFT, structure your answer in three layers: **(1) Definition**, **(2) Structural breakdown with offsets/hex values**, **(3) Forensic implication**. This three-layer structure mirrors the KTU marking scheme and earns full marks consistently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EXT (Extended File System)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EXT (Extended File System)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Extended File System (EXT)** is a family of journaling and non-journaling file systems primarily designed for the **Linux operating system kernel**. It is the default file system for most Linux distributions and has evolved through four major versions: **ext (1992), ext2 (1993), ext3 (2001), and ext4 (2008)**. EXT is a classic example of a **Unix-style file system architecture** that organizes storage using three core data structures — **superblocks, inodes, and data blocks** — arranged into logical partitions called **block groups**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST754 Module 1):**
> Understanding the EXT family is foundational for **Linux Disk Forensics**, **Data Recovery**, and **File System Carving**. The examiner frequently tests structural awareness and timestamp interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Official Specification Origin:**
> The EXT family was designed by **Rémy Card**, Stephen Tweedie, and Theodore Ts'o under the GNU Project. The current mainstream version **ext4** was added to the Linux Kernel mainline in **2008** and remains the default FS for major distributions (Ubuntu, Debian, Fedora, CentOS Stream).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Imagine a massive central library** with the following parallel:

| Library Component | EXT File System Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Library Master Register** (the giant ledger at the entrance) | **Superblock** | Stores global metadata: total size, block size, inode count, mount count |
| **Catalog Cards** (one per book) | **Inodes** | Stores per-file metadata: permissions, ownership, timestamps, data block pointers |
| **Actual Bookshelves with books** | **Data Blocks** | Stores the actual file content |
| **Reading Room Sections** (numbered zones) | **Block Groups** | Logical partitions of the disk for locality and reduced fragmentation |
| **Section Index Boards** | **Group Descriptors** | Maps where inodes and bitmaps live inside each block group |
| **Library Checkout Logbook** | **Journal (ext3/ext4)** | A transaction log ensuring crash-recovery consistency |

> **Plain English Summary:**
> When Linux boots and mounts an `/dev/sda1` partition as EXT4, it first reads the **Superblock** to learn the disk's geometry, then uses **Inodes** (like library index cards) to find where each file's data lives. Every file has exactly **one inode**, but the inode is a tiny structure separate from the actual content.

### 1.3 Key Technical Metrics & Standard Boundaries

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized EXT Constants & Limits:**
> - **Default Block Size:** **4096 bytes (4 KiB)**
> - **Maximum Block Size:** **64 KiB** (ext4)
> - **Maximum File Size:** **16 TiB** (ext4) | **2 TiB** (ext3) | **2 GiB** (ext2 with 1 KiB blocks)
> - **Maximum Volume Size:** **1 EiB (Exbibyte)** (ext4 theoretical) | **16 TiB** practical
> - **Default Inode Ratio:** **1 inode per 16,384 bytes (16 KiB)** of disk space
> - **Inode Size:** **256 bytes** (ext4 default) | **128 bytes** (ext2/ext3 default)
> - **Number of Inodes:** Decided at **mkfs** time and is **fixed** — you cannot add inodes later without reformatting.

---

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structural Breakdown

The EXT family is built on a strict on-disk layout. The disk is divided into **three regions**:

1. **Reserved Area (0th block group special):** Boot sector, superblock, redundant copies
2. **Block Group Region:** Repeated block groups (BG = Block Group)
3. **Trailing Region:** Backup superblock, backup group descriptors, unused space

#### 2.1.1 The Superblock (Global Metadata Master)

The **superblock** is the **most critical structure** in EXT. It is replicated across multiple block groups for **redundancy** (specifically at block groups 0, 1, and powers of 3, 5, 7).

- **Magic Number:** `0xEF53` identifies an EXT2/3/4 filesystem
- **Contains:** Total inodes, total blocks, free inodes, free blocks, first data block, block size, fragment size, blocks per group, inodes per group, mount count, last checked time, UUID, volume name

#### 2.1.2 The Inode Structure (Per-File Metadata)

Each **inode** is a **256-byte (ext4)** record that contains everything about a file **except its name** and **actual data**. The name lives in the **directory entry**.

An inode contains:

- **File mode** (permissions, setuid/setgid, sticky bit)
- **Owner UID & GID**
- **File size** (in bytes)
- **Timestamps:**
  - `i_atime` — Last **access** time (read)
  - `i_mtime` — Last **modification** time (content change)
  - `i_ctime` — Last **inode/status change** time (metadata change)
  - `i_crtime` — File **creation** time (ext4 only, since Linux 4.11)
  - `i_dtime` — **Deletion** time (forensic gold!)
- **Link count**
- **Block pointers:** 12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect (ext2/3) OR **extents** (ext4 default)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Significance of `i_dtime`:**
> When a file is deleted, the inode is *not* wiped — its `i_dtime` field is set to the deletion timestamp, and its bitmaps are cleared. Forensic tools like **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)**, **Autopsy**, and **`istat`** can read this metadata to reconstruct deletion events.

#### 2.1.3 Data Blocks and Block Pointers

In **ext2/ext3**, an inode points to data using up to **15 block pointers** in a tree structure:

- **12 Direct Pointers** → point to the first 12 data blocks (fast access for small files)
- **1 Indirect Pointer** → points to a block containing more pointers
- **1 Double Indirect Pointer** → points to a block of indirect pointers
- **1 Triple Indirect Pointer** → points to a block of double-indirect pointers

In **ext4**, this scheme was replaced with **extents**:
- An **extent** is a tuple `(logical_block, physical_block, length)` allowing **contiguous block runs** to be described in a single entry.
- This eliminates metadata overhead and reduces fragmentation.
- **Inline data** feature allows small files (< 160 bytes) to be stored **inside the inode itself**.

### 2.2 The Journal (ext3/ext4 Crash Recovery)

The **journal** is a circular log that records **pending metadata transactions** *before* they hit the disk. On boot, the kernel **replays** the journal to ensure consistency.

- **Journal Modes:**
  1. `journal` — Metadata + Data both journaled (slowest, safest)
  2. `ordered` — Only metadata journaled, data written before metadata (default)
  3. `writeback` — Only metadata journaled, no data ordering (fastest, weakest)

> [!WARNING]
> **Forensic Pitfall:** The journal itself is a **forensic treasure trove** containing recently modified data that may not be flushed to its final file location yet. Tools like `extundelete` and `jls` (Sleuth Kit) parse the journal for evidence.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the **mathematical relationships** needed to calculate file system capacities — frequently asked in KTU numerical and short-answer questions.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Total Inodes | $N_{inodes} = \dfrac{V}{R_{inode}}$ | $V$ = Volume size, $R_{inode}$ = Bytes-per-Inode ratio (default 16384) |
| Block Group Count | $N_{BG} = \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{inodes}}{I_{BG}} \right\rceil$ | $I_{BG}$ = Inodes per Block Group (default 8192) |
| Block Bitmap Size | $S_{bitmap} = \left\lceil \dfrac{B_{BG}}{8} \right\rceil$ bytes | $B_{BG}$ = Blocks per Block Group |
| Inode Bitmap Size | $S_{ibitmap} = \left\lceil \dfrac{I_{BG}}{8} \right\rceil$ bytes | One bit per inode |
| Max File Size (ext2/3) | $F_{max} = B \times \left(12 + P + P^2 + P^3\right)$ | $B$ = Block size, $P$ = Pointers per block ($P = B/4$) |
| Max File Size (ext4) | $F_{max} = 16 \text{ TiB}$ (theoretical) | With extents and 48-bit block addresses |
| Max Extent Length | $L_{extent} = 2^{15} = 32768 \text{ blocks}$ | Per single extent entry |
| Effective Addressable Space | $V_{eff} = B \times 2^{48}$ | $B$ = 4 KiB → $2^{48} \times 2^{12} = 2^{60}$ bytes = 1 EiB |
| Journal Transaction Size | $J_{txn} = 128 \text{ MiB}$ (default ext4) | Configurable via `-J size=` |
| Timestamp Resolution | $1 \text{ second}$ (legacy), $1 \text{ nanosecond}$ (ext4 with `statx`) | ext4 supports nanosecond timestamps since kernel 2.6 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute values and conditional bar operators in the formulas above have been written using LaTeX's `\left\lceil` and `\right\rceil` to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linux Servers:** ext4 powers ~75% of production Linux deployments.
- **Digital Forensics Labs:** TSK (`fls`, `icat`, `istat`, `mmls`) reads EXT structures to recover deleted files, parse timelines, and build evidence.
- **Data Recovery:** Tools like `extundelete`, `TestDisk`, `PhotoRec` operate specifically on EXT structures.
- **Embedded & IoT:** ext2 (no journal) is preferred in low-power devices to reduce write amplification on flash storage.
- **Containers:** Docker and Kubernetes layers frequently use ext4 as the host overlay FS.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum File Size for ext2/ext3 (Direct + Indirect Block Trees)

We must compute the **maximum number of data blocks addressable** by an inode using the 15-pointer tree, then multiply by block size.

Let:
- $B$ = Block size (bytes)
- $P$ = Pointers per block = $B / 4$ (since each pointer is 4 bytes / 32 bits)

The 15 pointers in the inode are:
- **12 direct** → each points to 1 data block → 12 blocks
- **1 single indirect** → points to a block of $P$ pointers → $P$ data blocks
- **1 double indirect** → points to a block of $P$ single-indirect blocks → $P^2$ data blocks
- **1 triple indirect** → points to a block of $P$ double-indirect blocks → $P^3$ data blocks

Total addressable data blocks:
$$
N_{blocks} = 12 + P + P^2 + P^3
$$

Maximum file size:
$$
F_{max} = B \cdot (12 + P + P^2 + P^3)
$$

**Step-by-step numerical evaluation for B = 4096 bytes (4 KiB):**

$$
P = \frac{B}{4} = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024 \text{ pointers/block}
$$

$$
P^2 = 1024 \times 1024 = 1{,}048{,}576
$$

$$
P^3 = 1024 \times 1{,}048{,}576 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824
$$

$$
N_{blocks} = 12 + 1024 + 1{,}048{,}576 + 1{,}073{,}741{,}824
$$

$$
N_{blocks} = 1{,}074{,}791{,}436 \text{ blocks}
$$

$$
F_{max} = 4096 \times 1{,}074{,}791{,}436 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
F_{max} = 4{,}402{,}345{,}482{,}240 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ TiB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?** The 12 direct pointers cover small files instantly. The single indirect covers medium files. The double and triple indirection provide a logarithmic tree that can address astronomically large files, all from a 256-byte inode.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Inode Count and Disk Overhead

**Problem:** Format a **100 GiB** ext4 partition with **4 KiB** blocks and a **bytes-per-inode ratio of 16,384**. Calculate total inodes and total block groups.

**Given:**
- $V = 100 \text{ GiB} = 100 \times 2^{30} \text{ bytes} = 107{,}374{,}182{,}400 \text{ bytes}$
- $R_{inode} = 16{,}384 \text{ bytes/inode}$
- $B = 4{,}096 \text{ bytes/block}$
- $I_{BG} = 8{,}192 \text{ inodes/block group}$ (ext4 default)

**Step 1: Total Inodes**
$$
N_{inodes} = \frac{V}{R_{inode}} = \frac{107{,}374{,}182{,}400}{16{,}384}
$$

$$
N_{inodes} = 6{,}553{,}600 \text{ inodes}
$$

**Step 2: Total Data Blocks**
$$
N_{blocks} = \frac{V}{B} = \frac{107{,}374{,}182{,}400}{4{,}096} = 26{,}214{,}400 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3: Number of Block Groups**
$$
N_{BG} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{inodes}}{I_{BG}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{6{,}553{,}600}{8{,}192} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{BG} = \left\lceil 800 \right\rceil = 800 \text{ block groups}
$$

**Step 4: Inode Table Size per Block Group**
$$
T_{inode} = I_{BG} \times 256 \text{ bytes (ext4 inode size)} = 8{,}192 \times 256 = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ MiB}
$$

This fits comfortably inside one block group since each block group is sized to occupy 8 MiB of metadata space (128 blocks × 4 KiB + bitmaps + inode table).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Forensic Recovery of a Deleted File's Block Pointers

**Forensic Scenario:** A 4 KiB-block ext4 partition. A file `/evidence.txt` of size 5,000 bytes is deleted. Recover its data blocks.

**Reasoning:**
1. The file's inode still exists; its `i_dtime` is now set.
2. The inode's first 2 data block pointers (direct) are still valid: addresses B1, B2.
3. The 5000 bytes occupy 2 blocks: bytes 0–4095 in B1, bytes 4096–4999 in B2.
4. Tools like `icat /dev/sda1 INODE_NUM` read the data blocks back.

**Recovery Probability:**
- Inode still intact → **High (~90%)** if no new files have been created.
- Data blocks overwritten → **Low** depending on disk activity.
- Journal contains prior versions → **Recoverable** using `jcat`.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: File System Comparison (KTU High-Yield)

| **Feature** | **ext2** | **ext3** | **ext4** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year Released | 1993 | 2001 | 2008 |
| Journaling | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (HTree) | ✅ Yes (HTree, JBD2) |
| Max File Size | 2 TiB | 2 TiB | 16 TiB |
| Max Volume | 32 TiB | 32 TiB | 1 EiB (theoretical) |
| Allocation | Block bitmap | Block bitmap | Extents + bitmap |
| Timestamp Resolution | 1 sec | 1 sec | Nanosecond |
| Inline Data | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Checksums | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (metadata) |
| Forensic Tool Support | TSK, e2fsck | TSK, e2fsck | TSK, e2fsck, extundelete |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 On-Disk Layout of an EXT4 Partition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Boot["Boot Sector (1024 bytes)"]
    SB1["Superblock (1024 bytes) - Primary"]
    GDT["Group Descriptor Table"]
    DBG["Data Block Bitmap"]
    IBG["Inode Bitmap"]
    IT["Inode Table"]
    DAT["Data Blocks"]
    SBX["Superblock Backup Copy"]
    GDTX["Group Descriptor Backup"]
    JNL["Journal (JBD2)"]

    Boot --> SB1
    SB1 --> GDT
    GDT --> DBG
    DBG --> IBG
    IBG --> IT
    IT --> DAT
    DAT --> SBX
    SBX --> GDTX
    GDTX --> JNL

    BG1["Block Group 0 (contains primary SB)"]
    BG2["Block Group 1 (SB backup)"]
    BGN["Block Group N (data)"]

    BG1 --> BG2 --> BGN

    InodeA["Inode: i_mode, i_uid, i_size, i_atime, i_mtime, i_ctime, i_crtime, i_dtime, i_blocks, i_block[15]"]
    Ext["Extent Tree: leaf + internal nodes describing contiguous block runs"]
    InodeA --> Ext
```

### 4.2 Inode Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inode["Inode Structure (256 bytes)"]
        A["i_mode (2B): permissions + type"]
        B["i_uid (2B): owner user ID"]
        C["i_size (8B): file size in bytes"]
        D["i_atime (12B): access time"]
        E["i_ctime (12B): inode change time"]
        F["i_mtime (12B): modification time"]
        G["i_dtime (4B): deletion time (FORENSIC)"]
        H["i_gid (2B): group ID"]
        I["i_links_count (2B): hard link count"]
        J["i_blocks (4B): 512-byte block count"]
        K["i_flags (4B): ext4 flags"]
        L["i_block[60B]: extents or direct pointers"]
    end

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K --> L
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["Acquire Disk Image (dd / dc3dd)"]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1["Kernel reads Superblock at offset 1024"]
    R2["Validate magic 0xEF53"]

    R4["Read Group Descriptor Table"]

    R6["Build Inode Cache in RAM"]
    R7["Mount successful → File system live"]

    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 --> R7
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Explain the role of the superblock in the EXT file system. What is the significance of the magic number `0xEF53`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **superblock** is a 1024-byte structure located at byte offset 1024 of an EXT partition. It is the **master metadata record** containing critical information such as total inode count, total block count, free blocks/inodes, block size, blocks per group, mount count, last `fsck` time, filesystem UUID, and volume name.

The **magic number `0xEF53`** is a 2-byte signature at the beginning of the superblock that identifies the partition as an EXT2/3/4 filesystem. If this value is corrupted or zeroed, the kernel will refuse to mount the partition. Forensically, searching for `0xEF53` across the disk is a **signature-based method** to locate backup superblocks for recovery.

The superblock is **replicated** in multiple block groups (specifically groups 0, 1, and those at powers of 3, 5, 7) for redundancy. The `mke2fs` tool can restore a corrupted primary superblock from any backup.

*(1 mark: Definition | 1 mark: Magic number role | 1 mark: Backup redundancy)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**List and explain the four timestamps maintained in an EXT4 inode. Why is `i_dtime` important forensically?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An EXT4 inode maintains the following four (now five) timestamps:

| **Field** | **Meaning** | **Updated When** |
|---|---|---|
| `i_atime` | Access time | File is read |
| `i_mtime` | Modification time | File content is changed |
| `i_ctime` | Inode change time | Metadata (permissions, ownership) is changed |
| `i_crtime` | Creation / birth time | File is first created (ext4 only, kernel 4.11+) |
| `i_dtime` | Deletion time | File is deleted |

**Forensic significance of `i_dtime`:** When a user issues `rm file.txt`, the file's content is **not erased**. The kernel sets `i_dtime` to the current Unix epoch time, frees the inode and data block bitmaps, and decrements the link count. The inode itself, including the data block pointers, **persists on disk** until overwritten. Tools like `fls` (Sleuth Kit) and `extundelete` detect these "orphaned" inodes by scanning for valid inodes whose `i_dtime > 0` and reconstruct deleted files.

*(1 mark: List of 4 timestamps | 1 mark: Explanation | 1 mark: Forensic importance of i_dtime)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Recommended Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**(a)** Describe the complete on-disk layout of the EXT4 file system. Explain the role of **superblock, group descriptors, inodes, data blocks, bitmaps, and the journal** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a clear example, explain the **inode block pointer tree structure** used in ext2/ext3. How does ext4 improve this using **extents**? Calculate the maximum file size for a 4 KiB block size file system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q1(a):**

The **on-disk layout** of an EXT4 file system is logically divided into several regions:

**1. Reserved Area (Block 0):**
- Boot sector (first 1024 bytes) — typically unused by EXT, may contain bootloader.
- Primary Superblock (bytes 1024–2047) — 1024-byte global metadata structure.

**2. Block Group Region:**
The disk is divided into **block groups**, each containing a contiguous range of blocks. Each block group contains:
- **Group Descriptor Table (GDT):** Describes the location of bitmaps and inode tables for that group.
- **Data Block Bitmap:** One bit per data block in the group (1 = allocated, 0 = free).
- **Inode Bitmap:** One bit per inode in the group.
- **Inode Table:** Array of inodes (default 8192 per group, 256 bytes each in ext4).
- **Data Blocks:** Actual file content.

**3. Journal Region:**
A separate area (typically at the end of the partition) holds the **JBD2 journal** — a circular log of pending metadata transactions.

**4. Backup Copies:**
The superblock and GDT are **replicated** in block groups 0, 1, and at powers of 3, 5, 7 to enable crash recovery.

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
| Boot | Superblock | GDT | Block Bitmap | Inode Bitmap | Inode Table | Data Blocks | ... | Journal |
| (BG0)                            | (BG1) ... |
```

**Role of Each Structure:**

- **Superblock:** Master record of FS geometry, total/used inodes and blocks.
- **Group Descriptors:** Per-block-group metadata (where bitmaps, inode tables live).
- **Bitmaps:** Fast lookup of free/allocated blocks and inodes.
- **Inode Table:** Per-file metadata.
- **Data Blocks:** File content.
- **Journal:** Crash-consistency log.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for diagram, 3 for explanation, 2 for journal + backup role)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q1(b):**

In **ext2/ext3**, an inode contains 15 × 4-byte block pointers arranged as a tree:

```
| ptr0 | ptr1 | ... | ptr11 | indirect | dbl_indirect | trpl_indirect |
  Direct (12)         Single       Double         Triple
```

- **Direct pointers (12):** Point directly to data blocks. Fast for small files.
- **Single indirect (1):** Points to a block containing pointers to data blocks.
- **Double indirect (1):** Points to a block of single-indirect blocks.
- **Triple indirect (1):** Points to a block of double-indirect blocks.

**Example:** For a 10 KiB file with 4 KiB blocks → 3 blocks needed → stored in `ptr0`, `ptr1`, `ptr2`. The remaining 9 direct pointers and 3 indirection pointers are **NULL**.

**ext4 Improvement — Extents:**
ext4 replaces the 15-pointer scheme with a **B-tree of extents**. An **extent** is a single record: `(logical_start_block, physical_start_block, block_count)`. For a contiguous 10 KiB file, instead of 3 separate pointers, ext4 stores **1 extent** describing 3 contiguous blocks. This:
- Reduces inode metadata size
- Reduces fragmentation
- Speeds up large file I/O
- Enables `ext4_bigalloc` for cluster-aware allocation

**Maximum File Size Calculation (ext2/ext3, B = 4 KiB):**

Given in **Section 3.1 Derivation 1**:
- $P = 1024$ pointers/block
- $F_{max} = 4096 \times (12 + 1024 + 1{,}048{,}576 + 1{,}073{,}741{,}824)$
- $F_{max} \approx 4 \text{ TiB}$ (theoretical for ext2/3)

For **ext4**, the maximum file size is **16 TiB** (limited by 48-bit block addressing and 32-bit extent length, though patches exist to extend it to 1024 PB with 56-bit addresses).

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for tree explanation, 2 for extents improvement, 3 for calculation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**
**(a)** Compare the architectural features of **ext2, ext3, and ext4** in a tabular form. Highlight the key forensic advantages of ext4 over its predecessors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **journaling mechanism** in ext3/ext4. Discuss the three journal modes with their trade-offs. How does the journal become a **forensic data source**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q2(a):**

*(Comparison table as in Section 3.4 above — 5 marks)*
*(Forensic advantages — 2 marks):*
- **Nanosecond timestamps** allow precise timeline reconstruction.
- **Inline data** means small files (e.g., malware configs) may be hidden inside the inode itself — investigators must parse inodes, not just data blocks.
- **Extents** provide better recovery of large deleted files because the metadata is more compact.
- **Metadata checksums** help detect tampering and identify corrupt regions.
- **Journal** retains recent transactions that may contain evidence not yet flushed.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q2(b):**

The **journal** is a circular log structure that records metadata changes *before* they are committed to their final on-disk location. This guarantees **POSIX crash-consistency**: after a power failure, the kernel can replay the journal to restore the FS to a consistent state.

**Three Journal Modes:**

1. **`journal` (full journaling):**
   - Both data and metadata are written to the journal first, then committed.
   - **Pros:** Highest data integrity.
   - **Cons:** Double disk I/O → severe performance penalty.

2. **`ordered` (default):**
   - Only metadata is journaled. Data blocks are flushed *before* the metadata is journaled.
   - **Pros:** Guarantees that data on disk matches metadata. Good balance.
   - **Cons:** Slight performance hit vs. writeback.

3. **`writeback`:**
   - Only metadata is journaled. No data ordering guarantee.
   - **Pros:** Fastest performance.
   - **Cons:** After crash, file contents may be corrupted (e.g., old data in new file).

**Journal as a Forensic Source:**

The journal is itself a file (`/.journal`, typically block `8` onwards) that contains **recent transaction records**. These records include:
- Block descriptors (which blocks are being modified)
- Revocation records
- Commit blocks
- Sometimes, complete inode and data payloads

Tools like **`jls`** (Sleuth Kit) list journal transactions; **`jcat`** extracts data blocks referenced by a transaction. Investigators can recover:
- Recently deleted file data
- File system changes just before a crash
- Evidence of anti-forensic activity (e.g., secure deletion attempts that left journal traces)

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for journaling mechanism, 2 for journal modes with trade-offs, 3 for forensic application)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ext3 with ext4 timestamps** — ext3 does NOT have nanosecond resolution; only ext4 does. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you claim all EXT versions have nanosecond timestamps.
> 2. **Wrong superblock offset** — The superblock is at byte **1024**, NOT at sector 0. Many students write "block 0" which is incorrect.
> 3. **Misidentifying `i_dtime` and `i_crtime`** — These are different fields. `i_crtime` is set at creation; `i_dtime` is set at deletion. Do not swap them.
> 4. **Confusing block size and sector size** — A sector is **always 512 bytes**; a block is configurable (commonly 4 KiB). They are not the same.
> 5. **Forgetting that ext2 has NO journal** — If a question asks "journaling in ext2", the correct answer is that ext2 has **no journal**; you must use `e2fsck` for recovery.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets:**

- ⭐ EXT = Extended File System family (ext, ext2, ext3, ext4) for Linux.
- ⭐ **Superblock** at offset 1024, magic `0xEF53`, replicated in multiple block groups.
- ⭐ **Inode** = per-file metadata; **no filename** and **no data** stored inside.
- ⭐ Default **block size = 4 KiB**, default **inode size = 256 bytes (ext4)**.
- ⭐ **Inode ratio = 1 inode per 16 KiB** of disk (default).
- ⭐ Four timestamps: `atime`, `mtime`, `ctime`, `crtime` + forensic `dtime`.
- ⭐ **Block pointer tree (ext2/3):** 12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double + 1 triple.
- ⭐ **ext4 extents** = `(start_logical, start_physical, length)` tuples in B-tree.
- ⭐ **Max file size:** 2 TiB (ext2/3) | 16 TiB (ext4) | 1 EiB (ext4 volume).
- ⭐ **Journal** in ext3/ext4: `journal`, `ordered`, `writeback` modes.
- ⭐ Forensics tools: **TSK (`fls`, `istat`, `icat`)**, **Autopsy**, **extundelete**, **TestDisk**, **PhotoRec**.
- ⭐ **Deleted file recovery** depends on `i_dtime`, intact inode, and unallocated block availability.
- ⭐ **Journal analysis** (`jls`, `jcat`) recovers recent transactions and pre-crash data.
- ⭐ Inode count is **fixed at mkfs time** — cannot grow without reformatting.
- ⭐ **Block group** = locality optimization with its own bitmaps, GDT entry, and inode table.
- ⭐ **Inline data** in ext4 = small files stored inside the inode (forensic trap).
- ⭐ **Metadata checksums** in ext4 detect tampering and corruption.
- ⭐ Boot sequence: read superblock → validate magic → replay journal → mount.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EXT2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EXT2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXT2 (Second Extended File System)** is the standard Linux file system that was the default file system for many Linux distributions before being succeeded by EXT3 and EXT4. It is a **block-based, hierarchical, inode-centric file system** designed by Rémy Card in 1993 for the Linux kernel, and remains a foundational subject of study in digital forensics due to its deterministic, statically allocated metadata structures.

## Formal Definition

**EXT2** is a **non-journaling, fixed-block-size, contiguous-block-grouping file system** that organizes a disk partition into a series of structural blocks. It divides the partition into three primary logical regions:
1. **Boot Sector (Block 0)** – Reserved for bootstrapping code (typically unused by the file system itself).
2. **Group Blocks (Block 1 to N)** – The main file system data area, divided into uniform **Block Groups**.
3. **Superblock Backup** – Redundant copies stored at the start of specific block groups (1, 3, 5, 7 powers of 3, 5, 7) for crash recovery.

The file system uses **inodes (index nodes)** as the primary metadata structure to describe every file and directory. Each file in EXT2 is described by exactly **one inode**, which contains all metadata except the file name (which is stored in directory entries).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Forensic Insight**: Because EXT2 does not use a journal, every metadata operation is written *synchronously* to its final on-disk location. This makes EXT2 forensics more deterministic and allows for the recovery of deleted file data through inode analysis — a critical investigative technique.

## Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **massive library**:
- The **Superblock** is the *master catalog* at the entrance — it tells you how many shelves exist, where they are, and the library's overall layout.
- The **Block Groups** are *floors* of the library, each holding a subset of books.
- The **Group Descriptor Table** is a *directory board* on every floor showing where the books (data), catalogs (inodes), and free space maps are on that floor.
- The **Inode Table** is a *card catalog* — every book has exactly one catalog card listing its title, author, shelf, size, and access times.
- **Data Blocks** are the *actual books on the shelves*.
- A **Directory** is a *special shelf* that doesn't hold books but holds index cards pointing to other shelves (sub-directories) or book cards (files).

When a book is "deleted" from the library, the card is marked "RETURNED" (inode unlinked), but the book physically remains on the shelf until someone puts a new book there. A forensic investigator can still read the book if the shelves haven't been overwritten — this is the foundation of EXT2 data recovery.

## Key Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Magic Number** | **0xEF53** | Superblock signature identifying EXT2/3/4 |
| **Block Size** | **1024, 2048, 4096 bytes** | Configurable at creation |
| **Max File Size** | **2 TB** (with 4 KB blocks) | Limited by 32-bit block pointers |
| **Max Volume Size** | **16 TB** | With 4 KB blocks |
| **Inode Size** | **128 bytes** (default) | Fixed in EXT2 |
| **Reserved UID/GID** | **0** | Root user |
| **Block Group Count** | Variable | Partition size ÷ blocks per group |

> [!TIP]
> The magic number **0xEF53** is the first thing a forensic tool checks to confirm an EXT2 file system. If you see this hex value at offset 0x438 in a partition image, you are looking at a valid EXT2 superblock.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Three-Tier On-Disk Layout

The EXT2 partition is structured as a strict hierarchy. Understanding this layout is essential for forensic analysis, as each component reveals a specific class of evidence.

### Tier 1: The Superblock (Block 1, Offset 1024 bytes)

The **superblock** is the most critical metadata structure in EXT2. It is the first structure read by the kernel when mounting the file system, and it defines all global parameters.

**Location**: Always at **byte offset 1024** (block 1) from the start of the partition. Backup copies are stored at the start of specific block groups (group numbers that are 0, 1, 3, 5, 7 powers of 3, 5, and 7).

**Contents include**:
- Total inode count
- Total block count
- Block size
- Blocks per group
- Inodes per group
- Mount count and max mount count
- File system state (clean, errors)
- Last mounted path
- Magic signature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Significance**: A corrupted or overwritten superblock can be reconstructed by forensic tools (e.g., `mke2fs -S`, `e2fsck -b`) using any of the backup copies. Investigators always verify the magic number **0xEF53** first to validate the file system.

### Tier 2: Block Groups (The Partitioned Workspace)

EXT2 divides the entire partition into **Block Groups** of equal size. Each block group contains a redundant copy of structural data to improve performance and reliability.

**Block Group Layout (in order)**:
1. **Superblock** (only in groups 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, powers of 3, 5, 7; else unused)
2. **Group Descriptors** (a table describing the group)
3. **Data Block Bitmap** (one block, tracks allocated data blocks)
4. **Inode Bitmap** (one block, tracks allocated inodes)
5. **Inode Table** (multiple blocks, contains all inodes for this group)
6. **Data Blocks** (actual file content)

### Tier 3: Inodes and Data Blocks

Each **inode** is a 128-byte structure containing 15 block pointers (12 direct, 1 indirect, 1 double-indirect, 1 triple-indirect), file mode, ownership, timestamps, and size.

## Why "Block Groups" Matter in Forensics

> [!NOTE]
> **Fragmentation Insight**: Because related files and their inodes are kept in the same block group, EXT2 achieves good performance. Forensically, this means **temporal locality** — files created around the same time are often clustered in the same block group, helping investigators reconstruct activity timelines.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Group Size** | $BG_{size} = s\_blocks\_per\_group \times s\_block\_size$ | Size in bytes of one block group |
| **Number of Block Groups** | $N_{bg} = \lceil s\_blocks\_count \; / \; s\_blocks\_per\_group \rceil$ | Total block groups in partition |
| **Inode Number to Group** | $bg = (inode\_num - 1) \; / \; s\_inodes\_per\_group$ | Locate which group an inode belongs to |
| **Inode Offset in Group** | $offset = (inode\_num - 1) \; \bmod \; s\_inodes\_per\_group$ | Index within the group's inode table |
| **Inode Byte Offset** | $byte\_off = bg \times BG_{size} + inode\_table\_start + offset \times 128$ | Absolute disk offset of inode |
| **Maximum File Size** | $Max_{size} = 12 + (b/s) + (b/s)^2 + (b/s)^3$ blocks | Where b = block size, s = pointer size (4 bytes) |
| **Block Bitmap Location** | Group start + descriptor table size | Find free block map |
| **Inode Bitmap Location** | After block bitmap | Find free inode map |
| **Magic Number Check** | $0xEF53$ at offset 0x438 | Validate EXT2 superblock |

> [!WARNING]
> All offsets and structural locations above assume **little-endian byte order** (x86 architecture). Forensic tools like `dd`, `hexdump`, and Sleuth Kit's `fsstat` handle this conversion automatically, but manual analysis in KTU exams requires explicit byte-order notation.

## Real-World Utility in Engineering

In **digital forensics and incident response**, EXT2 analysis enables:
- **Deleted file recovery** by scanning unallocated inodes and data blocks.
- **Timeline reconstruction** using MAC (Modified, Accessed, Changed) timestamps from inodes.
- **Slack space analysis** to find hidden data written between file end and block boundary.
- **Metadata forensics** to detect tampering by comparing inode timestamps against expected values.
- **Anti-forensics detection** by identifying `wipe`, `shred`, or `srm` operations that zero-out data blocks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Calculating the Number of Block Groups

Given an EXT2 partition with:
- Total blocks ($s\_blocks\_count$) = **8192 blocks**
- Blocks per group ($s\_blocks\_per\_group$) = **8192 blocks**

**Step-by-step derivation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bg} &= \lceil s\_blocks\_count \; / \; s\_blocks\_per\_group \rceil \\[6pt]
N_{bg} &= \lceil 8192 \; / \; 8192 \rceil \\[6pt]
N_{bg} &= \lceil 1.0 \rceil \\[6pt]
N_{bg} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: The ceiling function ensures that any remaining partial block group is counted as a full group. Since 8192 divides exactly into 1 group, we have exactly 1 block group. If we had 16384 total blocks, we would have 2 groups.

## Derivation 2: Locating an Inode on Disk

**Problem**: An investigator finds inode number **4721** in an EXT2 file system. The parameters are:
- $s\_inodes\_per\_group = 1024$
- $s\_blocks\_per\_group = 8192$
- $s\_block\_size = 4096$ bytes
- Inode table starts at block 5 within each group

**Step 1: Find the block group containing the inode**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bg &= (inode\_num - 1) \; / \; s\_inodes\_per\_group \\[6pt]
bg &= (4721 - 1) \; / \; 1024 \\[6pt]
bg &= 4720 \; / \; 1024 \\[6pt]
bg &= 4.609375 \\[6pt]
bg &= 4 \quad \text{(integer division, group index)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: Inode numbers start at 1, not 0. Subtracting 1 before division maps 1-based inode numbering to 0-based indexing. Group 0 contains inodes 1–1024, group 1 contains 1025–2048, group 2 contains 2049–3072, group 3 contains 3073–4096, and group 4 contains 4097–5120. Since 4721 is in range 4097–5120, it is in group 4.

**Step 2: Find the offset within the group's inode table**

$$
\begin{aligned}
offset &= (inode\_num - 1) \; \bmod \; s\_inodes\_per\_group \\[6pt]
offset &= (4721 - 1) \; \bmod \; 1024 \\[6pt]
offset &= 4720 \; \bmod \; 1024 \\[6pt]
offset &= 4720 - (4 \times 1024) \\[6pt]
offset &= 4720 - 4096 \\[6pt]
offset &= 624
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: The modulo operation gives the position within the group. Inode 4721 is the 625th inode (0-indexed: 624) within group 4's inode table.

**Step 3: Calculate the byte offset on disk**

$$
\begin{aligned}
byte\_off &= (bg \times s\_blocks\_per\_group + inode\_table\_start\_block) \times s\_block\_size + offset \times 128 \\[6pt]
byte\_off &= (4 \times 8192 + 5) \times 4096 + 624 \times 128 \\[6pt]
byte\_off &= (32768 + 5) \times 4096 + 79872 \\[6pt]
byte\_off &= 32773 \times 4096 + 79872 \\[6pt]
byte\_off &= 134,295,808 + 79,872 \\[6pt]
byte\_off &= 134,375,680
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: The first term locates the start of group 4's inode table in bytes; the second term locates the specific 128-byte inode record within that table.

## Derivation 3: Maximum File Size Calculation

**Setup**: For a 4 KB block size and 4-byte pointers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Max_{file} &= (12 + (b/s) + (b/s)^2 + (b/s)^3) \times b \text{ bytes} \\[6pt]
\text{where } b &= 4096, \quad s = 4 \\[6pt]
b/s &= 4096 / 4 = 1024 \\[6pt]
Max_{file} &= (12 + 1024 + 1024^2 + 1024^3) \times 4096 \text{ bytes} \\[6pt]
Max_{file} &= (12 + 1024 + 1{,}048{,}576 + 1{,}073{,}741{,}824) \times 4096 \\[6pt]
Max_{file} &= 1{,}074{,}792{,}436 \times 4096 \\[6pt]
Max_{file} &= 4{,}402{,}346,\!538,\!496 \text{ bytes} \\[6pt]
Max_{file} &\approx 4 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: 12 direct blocks hold small files efficiently. The single indirect block can address up to 1024 more blocks. Double-indirect handles up to 1M blocks. Triple-indirect handles up to 1G blocks. Multiplying by block size gives the maximum logical file size.

## Analytical Matrix: EXT2 Forensic Operations

| Forensic Task | Data Structure Examined | Tool / Method | Information Recovered |
|---|---|---|---|
| Verify file system | Superblock | `fsstat`, `hexdump` | Magic number, block size, state |
| List deleted files | Unallocated inodes | `icat`, `istat`, `ils` | File metadata, block pointers |
| Recover file content | Data blocks of unallocated inodes | `icat -r`, `dd` | Actual deleted file data |
| Reconstruct timeline | Inode MAC timestamps | `istat`, `fls -m` | Created, modified, accessed times |
| Find slack space | Last data block of file | `blkls`, manual extraction | Hidden data between EOF and block end |
| Detect file system tampering | Multiple superblock copies | `dumpe2fs`, `e2fsck` | Discrepancies indicate corruption or attack |
| Identify file system type | Superblock magic number | `file` command | EXT2 vs EXT3 vs EXT4 |
| Map unallocated space | Block & inode bitmaps | `ifind`, `ffind` | Free clusters, available inodes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Overall EXT2 Partition Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Boot Sector Block 0 - 1024 bytes] --> B[Superblock Block 1 - 1024 bytes]

    C0[Group 0 Contains] --> C1[Superblock Backup]
    C0 --> C2[Group Descriptor Table]

    C0 --> C4[Inode Bitmap]
    C0 --> C5[Inode Table]
    C0 --> C6[Data Blocks]
```

## Diagram 4: Forensic Investigation Workflow on EXT2

```mermaid

    A[Acquire Disk Image with dd] --> B[Verify Integrity with SHA256]
    B --> C[Identify File System with file command]

    D -->|Yes| F[Run fsstat for Superblock Analysis]
```

## Diagram 5: File Deletion Lifecycle in EXT2

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[File Allocated] --> B[Inode Marked Used in Bitmap]
```
