- ⭐ **EXT4 Recovery Speed:** 2–3× faster than EXT3 due to checksum-based skipping.
- ⭐ **Pitfall to Avoid:** Do not say `writeback` is "unsafe"; it is *metadata-safe but data-unsafe*.
- ⭐ **Pitfall to Avoid:** Do not forget the journal recovery's `s_tail` boundary check.
- ⭐ **Pitfall to Avoid:** Do not confuse EXT3/EXT4 journal superblock with the primary FS superblock — they are **separate** structures at **different** offsets.
- ⭐ **Examiner Favourite Question:** "Explain the role of the descriptor block in a journal transaction." — Practice this!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** Memorise the three journaling modes in a single table, the journal magic `0xC03B3998`, and the recovery boundary `s_tail → s_sequence`. These three facts alone account for **40%** of typical ESE marks on this topic.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HFS (Hierarchical File System)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** HFS (Hierarchical File System)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 1 — HFS (HIERARCHICAL FILE SYSTEM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hierarchical File System (HFS)** is a proprietary file system developed by **Apple Computer** in **1985** for the original Macintosh (System 1.0 through Mac OS 9.2.2). It was the first commercial file system to introduce a true **hierarchical (tree-structured) directory** model for organising files on a volume, replacing the flat **MFS (Macintosh File System)** that shipped with the very first Macs in 1984.

From a KTU 2024 Digital Forensics perspective, HFS is a **legacy forensic artefact** of immense value because:

- It introduces the **B-Tree (Balanced Tree)** on-disk indexing paradigm for catalog searches.
- It separates file content from file metadata into two distinct streams called **forks** (Data Fork and Resource Fork) — a feature unique to classic Mac OS that heavily influences forensic data recovery.
- It uses a peculiar **epoch date of January 1, 1904 (04:00:00 UTC)**, which is critical for correct timestamp interpretation.
- It supports **dual file forks**, a multi-fork metadata scheme rarely found in other operating systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HFS vs HFS+ (Plus) — A Common Confusion Point**
> When KTU examiners ask about "HFS", they generally expect an answer that contrasts the **original HFS** (1985) with its successor **HFS+ (1998)** and the modern **APFS (2017)**. The original HFS is sometimes called **HFS Standard** or **HFS Classic**, with a 16-bit allocation table that limited volumes to **65,535 allocation blocks**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Box Library" Intuition

Imagine a small public library in 1985 that needs a new catalogue system.

- **The Old System (MFS):** All books are dumped into one giant cardboard box. To find a book, the librarian has to dig through every single book. Slow, painful, and there is no concept of sub-sections.
- **The New System (HFS):** The library is reorganised into **drawers (directories) → folders (sub-directories) → files (books)**. The library has a **Master Index (the Catalog File)** that lists which drawer contains which folder, and which folder contains which book. The index itself is structured as a **balanced binary-tree catalogue** (a B-Tree) so that even with millions of entries, the librarian can find any book in roughly the **same number of steps** (logarithmic time).
- **The "Two-Box" Twist:** Every book in this library actually lives in **two separate boxes on the shelf**:
  - **Box A (Data Fork):** Contains the actual readable content — the story, the diagrams, the text.
  - **Box B (Resource Fork):** Contains the metadata — the index, glossary, font, the icons, the menu definitions.
  - A librarian pulling a book must consult both boxes to fully serve the patron.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters in forensics:** When a file is "deleted" in HFS, the *catalog entry* is removed (so the OS forgets the file), but the *blocks holding both forks* are simply marked as free. A forensic investigator can therefore often recover **both the visible content AND the hidden metadata** of deleted files — making HFS a goldmine for evidence recovery.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in HFS

| Metric | Value | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Epoch date** | **January 1, 1904 (00:00:00)** | All timestamps are 32-bit unsigned seconds counted from this date. |
| **Maximum volume size (theoretical)** | **2 TB (terabytes)** | 2³² allocation blocks × 65,536 bytes/block |
| **Maximum file size (theoretical)** | **2³¹ − 1 bytes ≈ 2 GB** | 32-bit logical file size field |
| **Maximum filename length** | **255 characters** | UTF-16 friendly (HFS+) |
| **Block size (allocation block)** | **512 bytes to 65,536 bytes** | Configurable; affects wasted space |
| **Case sensitivity** | **Case-INSENSITIVE but case-PRESERVING** | `MyFile.txt` and `myfile.TXT` are the same file. |
| **Unicode** | Originally **MacRoman**; HFS+ supports **UTF-16** | Critical for cross-platform evidence sharing. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Always mention the **1904 epoch** in your answer. It is the single most-tested fact in any HFS-related examination question. Many forensic tools default to the **Unix epoch (1970)** and will display Mac dates as huge negative numbers if not configured correctly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Pillars of HFS

HFS volume layout is composed of **five core structural on-disk artefacts** that every forensic investigator must understand. They are organised into a layered architecture.

### 2.1.1 The Volume Header (Boot Block Region)

The first **1024 bytes (2 sectors of 512 bytes each)** of an HFS volume are reserved as the **Boot Block** area. In Apple terminology, this region is actually divided into:

1. **Bytes 0–1023: Boot Blocks (BB)** — Contains the system startup code. On non-bootable volumes (e.g., a data drive) this is usually zeroed out.
2. **Bytes 1024–2047: Volume Header (VH)** — The **true master record** of the volume.

The Volume Header stores **the four most critical pointers** to the rest of the file system:

| Pointer | Field Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **drSigWord** | Signature Word | Always `0x4244` ('BD' in ASCII = "Big Difference"). Verifies the volume is HFS-formatted. |
| **drVHBkSz** | Volume Header Block Size | **2 bytes** — typically 512. |
| **drAlBlkSiz** | Allocation Block Size | **4 bytes** — usually 4096 or 8192 bytes. |
| **drNmAlBlks** | Number of Allocation Blocks | **2 bytes** — limits HFS volumes to **65,535** blocks. |
| **drCTExtRec** / **drCTFlBk** | Catalog File extent & first block | Points to the **Catalog B-Tree**. |
| **drXTExtRec** / **drXTFlBk** | Extents Overflow File | Tracks fragments of badly fragmented files. |
| **drAllocPtr** | Allocation Bitmap File | Tracks which allocation blocks are in use. |

> [!CAUTION]
> The **signature word `0x4244` ('BD')** at offset 1024 is the first thing a forensic tool checks to *identify* an HFS volume. A missing or corrupted signature does not mean data is gone — it just means the OS can no longer mount the volume, and forensic carving tools must step in.

### 2.1.2 The Catalog File (The Master Index)

This is the **heart of HFS** and the most forensically valuable structure.

- It is implemented as a **B-Tree** (specifically, a B*Tree variant — each node uses 75% density minimum).
- Each **node** in the Catalog is **512 bytes** in HFS (in HFS+ it can be up to 65,536 bytes).
- A **node descriptor** (14 bytes) precedes every node and identifies:
  - `ndType` (Node type)
  - `ndNHeight` (Height in tree: 0=leaf, 1=branch, 2=root, 3=map)
  - `ndNRecs` (Number of records)
  - `ndFLink` / `ndBLink` (Forward and Backward sibling links in a balanced tree)

**Catalog records** store:

- `cFilNam` — Filename (up to 255 characters)
- `cParID` — Parent directory ID
- `cNodeID` — Catalog Node ID (CNID) — unique ID for every file/folder
- **For folders:** `cThngRec` (Thread Record) — links back to parent
- **For files:** `cFilRec` (File Record) — includes data fork extent record, resource fork extent record, file type/creator codes, Finder flags, creation/modification dates.

> [!TIP]
> **CNIDs (Catalog Node IDs):** These are 32-bit identifiers (1–2³²−1). Reserved CNIDs include:
> - `2` = Root directory
> - `16` = Trash folder
> - `1, 3, 5–15` = Special system files
>
> Any other CNID in the Catalog corresponds to a user-created file or folder. This makes **CNID 2** the **most important ID in HFS forensics** — it is the root of the directory tree.

### 2.1.3 The Extents Overflow File (The Fragmentation Tracker)

HFS, like FAT, stores file extents (contiguous runs of allocation blocks) directly inside the Catalog record. However, a **single file record has only 3 extent descriptors** (each describing a contiguous run of blocks). If a file is more fragmented than that, **extra extents** are stored in the **Extents Overflow File** (also a B-Tree).

> Each extent descriptor in HFS is **4 bytes for the starting block + 2 bytes for the block count** = 6 bytes.

### 2.1.4 The Allocation Bitmap File (The Free-Space Map)

HFS uses a **Bitmap** to track which allocation blocks are in use.

- `0 bit` = block is free.
- `1 bit` = block is allocated.
- Stored in a regular file whose **Catalog Node ID (CNID) is 6**.

The starting block of the Allocation file is in the Volume Header's `drAllocPtr` field. **Forensic Note:** The bitmap is *not* updated until the OS performs a `flush` operation, so the bitmap is often **inconsistent with the Catalog** — and those inconsistencies are the bread and butter of forensic analysis.

### 2.1.5 The Attributes File (Reserved in HFS)

HFS reserves space for an Attributes B-Tree (used to store extended attribute records such as long filenames, file aliases, etc.) but the original HFS does **not actively use** it — this came into play in HFS+. In HFS, this file is allocated but its B-Tree is usually empty.

## 2.2 The Dual-Fork Architecture (Data Fork + Resource Fork)

This is the **single most unique feature** of classic Mac file systems and the most heavily tested concept in KTU 2024 ESE questions on HFS.

| Fork | Purpose | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Fork** | Contains the "raw" data of the file. Treated as an **opaque byte stream**. | Plain text, image pixels, audio samples, executable code. |
| **Resource Fork** | A structured **key-value metadata store**, segmented into 256-byte "resource records" each with a 4-byte type code, 2-byte ID, and a string (optional name). | Icons, menu templates, fonts, dialog boxes, version info, localisation data, code segments. |

Both forks have:
- Independent **logical lengths**.
- Independent **physical extents** on disk.
- Independent **lock bits**.

When a user opens a file in a Mac application, the application typically opens **both forks simultaneously**:
```text
Open File "MyDocument"
  ├── OpenDataFork(...)    → reads the document's content
  └── OpenRsrcFork(...)    → reads the icons, fonts, etc.
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-platform implications:** When a Mac HFS volume is connected to a Windows or Linux machine, only the **Data Fork** is visible. The **Resource Fork** appears as either an invisible hidden file with a `%` prefix (e.g., `MyDocument.text` and `._MyDocument.text`) or, more commonly, is simply lost in transfer. **Forensically, this means valuable evidence often lives in the Resource Fork and survives only on the original Mac disk image.** This is a frequent KTU essay question.

## 2.3 HFS+ (Apple HFS Extended) — The Modern Successor

When students refer to "HFS" in current forensic practice, they almost always mean **HFS+** (also called **Mac OS Extended** or simply **HFS Plus**), introduced in **1998 with Mac OS 8.1**. Key improvements:

| Feature | HFS (Classic) | HFS+ |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum volume size | **2 GB** (16-bit) | **2 TiB** (32-bit allocation blocks) |
| Maximum file size | **2 GB** | **2 TiB** (then 8 EiB in APFS) |
| Catalog record size | 512 bytes | **8 KB** (variable) |
| Node size | 512 bytes (fixed) | **Up to 65,536 bytes** |
| Unicode | No (MacRoman) | **Yes (UTF-16)** |
| File forks | 2 (Data + Resource) | Still 2, but more structured |
| Journaling | No | Optional, then standard |
| B-Tree type | B*Tree | **B-Tree (full)** |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — HFS Forensics

| Concept | Value / Definition | Forensic Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume Signature** | `0x4244` at offset 1024 | First step in volume identification |
| **Epoch** | `01-Jan-1904 00:00:00 UTC` | All timestamps based on this. |
| **Date range** | `01-Jan-1904 → 06-Jun-2040` (32-bit overflow) | Add timestamps to forensic report. |
| **Root directory CNID** | `2` | Top of the directory hierarchy. |
| **Trash folder CNID** | `16` | Recover "deleted" files here first. |
| **Allocation File CNID** | `6` | Located via Volume Header `drAllocPtr`. |
| **Max allocation blocks** | `65,535` (16-bit) | Why HFS Classic caps at ~2 GB. |
| **Filename length** | `255 chars` | Truncated to 31 in MFS. |
| **Case handling** | Insensitive, Preserving | Match by canonical form. |
| **Number of forks** | `2` (Data + Resource) | Crucial for evidence preservation. |
| **Resource fork record size** | `256 bytes` | Each is a typed entry. |
| **B-Tree node size** | `512 bytes` (Classic HFS) | Hard upper limit on record size. |
| **Max extent descriptors in catalog** | `3 per fork` | Overflow goes to Extents file. |
| **B-Tree header node ID** | `0` | Identifies the master B-Tree node. |
| **B-Tree map node ID** | `1` | Tracks which node records are used. |
| **B-Tree header root node ID** | `2` | The actual root of the B-Tree. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Calculating HFS Capacity

## 3.1 Problem Statement

You are given an HFS Classic volume with the following parameters reported by a forensic imaging tool:

- `drAlBlkSiz` (Allocation Block Size) = **4096 bytes**
- `drNmAlBlks` (Number of Allocation Blocks) = **12,345**
- `drVHBkSz` (Volume Header Block Size) = **512 bytes**
- `drCTExtRec` (Catalog File Extents) = occupies 32 allocation blocks starting at block 8

**Task 1:** Calculate the **total usable capacity** of the volume.
**Task 2:** Calculate the **percentage of the volume consumed by the Catalog File**.
**Task 3:** Determine the **maximum date value** that can be stored in a 32-bit HFS timestamp field before overflow.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation

### Step 1 — Total Usable Capacity

The total logical capacity of an HFS volume is the product of the **allocation block size** and the **number of allocation blocks**. This is because the volume is partitioned into a fixed number of equal-sized chunks, and each chunk is the smallest unit of allocation.

$$
C_{\text{total}} \;=\; \text{drAlBlkSiz} \times \text{drNmAlBlks}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
C_{\text{total}} \;=\; 4096 \, \frac{\text{bytes}}{\text{block}} \times 12{,}345 \, \text{blocks}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} \;=\; 4096 \times 12{,}345 \;=\; 50{,}565{,}120 \text{ bytes}
$$

To express this in human-readable units, we divide by $1024^2$ (since $1 \text{ MiB} = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes):

$$
C_{\text{total in MiB}} \;=\; \frac{50{,}565{,}120}{1{,}048{,}576} \;\approx\; 48.2266 \text{ MiB}
$$

To express in megabytes (decimal, $1 \text{ MB} = 1{,}000{,}000$ bytes):

$$
C_{\text{total in MB}} \;=\; \frac{50{,}565{,}120}{1{,}000{,}000} \;\approx\; 50.5651 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Result:** The total capacity is **50,565,120 bytes ≈ 48.23 MiB ≈ 50.57 MB**.

---

### Step 2 — Catalog File Size & Percentage

The Catalog File occupies 32 allocation blocks, so its size in bytes is:

$$
S_{\text{catalog}} \;=\; 32 \, \text{blocks} \times 4096 \, \frac{\text{bytes}}{\text{block}} \;=\; 131{,}072 \text{ bytes}
$$

The percentage of the volume consumed by the Catalog is:

$$
P_{\text{catalog}} \;=\; \frac{S_{\text{catalog}}}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

$$
P_{\text{catalog}} \;=\; \frac{131{,}072}{50{,}565{,}120} \times 100 \;\approx\; 0.2592\,\%
$$

Forensic implication: even though the Catalog is a tiny fraction of the disk, it is the **most information-dense structure on the volume**. A skilled forensic analyst can reconstruct the entire directory tree from the Catalog alone.

---

### Step 3 — Maximum 32-bit Timestamp Before Overflow

HFS uses a **32-bit unsigned integer** to count seconds elapsed since **01-Jan-1904 00:00:00 UTC**. The maximum representable value is:

$$
T_{\max} \;=\; 2^{32} - 1 \;=\; 4{,}294{,}967{,}295 \text{ seconds}
$$

Converting to days:

$$
T_{\max \text{ in days}} \;=\; \frac{4{,}294{,}967{,}295}{86{,}400} \;\approx\; 49{,}710.27 \text{ days}
$$

Converting to years (using 365.25 days/year for leap-year averaging):

$$
T_{\max \text{ in years}} \;=\; \frac{49{,}710.27}{365.25} \;\approx\; 136.10 \text{ years}
$$

Adding this to the epoch date **01-Jan-1904**:

$$
\text{Overflow Date} \;\approx\; \text{01-Jan-1904} + 136.10 \text{ years} \;\approx\; \text{06-Feb-2040}
$$

> **Result:** An HFS 32-bit timestamp will overflow on approximately **06 February 2040**. After this date, the timestamp will wrap around to 1904, and forensic timestamps will appear nonsensical. This is a **real concern** for long-term evidence archiving and one of the reasons Apple transitioned to APFS.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — HFS vs MFS vs HFS+ vs APFS

| Property | **MFS (1984)** | **HFS Classic (1985)** | **HFS+ (1998)** | **APFS (2017)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hierarchy | Flat | Hierarchical | Hierarchical | Hierarchical |
| Catalog index | Linear | B*Tree (512 B nodes) | B-Tree (up to 64 KB nodes) | B-Tree (in-memory) |
| Max volume | 2 MB | ~2 GB | ~2 TiB (8 EiB on 64-bit) | 8 ZiB |
| Max file | 2 MB | 2 GB | 2 TiB | 8 ZiB |
| Forks | 1 | 2 | 2 | 1 (extended attrs) |
| Journaling | No | No | Optional → Standard | Yes (atomic) |
| Clones / Snapshots | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Encryption | No | No | Optional (FileVault) | Native |
| Unicode | No | No (MacRoman) | Yes (UTF-16) | Yes (UTF-8) |
| Epoch | 1904 | 1904 | 1904 | 1970 (nanoseconds) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HFS Volume On-Disk Layout (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Vol["HFS VOLUME — Logical Layout (from byte 0)"]
        direction TB
        BB["Boot Blocks (Bytes 0-1023) — 2 sectors of 512 B each. Contains system startup code. Often zeroed on data volumes."]
        VH["Volume Header (Bytes 1024-2047) — Master record. Signature 0x4244. Stores pointers to all 5 system files."]
        AF["Allocation Bitmap File (CNID 6) — Bitmap tracking which allocation blocks are free or in use."]
        CF["Catalog File (CNID 4) — B-Tree. Stores hierarchy of all files and folders. Each file/folder has a unique CNID."]
        EOF["Extents Overflow File (CNID 3) — B-Tree. Stores extra extent descriptors for highly fragmented files."]
        ATTR["Attributes File (CNID 8) — Reserved B-Tree. Originally unused in HFS Classic; enabled in HFS+."]
        USR["User Data Region — The remaining allocation blocks hold actual file content (data forks, resource forks)."]
    end

    BB --> VH
    VH -- "drAllocPtr" --> AF
    VH -- "drCTFlBk" --> CF
    VH -- "drXTEFlBk" --> EOF
    VH -- "drAttrPtr" --> ATTR
    CF --> USR
    EOF --> USR
    AF -.-> USR
```

## 4.2 Catalog B-Tree Node Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Node["HFS B-Tree Node (512 bytes — Classic HFS)"]
        direction TB
        ND["Node Descriptor (14 bytes) — Stores: ndFwd, ndBwd, ndType, ndHeight, ndNRecs, ndResv2."]
        REC["Records Area (498 bytes) — Catalog records (cFilRec, cDirRec, cThngRec). Each record has variable length up to 512 bytes."]
        FREE["Free Space (offset at end) — Unused bytes in this node."]
    end
    ND --> REC --> FREE
```

## 4.3 Dual-Fork File Storage — Visual Block Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F["A Single HFS File (e.g., 'MyDocument')"]
        direction LR
        F1["Catalog Record: cFilRec, CNID = 42, filename = MyDocument"]
    end

    subgraph DF["Data Fork"]
        direction TB
        DF1["Ext1: Blocks 100-103 (4 blocks × 4096 B = 16 KB)"]
        DF2["Ext2: Blocks 220-225 (6 blocks × 4096 B = 24 KB)"]
    end

    subgraph RF["Resource Fork"]
        direction TB
        RF1["Ext1: Blocks 500-500 (1 block × 4096 B = 4 KB)"]
    end

    F1 -- "data fork extents" --> DF
    F1 -- "resource fork extents" --> RF
    DF1 --- DF2
```

## 4.4 Forensics Workflow — HFS Volume Examination (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Acquire Bit-Stream Image (e.g., using dd, FTK Imager, or Guymager)"]
    B["Step 2: Verify Signature 0x4244 at offset 1024 — confirms HFS volume"]
    C["Step 3: Parse Volume Header — extract pointers to Catalog, Extents, Bitmap, Attributes files"]
    D["Step 4: Walk the Catalog B-Tree starting at CNID 2 (root) — rebuild full directory hierarchy"]
    E["Step 5: For each file, extract both Data and Resource Fork extents — preserve both for evidence"]
    F["Step 6: Check Allocation Bitmap vs Catalog — identify inconsistencies to find deleted files"]
    G["Step 7: Search CNID 16 (Trash) — recover 'moved' deleted items first"]
    H["Step 8: Apply Epoch Correction (1904 base) to all timestamps before reporting"]
    I["Step 9: Generate Forensic Report with all metadata, hashes, and timeline"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]
**Q: Define the Hierarchical File System (HFS). List any three of its key structural features.**

**Model Answer:**
HFS (Hierarchical File System) is a file system developed by Apple Computer in 1985 for use in the Macintosh operating system. It was the first to introduce a true hierarchical (tree-structured) directory model for organising files on a volume, replacing the flat MFS.

Three key structural features:
1. **B-Tree Catalog:** File and directory metadata is stored in a B-Tree indexed file (the Catalog File) for fast lookups.
2. **Dual Fork Architecture:** Every file has two independent forks — a Data Fork (raw content) and a Resource Fork (structured metadata).
3. **Epoch Date of 1904:** All timestamps are stored as 32-bit unsigned seconds elapsed since 01-Jan-1904 00:00:00 UTC.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]
**Q: What is the significance of Catalog Node ID (CNID) 2 in HFS? Mention two other reserved CNIDs.**

**Model Answer:**
CNID 2 in HFS is reserved for the **root directory** — it is the topmost entry in the hierarchical directory tree from which the entire folder structure branches. Every other file or folder in the system can be traced back to CNID 2 through its `cParID` (Parent ID) chain.

Two other reserved CNIDs:
- **CNID 3:** Extents Overflow File (tracks fragmented file extents).
- **CNID 6:** Allocation Bitmap File (tracks which allocation blocks are in use).
- **CNID 16:** Trash folder.
- **CNID 4:** Catalog File itself.
- **CNID 8:** Attributes File.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **on-disk volume structure** of an HFS Classic volume with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the **Volume Header** and the **Allocation Bitmap File** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **dual-fork architecture** of HFS. Explain how the **Data Fork** and **Resource Fork** differ in their structure, and discuss **three forensic implications** of this dual-fork design. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The HFS Classic volume on-disk structure is laid out as follows (from byte 0):

**1. Boot Blocks (0–1023):** The first two sectors of the volume, holding system startup code. On non-bootable volumes these are typically zeroed.

**2. Volume Header (1024–2047):** This is the most critical structure of the volume. It contains:
- `drSigWord` (2 bytes) — Signature `0x4244` ('BD' = "Big Difference"). Used to identify the volume as HFS.
- `drVHBkSz` (2 bytes) — Volume Header block size.
- `drAlBlkSiz` (4 bytes) — Allocation block size (e.g., 4096 bytes).
- `drNmAlBlks` (2 bytes) — Total number of allocation blocks (max 65,535 in HFS Classic).
- Pointers to the four special system files:
  - `drCTExtRec` and `drCTFlBk` → Catalog File.
  - `drXTExtRec` and `drXTEFlBk` → Extents Overflow File.
  - `drAllocPtr` → Allocation Bitmap File.
  - `drAttrPtr` → Attributes File.

The Volume Header is **duplicated at the last 1024 bytes** of the volume for redundancy.

**3. Special System Files (Catalog, Extents Overflow, Attributes, Bitmap):** These are stored as regular files at known CNIDs, occupying allocation blocks 3 through ~16.

**4. User Data Region:** The remaining allocation blocks store the actual file content (forks).

**Role of the Volume Header:** Acts as the **master record / table of contents** of the volume. Without it, the OS cannot locate any of the system files, and the volume is effectively unmountable. Forensically, the Volume Header is the **first structure a tool reads** to begin parsing the file system.

**Role of the Allocation Bitmap File (CNID 6):** Maintains a **bit array** in which each bit corresponds to one allocation block. A `0` bit means the block is free; a `1` bit means the block is allocated. Its starting block is given in the Volume Header's `drAllocPtr`. The bitmap is used by the OS during file allocation and deallocation.

*Forensic Insight:* The bitmap is updated **lazily** by the OS — it may not reflect the most recent changes if the system crashed or was improperly shut down. **Inconsistencies between the bitmap and the Catalog** (e.g., a block marked free in the bitmap but still referenced by a Catalog record) can be used to identify **deleted files** whose content can be recovered.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The **dual-fork architecture** is the defining characteristic of HFS file storage. Every file in HFS Classic is logically composed of **two independent byte streams**, stored separately on disk:

**Data Fork:**
- An **opaque (unstructured) sequence of bytes**.
- The application reads it using a simple `Read(offset, length)` interface.
- Stores the actual content of the file (text, pixels, audio samples, executable code).
- Has its own logical length, extent record, and lock flag.

**Resource Fork:**
- A **structured collection of 256-byte resource records**.
- Each record has a 4-byte type code, 2-byte ID, optional name string, and the actual data.
- Stores application-specific structured data: icons, fonts, menus, dialog definitions, code segments, version info.
- Has its own independent logical length, extent record, and lock flag.

When a Mac application opens a file, it typically opens **both forks in parallel**:
```text
Open File "MyDocument"
   ├── Open Data Fork  → reads the text/image
   └── Open Resource Fork → reads the icons/fonts/version
```

**Three Forensic Implications:**

1. **Evidence hiding in Resource Fork:** A user can conceal data inside the Resource Fork. The file may *appear* to be a 1 KB text file (Data Fork), but the Resource Fork could hold megabytes of hidden documents. The `GetFileInfo` command in Mac OS reveals the Resource Fork size, but most casual users never check it.

2. **Cross-platform evidence loss:** When an HFS volume is mounted on Windows or Linux, **only the Data Fork is visible**. The Resource Fork is either lost or stored as a separate hidden file prefixed with `._`. This means investigators must image the disk in its **native HFS environment** before opening it on a foreign OS, or critical evidence will be lost.

3. **Forensic tools must extract both forks:** Standard tools designed for FAT/NTFS may only extract the Data Fork, missing crucial metadata (such as file version, original creator application, icon thumbnails) that lives in the Resource Fork. Specialised Mac forensic tools like **BlackLight** or **MacQuisition** extract both forks by default.

---

### **Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **B-Tree Catalog structure** of HFS. Describe the **Node Descriptor** fields and the role of **catalog records** in building the directory hierarchy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **HFS Classic, HFS+, and APFS** in terms of (i) maximum volume size, (ii) maximum file size, (iii) timestamp epoch, (iv) journaling support, and (v) fork support. Discuss why Apple deprecated HFS in favour of APFS. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Catalog File** in HFS is implemented as a **B*Tree (a B-Tree variant where each node must be at least 75% full)** indexed file. It is the **master index** of the entire volume — without it, the OS cannot locate any file or folder.

**Node Descriptor (14 bytes):** Prepended to every B-Tree node. Contains:
- `ndFwd` (4 bytes) — Forward link to the next node at the same level in the linked list of nodes.
- `ndBwd` (4 bytes) — Backward link to the previous node.
- `ndType` (1 byte) — Type of node: 0x00=index, 0x01=header, 0x02=map, 0x03=leaf.
- `ndHeight` (1 byte) — Height in the B-Tree: 0=leaf, 1=branch, 2=root, 3=header.
- `ndNRecs` (2 bytes) — Number of records stored in this node.
- `ndResv2` (2 bytes) — Reserved.

**Catalog Records:** Stored in the variable-length records area of each node. Three main types:

- **Folder Record (`cDirRec`):** Stored for each directory in the hierarchy. Contains:
  - `cDirName` — Folder name (up to 255 chars).
  - `cDirID` — Folder's CNID.
  - `cDirParID` — Parent folder's CNID.
  - `cDirOff` — Position of the folder in the parent's sub-folder list.
  - `cDirDat` — Folder's modification timestamps.

- **File Record (`cFilRec`):** Stored for each file. Contains:
  - Filename, CNID, parent CNID, position in parent.
  - Data fork extent record (3 extent descriptors).
  - Resource fork extent record (3 extent descriptors).
  - Finder info: file type (4 bytes), creator code (4 bytes), Finder flags.
  - Creation, modification, attribute modification, access timestamps.

- **Thread Record (`cThngRec`):** Links each file/folder back to its parent. This is essential because B-Tree leaves are not stored in hierarchical order — the thread record rebuilds the parent-child link.

**Building the Directory Hierarchy:**
The forensic investigator (or the OS) starts at **CNID 2** (the root directory's folder record), then walks the B-Tree. For each entry, the `cParID` indicates its parent. Recursively traversing all records rebuilds the complete tree.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Property | HFS Classic (1985) | HFS+ (1998) | APFS (2017) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) **Max volume size** | ~2 GB (16-bit blocks) | 2 TiB on 32-bit, 8 EiB on 64-bit | 8 ZiB |
| (ii) **Max file size** | 2 GB (2³¹−1 bytes) | 2 TiB on 32-bit, 8 EiB on 64-bit | 8 ZiB |
| (iii) **Timestamp epoch** | 01-Jan-1904 | 01-Jan-1904 | 01-Jan-1970 (nanoseconds) |
| (iv) **Journaling** | None | Optional (10.2.2) → Standard (10.3) | Native (atomic transactions) |
| (v) **Fork support** | 2 forks (Data + Resource) | 2 forks (Data + Resource) | 1 fork + Extended Attributes (xattrs) |

**Why Apple deprecated HFS in favour of APFS:**

1. **Size limitations:** HFS+ tops out at 2 TiB on 32-bit, which is insufficient for modern SSDs and storage arrays. APFS scales to 8 ZiB.
2. **Performance:** HFS+ uses synchronous writes that block on metadata operations. APFS uses **copy-on-write** and **atomic transactions** that are non-blocking.
3. **Modern features:** APFS supports **clones (instant file copies)**, **snapshots (point-in-time views)**, **native encryption**, and **space sharing** across volumes — none of which HFS+ supports.
4. **Crash safety:** APFS's journaling and copy-on-write ensure the file system is always consistent after a power failure; HFS+ can become corrupted and require disk repair.
5. **Mobile-first design:** APFS is designed to work efficiently on flash/SSD storage (which has no seek time penalty) and on iOS devices where storage is shared between apps.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1904 epoch:** Many students write "timestamps are stored in Unix time". This is a **direct loss of 1–2 marks** in Part A and may cost you 2–3 marks in Part B if it affects your examples.
>
> 2. **Confusing HFS Classic with HFS+:** When asked about "HFS", many students describe HFS+ features (64 KB nodes, UTF-16, journaling) as if they apply to HFS Classic. Examiners will deduct marks. **Always state the variant explicitly** (HFS Classic vs HFS+ vs APFS).
>
> 3. **Ignoring the Resource Fork:** In dual-fork questions, students often describe only the Data Fork. This loses at least **3 of 7 marks** in Part (b). Always state: "Both forks are stored independently and must be extracted for forensic completeness."
>
> 4. **Skipping the bitmap inconsistency discussion:** In the Volume Header question, students describe the header fields but forget the **forensic significance** of bitmap-vs-catalog inconsistencies. Examiners want to see that you understand *why* the bitmap is forensically valuable — not just *what* it stores.
>
> 5. **Not drawing/labeling diagrams:** A question worth 7 marks that asks for a "neat diagram" must include a labelled block diagram of the volume structure. Verbal description alone will lose **2–3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **HFS = Hierarchical File System**, introduced by Apple in **1985**, replacing flat MFS.
- ✅ **First commercial file system** to use a B-Tree indexed Catalog for file lookup.
- ✅ **Volume Header signature** is `0x4244` ('BD' = Big Difference) at offset **1024** of the volume.
- ✅ **Epoch date is 01-Jan-1904 00:00:00 UTC** — *all* timestamps derive from this.
- ✅ **Allocation block size** is configurable (512 B to 65,536 B); **HFS Classic** supports a maximum of **65,535 allocation blocks** (limiting volumes to ~2 GB).
- ✅ **Five core on-disk structures:** Boot Blocks, Volume Header, Catalog File (CNID 4), Extents Overflow File (CNID 3), Allocation Bitmap File (CNID 6), Attributes File (CNID 8).
- ✅ **B-Tree node size in HFS Classic = 512 bytes (fixed)**, with a **14-byte node descriptor** at the start.
- ✅ **Every file has TWO forks:** Data Fork (raw content) and Resource Fork (structured metadata in 256-byte records).
- ✅ **CNID 2 = root directory**, CNID 16 = Trash, CNID 4 = Catalog, CNID 6 = Allocation Bitmap.
- ✅ **Case insensitive but case preserving** — `MyFile.txt` and `myfile.TXT` are the same file.
- ✅ **Filename length = 255 characters** (vs 31 in MFS).
- ✅ **32-bit timestamp overflows on 06-Feb-2040**, which is why Apple moved to APFS.
- ✅ **HFS+ (1998) increased max volume to 2 TiB, supports UTF-16, journaling, larger B-Tree nodes.**
- ✅ **APFS (2017)** uses **copy-on-write**, **clones**, **snapshots**, **native encryption**, **Unix epoch (1970)**, and **no dual forks** (replaced by extended attributes).
- ✅ **Forensic tools must image the disk in its native HFS environment** to preserve the Resource Fork.
- ✅ **Bitmap inconsistencies with the Catalog** are the primary forensic indicator of **deleted files**.
- ✅ **Three reserved record types in the Catalog:** Folder Record, File Record, Thread Record.
- ✅ **Thread records** are critical — they rebuild the parent-child link lost when B-Tree leaves are not in hierarchical order.
- ✅ **HFS Classic max file size = 2³¹ − 1 bytes ≈ 2 GB** — 32-bit signed field.
- ✅ **Extents Overflow File** stores extra extent descriptors when a file's fragmentation exceeds the 3 extents allowed per fork in the Catalog record.
- ✅ **Two B-Tree header records always present:** Header Node (CNID 0) and Map Node (CNID 1).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HFS and HFS+: Structure and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** HFS and HFS+: Structure and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is HFS (Hierarchical File System)?

**HFS (Hierarchical File System)** is the original file system developed by **Apple Inc.** for use on Macintosh computers, introduced in **September 1985** with the first Macintosh computers equipped with the **Motorola 68000** processor. It replaced the earlier flat **MFS (Macintosh File System)**. HFS is often referred to as **Mac OS Standard** and was the default file system for Mac systems from System 2.1 through Mac OS 8.1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> HFS is a file system that organizes files in a hierarchical directory structure using **B-tree** data structures for catalog management, supports resource forks, and uses **16-bit allocation block addressing** to manage disk space.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Old Filing Cabinet

Imagine HFS as an **old-school corporate filing cabinet**:
- Each **drawer** is an *allocation block* (fixed-size container, 512 bytes)
- Each **folder** in the cabinet is a *directory* with a *thread* that points back to its parent folder
- A **master index card file** (the Catalog) at the front tells you which drawer contains what
- Only **65,535 drawers** are addressable (16-bit limit) — this is the cabinet's hard limit
- Labels are in a **single language (MacRoman)** — no international support

This is exactly what early Mac users had: functional, but limited in capacity, multilingualism, and modern features.

## 1.2 What is HFS+ (Hierarchical File System Plus)?

**HFS+**, also known as **Mac OS Extended** or **HFS Extended**, is the successor to HFS. It was introduced in **January 1998** with the release of Mac OS 8.1. HFS+ is currently the default file system for Mac OS X (10.0 onwards) up to macOS versions that later transitioned to **APFS (Apple File System)** in 2017.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> HFS+ is an advanced file system that uses **32-bit allocation block addressing**, supports **Unicode UTF-16** filenames up to **255 characters**, stores all critical metadata in **B-tree structures**, and provides journaling capability (in HFS+ Journaled variant) for improved crash recovery and forensic consistency.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Modern Digital Archive

Think of HFS+ as a **modern, fully computerized archive system**:
- **Massive storage** — billions of drawers (32-bit addressing, ~4.29 billion blocks)
- **Multi-language labels** — Unicode support, so a folder can be named in any language simultaneously
- **Detailed history tracking** — multiple timestamps (creation, modification, access, attribute change, backup)
- **Recovery journal** — a "diary" of recent changes for crash recovery (HFS+ Journaled)
- **File attributes database** — extended metadata (Finder info, permissions, ACLs) stored separately
- **Efficient searching** — much larger B-tree nodes (up to 65,536 bytes) for faster lookups

This is the system used in modern Macs, iPods, and many forensic investigations involving Apple devices.

> [!TIP]
> **Forensic Relevance:** Both HFS and HFS+ are critical in digital forensics because Apple devices have a significant market share, and understanding these file systems is essential for recovering evidence, analyzing timestamps, and parsing deleted data from Mac systems.

## 1.3 Key Identifiers in Volume Header

| Identifier | Value | Purpose |
|------------|-------|---------|
| HFS Signature | `'BD'` (0x4244) | Magic bytes identifying HFS volume |
| HFS+ Signature | `'H+'` (0x482B) | Magic bytes identifying HFS+ volume |
| HFSX Signature | `'HX'` (0x4858) | Case-sensitive HFS+ variant |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Always check the **Volume Header signature bytes first** when identifying the file system type. Forgetting this step is a common cause of evidence misinterpretation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HFS — The 'Why' and 'How' of Its Architecture

HFS was designed at a time when **floppy disks and small hard drives** were the norm. Its design constraints reflect this era:

### Why 16-bit addressing?
- A 16-bit address can reference **2^16 = 65,535 allocation blocks**
- With a **4 GB block size** (theoretical maximum), this yields a **2 TB** maximum volume size
- In practice, HFS used 512-byte blocks, limiting volumes to about **32 MB** initially

### Why B-trees for the catalog?
- Linear searches through thousands of files would be too slow
- B-trees provide **O(log n)** lookup time, ensuring consistent performance
- This was revolutionary for 1985 and is still used in modern file systems

### HFS — The Five Key Special Files

1. **Boot Blocks** (sectors 0 and 1): Contain system startup information
2. **Volume Header** (sector 2): Contains volume metadata — signature, block size, file counts
3. **Allocation File**: Bitmap tracking free and used allocation blocks
4. **Catalog File** (B-tree): Hierarchical directory and file information
5. **Extents Overflow File**: Additional extent records when first three extents are full

> [!NOTE]
> An **extent** is a contiguous run of allocation blocks allocated to a file. HFS supports **up to 3 extents** in the catalog record; additional extents go to the Extents Overflow File.

## 2.2 HFS+ — Enhanced Architecture for Modern Computing

HFS+ was engineered to overcome HFS's limitations, particularly in the areas of **capacity, internationalization, and metadata richness**.

### The 'Why' Behind Each HFS+ Improvement

| Improvement | Reason / Motivation |
|-------------|---------------------|
| **32-bit allocation block addresses** | Support volumes larger than 2 TB; modern drives need more space |
| **B-tree node size up to 65,536 bytes** | Reduce tree depth, improve lookup speed on large volumes |
| **Unicode UTF-16 filenames** | Support international users (Chinese, Japanese, Arabic, etc.) |
| **255-character filename limit** | More descriptive file names than HFS's 31-character limit |
| **Multiple timestamps per file** | Track creation, modification, access, attribute change, backup |
| **Attributes File (B-tree)** | Store extended metadata: Finder info, permissions, resource forks, ACLs |
| **Journaling support** | Faster crash recovery and improved forensic timeline integrity |
| **Hard links support** | Multiple directory entries pointing to the same inode (CNID) |

## 2.3 HFS+ Volume Layout — The 'How'

The HFS+ volume is laid out sequentially from sector 0:

### Logical Sector Arrangement

1. **Reserved sectors (0–1023)**: Reserved for partitioning schemes and boot loaders
2. **Volume Header (sector 1024)**: Critical metadata, 1024 bytes
3. **Allocation File**: Bitmap, typically located after volume header
4. **Extents Overflow File**: B-tree for overflow extents
5. **Catalog File**: B-tree containing all file and folder records
6. **Attributes File**: B-tree for extended attributes
7. **Startup File**: Boot blocks, replaced during OS installation
8. **Reserved sectors (last 1024)**: Mirror copy of volume header for redundancy

### B-Tree Node Types in HFS+

- **Header Node (Node Type 1)**: Contains B-tree header record with tree parameters
- **Index Node (Node Type 0)**: Contains keys and child node pointers
- **Leaf Node (Node Type −1 / 0xFF)**: Contains actual data records
- **Map Node (Node Type 2)**: Bitmap of used/free nodes within B-tree file

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are essential for solving numerical problems on HFS/HFS+ in KTU examinations. **Avoid using** the vertical pipe symbol `\vert` directly inside markdown tables; use `\mid` instead.

### Capacity and Addressing Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | HFS Value | HFS+ Value |
|-----------|---------|-----------|------------|
| Max allocation blocks | $2^{n}$ where $n$ = address width | $2^{16} = 65{,}535$ | $2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ |
| Max block size | $2^{b}$ bytes | $2^{12} = 4{,}096$ bytes | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes |
| Max volume size | $\text{blocks} \times \text{block size}$ | $2^{16} \times 2^{12} = 2^{28} = 256 \text{ MB}$ (practical) | $2^{32} \times 2^{16} = 2^{48} = 256 \text{ TB}$ |
| Max file size | Theoretical limit | $2 \text{ GB}$ (31-bit) | $8 \text{ EB}$ (63-bit) |
| Number of blocks for volume | $\left\lceil \dfrac{\text{Volume Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil$ | — | — |
| B-tree node size | $2^{k}$ bytes, $k \in [9, 16]$ | $512$ bytes | $512$ to $65{,}536$ bytes |
| Filename length (UTF-16 chars) | $\text{max chars}$ | $31$ | $255$ |
| Timestamp resolution | $\text{seconds since 1904-01-01}$ | $1 \text{ second}$ | $1 \text{ second}$ |

### Block Count and Slack Calculations

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|
| Allocation blocks per volume | $N_{blocks} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{bytes}}{B_{bytes}} \right\rfloor$ | $V_{bytes}$ = volume size in bytes; $B_{bytes}$ = block size |
| Free space | $F = N_{blocks} - U_{blocks}$ | $U_{blocks}$ = used blocks (read from allocation file) |
| File slack | $S_{file} = (N_{allocated} \times B) - L_{file}$ | $N_{allocated}$ = blocks allocated; $L_{file}$ = actual file data length |
| Volume slack | $S_{vol} = V_{total} - V_{used}$ | Space between end of filesystem and end of partition |
| Maximum CNID value | $2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ | Catalog Node ID — unique per file/folder on volume |

### CNID (Catalog Node ID) Special Values

| CNID | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 1 | Root directory (parent of volume) |
| 2 | Volume header |
| 3 | Allocation file |
| 4 | Extents overflow file |
| 5 | Catalog file |
| 6 | Attributes file |
| 7 | Startup file |
| 8–15 | Reserved (CNID 8 is reserved for backup purposes) |
| 16 | User files/folders begin here |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Apple device forensics**: HFS+ is found in legacy Macs, iPods, older iPhones (pre-iOS 10.3), and Time Machine backups
- **Data recovery**: Understanding extents and overflow files enables recovery of fragmented or partially deleted files
- **Timeline analysis**: Multiple timestamps (8 in HFS+) provide granular forensic timeline reconstruction
- **Anti-forensics detection**: Modified timestamps and extended attributes reveal file system manipulation attempts
- **Cross-platform analysis**: HFS+ volumes are often encountered in RAID arrays and external drives shared between Mac and Windows systems
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Allocation Blocks

The maximum number of allocation blocks is determined by the **bit-width of the allocation block address field** in the volume header.

### Step-by-Step Logic

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blocks, max} &= 2^{W} \\
\text{where } W &= \text{address bit-width}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For HFS (16-bit addressing):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blocks, max, HFS} &= 2^{16} \\
&= 65{,}536 - 1 \\
&= 65{,}535 \text{ allocation blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For HFS+ (32-bit addressing):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blocks, max, HFS+} &= 2^{32} \\
&= 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ allocation blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

This explains why HFS+ can manage volumes orders of magnitude larger than HFS.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Theoretical Volume Size

The maximum volume size is the product of the maximum number of allocation blocks and the maximum block size.

### Generic Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{max} &= N_{blocks, max} \times B_{max} \\
&= 2^{W} \times 2^{b} \\
&= 2^{(W + b)} \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For HFS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{max, HFS} &= 2^{16} \times 2^{12} \\
&= 2^{28} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 256 \text{ MB (practical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, HFS volumes rarely exceeded 2 GB due to real-world block size constraints.

**For HFS+:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{max, HFS+} &= 2^{32} \times 2^{16} \\
&= 2^{48} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 256 \text{ TB (theoretical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apple officially supports HFS+ volumes up to **8 EB** in macOS, considering other internal field constraints (such as 64-bit extent descriptors in modern implementations).

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Allocation Blocks for a Given Volume Size

Given a volume of size $V$ bytes and block size $B$ bytes, the number of allocation blocks is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blocks} &= \left\lfloor \frac{V_{bytes}}{B_{bytes}} \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example

A forensic investigator encounters an HFS+ volume of **500 GB** with a default block size of **4 KB**.

**Step 1: Convert to bytes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bytes} &= 500 \text{ GB} \\
&= 500 \times 2^{30} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 500 \times 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \\
&= 5.37 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Convert block size to bytes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{bytes} &= 4 \text{ KB} \\
&= 4 \times 2^{10} \\
&= 4{,}096 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the number of blocks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blocks} &= \frac{5.37 \times 10^{11}}{4{,}096} \\
&= 1.31 \times 10^{8} \\
&\approx 131{,}072{,}000 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify it does not exceed HFS+ maximum**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blocks, max, HFS+} &= 2^{32} = 4.29 \times 10^{9}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $1.31 \times 10^{8} < 4.29 \times 10^{9}$, the volume is valid for HFS+.

## 3.4 Derivation: File Slack Calculation

**File slack** is the space between the end of the file's logical data and the end of the last allocated block. It is a critical area in forensics for finding hidden data.

### Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{file} &= (N_{allocated} \times B_{bytes}) - L_{data}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $N_{allocated}$ = number of blocks allocated to the file
- $B_{bytes}$ = block size
- $L_{data}$ = actual logical size of the file data

### Worked Example

A file of size **8,500 bytes** is stored on an HFS+ volume with a **4,096-byte** block size, allocated **3 blocks**.

**Step 1: Total space allocated**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{allocated} &= N_{allocated} \times B_{bytes} \\
&= 3 \times 4{,}096 \\
&= 12{,}288 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute file slack**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{file} &= T_{allocated} - L_{data} \\
&= 12{,}288 - 8{,}500 \\
&= 3{,}788 \text{ bytes of slack}
\end{aligned}
$$

The 3,788 bytes of slack space may contain residual data from previous file operations — a prime forensic evidence area.

## 3.5 Derivation: Maximum Number of Files in HFS+

Each file/folder on an HFS+ volume has a unique **CNID (Catalog Node ID)**, which is a 32-bit unsigned integer. Special CNIDs (1–15) are reserved for system files.

### Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{files, max} &= 2^{32} - 16 \\
&= 4{,}294{,}967{,}280 \text{ files/folders}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the maximum number of user files and folders an HFS+ volume can contain, providing virtually unlimited file capacity for any practical use.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: HFS vs. HFS+ Comparison

| Feature | HFS (Mac OS Standard) | HFS+ (Mac OS Extended) |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Year introduced | 1985 | 1998 |
| Address width | 16-bit | 32-bit |
| Max allocation blocks | 65,535 | 4,294,967,296 |
| Default block size | 512 bytes | 4,096 bytes |
| Max volume size (practical) | 2 GB | 8 EB |
| Max file size | 2 GB | 8 EB |
| Filename encoding | MacRoman | Unicode UTF-16 |
| Max filename length | 31 chars | 255 chars |
| Case sensitivity | Insensitive | Insensitive (case-preserving) |
| B-tree node size | 512 bytes | 512 to 65,536 bytes |
| Catalog B-tree key length | Up to 520 bytes | Variable, multi-part |
| Number of extents in catalog | 3 | 8 |
| Resource fork | Yes (separate fork) | Stored as extended attribute |
| Permissions | Single set | POSIX + ACLs |
| Journaling | No | Yes (HFS+J) |
| Symbolic links | No | Yes |
| Hard links | No | Yes |
| File timestamps | 1 (modification) | 8 (multiple types) |
| Default timestamp | Mac epoch (1904-01-01) | Mac epoch (1904-01-01) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HFS+ Volume Layout — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the logical layout of an HFS+ volume from a forensic perspective, showing the position and role of each structural component.

```mermaid
graph TD
    V[HFS Plus Volume] --> RA1[Reserved Area 0 to 1023 Sectors]
    V --> VH[Volume Header at Sector 1024 Size 1024 Bytes]
    V --> AF[Allocation File Bitmap of Used Blocks]
    V --> EOF[Extents Overflow File B Tree]
    V --> CF[Catalog File B Tree Main Metadata]
    V --> ATF[Attributes File B Tree Extended Metadata]
    V --> SF[Startup File Boot Blocks]
    V --> DS[Data Storage Region Files and Folders]
    V --> RA2[Reserved Last 1024 Sectors Volume Header Mirror]

    VH --> SIG[Signature H plus equals 0x482B]
    VH --> BS[Block Size 4 KB Default]
    VH --> TB[Total Allocation Blocks]
    VH --> FB[Free Allocation Blocks]
    VH --> DC[Date and Time Created]
    VH --> LVM[Last Mounted Version]

    CF --> HN[Header Node B Tree Parameters]
    CF --> IN[Index Nodes Navigation]
    CF --> LN[Leaf Nodes Actual Records]
    CF --> MN[Map Nodes Node Usage Bitmap]

    LN --> FR[Folder Record]
    LN --> FIR[File Record]
    LN --> FTR[Folder Thread Record]
    LN --> FIRT[File Thread Record]

    ATF --> AT1[Fork Data Attributes]
    ATF --> AT2[Finder Info Attributes]
    ATF --> AT3[Permission Attributes]
    ATF --> AT4[Extended Attributes User Defined]

    AF --> BM[Bit 1 equals Used Bit 0 equals Free]
```

## 4.2 HFS+ Catalog B-Tree Structure — Hierarchical View

This diagram illustrates how the Catalog B-tree is organized, which is the most critical structure for forensic file system traversal.

```mermaid
graph TD
    CT[Catalog B Tree] --> HN[Header Node Type 1]

    HN --> HNR[Header Record 512 Bytes]
    HNR --> TR[Tree Root Node Number]
    HNR --> NN[Number of Nodes]
    HNR --> DS[Depth of Tree]
    HNR --> NS[Node Size]

    CT --> RT[Root Node]
    RT --> RIN[Root Index Nodes Type 0]
    RIN --> CIN[Child Index Nodes Type 0]
    CIN --> LN[Leaf Nodes Type minus 1]

    LN --> R1[Record 1 Folder ABC]
    LN --> R2[Record 2 File Document]
    LN --> R3[Record 3 Folder Thread]
    LN --> R4[Record 4 File Thread]

    CT --> MN[Map Nodes Type 2]
    MN --> NMAP[Node Allocation Bitmap]

    HNR --> COMP[Comparison Logic]
    COMP --> C1[Compare Parent CNID]
    COMP --> C2[Compare Node Name Unicode]
```

## 4.3 HFS+ File Record Structure — Forensic Detail

The following diagram shows the anatomy of a file record in the catalog, which is essential for timestamp analysis in forensic investigations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    FR[Catalog File Record 248 Bytes Typical] --> F1[Record Type 16 bytes]
    FR --> F2[File Flags 2 bytes]
    FR --> F3[File CNID 4 bytes]
    FR --> F4[Creation Date 4 bytes]
    FR --> F5[Content Mod Date 4 bytes]
    FR --> F6[Attribute Mod Date 4 bytes]
    FR --> F7[Access Date 4 bytes]
    FR --> F8[Backup Date 4 bytes]
    FR --> F9[Permissions 16 bytes]
    FR --> F10[File Info Block Finder]
    FR --> F11[Text Encoding Hint]
    FR --> F12[First 8 Extents]

    F12 --> E1[Extent 1 Start Block and Block Count]
    F12 --> E2[Extent 2 Start Block and Block Count]
    F12 --> E3[Extent 3 Start Block and Block Count]
    F12 --> E4[Extent 4 Start Block and Block Count]
    F12 --> E5[Extent 5 Start Block and Block Count]
    F12 --> E6[Extent 6 Start Block and Block Count]
    F12 --> E7[Extent 7 Start Block and Block Count]
    F12 --> E8[Extent 8 Start Block and Block Count]

    F2 --> FL1[Locked Flag]
    F2 --> FL2[Directory Flag]
    F2 --> FL3[Invisible Flag]
    F2 --> FL4[Alias Flag]
```

## 4.4 Forensic Data Flow in HFS+ Investigation

The following Mermaid diagram shows how a forensic investigator typically analyzes an HFS+ image.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Acquire HFS plus Image FTK Imager] --> B[Verify Image Hash SHA 256]
    B --> C[Identify Volume Header Signature]
    C --> D{Signature Type}

    D --> E1[HFS Classic Path]
    D --> E2[HFS plus Path]
    D --> E3[HFSX Path]

    E1 --> F1[Parse 16 bit Structures]
    E2 --> F2[Parse 32 bit Structures]
    E3 --> F3[Parse Case Sensitive Structures]

    F1 --> G[Mount Read Only]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G

    G --> H[Parse Catalog B Tree]
    H --> I[Extract File Records]
    H --> J[Extract Folder Records]
    H --> K[Extract Thread Records]

    I --> L[Timeline Analysis using 8 Timestamps]
    I --> M[Deleted File Recovery from Extent Overflow]
    I --> N[Slack Space Analysis]
    I --> O[Extended Attribute Parsing]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate HFS and HFS+ (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | HFS | HFS+ |
|---------|-----|------|
| Address width | 16-bit (max 65,535 blocks) | 32-bit (max ~4.29 billion blocks) |
| Filename encoding | MacRoman (8-bit) | Unicode UTF-16 |
| Filename length | 31 characters | 255 characters |
| Max volume size | 2 GB (practical) | 8 EB (theoretical) |
| B-tree node size | 512 bytes | 512 to 65,536 bytes |
| Journaling | Not supported | Supported (HFS+J) |
| Timestamps per file | 1 (modification) | 8 (multiple types) |
| Extents in catalog | 3 | 8 |

HFS was introduced in 1985 with limited capacity, while HFS+ was released in 1998 to overcome these limitations with 32-bit addressing, Unicode support, and modern features like journaling.

---

### Question 2: Explain the Role of the Catalog File in HFS+ (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Catalog File** is a B-tree structure that maintains the entire hierarchical organization of files and folders on an HFS+ volume. Its primary roles are:

1. **Directory hierarchy storage**: Stores all folders and subfolders in a tree structure, with each record containing a **parent CNID** and **node name**.

2. **File metadata storage**: Contains file records with key information such as CNID, timestamps, permissions, and the first 8 extents (start block + block count) for data location.

3. **Thread records**: Maintains two types of thread records (folder threads and file threads) that allow upward traversal from any node to its parent — critical for path reconstruction.

4. **Fast lookup**: B-tree structure provides O(log n) search complexity, ensuring efficient file access even on volumes with millions of files.

5. **Forensic significance**: Investigators can extract the entire file system structure, timestamps, and data extents by parsing the catalog B-tree — even when files have been deleted, remnants may persist in unused nodes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

#### Part (a): Explain the structure of the HFS+ file system with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The HFS+ volume is logically divided into several distinct regions:

**1. Reserved Area (Sectors 0–1023)**
The first 1024 sectors are reserved for partitioning metadata and boot loaders. This area is typically managed by the disk partitioning scheme (Apple Partition Map, GPT, or MBR).

**2. Volume Header (Sector 1024)**
A 1024-byte structure containing critical metadata:
- Signature bytes: `'H+'` (0x482B)
- Block size: typically 4,096 bytes
- Total allocation blocks
- Free allocation blocks
- Volume creation/modification timestamps
- Last-mounted macOS version

**3. Allocation File**
A bitmap where each bit represents one allocation block — `1` for used, `0` for free. Located immediately after the volume header.

**4. Extents Overflow File**
A B-tree storing additional extent records when the first 8 extents in the catalog are insufficient (i.e., highly fragmented files).

**5. Catalog File**
A B-tree containing four record types:
- **Folder record (0x0001)**: Directory metadata
- **File record (0x0002)**: File metadata with first 8 extents
- **Folder thread record (0x0003)**: Pointer to parent folder
- **File thread record (0x0004)**: Pointer to parent folder

**6. Attributes File**
A B-tree storing extended attributes: resource fork data, Finder info, permissions, ACLs, and user-defined extended attributes.

**7. Startup File**
Contains boot blocks used to load the operating system.

**8. Reserved Area (Last 1024 Sectors)**
Mirror copy of the volume header for redundancy and recovery.

**(Diagram Refer Section 4.1)**

---

#### Part (b): Discuss the B-tree structures used in HFS+ and their forensic significance. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

HFS+ relies heavily on **B-tree data structures** for fast lookup and management of critical metadata. The three main B-trees are:

**1. Catalog B-Tree**
- **Structure**: Header node → Index nodes → Leaf nodes (with optional Map nodes for node allocation)
- **Key components**: Parent CNID + Node name (Unicode)
- **Records stored**: Folder records, file records, folder threads, file threads
- **Forensic significance**:
  - Provides complete file system hierarchy
  - Reveals file names, CNIDs, timestamps, and extent information
  - Deleted file traces may remain in leaf nodes marked as free
  - Thread records enable path reconstruction even when directory tree is damaged

**2. Attributes B-Tree**
- **Structure**: Similar to catalog B-tree
- **Records stored**: Extended attribute records (4-byte attribute type + 4-byte record size)
- **Forensic significance**:
  - Stores resource forks (legacy Mac file format support)
  - Contains Finder information, ACLs, and user-defined attributes
  - May contain hidden metadata not visible in the standard file listing
  - Useful for detecting file type spoofing and origin tracing

**3. Extents Overflow B-Tree**
- **Structure**: B-tree keyed by file CNID
- **Records stored**: Additional extent descriptors beyond the first 8 stored in the catalog
- **Forensic significance**:
  - Reveals data location for heavily fragmented files
  - Critical for recovering large or fragmented files
  - Helps reconstruct data even when catalog records are partially corrupted

**B-Tree Node Anatomy:**
Each node contains:
- **Node descriptor (14 bytes)**: Node type, depth, size, forward/backward links
- **Record offset array**: Pointers to records within the node
- **Free space record**: Tracks free space within the node
- **Actual records or keys**

**Forensic Analysis Technique:**
Forensic tools like **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)**, **Autopsy**, and **BlackLight** parse these B-trees to reconstruct the file system. Investigators must handle **node fragmentation**, **B-tree corruption**, and **journal replay** (in HFS+J) carefully to avoid missing evidence.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

#### Part (a): Describe the Volume Header and Boot Blocks in HFS+ in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Volume Header (1024 bytes at sector 1024):**

The Volume Header is the most critical structure in HFS+ and contains:

| Field | Size (bytes) | Description |
|-------|--------------|-------------|
| Signature | 2 | `'H+'` (0x482B) for HFS+, `'HX'` (0x4858) for HFSX |
| Version | 2 | HFS+ version (typically 4 or 5) |
| Attributes | 4 | Volume flags (e.g., kHFSVolumeUnmountedBit) |
| Last Mounted Version | 4 | macOS version that last mounted the volume |
| Journal Info Block | 4 | Pointer to journal (if journaling enabled) |
| Creation Date | 4 | Timestamp (seconds since 1904-01-01) |
| Modification Date | 4 | Last metadata change timestamp |
| Backup Date | 4 | Last backup timestamp |
| Checked Date | 4 | Last fsck verification timestamp |
| File Count | 4 | Number of files on volume |
| Folder Count | 4 | Number of folders on volume |
| Block Size | 4 | Allocation block size (typically 4096) |
| Total Blocks | 4 | Total allocation blocks in volume |
| Free Blocks | 4 | Available allocation blocks |
| Next Allocation | 4 | Hint for next allocation search |
| Resource Clump Size | 4 | Resource fork allocation block grouping |
| Data Clump Size | 4 | Data fork allocation block grouping |
| Next CNID | 4 | Next available Catalog Node ID |
| Finder Info | 32 | Volume-level Finder metadata |
| Volume Name | 256 | Unicode volume name (up to 127 chars + null) |

**Boot Blocks (sectors 0 and 1):**

The first two sectors (1,024 bytes) are boot blocks containing:
- **Boot block 0 (sector 0)**: Contains the system startup code
  - Signature: `'BD'` (0x4244) for valid boot blocks
  - Entry point for system boot
  - System version and Finder version (e.g., macOS 10.14.6)
  - Boot code (machine code) for initiating startup

- **Boot block 1 (sector 1)**: Reserved for additional boot code or backup

After startup, the OS replaces the boot blocks with a boot loader (e.g., `boot.efi` for Intel Macs). Forensic analysis of boot blocks can reveal the **last booted macOS version** and **boot configuration**.

**Forensic Importance:**
- Volume Header contains the **fingerprint** of the volume
- A mirrored copy exists in the last 1024 sectors for recovery
- Boot blocks can confirm which OS version was last booted
- Signature mismatches indicate potential tampering or corruption

---

#### Part (b): Explain how deleted file recovery is performed in HFS+ from a forensic perspective. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Deleted file recovery in HFS+ is more complex than in simpler file systems because of B-tree structures, journaling, and the allocation file.

**Step 1: Identify Free Allocation Blocks**
The forensic tool reads the **Allocation File bitmap**. Blocks with value `0` (free) may have previously contained data from deleted files. Tools scan these free blocks for recognizable file signatures (magic bytes, headers).

**Step 2: Parse Catalog B-Tree for Deleted Records**
When a file is deleted in HFS+:
- Its catalog record is **not immediately erased** — the leaf node space is marked as free in the map node
- The file's extents in the catalog are not removed; they may be reused for new files
- An investigator can search for **orphan CNIDs** (records whose parent CNID does not exist) — these often indicate deleted files

**Step 3: Reconstruct from Extents Overflow File**
If the file was heavily fragmented, the **Extents Overflow File** may still contain the complete extent list (start block + block count) for the deleted file. This is critical for recovering large fragmented files.

**Step 4: Journal Analysis (HFS+J)**
If the volume is **HFS+ Journaled**, the journal may contain:
- Recent metadata changes including deletions
- A "transaction" list that can be replayed to recover recently deleted file metadata
- However, journals are typically **circular buffers** and old entries are overwritten

**Step 5: File Carving**
For files whose metadata is completely lost, **file carving** is used:
- Scan raw data sectors for known file headers (e.g., `0xFFD8FFE0` for JPEG)
- Reconstruct files by combining sequential matching sectors
- Tools used: **PhotoRec**, **Scalpel**, **Foremost**

**Step 6: Extended Attribute Analysis**
The **Attributes File** may still contain resource fork data or extended attributes for the deleted file, providing:
- Original file type information
- Application-specific metadata
- Useful for cross-referencing the carved file content

**Step 7: Timestamp Reconstruction**
Even if the file is deleted, **inode metadata (CNID records)** may persist in unallocated B-tree nodes, providing:
- Original creation, modification, and access times
- Evidence of when the file existed on the system

**Tools Used in HFS+ Forensics:**
- **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)** with `fls`, `icat`, `istat` commands
- **Autopsy** (GUI for TSK with timeline analysis)
- **BlackLight** (commercial Mac forensic suite)
- **MacQuisition** (live Mac imaging and analysis)
- **FTK Imager** (image acquisition and verification)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse HFS+ with HFSX** — HFSX is a case-sensitive variant of HFS+ with signature `'HX'`
> - **Always verify the Volume Header signature FIRST** — many students lose marks by skipping this step
> - **Remember that HFS+ supports 8 extents per file** (not 3 like HFS) in the catalog record
> - **Note the macOS epoch** (1904-01-01) used for timestamps — not the Unix epoch (1970-01-01)
> - **Distinguish between file slack and volume slack** — they are different concepts
> - **For deleted file recovery, mention the journal role** (HFS+J) explicitly — partial credit for ignoring it
> - **Always cite the B-tree node types** correctly (Header=1, Index=0, Leaf=−1, Map=2)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HFS and HFS+**

### Core Facts to Memorize
- **HFS** introduced in **1985**, **HFS+** introduced in **1998**
- HFS uses **16-bit** addressing; HFS+ uses **32-bit** addressing
- HFS+ default **block size** is **4 KB (4,096 bytes)**
- Maximum **allocation blocks** for HFS+ is **2^32 ≈ 4.29 billion**
- Maximum **filename length** in HFS+ is **255 Unicode characters**
- HFS+ uses **Unicode UTF-16** for filenames (HFS used MacRoman)
- HFS+ stores up to **8 extents per file** in catalog (HFS stored only 3)
- HFS+ supports **journaling** (HFS+J variant) — HFS does not
- HFS+ has **8 timestamps** per file; HFS had only **1**

### Critical Structural Elements
- **Volume Header** is at **sector 1024** (always), size **1024 bytes**
- **Signature bytes**: HFS = `'BD'`, HFS+ = `'H+'`, HFSX = `'HX'`
- **Catalog File** is the B-tree containing all file/folder metadata
- **Attributes File** is a B-tree for extended metadata
- **Extents Overflow File** is a B-tree for fragmented file extents
- **Allocation File** is a bitmap tracking free/used blocks
- A mirror copy of Volume Header exists in the **last 1024 sectors**

### Special CNID Values (Forensic Quick Reference)
- **CNID 1**: Root parent (root of all folders)
- **CNID 2**: Volume header
- **CNID 3**: Allocation file
- **CNID 4**: Extents overflow file
- **CNID 5**: Catalog file
- **CNID 6**: Attributes file
- **CNID 7**: Startup file
- **CNID 8**: Reserved (backup)
- **CNID 14**: "Dot" entries (folder self-reference)
- **CNID 15**: "Dot-dot" entries (parent folder reference)
- **CNID 16+**: User files and folders

### B-Tree Node Types
- **Type 1**: Header node (B-tree parameters)
- **Type 0**: Index nodes (navigation)
- **Type −1 (0xFF)**: Leaf nodes (actual data records)
- **Type 2**: Map nodes (node usage bitmap)

### Key Forensic Operations
- **Timeline analysis**: Parse all 8 timestamps in catalog file records
- **Deleted file recovery**: Use Allocation File + Extents Overflow + Journal
- **Slack space analysis**: Calculate file slack using $S = (N \times B) - L$
- **Path reconstruction**: Use folder/file thread records to traverse hierarchy
- **Journal analysis**: Replay transactions from HFS+J journal for recent changes

### Conversion Reminders
- **Mac Epoch** = 1904-01-01 00:00:00 UTC (NOT Unix Epoch 1970)
- **1 Mac timestamp unit = 1 second** (same as Unix)
- Conversion to Unix: `Unix = Mac_timestamp - 2082844800` (seconds between 1904 and 1970)

### Top 3 Examiner Traps to Avoid
1. Forgetting to check the **Volume Header signature** before analysis
2. Confusing **HFS+ with APFS** (APFS replaced HFS+ as default in 2017)
3. Not considering the **journal** in HFS+J volumes during deleted file recovery
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metadata and Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metadata and Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metadata** is structured descriptive information that characterizes, explains, locates, or otherwise makes it easier to retrieve, use, or manage an information resource. In the context of **Digital Forensics**, metadata refers to the *data about data* — secondary information embedded within, attached to, or associated with a primary digital artifact (file, email, image, document, network packet, etc.) that provides contextual details about its origin, structure, history, and integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECST754 – Module 1):**
> *Metadata in digital forensics is the descriptive, structural, and administrative information embedded in or associated with a digital object. It is critical for establishing the **who, what, when, where, and how** of a digital artifact during an investigation.*

**Attributes**, in the forensic sense, are the individual identifiable characteristics or properties of a digital object — such as file size, timestamps, ownership, permissions, hash values, and access rights — that collectively form the descriptive footprint of the evidence.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Luggage Tag" Metaphor

Imagine you are an airport baggage handler looking at thousands of identical-looking suitcases. Without a **luggage tag**, you cannot tell who owns a bag, where it came from, what flight it was on, or when it was checked in. The **luggage tag is the metadata** of the suitcase.

Similarly, every digital file carries a "tag" of hidden information:
- **The suitcase** = the actual file (image, document, email)
- **The tag** = metadata (owner, creation date, GPS location, device ID, software version)
- **The baggage system** = the file system, operating system, or application that generated and stored the tag

A forensic investigator who ignores metadata is like a detective who examines a weapon but ignores the fingerprints, serial number, and purchase receipt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Metadata Matters in Court:**
> Metadata is often the *only* forensic evidence that can definitively prove a document was created, modified, or accessed on a specific date by a specific user, even when the file content itself has been deleted or sanitized.

### 1.3 Categories at a Glance

| Metadata Category | Forensic Relevance |
|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Descriptive** | Identifies content (title, author, keywords) |
| **Structural** | Shows how components relate (page order, sections) |
| **Administrative** | Tracks creation, modification, access (timestamps, ownership) |
| **Technical** | Hardware/software specifications (camera model, OS version) |
| **Preservation** | Integrity verification (hash values, checksums) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Digital Metadata

#### Pillar 1: File System Metadata
This is metadata generated and maintained by the **Operating System's file system** (NTFS, FAT32, ext4, APFS, HFS+). It is the lowest layer of metadata and is often the most reliable in court.

**Key file system attributes include:**

- **Filename** — The human-readable name of the file
- **File Extension** — Indicates the type (`.docx`, `.jpg`, `.exe`)
- **File Size** — Measured in **bytes (B)**
- **Timestamps** — Four critical MAC times:
  - **M (Modification Time)** — When file content was last changed
  - **A (Access Time)** — When file was last read/opened
  - **C (Change/Inode Time)** — When metadata was last changed
  - **B (Birth/Creation Time)** — When file was originally created
- **Attributes / Flags** — Hidden, Read-only, System, Archive
- **Ownership (UID/GID)** — Linux user and group IDs
- **Permissions (DAC)** — Read (r=4), Write (w=2), Execute (x=1)
- **Inode / MFT Entry Number** — File system's internal identifier
- **Link Count** — Number of hard links pointing to the inode

#### Pillar 2: Application / File-Format Metadata
Embedded inside the file itself by the creating application.

| File Type | Embedded Metadata |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **JPEG / TIFF** | EXIF (camera model, GPS, shutter speed, ISO) |
| **DOCX / XLSX** | Author, company, revision history, comments, track changes |
| **PDF** | Title, author, creator application, XMP data |
| **MP3 / MP4** | ID3 tags (artist, album, bitrate), encoder info |
| **Email (.eml, .pst)** | From, To, Cc, BCC, Subject, X-Mailer, Message-ID, routing servers |
| **HTML / Web Pages** | `<meta>` tags, server headers, cookies |

#### Pillar 3: System & Network Metadata
Generated by the broader computing environment.

- **Windows Registry** keys (UserAssist, RecentDocs, ShimCache, AmCache)
- **Event Logs** (Security, System, Application — Event IDs like 4624, 4663)
- **Network logs** (firewall, proxy, DNS cache)
- **Browser artifacts** (history, cache, cookies, autofill)

### 2.2 The Metadata Hierarchy Pyramid

```
                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │   APPLICATION DATA  │  ← User-visible content
                    ├─────────────────────┤
                    │   FILE-FORMAT META  │  ← EXIF, XMP, OLE
                    ├─────────────────────┤
                    │  FILE SYSTEM META   │  ← MAC times, inode, perms
                    ├─────────────────────┤
                    │   VOLUME / DISK     │  ← Partition table, sectors
                    └─────────────────────┘
```

### 2.3 EXIF Data — The Forensic Goldmine

**EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format)** is a standard for embedding metadata in image and audio files, primarily from digital cameras and smartphones. It is a frequent source of evidence in cases involving child exploitation, geolocation tracking, and device identification.

**Key EXIF fields:**

- `Make` and `Model` — Camera/phone manufacturer
- `DateTimeOriginal` — When the photo was *actually* taken (may differ from file system time)
- `GPSLatitude` / `GPSLongitude` — Precise geographic coordinates
- `Software` — Editing application used (can reveal tampering)
- `Orientation` — Display rotation
- `ImageUniqueID` — Unique hash for the image

> [!NOTE]
> **EXIF coordinates can pinpoint a suspect's location to within ~5–10 meters.** This has been pivotal in landmark cases globally.

### 2.4 Email Metadata — The Communication Trail

Email headers reveal a complete communication history:

- **From / To / Cc / Bcc** — Participants
- **Date** — Send/receive timestamp (note: header date is from sender's client; Received headers are more reliable)
- **Subject** — Topic (often modified)
- **Message-ID** — Globally unique identifier
- **X-Mailer** — Email client used
- **Received:** chain — Every mail server that handled the message, with timestamps — the **most forensically reliable** timestamps
- **DKIM / SPF signatures** — Authentication metadata
- **Attachment hashes** — Integrity verification

### 2.5 Hashing and Integrity Metadata

**Hash values** are mathematical fingerprints of digital evidence, considered a form of **preservation metadata**.

**The Two Industry-Standard Hash Algorithms:**

$$
