# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define a Hex Viewer. List any two forensic applications of a Hex Viewer in digital investigations.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hex Viewer** is a forensic software tool that displays the raw binary contents of a file or storage device in hexadecimal (Base-16) format, along with the corresponding ASCII representation and byte offset. It operates at the byte level, bypassing the operating system's file system abstraction.

**Two forensic applications:**

1. **File Signature Verification:** Identifies the true file type by examining the magic bytes, even if the file extension has been altered to deceive investigators (e.g., a malware file renamed as `.txt`).

2. **Deleted Data Recovery:** Locates and recovers data from unallocated clusters, slack space, and orphan sectors that the operating system no longer recognizes, by directly reading the raw bytes from the disk image.

*(Any two valid applications: steganography detection, metadata extraction, malware analysis, anti-forensics detection, etc. — Award full 2 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]

**Q: What are magic bytes? Why is it necessary to verify magic bytes using a hex viewer during forensic analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

**Magic bytes** (also called **file signatures** or **magic numbers**) are a fixed sequence of bytes at the beginning of a file that uniquely identifies the file format. Examples include `FF D8 FF` for JPEG and `89 50 4E 47` for PNG.

**Necessity of verification:**

1. **Extension Spoofing:** Criminals frequently rename malicious files with innocent extensions (e.g., `malware.exe` → `report.pdf`). The operating system relies on extensions, but a hex viewer reveals the true file type through magic bytes, preventing deception.

2. **Data Integrity:** Magic bytes confirm that the file has not been corrupted during transfer or storage. A truncated or damaged file will lack the expected signature.

3. **Legal Admissibility:** Courts require forensic examiners to verify evidence authenticity. Magic byte verification provides a technical foundation for asserting that the evidence is what it is claimed to be.

4. **Hidden File Detection:** Files with stripped or missing extensions (e.g., from unallocated space) can only be identified by their magic bytes.

*(Full 2 marks for a clear definition and at least two valid reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice** between two alternatives. The structure is typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both alternatives must be attempted or one full question must be answered as per instructions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the hexadecimal number system and its relevance to digital forensics. With a suitable example, demonstrate how a hex viewer interprets a 16-byte sector from a disk image. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forensic examiner opens a suspicious file in a hex viewer and observes the following first 32 bytes:

```
Offset    Hex Values
00000000  4D 5A 90 00 03 00 00 00 04 00 00 00 FF FF 00 00
00000010  B8 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 40 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
```

Identify the file type, explain the significance of the first two bytes, and describe what forensic conclusions can be drawn from this signature. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The **hexadecimal (Base-16) number system** uses 16 distinct symbols: `0-9` and `A-F` (where A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15). Each hex digit represents exactly **4 binary bits (one nibble)**, and two hex digits represent one **byte (8 bits)**.

**Relevance to digital forensics:**

1. Computers store all data as binary (0s and 1s). Hexadecimal provides a **compact, human-readable representation** of binary data — one byte requires only 2 hex digits instead of 8 binary digits.
2. Memory addresses, offsets, and sector numbers in forensic tools are typically displayed in hex.
3. File signatures, hash values (MD5 = 32 hex chars, SHA-256 = 64 hex chars), and cryptographic keys are universally expressed in hex.

**Example — Interpreting a 16-byte sector:**

Consider the following 16-byte row from a hex viewer at offset `0x00001000`:

| Offset | Hex Bytes | Decimal | ASCII |
|--------|-----------|---------|-------|
| `0x1000` | `48 65 6C 6C 6F 20 57 6F 72 6C 64 21 0A 00 FF FF` | `72 101 108 108 111 32 87 111 114 108 100 33 10 0 255 255` | `Hello World!...` |

- The bytes `48 65 6C 6C 6F` decode to ASCII `Hello`.
- The byte `0A` is a **Line Feed (LF)** character.
- The bytes `FF FF` are non-printable and displayed as `.` in ASCII.

**Conclusion:** The hex viewer reveals that the sector contains the ASCII string "Hello World!" followed by a newline and two null/padding bytes. The forensic examiner can therefore conclude that this sector stores **human-readable text data**, likely from a log file or document.

*(Award full 7 marks for: (1) hex system definition — 1 mark, (2) forensic relevance — 2 marks, (3) example with byte-by-byte interpretation — 3 marks, (4) conclusion — 1 mark.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Convert First Two Bytes to ASCII**

$$\text{Byte 1: } 0x4D = 77 \text{ decimal} = 'M' \text{ (ASCII)}$$
$$\text{Byte 2: } 0x5A = 90 \text{ decimal} = 'Z' \text{ (ASCII)}$$

The first two bytes spell **"MZ"**.

**Step 2: Identify the File Type**

The signature `4D 5A` (ASCII: `MZ`) is the **DOS/Windows executable header**, known as the **IMAGE_DOS_HEADER**. It was named after **Mark Zbikowski**, one of the original architects of MS-DOS. Every Windows `.exe` and `.dll` file begins with this signature, making it a definitive marker of a **Portable Executable (PE) file**.

**Step 3: Interpret the Header Structure**

| Offset | Hex Value | Field | Meaning |
|--------|-----------|-------|---------|
| `0x0000-0x0001` | `4D 5A` | e_magic | DOS Signature ("MZ") |
| `0x0002-0x0003` | `90 00` | e_cblp | Bytes on last page of file = 144 |
| `0x0004-0x0005` | `03 00` | e_cp | Number of pages in file = 3 |
| `0x0006-0x0007` | `00 00` | e_crlc | Relocations count = 0 |
| `0x0008-0x0009` | `04 00` | e_cparhdr | Size of header in paragraphs = 4 |
| `0x000A-0x000B` | `00 00` | e_minalloc | Minimum extra paragraphs needed = 0 |
| `0x000C-0x000D` | `FF FF` | e_maxalloc | Maximum extra paragraphs needed = 65535 |
| `0x000E-0x000F` | `00 00` | e_ss | Initial (relative) SS value = 0 |
| `0x0010-0x0011` | `B8 00` | e_sp | Initial SP value = 184 |
| `0x0012-0x0013` | `00 00` | e_csum | Checksum = 0 |

**Step 4: Forensic Conclusions**

1. **File Type:** The file is a **Windows PE executable** (`.exe` or `.dll`), despite any extension the user may have assigned to it.
2. **PE Header Location:** The `e_lfanew` field (located at offset `0x003C`) points to the beginning of the **PE/NT header**, where the examiner can find the **entry point, section table, and import table**.
3. **Malware Indicator:** If this file was found on a system with a non-executable extension (e.g., `.txt`, `.pdf`, `.jpg`), it is a strong indicator of **malware masquerading as a benign file** — a common social engineering and phishing technique.
4. **Recommended Action:** The examiner should submit the file to a **sandboxed environment** (e.g., Cuckoo Sandbox, Any.Run) for dynamic analysis and generate a **VirusTotal hash lookup** to check against known malware signatures.

*(Award full 7 marks for: (1) ASCII conversion — 1 mark, (2) file type identification — 2 marks, (3) header field interpretation — 2 marks, (4) forensic conclusions — 2 marks.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **forget to state the endianness** when interpreting multi-byte header fields. Always specify **little-endian** for Windows PE files.
> - Do not confuse the **DOS header** (`MZ`) with the **PE header** (`PE\0\0` = `50 45 00 00`). The MZ header is always first; the PE header is at the offset specified by `e_lfanew`.
> - Simply stating "it is an EXE file" without explaining the forensic significance earns **only 1 mark** out of 7. You must elaborate on the **malware analysis and anti-forensics** implications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]

**Q: (a)** Discuss the role of a hex viewer in detecting **polyglot files** and **steganographic artifacts**. Provide a real-world example of a polyglot file and explain how a forensic examiner would identify it using a hex viewer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **standard three-column layout** of a forensic hex viewer. For the hex dump given below, identify the file type and extract the file size field, assuming a little-endian architecture:

```
Offset    Hex Values
00000000  50 4B 03 04 14 00 08 00 08 00 21 00 41 42
00000010  43 44 45 46 47 48 49 4A 4B 4C 4D 4E 4F 50
```

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Polyglot Files — Definition:**

A **polyglot file** (from Greek *poly* = many, *glot* = language) is a file that is **simultaneously valid in two or more distinct file formats**. In digital forensics, polyglot files are often used as an **anti-forensic technique** to smuggle malicious payloads past security filters that only inspect the "outer" format.

**Real-World Example: JPEG/ZIP Polyglot:**

A common polyglot is a **JPEG image with an embedded ZIP archive**. The JPEG header (`FF D8 FF E0`) appears at offset 0, followed by valid JPEG image data. At the **JPEG End-of-Image (EOI) marker** (`FF D9`), a ZIP archive is appended, beginning with the ZIP local file header signature `50 4B 03 04`. The file has a `.jpg` extension and displays correctly as an image, but extracting it as a ZIP reveals hidden files.

**How a Hex Viewer Detects Polyglot Files:**

1. **Verify the outer signature:** The hex viewer displays the first bytes, confirming the JPEG header.
2. **Navigate to the EOF:** The examiner jumps to the byte offset reported by the OS as the file size.
3. **Locate the inner signature:** Immediately after the JPEG EOI marker (`FF D9`), the hex viewer reveals the `PK` signature (`50 4B 03 04`), indicating an embedded ZIP.
4. **Extract and analyze:** The examiner uses a ZIP utility to extract the hidden archive, which may contain **malware, exfiltrated data, or contraband**.

**Steganographic Artifacts in the Hex View:**

Steganography hides data within other files. Common hex-level indicators include:

- **Data appended after the EOF marker** (concatenation steganography).
- **Abnormal padding patterns** at the end of the file (e.g., repeated `00` bytes that are inconsistent with the file format).
- **LSB (Least Significant Bit) modifications** in image or audio files — visible as subtle deviations in the hex stream when compared to a known-good reference file.
- **Anomalous high-entropy regions** (random-looking bytes) embedded within structured data.

The hex viewer is the **first line of defense** for detecting both polyglot and steganographic artifacts, because it exposes the raw byte structure without any OS-level filtering.

*(Award full 7 marks for: (1) polyglot definition — 1 mark, (2) JPEG/ZIP example — 2 marks, (3) hex viewer detection steps — 2 marks, (4) steganography indicators — 2 marks.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Standard Three-Column Layout of a Forensic Hex Viewer:**

*(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes for the visual layout.)*

The standard forensic hex viewer displays data in **three synchronized vertical columns**:

- **Column 1 — Offset (Address):** Displays the **byte offset** in hexadecimal, indicating the position of each row from the beginning of the file or disk.
- **Column 2 — Hexadecimal Data:** Shows **16 bytes per row** in hexadecimal notation, separated by spaces. This is the **raw binary content** of the file.
- **Column 3 — ASCII Text:** Interprets each byte as an **ASCII character**. Printable characters (0x20–0x7E) are displayed; non-printable bytes are represented by a dot (`.`).

**File Type Identification:**

The first four bytes of the hex dump are: `50 4B 03 04`

Converting to ASCII:

$$\text{Byte 1: } 0x50 = 'P', \quad \text{Byte 2: } 0x4B = 'K', \quad \text{Byte 3: } 0x03 = \text{(non-printable)}, \quad \text{Byte 4: } 0x04 = \text{(non-printable)}$$

The signature `50 4B 03 04` corresponds to the **ZIP Local File Header**. This indicates the file is a **ZIP archive** or a ZIP-based format (e.g., `.docx`, `.xlsx`, `.pptx`, `.jar`, `.apk`).

**Extracting the File Size Field (Little-Endian):**

The ZIP Local File Header structure is:

| Offset | Size | Field |
|--------|------|-------|
| `0x0000` | 4 bytes | Local file header signature (`50 4B 03 04`) |
| `0x0004` | 2 bytes | Version needed to extract |
| `0x0006` | 2 bytes | General purpose bit flag |
| `0x0008` | 2 bytes | Compression method |
| `0x000A` | 2 bytes | Last mod file time |
| `0x000C` | 2 bytes | Last mod file date |
| `0x000E` | 4 bytes | **CRC-32** |
| `0x0012` | 4 bytes | **Compressed size** |
| `0x0016` | 4 bytes | Uncompressed size |
| `0x001A` | 2 bytes | File name length |
| `0x001C` | 2 bytes | Extra field length |

The **Compressed Size** field is at offset `0x0012` and is 4 bytes wide. In the given hex dump, the bytes at this offset are:

$$\text{Offset 0x12: } 0x14, \quad 0x00, \quad 0x08, \quad 0x00$$

**Little-Endian Conversion:**

The compressed size in little-endian is calculated as:

$$S = B_0 \times 256^0 + B_1 \times 256^1 + B_2 \times 256^2 + B_3 \times 256^3$$
$$S = (0x14 \times 1) + (0x00 \times 256) + (0x08 \times 65536) + (0x00 \times 16777216)$$
$$S = 20 + 0 + 524288 + 0 = 524308 \text{ Bytes}$$

**Conclusion:** The compressed file size of the first entry in the ZIP archive is **524,308 bytes** (approximately 512 KB).

*(Award full 7 marks for: (1) three-column layout explanation — 2 marks, (2) ASCII conversion of magic bytes — 1 mark, (3) file type identification — 1 mark, (4) offset calculation for compressed size — 1 mark, (5) little-endian conversion with correct decimal answer — 2 marks.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls:**
> - **Endianness errors are the #1 reason for losing marks.** If the question says little-endian, you **MUST** read the bytes from right-to-left (least significant byte first). Reversing the calculation will give `0x00080014 = 524,308` by mistake, but the correct little-endian interpretation of the 4 bytes `14 00 08 00` is also `0x00080014 = 524,308` in this specific case. Always show the **explicit multiplication** to demonstrate understanding.
> - Students often **confuse the ZIP Local File Header offsets** with the ZIP Central Directory offsets. The Local File Header is at the beginning of each file entry; the Central Directory is at the end of the archive.
> - Forgetting to convert hex to ASCII before stating the file type loses **1 mark**. Always show both the hex value AND the ASCII interpretation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2: Hex Viewer**

- **Definition:** A Hex Viewer is a low-level forensic tool that displays raw file bytes in **hex, decimal, and ASCII** simultaneously, with a byte **offset** column.
- **Magic Bytes:** Fixed byte sequences at the start of a file that identify its true format regardless of the file extension. Common signatures: JPEG = `FF D8 FF`, PNG = `89 50 4E 47`, GIF = `47 49 46 38`, PDF = `25 50 44 46`, ZIP = `50 4B 03 04`, EXE = `4D 5A`.
- **File Type Mismatch:** If the hex signature does not match the file extension, the file is **suspicious** — possibly malware, steganography, or anti-forensic deception.
- **JPEG EOI Marker:** `FF D9` marks the end of a valid JPEG image. Data after this marker is **suspicious** and may indicate an embedded archive or steganographic payload.
- **Polyglot Files:** Files valid in multiple formats (e.g., JPEG/ZIP). Detected by finding a **second magic signature** after the first file's EOF marker.
- **Endianness:** Little-endian (Intel) stores the **least significant byte first**; big-endian (network/Motorola) stores the **most significant byte first**. Always specify endianness in exam answers.
- **Standard Row:** Hex viewers display **16 bytes per row** (32 hex characters), with a corresponding **16-character ASCII column**.
- **Slack Space:** The unused bytes between the end of a file's logical data and the end of its last cluster. Hex viewers can reveal data hidden in slack space.
- **Hash Verification:** After hex analysis, the examiner must compute a **cryptographic hash (MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256)** of the file for **chain of custody** and **evidence integrity**.
- **Read-Only Principle:** Always open forensic evidence in **read-only mode** to prevent accidental modification — a foundational principle of the **ACPO (Association of Chief Police Officers) guidelines** for digital evidence handling.
- **Common Tools:** WinHex, HxD, 010 Editor, FTK Imager, Autopsy, Hex Workshop, Bless.
- **Forensic Applications:** File signature verification, deleted file recovery, metadata extraction, malware analysis, anti-forensics detection, steganography detection, polyglot detection, and chain-of-custody documentation.
- **Address Space:** 32-bit systems use 8 hex digit offsets (4 GB max); 64-bit systems use 16 hex digit offsets (16 EB max).
- **Printable ASCII Range:** `0x20` (space) to `0x7E` (tilde `~`) — bytes outside this range are displayed as `.` in the ASCII column.
- **Binary-Hex Equivalence:** 1 nibble = 4 bits = 1 hex digit; 1 byte = 8 bits = 2 hex digits; 1 word (32-bit) = 4 bytes = 8 hex digits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FTK Imager
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** FTK Imager

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**FTK® Imager** (Forensic Toolkit Imager) is a free, lightweight, forensically sound data preview and **disk imaging tool** developed originally by **AccessData** (now part of Exterro). It is widely considered the *de-facto industry standard* for acquiring bit-stream (sector-by-sector) copies of digital storage media without altering the source evidence.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECST754 – Digital Forensics, Module 2)**, FTK Imager is positioned as a *non-commercial forensic acquisition utility* that supports the creation of disk images in multiple forensic container formats, performs on-the-fly **hashing** (MD5, SHA1, SHA256), and allows pre-acquisition verification of evidence integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Soundness Principle:** A tool is deemed "forensically sound" if its use does **NOT** modify, alter, or in any way affect the source evidence. FTK Imager satisfies this principle through **read-only access** at the hardware/software layer and the use of standardized hashing algorithms.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Disk Image** | A bit-for-bit duplicate of a storage device captured sector-by-sector. |
| **Bit-Stream Image** | A raw copy including file slack, unallocated space, and swap files. |
| **Hash Value** | A fixed-length alphanumeric string (digest) uniquely representing data. |
| **Forensic Container** | A file format that encapsulates the raw image along with metadata and hashes. |
| **E01 (EnCase Format)** | A proprietary, compressed, segment-based forensic image format. |
| **AFF (Advanced Forensic Format)** | An open-source extensible image format. |
| **AD1 (AccessData Logical Image)** | AccessData's logical evidence file format. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a museum archaeologist who finds an ancient clay tablet. Before transporting it to a lab, the archaeologist wants a **perfect replica** — every crack, every chip, every layer of dust. They cannot touch the original (it is evidence), but they need an exact copy to study and share with other researchers.

**FTK Imager is the digital equivalent of that perfect replica machine.**

- The **clay tablet** = the suspect's hard drive / USB / memory card.
- The **museum rules** = the legal requirement to not modify original evidence.
- The **replica** = the **forensic image** (DD, E01, AFF).
- The **museum's authentication seal** stamped on the replica = the **hash value (MD5/SHA1/SHA256)** that proves the replica is identical to the original.

The archaeologist also keeps a **logbook** noting the date, time, and the person who created the replica — this is the **case information / metadata** that FTK Imager embeds inside every forensic container it generates.

## 1.4 Why FTK Imager Matters in the KTU 2024 Curriculum

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 – Forensic Acquisition Tools Focus:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes hands-on familiarity with **freely available, court-accepted imaging tools**. FTK Imager is included because it is:
> 1. **Free** (student-friendly licensing).
> 2. **Globally accepted** in courts (US, EU, India).
> 3. **Cross-platform capable** via FTK Imager Lite (Windows PE bootable).
> 4. **Comprehensive** — supports imaging, preview, mounting, and exporting.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Operational Architecture of FTK Imager

FTK Imager is engineered around **four core operational pillars** that directly map to KTU Module 2 learning outcomes (CO2: *Apply forensic imaging techniques*).

### Pillar 1 — **Evidence Acquisition (Imaging)**
The primary function. FTK Imager creates a bit-stream copy of:
- **Physical drives** (HDD, SSD, NVMe)
- **Logical drives** (partitions, volumes)
- **Individual files / folders** (creates AD1 logical image)
- **Mobile devices** (via UFED integration, though limited)
- **RAM (volatile memory)** through custom memory acquisition plugins
- **CD/DVD** optical media

### Pillar 2 — **Hash Computation & Integrity Verification**
FTK Imager computes cryptographic hashes during and after imaging.

- **MD5** (128-bit) — fast, legacy, still widely used.
- **SHA-1** (160-bit) — moderate security, deprecated for new use.
- **SHA-256** (256-bit) — current recommended standard.

The general verification equation is:

$$
H_{\text{original}} \overset{?}{=} H_{\text{image}}
$$

If both hash digests are identical, the image is **forensically verified** as a true bit-stream copy.

### Pillar 3 — **Evidence Pre-Viewing (Read-Only Mount)**
FTK Imager allows mounting a forensic image as a **read-only logical drive letter** (e.g., `X:`). This enables:
- Previewing contents **without writing back** to the source.
- Listing files, folder structure, and metadata.
- **No modification** is permitted at the driver level.

### Pillar 4 — **Evidence Export & Conversion**
Imagers can convert between formats, e.g., `DD → E01` or `E01 → DD`, and export specific file sets for downstream analysis tools (FTK, EnCase, X-Ways, Autopsy).

## 2.2 Supported Forensic Image Formats

| Format | Extension | Compression | Metadata | Splittable | Origin |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw (DD) | `.dd`, `.raw`, `.img` | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Optional | Unix `dd` |
| EnCase | `.E01`, `.Ex01` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Guidance Software |
| SMART | `.s01` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ASR Data |
| AFF | `.aff`, `.afd` | ✅ Optional | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Simson Garfinkel |
| AD1 | `.ad1` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | AccessData |
| L01 | `.L01` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Logicube |

> [!NOTE]
> **E01 format** is the most popular in India and in KTU lab examinations. It is a segment-based format where the first segment contains the case header, examiner name, evidence number, acquisition date, MD5/SHA1 hashes, and the bit-stream data.

## 2.3 Acquisition Modes

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FTK Imager Acquisition Modes                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  1. Physical Drive   (entire disk, incl.      │
│     host-protected area, unallocated space)  │
│  2. Logical Drive    (single partition)      │
│  3. Image File       (image of a file)       │
│  4. Folder Contents  (creates .AD1)          │
│  5. Memory (RAM)     (via third-party plugin)│
│  6. CD/DVD           (optical media)         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Key Value / Setting | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default hashing algorithms | **MD5 + SHA1** simultaneously | Always state both in viva |
| 2 | Image compression | `0` (None), `1–9` (Fast→Best) | Default is `6` |
| 3 | E01 segment size | **640 MB / 1500 MB / 2000 MB / 4 GB** | Default = 1500 MB |
| 4 | Bad sector handling | **Retry 5 times, then skip** | Mention "fault-tolerant imaging" |
| 5 | Pre-imaging hash | ✅ Computed automatically | Cite *pre-imaging verification* |
| 6 | Post-imaging hash | ✅ Computed automatically | Cite *post-imaging verification* |
| 7 | Read-only mounting | Via "Mount Image" → "Read-Only" toggle | Critical for *non-modification* |
| 8 | Verifying match logic | $H_{\text{orig}} = H_{\text{img}}$ | If ≠, the image is rejected |
| 9 | Free vs Commercial | Free version has all core features | Good for student lab |
| 10 | Court admissibility | Accepted globally (Daubert / 65B) | Cite Indian IT Act 2000/2008 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **Information Security & Cyber Forensics industry**, FTK Imager is deployed for:
- **Incident Response (IR)**: First-response acquisition of compromised endpoints.
- **e-Discovery**: Imaging custodian devices in corporate litigation.
- **Law Enforcement (CBI, NIA, State Cyber Cells)**: Adheres to Indian Evidence Act (Sec 65B).
- **Enterprise Audits**: Validating data destruction by imaging the drive *before* and *after* sanitization.
- **Academic Labs (KTU)**: Mandatory tool in the Digital Forensics lab curriculum.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Imaging Workflow & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
This section provides the **exhaustive, full-length procedural walkthrough** of acquiring a forensic image using FTK Imager. Each step is precisely what a student would execute in the **KTU Digital Forensics Lab**.

## 3.1 Pre-Acquisition Phase

### Step 1 — Boot from a Forensically Clean Environment

$$
\text{Boot Source} = \text{FTK Imager Lite (Windows PE)} \quad \lor \quad \text{Live Linux (e.g., DEFT, PALADIN)}
$$

**Reason:** Booting the suspect machine from its own OS may trigger **auto-mount, registry writes, or pagefile swaps** that contaminate evidence. A write-blocker or bootable forensic OS ensures the source disk is **never written to**.

### Step 2 — Connect a Write-Blocker (Hardware or Software)

A hardware write-blocker (e.g., Tableau T8 USB) sits physically between the suspect drive and the examiner's machine. FTK Imager can also operate with software write-blockers (e.g., **EnCase's DOS version** or **USB write-block via registry** on Windows).

### Step 3 — Launch FTK Imager

Double-click the FTK Imager executable. The main window shows four primary menu options:
1. **Add Evidence Item**
2. **Obtain Protected Files**
3. **Create Disk Image**
4. **Export Logical Image (AD1)**

## 3.2 Acquisition Phase (Step-by-Step)

### Step 4 — Initiate Disk Image Creation
Navigate: `File → Create Disk Image`

A wizard opens with the following source options:

$$
\text{Source} = \begin{cases} \text{Physical Drive} \\ \text{Logical Drive} \\ \text{Image File} \\ \text{Contents of a Folder} \\ \text{Optical Drive (CD/DVD)} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 5 — Select the Source Drive

Choose `/dev/sda` (Linux notation) or `\\.\PhysicalDrive0` (Windows notation). For a USB stick on `/dev/sdb`, the corresponding physical drive is selected.

### Step 6 — Add Image Destination

Click **Add...** → Choose destination directory, e.g., `D:\Evidence\Case_2024_KTU\`. Provide a unique **Evidence Image Filename** (e.g., `Suspect_Laptop_HP_01`).

### Step 7 — Configure Image Format

For a typical KTU exam scenario, the configuration matrix is:

| Parameter | Recommended Value | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|
| Image Type | `E01` | Most popular in Indian labs |
| Compression | `6` (Balanced) | Default; higher = slower |
| Segment Size | `1500 MB` | Default; helps with FAT32 USBs |
| Hash | `MD5 + SHA1` | Always select at least 2 |

### Step 8 — Set Case Information (Metadata)

Examiner fills in:

$$
\text{Case Info} = \{ \text{Case Number}, \text{Evidence Number}, \text{Examiner}, \text{Date}, \text{Description} \}
$$

This metadata is **embedded inside the E01 header** and serves as a chain-of-custody record.

### Step 9 — Begin Imaging

Click **Start**. FTK Imager:
1. Computes the **pre-imaging MD5 + SHA1** of the source.
2. Reads the source disk **sector-by-sector** (sector = **512 bytes** for HDD, **4096 bytes** for Advanced Format drives).
3. Compresses each block (if enabled).
4. Writes the block to the destination `.E01` file.
5. Computes the **post-imaging MD5 + SHA1** of the resulting image.
6. Compares:

$$
H_{\text{pre}} \overset{?}{=} H_{\text{post}} \implies \begin{cases} \text{MATCH} \rightarrow \text{Image Verified} \\ \text{MISMATCH} \rightarrow \text{Image Rejected, re-acquire} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 10 — Completion & Logging

A detailed **log file** is generated containing:
- Image filename and path
- Source drive geometry
- Acquisition duration
- Number of bad sectors encountered
- Hash values
- Examiner and case details

This log is essential for the **chain of custody** and is **printable as a court exhibit**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Hashing Works Inside FTK Imager

The internal hashing pipeline can be expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For each sector } S_i \text{ of the source disk:} \\
\quad \text{Read } S_i \\
\quad \text{Compress } C_i = \text{Zlib}(S_i) \\
\quad \text{Append } C_i \text{ to image buffer} \\
\quad \text{Update } \text{MD5 accumulator } \leftarrow \text{MD5}(S_i) \\
\quad \text{Update } \text{SHA1 accumulator } \leftarrow \text{SHA1}(S_i) \\
\text{Final:} \quad H_{\text{MD5}} = \text{Accumulator}_{\text{MD5}}, \quad H_{\text{SHA1}} = \text{Accumulator}_{\text{SHA1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The mathematical guarantee is the **avalanche property** of cryptographic hashes:

$$
\forall \, \Delta x \neq 0 \implies H(x) \neq H(x \oplus \Delta x)
$$

Even a **single bit flip** in the source will cause the hash to change drastically, making tampering detectable.

## 3.4 Verification Phase (Post-Acquisition)

The examiner can re-verify the image at any later time:

`File → Verify Disk Image`

FTK Imager:
1. Recomputes the hash of the image file.
2. Compares it with the hash stored in the E01 header.
3. If they match → "**The image is identical to the source**" message is displayed.

$$
\text{Verification Status} = \begin{cases} \text{VERIFIED} & \text{if } H_{\text{computed}} = H_{\text{stored}} \\ \text{NOT VERIFIED} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

## 3.5 Pre-Viewing & Mounting

`File → Image Mounting`

Select the `.E01` file → Mount as `Read-Only` → Assign drive letter (e.g., `Z:`).

Now the examiner can browse the file system, view timestamps, and copy *individual files* out for analysis — all without modifying the image.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — FTK Imager Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC["SOURCE EVIDENCE LAYER"]
        A1[Hard Disk Drive HDD]
        A2[Solid State Drive SSD]
        A3[USB Pen Drive]
        A4[Optical Media CD DVD]
        A5[Mobile Device via UFED]
    end

    subgraph WB["WRITE BLOCKER LAYER"]
        B1[Hardware Write Blocker Tableau T8]
        B2[Software Write Blocker Registry Hook]
    end

    subgraph CORE["FTK IMAGER CORE ENGINE"]
        C1[Sector Reader Engine]
        C2[Compression Module Zlib]
        C3[Hashing Module MD5]
        C4[Hashing Module SHA1]
        C5[Hashing Module SHA256]
        C6[Format Encoder E01 DD AFF]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        D1[E01 Forensic Image]
        D2[DD Raw Image]
        D3[AFF Open Image]
        D4[Verification Log File]
        D5[Mounted Read Only Drive]
    end

    subgraph USR["EXAMINER INTERFACE"]
        E1[Create Disk Image Wizard]
        E2[Verify Disk Image Module]
        E3[Mount Image Module]
        E4[Export AD1 Module]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> B1

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1

    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C1 --> C5

    C2 --> C6
    C3 --> C6
    C4 --> C6
    C5 --> C6

    C6 --> D1
    C6 --> D2
    C6 --> D3

    C6 --> D4
    C6 --> D5

    E1 --> CORE
    E2 --> CORE
    E3 --> CORE
    E4 --> CORE
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — End-to-End Imaging Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Boot from Forensically Clean OS] --> P2[Step 2 Attach Write Blocker]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Connect Suspect Drive]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Launch FTK Imager]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Select Source Physical Drive]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Choose Destination and Format E01]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Enter Case Metadata]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Configure Compression and Hash]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Start Imaging Sector by Sector]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 Compute Pre and Post Hashes]
    P10 --> P11{Step 11 Hash Match?}
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[Step 12 Image Verified Export Log]
    P11 -->|No| P13[Step 13 Re Acquire Image]
    P13 --> P9
    P12 --> P14[Step 14 Mount Image Read Only for Analysis]
```

## 4.3 Layered Architecture — Read-Only Mounting Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Physical Disk Suspect HDD USB]
    L2[Layer 2 Forensic Image File E01]
    L3[Layer 3 Mount Manager Virtual Disk Driver]
    L4[Layer 4 Read Only Filter Driver]
    L5[Layer 5 Logical Drive Letter X Z]
    L6[Layer 6 Examiner Browser File System View]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6

    style L4 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#cc0000
    style L5 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Read-Only Filter Driver** (highlighted yellow) is the critical security layer that prevents the examiner's browsing actions from modifying the underlying forensic image — preserving *forensic soundness*.

## 4.4 Hashing Pipeline — Sector Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0[Suspect Disk Sector 0 512 bytes] --> R1[Read into Memory Buffer]
    S1[Suspect Disk Sector 1 512 bytes] --> R1
    Sn[Suspect Disk Sector N 512 bytes] --> R1
    R1 --> CMP[Compress Block Zlib Default Off]
    CMP --> WH[Write to Image File E01]
    CMP --> H1[Update MD5 Hash State]
    CMP --> H2[Update SHA1 Hash State]
    CMP --> H3[Update SHA256 Hash State]
    H1 --> FF[Final Hash Digests Stored in E01 Header]
    H2 --> FF
    H3 --> FF
    FF --> VC[Verification Compare on Re Open]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*
**Define forensic imaging. List any four features of FTK Imager.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Forensic imaging** is the process of creating a bit-for-bit (sector-by-sector) duplicate of a digital storage device in a forensically sound manner, preserving the original evidence without any modification.

Four features of **FTK Imager**:
1. **Free & lightweight** — distributed at no cost, runs on minimal hardware.
2. **Multiple image formats** — supports E01, DD, AFF, AD1, SMART.
3. **Built-in hashing** — computes MD5, SHA1, SHA256 simultaneously.
4. **Read-only image mounting** — previews contents without writing back to the image.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 for features = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*
**What is the role of hashing in forensic imaging? Name the hashing algorithms supported by FTK Imager.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role of hashing:** Hashing produces a unique fixed-length alphanumeric "fingerprint" of the data. If even a single bit of the source is altered, the hash changes. Thus hashing **verifies the integrity and authenticity** of the forensic image throughout the chain of custody.

Hashing algorithms supported:
1. **MD5** (128-bit)
2. **SHA-1** (160-bit)
3. **SHA-256** (256-bit)

*(1.5 marks for role + 0.5 × 3 = 1.5 marks for algorithms = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) under the 2024 Scheme offer an **internal choice** between two full 14-mark questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a) Explain the architecture and working of FTK Imager with a suitable block diagram.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture:** FTK Imager is structured into four functional layers:

1. **Source Layer:** Accepts physical drives, logical drives, optical media, and image files.
2. **Write-Blocker Layer:** Prevents any write operations to the source through hardware (Tableau) or software (Windows registry) methods.
3. **Core Engine:** Contains the sector reader, Zlib compression module, hashing modules (MD5, SHA1, SHA256), and format encoders (E01, DD, AFF).
4. **Output Layer:** Produces the forensic image file, the verification log, and exposes a read-only mounted drive.

**Working Principle:** The tool reads the source media sector by sector. Each sector is optionally compressed and simultaneously fed into one or more hashing accumulators. The compressed sector is then written to the destination image file. A header containing case metadata, hashes, and acquisition timestamp is embedded at the start of the file. Once acquisition is complete, the accumulated hash is compared to a freshly computed hash of the image file. If they match, the image is declared *verified*.

$$
\text{Verification:} \quad H_{\text{source}} = H_{\text{image}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Forensically Sound Copy Confirmed}
$$

*(Block diagram already shown in Section 4.1 — student may reproduce simplified version for full marks.)*

---

**(b) Describe the step-by-step procedure for creating a forensic image of a USB drive using FTK Imager in E01 format. Mention all required pre-acquisition precautions.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Pre-acquisition precautions (3 marks):**
1. **Boot from a forensically clean OS** (FTK Imager Lite / Live Linux like DEFT) to prevent the host OS from writing to the suspect drive.
2. **Attach a hardware write-blocker** (e.g., Tableau T8) between the USB and the examiner machine.
3. **Document the drive details** — vendor, model, serial number, capacity — and photograph the label.
4. **Maintain a chain-of-custody log** noting date, time, examiner name, and case number.
5. **Calculate MD5/SHA1 of the original** (pre-image hash) for integrity baseline.

**Acquisition procedure using FTK Imager (4 marks):**
1. `File → Create Disk Image`
2. Select source type: **Physical Drive**
3. Choose the suspect USB drive from the drop-down list.
4. Click **Add** to set the destination → enter image filename e.g., `USB_Evidence_001`.
5. Choose **Image Type = E01**.
6. Set **Compression = 6** (default), **Segment Size = 1500 MB**.
7. Select hash algorithms: **MD5 + SHA1**.
8. Fill in **Case Information** (case number, evidence number, examiner, date, notes).
9. Click **Start**.
10. Wait for imaging to complete. FTK Imager will:
    - Compute pre-imaging hash.
    - Acquire bit-stream copy.
    - Compute post-imaging hash.
    - Compare both hashes and report verification status.
11. Save and print the **acquisition log** for the case file.

**Post-acquisition step:** Mount the E01 image as read-only to preview contents without modifying the image.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative]*

**(a) Differentiate between Physical Imaging and Logical Imaging. Which one does FTK Imager use by default and why?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Physical Imaging | Logical Imaging |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scope | Entire drive including unallocated space, slack, swap | Only active files & folders visible to OS |
| 2 | Method | Bit-stream, sector-by-sector | File-system level (FAT/NTFS/ext4 parser) |
| 3 | Captures deleted files | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| 4 | Captures unallocated space | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| 5 | Captures file slack | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| 6 | Format example | E01, DD, AFF | AD1 (AccessData) |
| 7 | Forensic soundness | Highest | Lower |
| 8 | Time required | Longer | Shorter |

FTK Imager, by default for a "Physical Drive" source, uses **Physical Imaging** because:
- It captures **deleted files** and **unallocated space**, which are often the most critical evidence (e.g., fragments of a wiped PDF, remnants of a deleted chat).
- It preserves **file system metadata, timestamps, and slack space** that may contain user data leaked from memory.
- It satisfies the **forensic soundness** requirement, ensuring admissibility in court.

Logical imaging (AD1) is used only when the examiner specifically needs to acquire *selected files* (e.g., only the `Documents` folder), as in e-Discovery scenarios.

---

**(b) Explain the importance of hashing in digital forensics. With a neat diagram, describe the hashing workflow inside FTK Imager and the verification process.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Importance of Hashing (3 marks):**
1. **Integrity verification** — confirms the image is a true copy of the source.
2. **Tamper detection** — any modification changes the hash dramatically.
3. **Chain of custody** — hash acts as a digital fingerprint accompanying the evidence.
4. **Non-repudiation** — the original examiner can prove they produced a specific image.
5. **Court admissibility** — under **Indian IT Act Sec 65B** and **Indian Evidence Act**, hash logs are accepted as proof of integrity.

**Hashing workflow inside FTK Imager (3 marks):**

(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.4 of these notes.)

- Each sector (typically 512 bytes) read from the source is fed into multiple hash accumulators (MD5, SHA1, SHA256) **in parallel**.
- After all sectors are read, the final digest is written to the **E01 file header**.
- A second pass computes the hash of the **written image file**, and the two are compared.

**Verification process (1 mark):**
When the examiner later re-opens the image via `File → Verify Disk Image`, FTK Imager recomputes the hash of the image and compares it to the header value. A match confirms the image is unchanged.

$$
H_{\text{stored}} = H_{\text{recomputed}} \implies \text{Verified}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention hashing algorithms** — students often write *only MD5*, losing 1 mark. Always mention **MD5 + SHA1 + SHA256**.
> 2. **Confusing Physical vs Logical** — in the exam, *default behavior* of FTK Imager is **Physical**. Students who say "Logical" lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the write-blocker step** — examiners award 1 mark specifically for the **write-blocker** in the pre-acquisition list.
> 4. **Not mentioning sector size** — 512 bytes (or 4K Advanced Format) is a favorite 1-mark question.
> 5. **Forgetting chain-of-custody** — mentioning the log file is a separate ½ mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Points (Print-Ready):**

- ✅ **FTK Imager** = Free forensic imaging tool by AccessData (Exterro).
- ✅ **Default formats** = E01, DD, AFF, AD1.
- ✅ **Default hash** = MD5 + SHA1 (also supports SHA256).
- ✅ **Physical imaging** captures deleted files, slack, and unallocated space.
- ✅ **Logical imaging** (AD1) captures only OS-visible files/folders.
- ✅ **Write-blocker** is **mandatory** to ensure forensic soundness.
- ✅ **Sector size** = **512 bytes** (HDD) or **4096 bytes** (Advanced Format).
- ✅ **E01 segments** = default **1500 MB** to fit on FAT32-formatted USBs.
- ✅ **Read-only mounting** lets examiners browse without modifying the image.
- ✅ **Verification** = `H_source ≡ H_image` (must be identical).
- ✅ **Hash recomputation** on re-open is a **post-verification** step.
- ✅ **Court admissibility** in India — IT Act 2000/2008, Sec 65B (electronic evidence).
- ✅ **Bad sector handling** — 5 retries, then skip; logged in the report.
- ✅ **Examiner metadata** is stored inside the E01 file header.
- ✅ **Log file** is printable as a chain-of-custody exhibit.
- ✅ **FTK Imager Lite** is a bootable Windows PE version for live acquisitions.
- ✅ **Mobile imaging** is limited; UFED (Cellebrite) is the industry standard.
- ✅ **Memory (RAM) imaging** requires third-party plugins (e.g., FTK Imager Memory Imager).
- ✅ **Compression level 6** is default; tradeoff = time vs space.
- ✅ **The hash guarantee** relies on the **avalanche property** of cryptographic hash functions.
- ✅ **Mermaid diagrams** in the answer script (if allowed) can earn extra presentation marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autopsy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Autopsy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autopsy** is a **GUI-based, open-source digital forensics platform** built on top of *The Sleuth Kit (TSK)* and other command-line forensic utilities. It is engineered to perform end-to-end analysis of disk images, file systems, mobile devices, and memory dumps in a structured, case-management-oriented workflow.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, Autopsy is classified as a **forensic analysis workstation tool** that abstracts the underlying complexity of command-line tools (like `fls`, `icat`, `mmls`, `fsstat`) into a unified graphical environment. It supports the **NIST CFTT (Computer Forensic Tool Testing)** validated engine and is widely used by law enforcement, military, and corporate forensic examiners.

### Key Architectural Pillars
- **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)** – The C-based backend library that performs the low-level disk and file system parsing.
- **Autopsy Graphical Front-End** – A Java-based interface providing case management, visualization, and reporting.
- **Ingest Modules / Plugins** – Modular analyzers for registry, email, EXIF, keyword search, hash filtering, etc.
- **Solr/Lucene Indexing Engine** – Provides high-speed keyword searching and faceted data filtering.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of Autopsy as a **"digital crime-scene investigation truck."**
>
> Just as a physical forensic van has specialized compartments — one for fingerprints, one for DNA swabs, one for ballistics — Autopsy has dedicated *modules* that each handle a specific type of digital evidence. The detective (forensic examiner) drives this truck to the crime scene (disk image), opens the compartments (modules), and extracts artifacts (files, emails, registry keys) without disturbing the original evidence (write-blocked source).

**Simplified Mental Model for a First-Time Reader:**
- Imagine a hard drive image is a sealed, dusty library.
- Old command-line tools let you read *one book* at a time using a flashlight.
- **Autopsy turns on all the lights, sorts books by author, highlights suspicious pages, takes photographs of evidence, and produces a court-ready report — all in one place.**

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Disk Image:** A bit-by-bit replica of a storage device (e.g., `.E01`, `.dd`, `.AFF`).
> - **Ingest Module:** A plug-in that processes evidence as it is added to the case.
> - **Data Source:** The input to a case (local disk, logical file, image file, or mobile device).
> - **Artifact:** A forensic-relevant object (file, registry key, event log, browser history).
> - **Hash Set:** A database of known-good (NSRL) or known-bad (e.g., Project Vic) file hashes.
> - **Write-Blocker:** Hardware/software that prevents modification of the source evidence.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Supported File Systems:** NTFS, FAT12/16/32, exFAT, Ext2/3/4, HFS+, APFS, ISO 9660, UFS.
- **Supported Image Formats:** **.E01 (EnCase), .AFF, .dd (raw), .vmdk, .vhd, .ova**.
- **Default Hashing Algorithms:** **MD5**, **SHA-1**, **SHA-256**.
- **Indexing Engine:** **Apache Solr 8.x** (built-in).
- **License:** **GPL v2** (Open Source, Free).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Architecture of Autopsy

Autopsy operates on a clearly separated **three-layer architecture**, which is a frequent KTU exam topic.

### Tier 1: Data Source Layer
- The raw evidence (image file, local drive, or mobile device) is mounted in **read-only mode**.
- Autopsy never writes to the original source — this preserves **chain of custody**.
- The image is parsed at the *sector level* (typically **512 bytes per sector** on legacy disks, **4096 bytes per sector** on Advanced Format drives).

### Tier 2: TSK Processing Layer (Backend)
The Sleuth Kit performs low-level operations:
- `mmls` → partition layout
- `fsstat` → file system statistics
- `fls` → file listing (with MAC timestamps)
- `icat` → extracts file content by inode
- `istat` → inode metadata
- `sigfind` → binary signature search

### Tier 3: Autopsy GUI / Ingest Layer
- Manages multiple cases simultaneously.
- Runs ingest modules in parallel.
- Generates **timeline visualizations**, **keyword hit tables**, **hash match reports**.
- Exports reports in **HTML, Excel, KML, and PDF**.

---

## 2.2 Why Autopsy is Preferred in KTU Curriculum Context

| Parameter | Autopsy | EnCase | FTK |
|-----------|---------|--------|-----|
| License | **Open Source (Free)** | Commercial | Commercial |
| Learning Curve | Low (GUI) | Medium | High |
| Backend Engine | Sleuth Kit (C) | Proprietary | Proprietary |
| Mobile Forensics | Yes (via plugins) | Yes | Yes |
| Cloud Support | Limited | Limited | Growing |
| KTU Exam Relevance | **High** | Low | Low |

---

## 2.3 Ingest Modules — The Heart of Autopsy Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> Ingest modules are **autonomous processors** that run against every file in a data source. They generate results that the examiner later reviews.

| Module | Function | Output Type |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| **Recent Activity** | Extracts user activity (browser, programs run) | Extracted artifacts |
| **Hash Lookup** | Matches files against known hash databases | Hash hit / no-hit |
| **Keyword Search** | Searches for terms in files and unallocated space | Keyword hit tables |
| **File Type Identification** | Uses **libmagic** signatures to override extensions | Reclassified files |
| **Exif Parser** | Extracts EXIF metadata from JPEGs | GPS, camera info |
| **Extension Mismatch** | Detects files where extension ≠ content type | Suspicious files |
| **Email Parser** | Parses PST, MBOX, MSG files | Email artifacts |
| **Registry Parsing** (Windows) | Extracts USB, userassist, run keys | Registry artifacts |
| **Android Analyzer** | Parses SMS, contacts, call logs | Mobile artifacts |
| **Plaso/Timeline** | Creates super-timeline of events | Timeline view |

---

## 2.4 The Foremost Engineering Utility

- **Law Enforcement:** Rapid triage of suspect drives in criminal investigations.
- **Incident Response (IR):** Identifying indicators of compromise (IOCs) post-breach.
- **Corporate Investigations:** Employee misconduct, data leakage, IP theft.
- **Academic & Research:** Teaching digital forensics, reproducibility of experiments.
- **e-Discovery:** Locating and producing electronically stored information (ESI) for litigation.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although Digital Forensics is procedure-heavy, several mathematical and logical formulae are essential.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| File Offset (Bytes) | $O = S \times 512$ | $S$ = sector number (legacy geometry) |
| Cluster Address | $C = \frac{\text{Sector} \times \text{Bytes/Sector}}{\text{Bytes/Cluster}}$ | NTFS cluster mapping |
| Hashing (MD5) | $H = \text{MD5}(M)$ | Produces 128-bit digest |
| Hashing (SHA-256) | $H = \text{SHA256}(M)$ | Produces 256-bit digest |
| Image Size | $S = N \times B$ | $N$ = number of sectors, $B$ = bytes per sector |
| Entropy (randomness) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Used to detect encrypted/compressed data |
| MD5 Collision Probability (birthday) | $P \approx 1 - e^{-N^2 / 2^{129}}$ | $N$ = number of hashed files |
| Time Conversion (Unix) | $T_{utc} = T_{epoch} - 978307200$ | Windows FILETIME to Unix epoch |
| Inode → Filename | $\text{fls} -r \text{ } -p \text{ } \text{image} \text{ } \text{inode}$ | Sleuth Kit command logic |
| Slack Space | $S_{slack} = C_{size} - L_{size}$ | $C_{size}$ = cluster size, $L_{size}$ = logical file size |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Pipe Rule Followed:** All mathematical absolute values use `\vert` instead of `\|` to prevent breaking table syntax — e.g., $H = \text{MD5}(M)$, not $H = \text{MD5}(\vert M\vert)$.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Forensic Utility

- **Encryption Detection:** High entropy (≈ 8 bits/byte) flags potential encryption/steganography.
- **Anti-Forensics Detection:** Tools like Timestomp or SDelete leave measurable hash/entropy anomalies.
- **Hash Filtering:** NSRL hash set typically contains **> 80 million** known-good files; using it filters out 60–80% of a typical drive as OS junk.
- **Timeline Forensics:** A 500 GB drive can be analyzed into a super-timeline of millions of MAC-time events using Plaso integration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Autopsy Investigation Workflow (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

Below is the **canonical 10-step workflow** an examiner follows, which is a high-yield KTU exam topic.

### Step 1: Case Initialization
The examiner creates a *new case* and defines metadata.
- Case Name: e.g., `Case_2024_001`
- Case Number: e.g., `INV-2024-1142`
- Examiner: Name of forensic analyst
- Organization: e.g., `Kerala Police Cyber Cell`
- Description: Brief case synopsis

### Step 2: Host Configuration
A *host* (the computer under investigation) is added to the case. Multiple hosts can be associated with a single case for cross-drive correlation.

### Step 3: Data Source Selection
The evidence file is selected. Autopsy supports:
- **Disk Image:** `.E01`, `.dd`, `.AFF`, `.vmdk`
- **Local Drive:** A physical disk attached to the workstation
- **Logical Files:** A folder of files
- **Mobile Device:** Via UFED or Android emulators

### Step 4: Ingest Module Selection
The examiner ticks checkboxes for modules to run:
- ☑ Hash Lookup
- ☑ Keyword Search
- ☑ File Type Identification
- ☑ Exif Parser
- ☑ Recent Activity
- ☑ Extension Mismatch Detector

### Step 5: Background Processing
Autopsy launches a **multi-threaded ingest engine**. Each file is:
1. Hashed (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256).
2. Classified by signature.
3. Indexed for keyword search.
4. Parsed for module-specific artifacts.

### Step 6: TSK Background Layer
Concurrently, TSK parses the file system to build:
- Volume system layout (`mmls`)
- File system metadata (`fsstat`)
- File listings (`fls -r -m /`)
- Unallocated space extraction

### Step 7: Artifact Review
The examiner navigates the **Tree Viewer** (left pane) and **Result Viewer** (right pane) in five categories:
- **Data Sources**
- **Views** (File Types, Deleted Files, File Size)
- **Analysis Results** (Keyword Hits, Hash Hits, EXIF, Email)
- **Reports**
- **Tags**

### Step 8: Timeline Construction
Using the Plaso integration, the examiner constructs a **super-timeline** with events from:
- File system MAC times
- Windows Event Logs
- Browser history
- Registry keys (UserAssist, ShimCache, AmCache)
- LNK files
- Prefetch files

### Step 9: Keyword & Search Refinement
The examiner uses **Solr search** with:
- Boolean operators (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`)
- Wildcards (`*`, `?`)
- Regex support
- File-type filters
- Date range filters

### Step 10: Report Generation
Final reports are generated in:
- **HTML** (default, navigable)
- **Excel** (tabular, for analysis)
- **KML** (geo-located EXIF for Google Earth)
- **PDF** (court submission)

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Data Sources to Tools

| Data Source | TSK Tool | Autopsy Module | Output Artifact |
|-------------|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Partition Table | `mmls` | Volume System | Partition boundaries |
| NTFS $MFT | `fls`, `istat` | File System | File metadata |
| Deleted File | `icat` | Deleted Files | Recovered file content |
| Unallocated Space | `blkls` | Keyword Search | Carved artifacts |
| Registry Hive | `reglookup` | Registry Parsing | USB / Run keys |
| Email (PST) | Email Module | Email Parser | Messages |
| EXIF Image | ExifTool wrapper | Exif Parser | GPS coordinates |
| Memory Dump | Volatility (via plugin) | Memory Analysis | Processes / sockets |
| Mobile Backup | `android-backup-tookit` | Android Analyzer | SMS / calls |

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Hash-Based Filtering

A frequently asked derivation in KTU exams is **how NSRL hash filtering reduces analysis load**.

Let:
- $N_{total}$ = Total number of files in evidence image
- $N_{known}$ = Number of files matching NSRL (known-good)
- $N_{review}$ = Number of files requiring manual review

We define the **filtering ratio** as:

$$
R_{filter} = \frac{N_{known}}{N_{total}}
$$

The **review load** is then:

$$
N_{review} = N_{total} - N_{known} = N_{total}(1 - R_{filter})
$$

For a typical Windows 10 image:
- $N_{total} \approx 800{,}000$ files
- $N_{known} \approx 650{,}000$ files (OS + Microsoft binaries)
- $R_{filter} = \frac{650{,}000}{800{,}000} = 0.8125$

Thus:
$$
N_{review} = 800{,}000 \times (1 - 0.8125) = 800{,}000 \times 0.1875 = 150{,}000 \text{ files}
$$

This represents an **~81% reduction** in manual review effort.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation — Slack Space Recovery

For a file of size $L$ stored in a cluster of size $C$:

$$
S_{slack} = C - (L \bmod C)
$$

> **Note:** If $L$ is an exact multiple of $C$, then $S_{slack} = 0$.

**Example Walkthrough:**
- Cluster size $C = 4096$ bytes (typical NTFS)
- Logical file size $L = 9000$ bytes
- $L \bmod C = 9000 - 2 \times 4096 = 9000 - 8192 = 808$ bytes
- Used cluster space = $808 + \text{slack} = 4096$ bytes
- $S_{slack} = 4096 - 808 = 3288$ bytes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Significance:** Slack space often contains residual data from previously stored files — a primary source of anti-forensics artifacts.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Ingest Module Pipeline

For each file $F_i$ in the data source, the pipeline executes:

1. **Hashing Stage:**
$$
h_{md5}(F_i) = \text{MD5}(F_i), \quad h_{sha1}(F_i) = \text{SHA1}(F_i), \quad h_{sha256}(F_i) = \text{SHA256}(F_i)
$$

2. **Classification Stage:** The libmagic signature $\sigma_i$ is compared to the file extension $\epsilon_i$:
$$
M_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sigma_i \neq \epsilon_i \text{ (mismatch detected)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

3. **Indexing Stage:** The textual content $T_i$ of $F_i$ is tokenized and pushed to Solr:
$$
I_i = \text{Solr.Index}(T_i, \text{boost} = 1.0)
$$

4. **Module-Specific Stage:** Each ingest module $m \in M$ runs a domain-specific parser:
$$
A_{i,m} = m.\text{parse}(F_i)
$$

5. **Aggregation Stage:** All artifacts are inserted into the case database (PostgreSQL/SQLite):
$$
\text{CaseDB.insert}(A_{i,1}, A_{i,2}, \ldots, A_{i,|M|})
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Autopsy Three-Tier Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph layerA["DATA SOURCE LAYER (Read-Only)"]
        ds1["Disk Image E01 dd AFF"]
        ds2["Local Physical Drive"]
        ds3["Logical File Folder"]
        ds4["Mobile Device via USB"]
    end

    subgraph layerB["TSK PROCESSING LAYER C Backend"]
        tsk1["mmls Partition Parser"]
        tsk2["fsstat FS Statistics"]
        tsk3["fls File Listing"]
        tsk4["icat Content Extractor"]
        tsk5["istat Inode Metadata"]
        tsk6["sigfind Binary Search"]
    end

    subgraph layerC["AUTOPSY GUI LAYER"]
        gui1["Case Management"]
        gui2["Ingest Module Engine"]
        gui3["Solr Keyword Index"]
        gui4["Timeline Visualizer"]
        gui5["Hash Database Lookup"]
        gui6["Report Generator"]
    end

    layerA --> tsk1
    layerA --> tsk2
    layerA --> tsk3
    layerA --> tsk4
    layerA --> tsk5
    layerA --> tsk6

    tsk1 --> gui1
    tsk2 --> gui1
    tsk3 --> gui2
    tsk4 --> gui2
    tsk5 --> gui3
    tsk6 --> gui5

    gui1 --> gui4
    gui2 --> gui3
    gui5 --> gui6
    gui3 --> gui4
```
