<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Autopsy. List any four of its key features.**

**Model Answer:**

**Autopsy** is an open-source, GUI-based digital forensics platform built on top of *The Sleuth Kit (TSK)*, used to perform forensic analysis of disk images, file systems, and mobile devices.

**Four Key Features:**
1. **Case Management** — Multi-case, multi-host database with full chain-of-custody tracking.
2. **Ingest Modules** — Pluggable analyzers for hash, keyword, EXIF, email, registry, and timeline.
3. **Hash Database Integration** — Supports NSRL and custom hash sets for known-good/known-bad filtering.
4. **Timeline Analysis** — Plaso-based super-timeline correlating file system, registry, and log events.
5. **Report Generation** — HTML, Excel, KML, and PDF export for court submission.

*(Any four — 1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for four features = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between The Sleuth Kit (TSK) and Autopsy.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | The Sleuth Kit (TSK) | Autopsy |
|-----------|----------------------|---------|
| **Interface** | Command-line only | Graphical User Interface (GUI) |
| **Primary Use** | Backend library + CLI tools | Front-end analysis platform |
| **Language** | Written in C and C++ | Written in Java (uses TSK as backend) |
| **Skill Requirement** | Advanced — requires Linux/CLI proficiency | Beginner-friendly — point-and-click |
| **Output** | Raw text to terminal | Visual trees, charts, and reports |
| **KTU Use** | Lower-level research | Standard exam topic |

*(3 marks — comparison with at least 4 distinguishing points)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice (Option A OR Option B). Each option is typically split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of Autopsy with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of The Sleuth Kit in the architecture.

**Model Solution:**

The Autopsy platform follows a **three-tier architecture**:

**Tier 1 — Data Source Layer:**
This is the bottom-most layer where evidence is acquired. It accepts disk images (`.E01`, `.dd`, `.AFF`, `.vmdk`), local physical drives, logical folders, or mobile devices. Crucially, the source is mounted in **read-only mode** to preserve evidentiary integrity.

**Tier 2 — Sleuth Kit (TSK) Processing Layer:**
TSK is the C-language backend engine that performs:
- Partition table parsing (`mmls`)
- File system metadata extraction (`fsstat`, `fls`, `istat`)
- File content extraction (`icat`)
- Unallocated space carving (`blkls`)
- Signature-based file detection (`sigfind`)

**Tier 3 — Autopsy GUI Layer:**
The Java-based front-end provides:
- Case/Host/Data Source management
- Ingest Module orchestration
- Solr-based keyword indexing
- Timeline visualization (via Plaso)
- Hash database lookups
- Report generation (HTML, Excel, PDF, KML)

**Role of TSK in Architecture:**
TSK acts as the *engine room* of Autopsy. Without TSK, Autopsy would be a graphical shell with no actual forensic parsing capability. TSK provides **forensically sound**, peer-reviewed, and NIST-validated file system parsers, ensuring that the artifacts extracted by the GUI are admissible in court.

*(Block diagram to be drawn — 3 marks; explanation — 2 marks; TSK role — 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss any five important Ingest Modules of Autopsy with their forensic significance.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Hash Lookup Module:**
Computes MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256 of every file and compares against NSRL (known-good) and custom (known-bad) databases. *Forensic significance:* Drastically reduces the number of files requiring manual review by filtering out OS files.

**2. Keyword Search Module:**
Uses Apache Solr to index and search file contents. *Forensic significance:* Locates emails, documents, and chat logs containing suspect terms (e.g., names, financial data).

**3. File Type Identification Module:**
Uses libmagic signatures to identify true file types regardless of extension. *Forensic significance:* Detects steganography attempts where executables are renamed as `.jpg`.

**4. Exif Parser Module:**
Extracts EXIF metadata from JPEGs. *Forensic significance:* GPS coordinates and timestamps help place a suspect/device at a crime scene.

**5. Extension Mismatch Detector:**
Flags files where the extension differs from the actual content type. *Forensic significance:* Identifies disguised malware or contraband files.

**6. Email Parser Module:**
Parses PST, MBOX, and MSG files. *Forensic significance:* Recovers deleted/archived emails for use in corporate or criminal investigations.

**7. Recent Activity Module:**
Extracts browser history, recently accessed files, and program execution artifacts. *Forensic significance:* Establishes user intent and timeline of actions.

*(Any 5 modules — 1 mark each + 2 marks for forensic significance synthesis = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step process of creating a new case in Autopsy and adding a disk image as a data source.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Launch Autopsy**
Open the application. The main window shows a list of *Recent Cases* and an option to create a *New Case*.

**Step 2: Create New Case**
Click **"New Case"**. Fill in the metadata:
- **Case Name** (e.g., `Case_2024_Burglary`)
- **Case Number** (e.g., `INV-1142/2024`)
- **Examiner** (Full name)
- **Organization** (e.g., `KTU Cyber Forensics Lab`)
- **Description** (Brief synopsis)

Click **Next**.

**Step 3: Add Host**
A *host* represents the computer/device under investigation. Enter:
- Host Name (e.g., `Suspect-Laptop-01`)
- Optional: time zone, hash calculation settings

Click **Next**.

**Step 4: Add Data Source**
Select the evidence type:
- Disk Image → Browse to `.E01`, `.dd`, or `.AFF` file
- Local Drive → Choose physical disk
- Logical Files → Select folder
- Unallocated Space → Select image directly

Click **Next**.

**Step 5: Configure Ingest Modules**
A checklist appears with modules:
- ☑ Hash Lookup
- ☑ Keyword Search
- ☑ File Type Identification
- ☑ Exif Parser
- ☑ Extension Mismatch Detector
- ☑ Recent Activity
- ☑ Email Parser

Click **Next** to start processing.

**Step 6: Processing**
Autopsy launches the ingest engine. Progress is displayed module-wise. Once complete, the case is ready for review.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per major step + 1 mark for ingest module selection = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain hash-based filtering in Autopsy. A Windows image contains 500,000 files, of which 410,000 match the NSRL known-good database. Calculate (i) filtering ratio, and (ii) the number of files requiring manual review.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Hash-Based Filtering:**
Hash-based filtering is a triage technique where every file in the evidence is cryptographically hashed (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) and compared against a database of *known-good* (e.g., NSRL — containing Microsoft binaries, OS files) or *known-bad* hashes (e.g., Project Vic). Files matching the known-good set are flagged as "no further review required," while the rest are prioritized for manual analysis.

**Forensic Significance:**
- Drastically reduces examiner workload.
- Highlights user-created vs. system files.
- Detects known malware instantly.

**Given Data:**
- $N_{total} = 500{,}000$ files
- $N_{known} = 410{,}000$ files

**(i) Filtering Ratio:**

$$
R_{filter} = \frac{N_{known}}{N_{total}} = \frac{410{,}000}{500{,}000} = 0.82 = 82\%
$$

**(ii) Files Requiring Manual Review:**

$$
N_{review} = N_{total} - N_{known} = 500{,}000 - 410{,}000 = 90{,}000 \text{ files}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
N_{review} = N_{total} \times (1 - R_{filter}) = 500{,}000 \times 0.18 = 90{,}000 \text{ files}
$$

**Conclusion:** 82% of the image is filtered automatically; only **90,000 files** need manual review — an 82% reduction in examiner effort.

*(3 marks concept + 2 marks formula + 2 marks calculation = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Autopsy-Related Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing TSK with Autopsy:** Many students write "Autopsy is a command-line tool." **INCORRECT.** TSK is the command-line engine; **Autopsy is the GUI front-end built ON TSK**. Losing 1–2 marks here is common.
>
> 2. **Omitting Ingest Modules:** When asked to "explain Autopsy," examiners expect at least **3–5 named ingest modules** with their purpose. A generic answer loses 3+ marks.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Read-Only Mount:** Forgetting to mention that Autopsy **never modifies the source evidence** (preserving chain of custody) costs marks in case-management questions.
>
> 4. **Wrong Hash Format:** Writing "Autopsy uses SHA-512" or "DES encryption" is factually wrong. Correct: **MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256**.
>
> 5. **Missing Time Conversion in Timeline Questions:** UNIX timestamps and Windows FILETIME differ by **11644473600 seconds**. Examiners deduct marks for unit errors.
>
> 6. **Not Drawing a Block Diagram:** In 7-mark architecture questions, a missing block diagram can cost up to 2 marks even if the text is perfect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Autopsy in 60 Seconds**

- ✅ Autopsy = **Open-source GUI forensic platform** built on **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)**.
- ✅ TSK = **C-based CLI backend**; Autopsy = **Java-based GUI front-end**.
- ✅ Default hashing: **MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256** (in parallel).
- ✅ Indexing engine: **Apache Solr**.
- ✅ Key image formats supported: **.E01, .dd, .AFF, .vmdk**.
- ✅ File systems supported: **NTFS, FAT, Ext, HFS+, APFS**.
- ✅ **NSRL** = known-good hash database; **Project Vic** = known-bad (CSAM).
- ✅ Case → Host → Data Source → Ingest Modules → Review → Report.
- ✅ Ingest modules to remember: **Hash Lookup, Keyword Search, Exif Parser, Email Parser, Extension Mismatch, Recent Activity, File Type ID, Registry Parser**.
- ✅ Hash filter formula: $R_{filter} = \frac{N_{known}}{N_{total}}$.
- ✅ Slack space: $S_{slack} = C - (L \bmod C)$.
- ✅ Always mount evidence **read-only**; **never write to source**.
- ✅ Reports exportable as **HTML, Excel, KML, PDF**.
- ✅ Timeline analysis = **Plaso super-timeline** integration.
- ✅ Autopsy is **GPL v2** licensed — **free and open-source**.
- ✅ KTU exam favorites: **architecture diagram, ingest module explanation, hash filtering numerical.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RegRipper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RegRipper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RegRipper** is an **open-source forensic utility**, written primarily in **Perl**, developed by **Harlan Carvey**, designed to extract, parse, analyze, and report data from **Windows Registry hive files** during digital forensic investigations. It operates as a **plugin-based framework** that allows investigators to automate the extraction of forensic artifacts (e.g., user activity, system configuration, malware persistence, network history) from the binary registry hive files (`SAM`, `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SECURITY`, `NTUSER.DAT`, `UsrClass.dat`).

The tool is officially hosted on **GitHub** (by Keydet89) and is widely referenced in SANS Institute forensic cheat sheets and the **NIST CFTT (Computer Forensic Tool Testing)** registry. RegRipper is a **read-only, non-invasive** tool — it never modifies the original evidence hive files, satisfying the **ACPO (Association of Chief Police Officers) Principle 1** of preserving original evidence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Forensic Librarian"**
>
> Imagine the Windows Registry as a **massive, old-fashioned library** where Windows secretly stores every important configuration and user activity record in a complex, coded filing system. The registry hives (`SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, etc.) are like **locked cabinets** in that library. Each cabinet contains **drawers (keys)**, each drawer contains **folders (subkeys)**, and each folder contains **documents (values)**.
>
> A forensic investigator walks into this library, but the cabinets are written in an obscure coded language (binary format). **RegRipper is the expert librarian** who:
> 1. Knows which cabinet to open (`hive file selection`).
> 2. Has a team of specialists (plugins) — one specialist knows user accounts, another knows USB history, another knows recently opened files.
> 3. Translates the coded language into a clean, readable report (`.txt` / `.csv` / `.html`).
>
> The investigator (you) doesn't need to learn the code; you just need to know **which specialist (plugin) to call**.

## 1.3 Core Highlights

- **Written in:** Perl (interpreted language — cross-platform capable, though primarily used on Windows).
- **Two Interfaces:** `rip.exe` (command-line) and `rr.exe` (GUI).
- **Default Output Folder:** `C:\RegRipper\output` (configurable).
- **Plugin Count:** Over **200 community plugins** maintained by Harlan Carvey.
- **Plugins Directory:** `C:\RegRipper\plugins\` (`.pl` files).
- **Supported Hives:** `NTUSER.DAT`, `UsrClass.dat`, `SAM`, `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SECURITY`.
- **Read-Only Operation:** Forensic integrity preserved.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> RegRipper follows a **pipeline architecture** divided into three logical phases. Understanding these phases is **high-yield** for KTU ESE questions.

### Phase 1 — Hive Acquisition
- The investigator first **acquires** the registry hive from the evidence image using tools like `FTK Imager`, `EnCase`, or `dd`.
- The hive is a **binary file** (e.g., `NTUSER.DAT`) that Windows locks while the OS is running — therefore, it must be acquired from a **mounted read-only image** or **shadow copy**.
- RegRipper **never accesses a live, locked hive**; it works strictly on **extracted copies**.

### Phase 2 — Plugin-Based Parsing
- The user selects a **hive file** and a **plugin** (or **plugin set** for automated batch runs).
- Each plugin is a Perl script that opens the hive using the **`Win32::Registry`** or **`Parse::Win32Registry`** module.
- The plugin walks the registry tree (keys → subkeys → values) and extracts the relevant forensic data.

### Phase 3 — Reporting
- Results are written to a **plain-text file** by default, or can be redirected to **CSV** or **HTML** using RegRipper's GUI.
- Timestamps are automatically converted from **Windows FILETIME (64-bit)** epoch format to **human-readable UTC** (e.g., `2024-09-15 14:32:11Z`).

## 2.2 Forensic Triage — Why RegRipper Matters in Investigation

| **Forensic Question** | **Relevant Registry Artifact** | **RegRipper Plugin** |
|---|---|---|
| What user accounts exist on the system? | `SAM` hive → `SAM\Domains\Account\Users` | `samparse.pl` |
| What USB devices were connected? | `SYSTEM` → `Enum\USBSTOR` | `usb.pl` |
| What programs ran on the system? | `NTUSER.DAT` → `...\Run` keys + `UserAssist` | `run.pl`, `userassist.pl` |
| What files were recently accessed? | `NTUSER.DAT` → `RecentDocs` | `recentdocs.pl` |
| What network shares were mapped? | `NTUSER.DAT` → `Network` | `network.pl` |
| What wireless networks were joined? | `SYSTEM` → `...\Interfaces` | `wpa_profiles.pl` |
| What is the system's timezone? | `SYSTEM` → `CurrentControlSet\Control\TimeZoneInformation` | `timezone.pl` |
| Who is the Registered Owner? | `SOFTWARE` → `Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion` | `winver.pl` |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Terminology Cheat Sheet

| **Term** | **Definition** | **Forensic Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| **Hive** | A logical group of keys, subkeys, and values stored as a binary file on disk | The unit of analysis for RegRipper |
| **Key** | A folder-like container in the registry (can contain subkeys and values) | Organizational node |
| **Value** | An actual data entry — contains a name, type (REG_SZ, REG_DWORD, REG_BINARY), and data | The "evidence" extracted by plugins |
| **Key Last Write Time (KLWT)** | The timestamp of the most recent modification to a key | Critical for **timeline analysis** |
| **UserAssist** | Rotating ROT-13 encoded list of executed programs in `NTUSER.DAT` | Reveals program execution despite anti-forensics |
| **Shimcache** | Application compatibility cache in `SYSTEM` | Indicates programs that ran (even if deleted) |
| **AmCache** | Program execution metadata in `SYSTEM` (Win 8+) | Strong evidence of program execution |
| **Last-Login Timestamp** | Stored in `SAM` | Establishes user activity window |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Forensics Fields

- **Incident Response (IR):** Rapidly triage a compromised machine to identify persistence mechanisms (Run keys, Services, Scheduled Tasks).
- **Law Enforcement:** Reconstruct user activity (browser history, recent documents, USB insertions) in criminal investigations.
- **e-Discovery:** Identify relevant custodians and their data locations for litigation.
- **Malware Analysis:** Trace registry modifications made by malicious payloads.
- **Insider Threat Investigations:** Detect policy violations, unauthorized software installation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Forensic Workflow Using RegRipper (Exhaustive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Explain the steps to perform a forensic analysis of the Windows Registry using RegRipper."* The following is the **board-exam-perfect** answer.

### Step 1 — Evidence Acquisition

The investigator first creates a **forensically sound image** of the target drive (e.g., using `FTK Imager` in **E01/DD format**). This image is then mounted **read-only** using tools like `FTK Imager` or Arsenal Image Mounter.

> A **write-blocker** (hardware or software) must be used during acquisition to prevent any modification of the original evidence.

### Step 2 — Locate the Registry Hives

The forensic analyst navigates to the standard Windows paths:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NTUSER.DAT} &\rightarrow \texttt{C:\textbackslash Users\textbackslash<username>\textbackslash NTUSER.DAT} \\
\text{UsrClass.dat} &\rightarrow \texttt{C:\textbackslash Users\textbackslash<username>\textbackslash AppData\textbackslash Local\textbackslash Microsoft\textbackslash Windows\textbackslash UsrClass.dat} \\
\text{SAM} &\rightarrow \texttt{C:\textbackslash Windows\textbackslash System32\textbackslash config\textbackslash SAM} \\
\text{SYSTEM} &\rightarrow \texttt{C:\textbackslash Windows\textbackslash System32\textbackslash config\textbackslash SYSTEM} \\
\text{SOFTWARE} &\rightarrow \texttt{C:\textbackslash Windows\textbackslash System32\textbackslash config\textbackslash SOFTWARE} \\
\text{SECURITY} &\rightarrow \texttt{C:\textbackslash Windows\textbackslash System32\textbackslash config\textbackslash SECURITY}
\end{aligned}$$

Each hive is **copied to a working directory** (e.g., `C:\Evidence\Hives\`) with a documented **chain of custody**.

### Step 3 — Launch RegRipper

The investigator opens the RegRipper GUI (`rr.exe`) or uses the command-line tool (`rip.exe`). The tool is **launched from the local workstation**, never on the suspect system.

> **Best Practice:** Copy `rip.exe`, `rr.exe`, and the `plugins/` folder to a USB drive or forensic workstation. Do not run RegRipper from the suspect's hard drive.

### Step 4 — Hive File Selection

In the GUI, the investigator:
1. Clicks **"Browse"** to select the hive file (e.g., `NTUSER.DAT`).
2. Selects the **"Report"** mode (recommended) or **"Lite"** mode.
3. Specifies the **Output file** path and name (e.g., `C:\Evidence\Reports\NTUSER_Report.txt`).
4. Clicks **"Rip!"** to begin the parsing.

> **Lite Mode vs. Report Mode:**
> - **Report Mode:** Runs **all** plugins for the selected hive (comprehensive; may take 10–30 minutes per hive).
> - **Lite Mode:** Runs only a **curated subset** of critical plugins for quick triage.

### Step 5 — Plugin Execution

RegRipper iterates through each plugin in the `plugins/` directory that matches the selected hive type. For example, when analyzing `SYSTEM`, it loads plugins like `system`, `services`, `usb`, `network`, `timezone`, etc.

$$\text{Output} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{Plugin}_i(\text{Hive})$$

where $n$ is the number of plugins relevant to the hive.

### Step 6 — Result Interpretation

The generated `.txt` report contains:
- Plugin name and version (e.g., `samparse v.20090718`).
- The full **key path** examined.
- Extracted **values** with their **data types**.
- **Decoded timestamps** (KLWT and value timestamps in UTC).
- Plugin author's notes (if any).

The investigator cross-references the timestamps with the **incident timeline** to establish or refute hypotheses.

### Step 7 — Documentation & Chain of Custody

Every step, every hash (MD5/SHA-1) of the hive, every tool version, and every analyst's name must be documented in the **forensic case notes** for court admissibility.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Plugin Categories

| **Category** | **Example Plugins** | **Hive Required** | **Evidentiary Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **User Activity** | `recentdocs.pl`, `userassist.pl`, `runmru.pl` | `NTUSER.DAT` | High |
| **Network Artifacts** | `network.pl`, `wpa_profiles.pl`, `lanman.pl` | `NTUSER.DAT`, `SYSTEM` | High |
| **Hardware/USB** | `usb.pl`, `mounteddevices.pl` | `SYSTEM`, `NTUSER.DAT` | High |
| **System Configuration** | `timezone.pl`, `winver.pl`, `compname.pl` | `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE` | Medium |
| **User Accounts** | `samparse.pl`, `sid.pl` | `SAM` | Critical |
| **Auto-Start Locations** | `run.pl`, `services.pl`, `appinitdlls.pl` | `NTUSER.DAT`, `SYSTEM` | Critical (persistence) |
| **Application Data** | `ie_settings.pl`, `office.pl` | `NTUSER.DAT`, `SOFTWARE` | Medium |

## 3.3 Command-Line Usage (Exhaustive)

For **scripted/automated** analysis across multiple hives (e.g., all user profiles), the command-line tool `rip.exe` is preferred.

$$\texttt{rip.exe -r C:\textbackslash Evidence\textbackslash Hives\textbackslash NTUSER.DAT -f system -o C:\textbackslash Evidence\textbackslash Reports\textbackslash ntuser\_report.txt}$$

Breaking down the flags:
- `-r` → Path to the **registry hive** to parse.
- `-f` → The **plugin** to run (e.g., `system`, `all`, `recentdocs`).
- `-o` → **Output file** path for the report.
- `-p` → Path to the **plugins** directory (if not in the default location).
- `-c` → **CSV** output format (optional).

> **KTU Tip:** When asked to *"demonstrate RegRipper usage"*, always show the **command-line syntax**. It is more technical, more precise, and earns higher marks than just mentioning the GUI.

## 3.4 Mathematical Note on Timestamps

Windows registry stores timestamps as a **64-bit FILETIME** value — the number of **100-nanosecond intervals** since **January 1, 1601 (UTC)**. To convert to Unix Epoch (seconds since Jan 1, 1970):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Unix}} &= \frac{T_{\text{Windows}} - 116444736000000000}{10000000} \\
\text{where } 116444736000000000 &= \text{offset in 100-ns intervals between 1601 and 1970}
\end{aligned}$$

RegRipper performs this conversion **automatically** and outputs human-readable UTC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RegRipper Forensic Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspect Drive Image E01 or DD] --> B[Mount Read-Only via FTK Imager]
    B --> C[Extract Registry Hives NTUSER DAT SAM SYSTEM SOFTWARE SECURITY]
    C --> D[Verify Hashes SHA-1 MD5]
    D --> E[Launch RegRipper rr.exe or rip.exe]
    E --> F{Select Hive Type}
    F -->|User Profile| G[Load NTUSER DAT and UsrClass dat Plugins]
    F -->|System| H[Load SAM SYSTEM and SOFTWARE Plugins]
    F -->|All| I[Run Report Mode Full Triage]
    G --> J[Parse Keys and Values]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Apply Timestamp Conversion Windows FILETIME to UTC]
    K --> L[Generate Report txt csv html]
    L --> M[Analyst Reviews and Builds Timeline]
    M --> N[Document Chain of Custody]
    N --> O[Court Admissible Evidence]
```

## 4.2 RegRipper Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        A1[Registry Hive File]
    end

    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3

    B3 --> C2

```

## 4.3 Plugin Execution Flow — Detailed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analyst Selects Hive] --> B[RegRipper Opens Hive Read-Only]
    B --> C[Plugin Loader Scans plugins Directory]
    C --> D{Plugin Applicable to Hive Type}

    D -->|No| F[Skip Plugin]
    E --> G[Parse Registry Key Path]
```

```mermaid
    B -->|What External Devices| E[Analyze SYSTEM and NTUSER DAT - USBSTOR MountedDevices]
    B -->|What Network Activity| F[Analyze NTUSER DAT and SYSTEM - Network TypedPaths WPA]
```
