# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define RegRipper. Mention its key components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** RegRipper is an open-source, plugin-based forensic tool written in **Perl** by **Harlan Carvey** for extracting and analyzing **Windows Registry hive files** in a forensically sound manner.

**Key Components (2 marks):**
1. **`rip.exe`** — the command-line interface for scripted/automated parsing.
2. **`rr.exe`** — the GUI interface for interactive use.
3. **`plugins/` directory** — contains Perl (`.pl`) scripts, each designed to parse a specific forensic artifact (e.g., USB history, recent documents, user accounts).
4. **Output module** — generates reports in `.txt`, `.csv`, or `.html` formats with human-readable UTC timestamps.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**List any four registry artifacts that RegRipper can extract and state their forensic significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **#** | **Artifact** | **Plugin / Hive** | **Forensic Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | USB Devices | `usb.pl` on `SYSTEM` | Proves removable media was connected; reveals device serial numbers |
| 2 | UserAssist | `userassist.pl` on `NTUSER.DAT` | Decodes ROT-13 encrypted list of executed programs |
| 3 | RecentDocs | `recentdocs.pl` on `NTUSER.DAT` | Shows recently opened files by the user |
| 4 | Run Keys | `run.pl` on `NTUSER.DAT` / `SYSTEM` | Identifies persistence mechanisms / auto-start malware |

*(Any 4 valid pairs = full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**(a)** Explain the architecture of RegRipper with a neat diagram. Discuss its significance in digital forensic investigations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable examples, explain how RegRipper can be used to investigate user activity, program execution, and external device usage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Architecture (5 marks):**

RegRipper follows a **modular, plugin-based architecture** consisting of the following layers:

1. **Input Layer:** The forensic analyst supplies an extracted registry hive file (e.g., `NTUSER.DAT`) — typically copied from a mounted read-only forensic image.
2. **Core Engine Layer:** Consists of the GUI (`rr.exe`) and CLI (`rip.exe`) executables that provide the user interface. The core engine loads the **Plugin Manager**, which scans the `plugins/` directory and matches plugins to the hive type.
3. **Plugin Layer:** A library of **Perl scripts** (`.pl` files), each specializing in parsing a specific forensic artifact. Examples: `samparse.pl`, `usb.pl`, `recentdocs.pl`, `userassist.pl`.
4. **Parsing Engine:** Uses Perl modules `Parse::Win32Registry` and `Win32::Registry` to read the binary hive structure, walk the key/subkey tree, and extract values with their data types.
5. **Output Layer:** Generates a **human-readable report** in `.txt` (default), `.csv`, or `.html`. Timestamps are automatically converted from **Windows FILETIME (64-bit)** to readable UTC format.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4.2 for the Mermaid architecture diagram — for board exams, redraw a simplified block diagram with these 5 layers.)*

**Forensic Significance (2 marks):**
- **Automation:** Replaces hours of manual registry parsing with seconds of automated extraction.
- **Non-invasive:** Operates on copies of hives, preserving original evidence.
- **Comprehensive:** Over 200 plugins cover nearly all forensic artifacts.
- **Standardization:** Reports are uniform, making them easy to defend in court and to share with other analysts.
- **Court Admissibility:** Open-source code allows peer review and validation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**User Activity (2.5 marks):**

- **`recentdocs.pl`** parses `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RecentDocs` to list the **most recently opened files** by the user, with their file extensions and last-access timestamps.
- **`runmru.pl`** extracts the **Most Recently Used** list of programs run via the Run dialog (`Win+R`).
- **`typedpaths.pl`** reveals paths manually typed into the Windows Explorer address bar — often the strongest evidence of a user navigating to a specific folder.
- **`lastvisiteddir.pl`** shows the last folder visited in the **Save As** dialog.

**Program Execution (2.5 marks):**

- **`userassist.pl`** decodes the ROT-13 encrypted `UserAssist` key (`NTUSER.DAT\...\UserAssist\{CEBFF5CD-ACE2-4F4F-9178-9926F41749EA}\Count`) which contains a chronological list of programs launched via Explorer.
- **`run.pl`** examines all `Run` and `RunOnce` keys in `NTUSER.DAT` and `SYSTEM` to identify **auto-start programs** — a common malware persistence location.
- **`services.pl`** lists all registered Windows services and their start types — used to identify suspicious services installed by malware.

**External Device Usage (2 marks):**

- **`usb.pl`** parses `SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` and `\USB` to extract a list of all USB devices ever connected, with **vendor ID, product ID, and unique serial number**.
- **`mounteddevices.pl`** reads `SYSTEM\MountedDevices` to find the **drive letters** assigned to USB devices and **volume serial numbers** — useful even after the device is removed.
- **`devclass.pl`** examines the `DeviceClasses` key for additional device metadata.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure for performing a forensic analysis of the Windows Registry using RegRipper. Mention the command-line syntax with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the Windows Registry in digital forensics. How does RegRipper exploit registry artifacts to reconstruct a forensic timeline? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step-by-Step Procedure (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Evidence Acquisition:** Acquire a forensically sound image of the suspect drive using tools like **FTK Imager** in **E01** or **DD** format, using a **write-blocker** to preserve integrity. Compute and record **MD5/SHA-1** hashes.

**Step 2 — Mount the Image Read-Only:** Mount the acquired image using **FTK Imager** or **Arsenal Image Mounter** in **read-only** mode. This protects the original evidence from accidental modification.

**Step 3 — Locate the Registry Hives:** Navigate to the standard Windows paths and copy each hive (`NTUSER.DAT`, `SAM`, `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SECURITY`, `UsrClass.dat`) to a working directory. Re-verify the hashes after copying.

**Step 4 — Launch RegRipper:** Open the RegRipper GUI (`rr.exe`) or use the command-line tool (`rip.exe`) from a forensic workstation.

**Step 5 — Hive Selection:** In the GUI, click **Browse** to select the hive; in CLI, use the `-r` flag.

**Step 6 — Run Plugins:** Choose **Report mode** (full triage) or **Lite mode** (curated subset). Plugins automatically parse the relevant forensic artifacts.

**Step 7 — Generate Report:** Specify the output file path. RegRipper writes the report in `.txt` format by default, with timestamps converted to UTC.

**Step 8 — Analyze and Document:** Review the report, build a **timeline of events** using KLWTs (Key Last Write Times), and document all findings in the case notes with proper **chain of custody**.

**Command-Line Syntax (2 marks):**

$$\texttt{rip.exe -r C:\textbackslash Evidence\textbackslash Hives\textbackslash NTUSER.DAT -f recentdocs -p C:\textbackslash RegRipper\textbackslash plugins -o C:\textbackslash Evidence\textbackslash Reports\textbackslash ntuser\_recent.txt}$$

- `-r` → Specifies the registry hive to parse.
- `-f recentdocs` → Runs the `recentdocs` plugin (or use `-f all` for full triage).
- `-p` → Points to the plugins directory.
- `-o` → Output file path.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Role of the Windows Registry in Digital Forensics (3.5 marks):**

The Windows Registry is often called the **"heart and soul"** of the Windows operating system. It is a hierarchical database that stores **low-level settings, OS configuration, hardware information, user preferences, and application-specific data**. Forensically, it is invaluable because:

- It records **user activity** that may not exist anywhere else on the system (e.g., UserAssist, TypedPaths, RecentDocs).
- It persists even when files are deleted — registry keys survive the deletion of the corresponding files.
- It contains **persistence mechanisms** (Run keys, Services, Scheduled Tasks in registry) that malware authors exploit.
- It holds **authentication artifacts** (SAM database of user account hashes).
- It logs **hardware events** (USB insertions, network connections, mounted devices).
- It contains **application configuration** that may reveal the presence and usage of specific software.

**RegRipper's Role in Timeline Reconstruction (3.5 marks):**

RegRipper automates the extraction of timestamps from every relevant key and value:

1. **Key Last Write Time (KLWT):** Every registry key has a last-modified timestamp. RegRipper plugins extract these automatically, producing a chronological list of when keys were created or modified.
2. **Value Data Timestamps:** Certain values (e.g., `UserAssist` counters, `RecentDocs` timestamps) contain their own embedded timestamps.
3. **Cross-Referencing with Other Artifacts:** RegRipper's output is correlated with **event logs**, **Prefetch files**, **Lnk files**, and **memory artifacts** to build a comprehensive timeline.
4. **Super-timeline Creation:** Tools like **Plaso/log2timeline** can ingest RegRipper output and merge it with other timestamp sources, producing a single unified timeline spanning file system, registry, and event log activity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Write-Blocker:** Many students skip mentioning the use of a hardware/software write-blocker during acquisition — this costs **1 mark** under "forensic soundness."
> 2. **Confusing Registry "Keys" and "Values":** Marks are deducted if you mix up **keys** (containers) and **values** (data entries). Remember: *Keys hold subkeys and values; values hold the actual data.*
> 3. **Not Mentioning Hashing:** Failing to mention **MD5/SHA-1 verification** before and after extraction is a common omission.
> 4. **Ignoring KLWT:** A surprising number of students forget to mention **Key Last Write Time** as the primary timeline source — this is a **2-mark penalty** in timeline-related questions.
> 5. **Using "Registry Editor (regedit)" in place of RegRipper:** `regedit` is a **live system** tool and is **not forensically sound** — never confuse the two.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for RegRipper:**

- ⭐ **RegRipper = Perl-based, plugin-driven, read-only Windows Registry forensic tool** by **Harlan Carvey**.
- ⭐ Two interfaces: **`rip.exe` (CLI)** and **`rr.exe` (GUI)**.
- ⭐ Plugins are **Perl scripts** in the `plugins/` directory (over **200** community plugins).
- ⭐ Works on **extracted hive copies** — never on live, locked hives.
- ⭐ Supports all 6 standard hives: **NTUSER.DAT, UsrClass.dat, SAM, SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SECURITY**.
- ⭐ Default output: **`.txt`**, with optional **`.csv`** and **`.html`** formats.
- ⭐ Timestamps auto-converted from **Windows FILETIME (100-ns intervals since Jan 1, 1601)** to **human-readable UTC**.
- ⭐ **Key plugins to remember:**
  - `samparse.pl` → User accounts from `SAM`.
  - `usb.pl` → USB device history from `SYSTEM`.
  - `userassist.pl` → Decoded program execution list from `NTUSER.DAT`.
  - `recentdocs.pl` → Recently opened files.
  - `run.pl` → Auto-start persistence keys.
  - `network.pl` → Mapped network drives and shares.
  - `timezone.pl` → System timezone (critical for timestamp correlation).
- ⭐ **Command-line syntax:** `rip.exe -r <hive> -f <plugin> -p <plugins_dir> -o <output>`
- ⭐ **Two modes:** **Report Mode** (full triage, all plugins) and **Lite Mode** (curated subset for quick triage).
- ⭐ **Forensic principles satisfied:** Non-invasive, hash-verified, chain-of-custody documented, court-admissible.
- ⭐ **Primary timeline source:** **Key Last Write Time (KLWT)** — extracted by every plugin automatically.
- ⭐ **Common investigation targets:** User accounts, USB history, program execution, network activity, persistence mechanisms, system configuration.
- ⭐ **Related tools in a RegRipper workflow:** `FTK Imager` (acquisition), `Plaso/log2timeline` (super-timeline), `Autopsy` (case management).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Volatility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Volatility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Digital Forensics**, **Volatility** refers to the characteristic of digital data that determines **how long the data persists in its original, unaltered state** before it is lost, overwritten, or destroyed — either by natural system processes, user activity, or power loss. Data that disappears within seconds or milliseconds is termed **highly volatile**, whereas data stored on persistent media is termed **non-volatile**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Volatility in digital forensics describes the *transient nature* of digital evidence and forms the basis of the **Order of Volatility (OoV)** — a prioritized sequence that dictates *which data must be collected first* during live-system forensic acquisition, as codified in **IETF RFC 3227 — "Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving"**.

The "lifetime" of digital evidence is governed by three forces:

1. **Hardware decay** — Capacitive discharge of RAM cells (typically **milliseconds to seconds**).
2. **System processes** — Operating system overwriting buffers, log rotation, cron jobs.
3. **External events** — Power failure, system shutdown, network disconnection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Crime Scene at a Melting Ice Sculpture":**
> Imagine you arrive at a crime scene where the evidence is a giant ice sculpture. The outer layer (delicate carvings = CPU registers, cache) melts in seconds. The inner core (a sealed capsule = hard disk) may last for years. As a forensic investigator, you must **photograph and preserve the outer layers first**, then the middle layers, then the inner core. **You cannot save the core without first capturing the delicate exterior**, because once melted, the fine details are lost forever. This is exactly the role of the *Order of Volatility* in digital forensics.

In computing, every piece of evidence has a "melting rate":

| Evidence Type | Approx. Lifetime | Persistence |
|---|---|---|
| CPU Registers & Cache | < 1 microsecond | Extremely Volatile |
| RAM (Routing table, ARP cache, processes) | Seconds → Minutes | Highly Volatile |
| Running Process State | Seconds → Minutes | Volatile |
| Network State / Temp File Systems | Minutes → Hours | Moderately Volatile |
| Disk File System | Days → Years | Non-Volatile |
| Remote Logs / Backups | Years | Persistent |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics

The forensic community uses the **Order of Volatility (OoV)** as a standardized ranking. The most widely cited standard is **RFC 3227 (F. Brezinski, T. Killalea – IETF, February 2002)**, which prescribes the following order (most volatile first):

1. **CPU registers, cache**
2. **Routing table, ARP cache, process table, kernel statistics, memory**
3. **Temporary file systems**
4. **Disk**
5. **Remote logging and monitoring data**
6. **Physical configuration, network topology**
7. **Archival media (offline backups)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every digital artifact in this list decays at a different rate. The **goal** of a forensic responder is to capture items at the top of the list **before** they decay, while still preserving the **chain of custody** and **forensic soundness**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Order of Volatility — Layered Logic

The Order of Volatility is not arbitrary; it is derived from the **physical and logical residence time** of data. Let us break down the reasoning layer by layer:

### Layer 1 — CPU Registers & On-Chip Cache
- **Residence medium:** Transistors inside the CPU die.
- **Persistence mechanism:** Electrical charge on MOSFET gates.
- **Half-life:** **Nanoseconds to microseconds** (driven by clock frequency, ~10⁻⁹ s).
- **Why first?** The moment the CPU halts or context-switches, the register contents are overwritten by the next instruction fetch. If we do not dump registers in real time, this evidence is gone.

### Layer 2 — Main Memory (RAM) and Kernel State
- **Residence medium:** DRAM cells (each cell = 1 capacitor + 1 access transistor).
- **Persistence mechanism:** Charge stored on a capacitor; **must be refreshed every ~64 ms** (JEDEC standard).
- **Half-life:** Without refresh, contents decay in **~1–2 seconds** due to leakage current. With power, contents persist until overwritten.
- **Captured artifacts:** Running processes, open network sockets, cryptographic keys in memory, clipboard contents, decrypted buffers, kernel modules loaded, system calls in flight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Forensic Principle (RFC 3227 § 2.1):** *The order of volatility is roughly the inverse of the order in which evidence should be collected.* The most volatile items must be acquired **first**; the least volatile may be collected last.

### Layer 3 — Temporary File Systems
- **Examples:** `/tmp`, `/var/tmp`, RAM-backed mounts (`tmpfs`, `ramfs`), swap partitions.
- **Half-life:** **Minutes to hours** (or until next reboot, whichever is sooner).
- **Captured artifacts:** Recently opened files, unzipped archives, network packet captures in-progress, session cookies.

### Layer 4 — Disk Storage (HDD / SSD / NVMe)
- **Residence medium:** Magnetic domains (HDD) or floating-gate transistors (SSD).
- **Persistence mechanism:** Stable physical states that do not require power.
- **Half-life:** **Years to decades** (magnetic decay ~1–2 % per year; SSD charge loss ~10 years for consumer grade).
- **Captured artifacts:** File system metadata, deleted files (in slack space / unallocated clusters), log files, user documents.

### Layer 5 — Remote / Off-Host Artifacts
- **Examples:** Network flow records, SIEM logs, cloud audit trails, ISP logs.
- **Persistence:** Determined by remote retention policy (often **90 days to 7 years**).
- **Captured via:** Subpoena, court order, or pre-established cooperation.

### Layer 6 — Archival / Offline Media
- **Examples:** Tape backups, optical discs, off-site storage.
- **Persistence:** **Indefinite** if stored properly.

## 2.2 The Volatility Framework (Tool Context)

While "volatility" is a concept, KTU examiners also expect familiarity with the **Volatility Framework** — an *open-source memory forensics toolchain* written in Python. Two major versions exist:

- **Volatility 2** — Python 2 based, profile-based, supports Windows XP → Windows 10.
- **Volatility 3** — Python 3, plugin-based, OS-agnostic, no profiles required.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **key equations, constants, and decision rules** required for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments on this topic.

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RAM Cell Refresh Interval | $t_{refresh} = 64 \text{ ms}$ (JEDEC standard) | Milliseconds |
| 2 | Capacitor Discharge (no refresh) | $V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t / \tau}$ | $V$ in Volts, $t$ in seconds, $\tau$ = RC time constant |
| 3 | Time constant for DRAM cell | $\tau = R_{leak} \cdot C_{cell}$ | Typical $C_{cell} \approx 25\text{ fF}$, $R_{leak} \approx 10^{12}\,\Omega$ |
| 4 | Approx. decay time of RAM contents | $t_{decay} \approx 5\tau \approx 1\text{–}2 \text{ s}$ | Without refresh |
| 5 | Order of Volatility priority index | $\Pi_{OoV} = \dfrac{1}{\log_{10}(t_{persist})}$ | Higher = capture first |
| 6 | Volatile data loss rate | $\dot{L} = -\dfrac{dN_{data}}{dt} = k \cdot N_{data}$ | First-order decay model |
| 7 | Data remaining after time $t$ | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$ | First-order kinetics analogy |
| 8 | Chain-of-custody hash integrity | $H_{sha256} = \text{SHA-256}(M_{image})$ | Output: 256-bit digest |
| 9 | Memory dump file size bound | $S_{dump} \approx N_{pages} \times 4 \text{ KB}$ | For 32-bit pages |
| 10 | Forensic soundness criterion | $H_{original} = H_{acquired}$ | Bit-for-bit equality |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Order of Volatility is not just academic — it is **operationally critical** in:

- **Incident Response (IR)** — First responders follow OoV to triage live systems under attack.
- **Malware Analysis** — Memory dumps capture fileless malware before it self-deletes.
- **Legal Proceedings** — Demonstrates the investigator followed industry-standard procedure.
- **Cloud & Container Forensics** — Containers (Docker, Kubernetes) have **sub-second** volatility; ephemeral pods must be snapshotted immediately.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)** — SCADA/PLC volatile states (setpoints, alarms) vanish on power-cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capacitor Discharge Model of DRAM Volatility

The decay of data stored in a DRAM cell follows the standard **RC discharge equation**. Let us derive it from first principles.

A DRAM bit cell is essentially a **MOSFET-accessed capacitor**. The stored charge $Q$ on the capacitor represents one bit. The leakage path is modeled as a parallel resistor $R_{leak}$ from the capacitor to ground.

**Step 1 — Define the circuit model.**

The capacitor holds charge $Q(t) = C \cdot V(t)$, where $C$ is the cell capacitance and $V(t)$ is the voltage across the cell.

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL).**

The leakage current through $R_{leak}$ equals the rate of charge leaving the capacitor:

$$I_{leak} = \frac{V(t)}{R_{leak}} = -\frac{dQ}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Q = C \cdot V(t)$.**

$$-\frac{dQ}{dt} = -C \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions.**

$$\frac{V(t)}{R_{leak}} = -C \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate.**

Rearranging gives:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{dt}{R_{leak} \cdot C}$$

Integrating from $V_0$ at $t = 0$ to $V(t)$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{V_0}^{V(t)} \frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{1}{R_{leak} C} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{V(t)}{V_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{R_{leak} C}$$

**Step 7 — Exponentiate to obtain the final discharge law.**

$$\boxed{\,V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t / \tau}\,} \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = R_{leak} \cdot C$$

This is the **fundamental decay equation** of a DRAM cell. Once $V(t)$ drops below the sense amplifier threshold $V_{th} \approx 0.5 V_0$, the bit is read as indeterminate or wrong.

**Step 8 — Calculate the time to data loss.**

Setting $V(t) = V_{th}$:

$$t_{loss} = -\tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{th}}{V_0}\right)$$

Substituting typical values $V_0 = 1.0\text{ V}$, $V_{th} = 0.5\text{ V}$, $C = 25\text{ fF}$, $R_{leak} = 10^{12}\,\Omega$:

$$\tau = (10^{12}) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-15}) = 25 \times 10^{-3} = 25 \text{ ms}$$

$$t_{loss} = -(25\text{ ms}) \cdot \ln(0.5) = (25\text{ ms}) \cdot 0.693 \approx 17.3 \text{ ms}$$

This is the **intrinsic decay time** of a single cell. In practice, JEDEC-mandated refresh every **64 ms** keeps cells well above threshold, but if power is lost, the bulk DRAM contents fade within **~1–2 seconds**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Volatility Priority Index $\Pi_{OoV}$

To rank evidence types, we assign a **volatility priority index** inversely proportional to the logarithm of persistence time (this dampens the dynamic range):

$$\Pi_{OoV} = \frac{1}{\log_{10}(t_{persist})}$$

> **Why logarithmic?** Persistence times span 14 orders of magnitude (nanoseconds to years). A linear scale would be unusable. The log-scale compression produces a tractable ranking.

**Worked Example — Comparing RAM vs. HDD:**

For RAM, $t_{persist}^{RAM} \approx 10^{0} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ s}$ (un-refreshed):

$$\Pi_{RAM} = \frac{1}{\log_{10}(1)} = \frac{1}{0} \rightarrow \text{undefined (treat as } \Pi \to \infty\text{)}$$

For HDD, $t_{persist}^{HDD} \approx 10^{8} \text{ s} \approx 3 \text{ years}$:

$$\Pi_{HDD} = \frac{1}{\log_{10}(10^{8})} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

Conclusion: $\Pi_{RAM} \gg \Pi_{HDD}$, so RAM must be acquired first. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Volatility Tier Comparison

| Tier | Evidence | $t_{persist}$ (s) | $\log_{10}(t_{persist})$ | $\Pi_{OoV}$ (rel.) | Acquisition Tool |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | CPU Register | $10^{-9}$ | $-9$ | $0.111$ | Custom CPU debugger, JTAG |
| 1 | L1/L2 Cache | $10^{-6}$ | $-6$ | $0.167$ | CPU performance counters |
| 2 | RAM (live) | $10^{1}$ | $1$ | $1.000$ | `dd`, `FTK Imager`, `WinPmem`, `LiME` |
| 3 | Swap / Pagefile | $10^{2}$ | $2$ | $0.500$ | Live extraction, Volatility |
| 4 | Temp FS | $10^{3}$ | $3$ | $0.333$ | `tar`, file copy |
| 5 | Disk | $10^{8}$ | $8$ | $0.125$ | `dd`, `EnCase`, `X-Ways` |
| 6 | Backups | $10^{8}$–$10^{9}$ | $8$–$9$ | $0.111$–$0.125$ | Vendor-specific tools |
| 7 | Archival | $10^{9+}$ | $9+$ | $\leq 0.111$ | Tape reader, etc. |

> [!NOTE]
> The relative $\Pi$ values are normalized to Tier 2 (RAM) = 1.0. Higher values denote higher priority for acquisition.

## 3.4 Forensic Soundness — Hash Verification

After acquisition, the integrity of the image $M$ is verified using a cryptographic hash:

$$H = \text{SHA-256}(M) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \text{rotR}_{i \bmod 32}(M_i) \oplus K_i \pmod{2^{32}}$$

Forensic soundness demands:

$$H_{acquired} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{original}$$

Any mismatch invalidates the evidence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Order of Volatility — Pyramid Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **Order of Volatility as an inverted pyramid** — the apex represents the most volatile evidence (capture first), the base the most persistent (capture last).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["Tier 0 - CPU Registers and Cache"] --> A1["Tier 1 - Kernel State and Routing Tables"]
    A1 --> A2["Tier 2 - RAM Main Memory"]
    A2 --> A3["Tier 3 - Temporary File Systems tmpfs and swap"]
    A3 --> A4["Tier 4 - Local Disk Storage HDD SSD NVMe"]
    A4 --> A5["Tier 5 - Remote Logs and SIEM Data"]
    A5 --> A6["Tier 6 - Offsite Archival Backups"]

    style A0 fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff
    style A1 fill:#ff8c66,stroke:#cc4400,color:#000000
    style A2 fill:#ffb84d,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    style A3 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
    style A4 fill:#a8d08d,stroke:#336600,color:#000000
    style A5 fill:#7fb3d5,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style A6 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#002060,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each downward arrow represents a *decrease in volatility* and an *increase in persistence*. Investigators begin at the apex (red) and proceed toward the base (blue).

## 4.2 Live-Acquisition Process Flow

The following diagram captures the **end-to-end workflow** of volatile evidence acquisition, as prescribed by RFC 3227 and modern IR playbooks.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0["Step 0 - Incident Detected and Authorization Verified"] --> S1["Step 1 - Document Host State Time Network User"]
    S1 --> S2["Step 2 - Capture Network State netstat and arp"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 - Snapshot Running Processes ps and tasklist"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 - Acquire RAM Dump LiME or WinPmem or dd"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 - Hash and Store Image SHA-256"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6 - Capture Temporary File Systems"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7 - Power Down System and Image Disk"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8 - Update Chain of Custody Log"]

    style S0 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style S4 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc6600
    style S7 fill:#a8d08d,stroke:#336600
    style S8 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#002060,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Volatility Decision Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q1{"Is power present"} -->|Yes| Q2{"Is data in CPU or cache"}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3["Decay imminent - move to RAM dump immediately"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| A1["Use JTAG or live register dump - Tier 0"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3
    Q3 --> Q4{"Can we run memory acquisition tool"}
    Q4 -->|Yes| A2["Acquire RAM with LiME or FTK - Tier 2"]
    Q4 -->|No| A3["Cold-boot attack if physical access available - Tier 2 fallback"]
    A2 --> Q5{"Need network state"}
    Q5 -->|Yes| A4["Capture netstat and routing - Tier 1"]
    Q5 -->|No| A6["Proceed to disk imaging - Tier 4"]

    style Q1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style A1 fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff
    style A2 fill:#ffb84d,stroke:#cc6600
    style A3 fill:#ff8c66,stroke:#cc4400
    style A4 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9900
    style A6 fill:#a8d08d,stroke:#336600
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define the term "Volatility" as used in Digital Forensics. Why is it important in incident response?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** *Definition:* Volatility refers to the rate at which digital evidence decays or becomes unavailable. Highly volatile data (e.g., RAM contents, CPU registers) is lost within seconds; non-volatile data (e.g., HDD contents) persists for years.
- **(1 mark)** *Order of Volatility:* The IETF RFC 3227 prescribes a priority sequence in which evidence must be collected — from most volatile to least volatile.
- **(1 mark)** *Significance:* During incident response, failing to capture volatile evidence first results in irretrievable loss of process memory, network connections, cryptographic keys, and login sessions, severely weakening any subsequent investigation or prosecution.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

**Q2. List the Order of Volatility as specified in RFC 3227. Which tier decays first and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** *List (most → least volatile):*
  1. CPU registers, cache
  2. Routing table, ARP cache, process table, kernel statistics, memory
  3. Temporary file systems
  4. Disk
  5. Remote logging and monitoring data
  6. Physical configuration, network topology
  7. Archival media
- **(1 mark)** *Decay rationale:* CPU registers decay first because their state is held by transient electrical charges on MOSFET gates that are overwritten within nanoseconds by the next instruction fetch (or by a context switch). No refresh mechanism sustains them once the clock stops.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper, Module 2]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Order of Volatility** in detail. Discuss each tier with examples and approximate persistence time. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for acquiring volatile evidence** from a live Linux system under investigation. Mention at least four specific commands and the rationale for each. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **(1 mark)** Definition of Volatility and citation of RFC 3227.
2. **(1 mark)** Introduction of the 7-tier Order of Volatility.
3. **(4 marks)** Tier-by-tier breakdown with examples:
   - **Tier 0 — CPU Registers/Cache:** Nanosecond persistence. Examples: Program counter, instruction register, accumulator.
   - **Tier 1 — Kernel/Routing State:** Seconds-to-minutes. Examples: ARP cache, routing table, listening sockets.
   - **Tier 2 — RAM:** Seconds-to-minutes (un-refreshed). Holds process memory, cryptographic keys, clipboard.
   - **Tier 3 — Temporary File Systems:** Minutes-to-hours. Examples: `/tmp`, `/var/tmp`, `tmpfs`, swap partition.
   - **Tier 4 — Disk:** Years. File system, deleted files in unallocated clusters.
   - **Tier 5 — Remote Logs:** Days-to-years (policy-dependent). SIEM, cloud audit logs.
   - **Tier 6 — Archival:** Indefinite. Tape backups, optical media.
4. **(1 mark)** Justification for the order: each tier's persistence time and decay mechanism.

#### Model Solution for (b):

1. **(1 mark)** Pre-acquisition: legal authorization, document the scene (hostname, IP, MAC, user, time).
2. **(1.5 marks)** **Network state** — `netstat -an` and `arp -a` to capture live connections and ARP entries (Tier 1).
3. **(1.5 marks)** **Process list** — `ps auxf` to capture the running process tree and their PIDs (Tier 1–2).
4. **(2 marks)** **RAM acquisition** — Use `sudo lime /path/to/ram.lime` (Linux Memory Extractor) or `avml` to dump physical memory; output file is hashed with `sha256sum` (Tier 2). Provide rationale: captures encryption keys, malware, and session tokens.
5. **(1 mark)** **Temporary FS** — `tar -cvf /evidence/tmp.tar /tmp /var/tmp` (Tier 3).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose marks by (i) *forgetting to hash the RAM image* immediately after acquisition, breaking the chain of custody; (ii) *omitting the volatile state before disconnecting the network* — once unplugged, ephemeral network connections are lost; and (iii) *writing the memory dump to the same disk under investigation*, thereby overwriting potential evidence. Always write to an **external, write-blocked** medium. Deduct up to **2 marks** for each omission.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a **neat diagram**, explain the Order of Volatility pyramid. Discuss why RAM is considered more volatile than disk, including a mathematical treatment. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Volatility 2** and **Volatility 3** frameworks. What are the key plugins in each version for analyzing memory images? **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **(2 marks)** Neat diagram of the Order of Volatility pyramid (apex = CPU, base = archival).
2. **(2 marks)** Explanation: RAM uses capacitive storage with refresh; disk uses magnetic domains (HDD) or floating-gate transistors (SSD) with multi-year retention.
3. **(3 marks)** Mathematical treatment:
   - DRAM decay: $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ with $\tau = R_{leak} C \approx 25 \text{ ms}$, giving $t_{loss} \approx 1\text{–}2 \text{ s}$ without refresh.
   - HDD retention: governed by superparamagnetic limit; $t_{persist} \approx 10$ years.
   - Comparison: $t_{persist}^{RAM}/t_{persist}^{HDD} \approx 10^{-8}$, so RAM is ~$10^{8}$ times more volatile.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Feature | Volatility 2 | Volatility 3 |
|---|---|---|
| Python | Python 2 (legacy) | Python 3 |
| Architecture | Profile-based | Plugin-based, OS-agnostic |
| OS Support | Windows XP → 10 | Win / Linux / Mac |
| Key Plugins | `pslist`, `cmdscan`, `filescan`, `hashdump`, `malfind` | `windows.pslist`, `windows.cmdline`, `windows.filescan`, `windows.hashdump`, `windows.malfind` |
| Symbol Tables | External profiles | Auto-resolved via ISF symbol servers |

(7 marks allocated: 2 marks for comparison, 3 marks for plugin listing, 2 marks for architecture/automation differences.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list Volatility 2 commands verbatim while answering a Volatility 3 question (or vice-versa). The 2024 Scheme syllabus expects awareness of **both** versions; mix-and-match leads to -1.5 mark penalty. Also, do not confuse the *concept of volatility* with the *Volatility tool* — they are related but not identical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 (Volatility)**

- **Definition:** Volatility = *rate at which digital evidence is lost*; governs the **Order of Volatility (OoV)**.
- **Standard:** **IETF RFC 3227 (Feb 2002)** is the authoritative reference.
- **7-Tier Order (most → least volatile):** CPU registers → Kernel state → RAM → Temp FS → Disk → Remote logs → Archival.
- **DRAM physics:** Capacitor decay $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$, with $\tau \approx 25 \text{ ms}$ and $t_{loss} \approx 1\text{–}2 \text{ s}$ un-refreshed.
- **JEDEC refresh interval:** $t_{refresh} = 64 \text{ ms}$.
- **Acquisition order is inverse of OoV** — capture the most volatile first.
- **RAM dump tools:** `dd`, `LiME` (Linux), `WinPmem` / `FTK Imager` (Windows), `avml` (cross-platform).
- **Never write the memory dump to the suspect disk** — use external, write-blocked media.
- **Always hash immediately:** SHA-256 of the image establishes chain-of-custody.
- **Volatility Framework (the tool):** Two versions — **Volatility 2** (Python 2, profiles) and **Volatility 3** (Python 3, plugins, OS-agnostic).
- **Key Volatility 3 plugins:** `pslist`, `psscan`, `cmdscan`, `filescan`, `hashdump`, `malfind`, `netscan`, `dlllist`.
- **Common examiner traps:** (i) powering down too soon, (ii) not hashing, (iii) writing to the suspect disk, (iv) confusing "volatility" (concept) with "Volatility" (tool).
- **OOO of priority:** RFC 3227 § 2.1 — *order of volatility is roughly the inverse of the order in which evidence should be collected*.
- **Live triage formula:** Document → Network → Processes → RAM → Temp FS → Disk → Chain of Custody.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dumpit
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dumpit

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Dumpit** is a lightweight, portable, command-line based **memory acquisition utility** developed originally by *MoonSols* and currently maintained by *Comae Starter Kit (now part of Comae Technologies)*. It is engineered to perform a **full physical memory (RAM) dump** of a live Windows operating system (both 32-bit and 64-bit architectures) and write it into a single raw `.raw` or `.dmp` binary file on a designated storage volume without requiring administrative installation on the target machine.

In KTU 2024 Scheme digital forensics terminology, Dumpit belongs to the category of **Live System Forensic Acquisition Tools** and addresses the capture of **volatile evidence** before system shutdown or power loss renders it unrecoverable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — Volatile Evidence Acquisition):**
> Dumpit is prescribed as a standard tool for demonstrating **physical memory imaging** under the *Order of Volatility* acquisition methodology, specifically for Windows workstations/servers.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crime scene where a thief is *currently inside a building*. The building's doors will lock permanently in 60 seconds. You have one chance to photograph the thief's face, fingerprints, footprints, and whatever is on the dining table. If you wait even one minute, **all volatile clues vanish**.

A live computer's RAM is exactly that "dining table":
- Running processes (the thief)
- Decrypted passwords (the footprints)
- Network connections (the getaway car)
- Encryption keys (the master key)
- Registry hives in memory (the fingerprints)

**Dumpit is the high-speed, no-flash camera** that snaps a complete picture of everything on that table — instantly, silently, and without disturbing the thief (i.e., without crashing the system or writing forensic junk to the disk).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not just pull the plug and image the disk later?**
> Powering off destroys: running process state, decrypted data, network sockets, kernel structures, clipboard contents, encryption keys in memory, malware artifacts that exist *only* in RAM (e.g., fileless malware). Dumpit preserves all of this in a single forensically sound file.

### Key Standard Metrics

- **Typical Acquisition Speed:** ~**2 GB to 8 GB per minute** (depends on RAM speed, USB bus, storage write speed).
- **File Format:** Raw binary (`.raw`) — directly compatible with **Volatility**, **Rekall**, **Magnet AXIOM**, **Autopsy**.
- **Architecture Support:** **32-bit (x86)** and **64-bit (x64)** Windows.
- **Recommended OS:** Windows XP through Windows 11 / Windows Server 2019+.
- **Footprint:** **Single `.exe` file**, ≈ **few hundred KB** — runs entirely from a USB stick.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### Operational Concept Breakdown

Dumpit's design is governed by four forensic engineering principles. Below is the structured logic chain that explains **why it works the way it does**:

- **Step 1 — Privilege Escalation via Raw Disk Access**
  Dumpit leverages the Windows kernel's `\Device\PhysicalMemory` object. To open this object, the executing user/process must hold the **SeLockMemoryPrivilege** (also called the *Lock Pages in Memory* privilege). On a live system, only members of the local **Administrators** group are granted this right by default. Dumpit requests elevation automatically through UAC (User Account Control) prompting.

- **Step 2 — Memory Mapping via MmMapIoSpace**
  Once `\Device\PhysicalMemory` is opened, Dumpit calls the kernel-mode function `MmMapIoSpace` to map the entire physical address space of RAM into a virtual address region accessible to user-mode code. This step is what makes Dumpit work at the **physical (not virtual) layer** — capturing *all* RAM, including memory used by the OS kernel itself.

- **Step 3 — Linear Block-Based Dump**
  Dumpit walks the physical address space in sequential chunks (commonly **4096-byte pages**, aligned to page boundaries). For each chunk:
  1. It reads `PAGE_SIZE` bytes from the mapped physical memory.
  2. It writes the bytes to the output file sequentially using buffered I/O.
  3. It updates a progress indicator (visible in the console).

- **Step 4 — Integrity Hash Generation**
  Upon completion, Dumpit computes a **cryptographic hash** of the generated `.raw` file using the **MD5** algorithm (and in some versions, **SHA1**). This hash becomes the **digital fingerprint** used later in the *chain of custody* to prove the memory image was not altered.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The MD5 hash generated by Dumpit is identical in purpose to the hash generated for disk images in FTK Imager. It satisfies the forensic principle of **Integrity Preservation** under the **ACPO (Association of Chief Police Officers) Guidelines**.

### Real-World Forensic Utility

| Domain | Engineering Utility of Dumpit |
|---|---|
| Incident Response | Captures ransomware encryption keys held only in RAM, enabling decryption of locked files. |
| Malware Analysis | Recovers fileless/PowerShell-based malware artefacts that never touch the disk. |
| Insider Threat Investigation | Extracts active network connections, logged-in user credentials, and chat session contents. |
| Legal/Court Admissible Evidence | Provides a tamper-evident copy of volatile system state, hash-verifiable. |
| Penetration Testing Post-Exploitation | Useful for credential harvesting (e.g., Mimikatz on memory dumps). |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dump File Size | $D = R_{total} - R_{reserved}$ | Bytes (B) | Typically equals installed physical RAM size |
| Acquisition Time | $T = \dfrac{D}{S_{w}}$ | seconds | $S_w$ = sustained write speed of target storage |
| Acquisition Throughput | $\theta = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | MB/s | Depends on USB bus (USB 2.0 ≈ 30 MB/s; USB 3.0 ≈ 100+ MB/s) |
| Hash Verification | $H(f) = \text{MD5}(f) \stackrel{?}{=} H'(f)$ | Hex string | Acquired-time hash vs. analysis-time hash |
| Page Size | $P = 2^{12}$ | Bytes | Standard Windows page = **4096 B** |
| Memory Coverage | $C = \dfrac{D}{R_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Should be **≥ 99%** for forensic acceptability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** If $C < 100\%$, the missing bytes represent the *memory-mapped I/O region* (e.g., video RAM, device buffers). This is expected and does **not** invalidate the dump, provided the missing region is documented in the case notes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### Exhaustive Content Mandate — Acquisition Workflow

Since Dumpit is a procedural tool rather than an analytical equation, the "derivation" here is the **end-to-end forensic acquisition protocol** that examiners must follow. Every step is enumerated explicitly so KTU students can reproduce the procedure in their lab practicals and answer procedural exam questions fully.

#### Step 3.1 — Pre-Acquisition Preparation

1. **Identify the Target Machine**
   Record: hostname, OS version + build, installed RAM (GB), local time, timezone, investigator name.

2. **Select Target Storage Volume**
   Plug in a USB 3.0+ external drive with sufficient free space $S_{free} \geq R_{total}$. Format as **NTFS** (avoid FAT32 — single file limit is 4 GB, which will fail for memory dumps > 4 GB).

3. **Verify Tool Integrity**
   Compute the MD5 hash of the `Dumpit.exe` file *before* copying it to the target machine. Compare against the official hash published on the Comae/Starter Kit distribution page.

4. **Document Chain of Custody Form**
   Open a chain-of-custody entry: *Date, Time, Tool, Tool Hash, Investigator, Witness.*

#### Step 3.2 — Executing Dumpit

```
[Command-Line Invocation — KTU Lab Standard]
```

$$
\text{DumpIt.exe} \;\; /OUTPUT \; \text{E:}\textbackslash\text{memdump.raw} \;\; /QUIET
$$

Where:
- `/OUTPUT <path>` → destination file path.
- `/QUIET` → suppresses interactive prompts (used in scripted batch acquisition).

On a fresh execution without flags:

$$
\text{DumpIt.exe} \rightarrow \text{UAC Prompt} \rightarrow \text{Administrator Token} \rightarrow \text{Memory Read} \rightarrow \text{Write .raw}
$$

#### Step 3.3 — Progress Monitoring

Dumpit prints the running progress:

```
       Output Path: E:\memdump.raw
          Dump Type: Raw (Physical Address Space)
       Dump Size:    8589934592 bytes (8192 MB)
       Status:       Reading... 47%
```

The examiner must photograph or screenshot this terminal output as part of the evidence record.

#### Step 3.4 — Post-Acquisition Hash Verification

When Dumpit finishes, the console prints:

$$
H_{acquired} = \text{MD5}(\text{memdump.raw}) = \texttt{a3f9...c71b}
$$

The examiner records this hash on the chain-of-custody form and verifies it again on the **forensic analysis workstation** before opening the dump:

$$
H_{analysis} \stackrel{!}{=} H_{acquired}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Mandated Rule:** If the hashes differ even by one bit, the dump is **forensically compromised** and must NOT be used as evidence. A new acquisition must be performed on the original (if still available) or the integrity issue must be disclosed in court.

#### Step 3.5 — Volatility Analysis Initialization

The verified `.raw` file is loaded into **Volatility 2.x or 3.x** using:

$$
\text{volatility} \; -f \; \text{memdump.raw} \; \text{imageinfo}
$$

or, for Volatility 3:

$$
\text{vol} \; -f \; \text{memdump.raw} \; \text{windows.info}
$$

The framework auto-detects the OS profile (e.g., `Win7SP1x64`, `Win10x64_19041`) and unlocks dozens of plugins (`pslist`, `netscan`, `hashdump`, `malfind`, etc.).

### Analytical Matrix — Dumpit vs. Competing Tools

| Forensic Criterion | Dumpit | FTK Imager (Lite) | WinPmem | Magnet RAM Capture |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Portability (single EXE) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Installer required | ✅ Yes | ❌ Installer required |
| Supports 64-bit Windows | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Hash on completion | ✅ MD5 | ✅ MD5/SHA1 | ✅ MD5 | ✅ MD5 |
| Page-file capture | ❌ | ✅ Optional | ❌ | ❌ |
| Memory-mapped I/O handling | ✅ Documented gaps | ✅ Acquired | ✅ Acquired | ✅ Acquired |
| Open-source / Auditable | ❌ Closed (freeware) | ❌ Closed (commercial) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Closed |
| Forensically Accepted in Court | ✅ (with documented chain of custody) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Dumpit is preferred for **field deployment** due to its zero-install nature. FTK Imager is preferred for **lab environments** where page-file acquisition and formal evidence-logging are required.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied
All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (prefixed with letters). Node labels contain raw uppercase text only — no bold/italic/HTML tags. Mermaid block fences are isolated with blank lines before and after.

### Diagram 4.1 — End-to-End Dumpit Acquisition & Verification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Incident Response] --> B[Identify Target Windows Host]
    B --> C[Document Host Specs RAM Size OS Version]
    C --> D[Prepare USB Storage NTFS Formatted]
    D --> E[Verify Dumpit EXE Hash MD5]
    E --> F[Execute Dumpit EXE as Administrator]
    F --> G[UAC Elevation Granted]
    G --> H[Open Physical Memory Device Object]
    H --> I[Map Physical Address Space via MmMapIoSpace]
    I --> J[Walk Physical Memory Page by Page 4096 Bytes]
    J --> K[Write Sequential Raw Bytes to Output RAW File]
    K --> L{Dump Complete?}
    L -->|NO| J
    L -->|YES| M[Compute MD5 Hash of RAW File]
    M --> N[Record Hash in Chain of Custody Log]
    N --> O[Transfer RAW to Forensic Workstation]
    O --> P[Recompute MD5 on Workstation]
    P --> Q{Hashes Match?}
    Q -->|NO| R[Quarantine Dump Restart Procedure]
    Q -->|YES| S[Load RAW into Volatility Framework]
    S --> T[Run Plugins pslist netscan hashdump malfind]
    T --> U[Generate Forensic Report]
    U --> V[END Evidence Archived]
```

### Diagram 4.2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dumpit Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER LAYER Dumpit EXE Console] --> B[PRIVILEGE LAYER UAC Token SeLockMemoryPrivilege]
    B --> C[KERNEL LAYER Device PhysicalMemory Handle]
    C --> D[MAPPING LAYER MmMapIoSpace Virtual Base Address]
    D --> E[ITERATION LAYER Page Walker 4096 Byte Chunks]
    E --> F[OUTPUT LAYER Buffered File Writer RAW Format]
    F --> G[INTEGRITY LAYER MD5 Hash Engine]
    G --> H[CHAIN OF CUSTODY LAYER Examiner Form Digital Signature]
```

### Diagram 4.3 — Order of Volatility Showing Dumpit's Target Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[LEVEL 1 CPU Registers Cache] --> L2[LEVEL 2 Routing Table ARP Cache Process Table Kernel Statistics Memory]
    L2 --> L3[LEVEL 3 Temporary File Systems]
    L3 --> L4[LEVEL 4 Disk Files]
    L4 --> L5[LEVEL 5 Remote Logging Monitoring Data]
    L5 --> L6[LEVEL 6 Physical Configuration Network Topology Archived Media]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Dumpit targets **Level 2 — Physical Memory (RAM)**. Per the **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)**, RAM must be acquired **before** any Level 3+ evidence, because it is the most volatile data the examiner can still capture.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> *Define Dumpit. State any two of its salient features relevant to forensic acquisition.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Dumpit is a lightweight, portable, command-line utility developed by Comae (formerly MoonSols) used to capture the entire physical memory (RAM) of a live Windows operating system into a single raw `.raw` file.

*Salient Features (any two — 1.5 marks each):*
1. **Zero-installation portability** — runs directly from a USB drive without setup, minimizing footprint on the suspect machine.
2. **Administrator-privilege escalation** — automatically prompts for UAC and uses the `SeLockMemoryPrivilege` to access `\Device\PhysicalMemory` securely.
3. **Built-in MD5 hash generation** — provides integrity verification of the dump on completion.
4. **Page-aligned raw output** — directly compatible with Volatility, Rekall, and Magnet AXIOM frameworks.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Why is memory acquisition using Dumpit preferred over traditional disk imaging in certain incident-response scenarios?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Memory acquisition captures **volatile evidence** that disk imaging cannot recover, including: *(1 mark)* running process memory, decrypted data, encryption keys (e.g., BitLocker, ransomware keys), active network sockets, registry hives in RAM, and fileless-malware artefacts. *(1 mark)* Disk imaging only captures *persisted* data, missing anything that exists solely in volatile storage. *(1 mark)* Dumpit also preserves evidence without requiring system shutdown, enabling live-system triage during active incidents — a critical requirement when time-sensitive data such as encryption keys must be preserved before they are lost on reboot.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and internal working of the Dumpit utility. Discuss how it interacts with the Windows kernel to acquire physical memory. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe, in detail, the end-to-end forensic procedure to be followed when using Dumpit for memory acquisition. Include pre-acquisition, execution, and post-acquisition steps along with chain-of-custody requirements. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Architecture & Internal Working — 7 Marks**

The Dumpit utility follows a **four-layer internal architecture**:

**1. User-Mode Console Layer (1 Mark):**
Dumpit is a single portable `.exe` binary that runs in user mode. It accepts command-line flags (`/OUTPUT`, `/QUIET`) or interactive key-press to trigger acquisition. The console prints live progress (percentage completed, file path).

**2. Privilege Escalation Layer (1.5 Marks):**
On launch, Dumpit requests UAC elevation. The Windows Local Security Authority (LSASS) issues an *Administrator* token containing **SeLockMemoryPrivilege** and **SeDebugPrivilege**, which are mandatory for opening the kernel-level physical memory object.

**3. Kernel Interaction Layer — `\Device\PhysicalMemory` (2.5 Marks):**
Using the elevated token, Dumpit calls `CreateFile()` with the path `\\.\PhysicalMemory` (or `\\.\Device\PhysicalMemory`). The Windows Object Manager grants a handle to the kernel-only memory device. Dumpit then invokes `NtMapViewOfSection` and the kernel function **`MmMapIoSpace`** to remap the entire physical address range (from physical address `0x00000000` up to `0xFFFFFFFF` on 32-bit, or up to the RAM ceiling on 64-bit) into the user-mode virtual address space. This is the most critical step — without this mapping, the user-mode process cannot read kernel memory regions.

**4. Sequential Read & Write Layer (2 Marks):**
Dumpit walks the mapped region in **4096-byte page-aligned chunks**. Each chunk is read with `memcpy` from the mapped virtual base and written to the destination `.raw` file via buffered file I/O (`WriteFile`). On completion, the MD5 hashing engine (using Windows CryptoAPI) computes the hash of the entire output file in a single pass and prints it to the console.

The architecture guarantees: (a) full physical RAM capture, (b) minimal CPU disturbance, and (c) tamper-evident output through built-in hashing.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**End-to-End Forensic Procedure — 7 Marks**

**Pre-Acquisition Phase (2 Marks):**
1. Document the scene: hostname, OS version + service pack, architecture (32/64-bit), total installed RAM, date/time/timezone.
2. Verify Dumpit integrity by computing its MD5 hash on the examiner's workstation before copying to the suspect USB.
3. Prepare a USB 3.0+ drive formatted as **NTFS** with free space ≥ installed RAM.
4. Photograph the target machine's desktop state (running applications visible on screen).
5. Fill in the **Chain of Custody Form** — investigator name, witness signature, tool used, tool hash.

**Execution Phase (2 Marks):**
6. Insert the USB into the target.
7. Right-click `DumpIt.exe` → *Run as Administrator*. Confirm UAC prompt.
8. The tool displays the destination file path. Verify it points to the USB. Press any key to start.
9. Monitor the percentage indicator. Photograph/screenshot the console.
10. Wait until completion message shows the MD5 hash. Record the hash on the form.

**Post-Acquisition Phase (3 Marks):**
11. Eject the USB safely. Do **not** shut down or reboot the target machine until other volatile data (e.g., network state) is captured separately, if required by SOP.
12. On the forensic workstation, recompute the MD5 hash of the `.raw` file using FTK Imager or `certutil -hashfile`.
13. **Verify hash equality:** $H_{analysis} = H_{acquisition}$. If mismatch, quarantine the dump and document the discrepancy.
14. Open the dump in Volatility: `vol -f memdump.raw windows.info` and proceed with plugin-based analysis (`pslist`, `netscan`, `hashdump`, `malfind`, `filescan`).
15. Generate a forensic report including: tool used, hash values, OS profile detected, processes/connections extracted, findings, examiner signature.
16. Archive the `.raw` file on write-protected storage as per the **ACPO Principle 2** (original evidence preserved unchanged).

---

> **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Alternative Set — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Discuss the *Order of Volatility* (RFC 3227). Where does Dumpit fit in this order, and why must memory be acquired before disk? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare Dumpit with two other memory-acquisition tools. Justify scenarios where Dumpit is the optimal choice. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Order of Volatility & Memory Acquisition Priority — 7 Marks**

The **Order of Volatility** (formalized in **IETF RFC 3227 — "Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving"**) prescribes a strict priority sequence for digital evidence collection, ordered from most volatile (must be captured first) to least volatile (most stable): *(2 Marks)*

$$
\text{CPU/Registers} \rightarrow \text{Routing Table, ARP Cache} \rightarrow \text{Process Table, Kernel Memory, RAM} \rightarrow \text{Temp File Systems} \rightarrow \text{Disk} \rightarrow \text{Remote Logs} \rightarrow \text{Physical Config / Archives}
$$

**Dumpit's Position in the Order (2 Marks):**
Dumpit operates at **Level 2** — capturing **physical RAM**. It cannot capture Level 1 (CPU registers/cache) because that requires hardware-level debugging interfaces (e.g., JTAG, Intel ITP). However, Dumpit captures everything *below* Level 1 — including kernel memory, process tables, network connections, decrypted buffers — in one operation.

**Why Memory Must Be Acquired Before Disk (3 Marks):**

1. **Volatility Window:** RAM contents are lost *permanently* on power-off or reboot. Disk contents persist. The investigator therefore has only one chance to capture volatile data — must be done first.

2. **Decryption Enablement:** Modern full-disk encryption (BitLocker, FileVault, VeraCrypt) holds the decryption keys in RAM while the system runs. If memory is not captured before shutdown, the disk image becomes undecryptable and useless.

3. **Live Process State:** Fileless malware, rootkits, and credential-stealers (e.g., Mimikatz) live exclusively in RAM. Acquiring disk first would miss them entirely — yet they vanish the instant the machine powers off.

4. **Forensic Chain Integrity:** Capturing memory first also lets the investigator run live-system commands (e.g., `netstat`, `tasklist`) for cross-correlation with the static dump, strengthening evidentiary reliability.

Hence, the principle is: *capture from most volatile → least volatile*, with Dumpit handling the critical Level-2 layer.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Comparative Study of Memory Acquisition Tools — 7 Marks**

*(Comparison structure: 2 marks per tool-pair comparison + 1 mark for justification table — see below.)*

| Comparison Axis | **Dumpit** | **FTK Imager (Lite Mode)** | **Magnet RAM Capture** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Portability** | ✅ Single EXE, runs from USB | ❌ Requires installation | ❌ Requires installation |
| **Page-File Capture** | ❌ RAM only | ✅ Optional (combined dump + pagefile) | ❌ RAM only |
| **Hash Generation** | ✅ MD5 (auto) | ✅ MD5 / SHA1 (auto) | ✅ MD5 (auto) |
| **Output Format** | `.raw` | `.raw` / `.e01` (AD1) | `.raw` |
| **Auditable Source Code** | ❌ Closed-source freeware | ❌ Closed-source commercial | ❌ Closed-source commercial |
| **Lab Use** | ⚠ Limited (no pagefile, no E01) | ✅ Preferred (court-grade E01 output) | ✅ Good |
| **Field Use** | ✅ Optimal | ❌ Bulky | ❌ Bulky |
| **Cost** | Free | Commercial (paid licence) | Commercial (paid licence) |

**Optimal Scenarios for Dumpit (2 Marks):**

1. **Field Deployment / On-Site Incident Response:** When the investigator arrives at an active scene with only a USB stick, Dumpit is the only tool that can be deployed without touching the suspect machine's hard drive (preserving forensic integrity of disk artefacts).

2. **Rapid Triage under Time Pressure:** Dumpit launches in seconds, requires no configuration, and completes acquisition without user interaction beyond a single keypress — ideal when ransomware encryption keys must be captured within minutes before they rotate.

3. **Resource-Constrained Environments:** In academic labs, government training setups, and KTU practical examinations, Dumpit's zero-cost, zero-install nature makes it the **pedagogically and economically optimal choice**.

4. **Cross-Verification:** Even when FTK Imager is the primary tool, Dumpit can be used as a **secondary verification tool** to cross-validate memory dumps — if both tools produce identical MD5 hashes, the integrity of the acquisition is doubly confirmed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Dumpit with disk-imaging tools** (FTK Imager, EnCase, dd). Dumpit is **RAM-only**. Students lose 2–3 marks for misclassification.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the hash verification step** in answers. The MD5 hash is **not optional** — it is the foundation of forensic admissibility. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Do NOT claim Dumpit captures page-file.sys.** It does not. FTK Imager does. This is a frequently tested distinction.
> 4. **Do NOT forget the Order of Volatility.** Always frame memory acquisition as *pre-disk* evidence collection.
> 5. **Do NOT write vague answers like "Dumpit captures RAM."** Examiners expect: *physical memory*, *raw format*, *MD5 hash*, *portable EXE*, *UAC privilege escalation*.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Dumpit = **portable, single-EXE, Windows RAM acquisition utility** by Comae (formerly MoonSols).
- ✅ Captures **physical memory** (not virtual, not pagefile) into a `.raw` file.
- ✅ Operates at **Level 2 of the Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)**.
- ✅ Requires **Administrator privileges** and **SeLockMemoryPrivilege** to open `\Device\PhysicalMemory`.
- ✅ Uses **kernel-mode `MmMapIoSpace`** to map physical RAM into user-mode address space.
- ✅ Reads memory in **4096-byte page-aligned chunks**.
- ✅ Generates **MD5 hash** of the output on completion — used for chain-of-custody integrity verification.
- ✅ Output is **directly compatible** with Volatility, Rekall, Magnet AXIOM, Autopsy.
- ✅ **Zero installation** — runs entirely from a USB drive (field-deployment advantage).
- ✅ **Does NOT capture** pagefile, hiberfil, or memory-mapped I/O regions (documented gaps).
- ✅ **Memory must be acquired BEFORE disk imaging** — preserves encryption keys, fileless malware, live process state.
- ✅ Forensic admissibility depends on: **documented chain of custody + hash verification (acquired-time vs. analysis-time)**.
- ✅ KTU Module 2 expects students to know: **tool purpose, internal architecture, forensic procedure, comparative analysis, and volatility ordering**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MobileCheck
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MobileCheck

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: MobileCheck — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MobileCheck** in the context of KTU Digital Forensics refers to the **systematic, forensically-sound procedure of examining, validating, and extracting evidentiary data from mobile devices** (smartphones, tablets, feature phones, IoT wearables) using standardized hardware/software tools while strictly preserving the **integrity, authenticity, and chain-of-custody** of the digital evidence.

It is a **structured forensic workflow** that combines:
- **Identification** of the device (make, model, OS, IMEI, IMSI)
- **Isolation** (Faraday bag, network isolation)
- **Acquisition** (logical, file-system, physical)
- **Examination & Analysis** (decoding, carving, correlation)
- **Documentation & Reporting**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** MobileCheck is the methodological framework (also embodied in tools like **Cellebrite UFED**, **MSAB XRY**, **MOBILedit Forensic**, and the open-source **Autopsy + Android Forensics** plugins) used to perform mobile forensics in a legally admissible manner.

> [!NOTE]
> The term *"MobileCheck"* originates from forensic tool suites that perform a **health check, integrity check, and evidence check** on mobile devices before and after acquisition — analogous to a pre-flight checklist in aviation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Model

Imagine a forensic investigator as an **airport security officer** handling a suspicious suitcase (the mobile device):

| Airport Analogy | MobileCheck Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Luggage tag scan (Barcode) | IMEI/IMSI/MSISDN identification |
| X-Ray scanner | Logical acquisition (readable files) |
| Full body search (TSA pat-down) | File-system acquisition (hidden files) |
| Explosion containment chamber | Physical acquisition (bit-by-bit image) |
| Evidence locker with CCTV | Chain-of-custody documentation |
| Customs declaration form | Forensic report |

Just as airport security **never opens the original suitcase in public** (preservation), MobileCheck **never writes to the original device** (write-blocking principle).

## 1.3 Standard MobileCheck Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Memory Unit:** **1 GB = 1024 MB = 2³⁰ bytes**
- **Hash Standard:** **MD5 (128-bit)**, **SHA-1 (160-bit)**, **SHA-256 (256-bit)**
- **Forensic Image Format:** **E01 (EnCase), DD (raw), AFF (Advanced Forensic Format)**
- **Isolation Voltage Threshold:** **~ 100 dB RF attenuation** (Faraday bag standard)
- **Write-Block Verification Latency:** **< 500 ms**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Mobile Check Cycle Time (industry benchmark):** **45–90 minutes** per device for logical acquisition, **2–6 hours** for physical acquisition on locked modern smartphones.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The MobileCheck Pipeline

## 2.1 The Five-Phase MobileCheck Architecture

The MobileCheck methodology is decomposed into the following sequential phases, each governed by forensically-sound principles:

### **Phase 1 — Pre-Examination (Identification & Isolation)**

- Verify the **legal authority** (warrant, consent, exigent circumstances).
- Record **device state** (powered on/off, screen lock status, network connectivity).
- Photograph the device from **6 angles** (front, back, all 4 sides).
- Document: **Make, Model, OS version, IMEI (15 digits), ICCID, MSISDN**.
- Isolate the device in a **Faraday bag** (RF attenuation ≥ 60 dB preferred).
- Disable **auto-lock, biometric, and remote wipe** mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Isolation?** Modern phones can be remotely wiped via *Find My iPhone*, *Find My Device*, or MDM commands. Isolation **prevents remote adversarial interference**.

### **Phase 2 — Acquisition (The Three Tiers)**

MobileCheck supports **three hierarchical acquisition levels**, each with increasing depth and decreasing compatibility:

| Tier | Method | Data Accessible | Tools Used | Legal Strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1** | **Manual Extraction** | Screen-shots, call logs, user-visible data | Camera, screen recorder | Low |
| **Tier 2** | **Logical Extraction** | Files, SMS, contacts, app data via APIs | Cellebrite UFED, MSAB XRY, MOBILedit | Moderate |
| **Tier 3** | **File-System Extraction** | App sandbox, system files, deleted metadata | ADB pull, iTunes backup (encrypted) | High |
| **Tier 4** | **Physical Extraction** | Bit-by-bit flash memory image (incl. deleted) | Cellebrite Premium, UFED 4PC, JTAG, chip-off | Very High |

### **Phase 3 — Examination (Parsing & Decoding)**

- Extract artifacts: **SMS, MMS, Call Logs, Contacts, Calendar, GPS, Browser History, Photos, Videos, App Data (WhatsApp, Telegram, Signal, etc.)**.
- Decode proprietary databases: **SQLite (.db), Plist (Apple), LevelDB (Android)**.
- Recover deleted data via **carving** (e.g., Scalpel, PhotoRec).

### **Phase 4 — Analysis (Correlation & Timeline)**

- Build **super-timelines** using tools like **Autopsy, Plaso/log2timeline, Magnet AXIOM**.
- Correlate **call detail records (CDR)** with device logs.
- Apply **hash filtering** against NSRL (National Software Reference Library) to exclude known-good files.

### **Phase 5 — Reporting (Documentation)**

- Generate **declaration of authenticity** with hash values.
- Include **chain-of-custody form** (every transfer timestamped and signed).
- Produce **executive summary + technical appendix**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Constant | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Device Storage Capacity** | $C_{device} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ | Total storage = sum of all partitions | Bytes (B) |
| **Hash Match Confidence** | $P_{match} = 1 - \frac{1}{2^{n}}$ | Where *n* = hash bit-length | Probability (0–1) |
| **SHA-256 Collision Probability** | $P_{collision} \approx \frac{1}{2^{128}}$ | Birthday paradox bound | Probability |
| **Acquisition Throughput** | $T_{acq} = \frac{D_{size}}{t_{elapsed}}$ | Data extracted per unit time | MB/s |
| **Image Compression Ratio** | $R_{comp} = \frac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | E01 vs DD ratio (typically 2:1) | Dimensionless |
| **Faraday Attenuation** | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{E_{incident}}{E_{transmitted}}\right)$ | RF shielding effectiveness | Decibels (dB) |
| **Storage Conversion** | $1 \text{ GiB} = 2^{30} \text{ bytes} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ B}$ | Gibibyte to bytes | Bytes |
| **Logical Address (IMEI)** | $\text{IMEI} = \text{TAC} + \text{SNR} + \text{CD}$ (8+6+1 = 15 digits) | Type Allocation + Serial + Check Digit | Digits |
| **Forensic Image Hash** | $H_{final} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Image} \oplus \text{Evidence})$ | Tamper-evident hash | 256 bits |
| **Recovery Yield** | $Y_{recovery} = \frac{N_{recovered}}{N_{deleted}} \times 100\%$ | Deleted file recovery rate | Percentage |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

MobileCheck is indispensable in:
- **Criminal Investigations** (murder, fraud, drug trafficking — CDR evidence)
- **Civil Litigation** (intellectual property theft, harassment — chat evidence)
- **Corporate E-Discovery** (employee misconduct — email/WhatsApp forensics)
- **Counter-Terrorism** (encrypted communication analysis)
- **Incident Response** (malware-infected BYOD devices in enterprises)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure — MobileCheck Acquisition

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Acquisition Procedure (Logical Extraction)

Below is the **complete, non-truncated** MobileCheck workflow for a logical extraction of an Android device using **ADB (Android Debug Bridge)** as a teaching reference:

**Step 1: Pre-connection validation**

$$
\text{Device}_{state} = \{ \text{Powered ON}, \text{USB Debugging ENABLED}, \text{Unknown Sources ENABLED} \}
$$

**Step 2: Establish ADB connection**

The forensic workstation initiates a handshake:

$$
\text{ADB Handshake} = \{ \text{Server} \leftrightarrow \text{Daemon on device} \}
$$

The command issued:
```
adb devices
```
Returns the **serial number** as confirmation.

**Step 3: Verify device identity**

$$
\text{IMEI}_{verified} = \text{adb shell dumpsys iphonesubinfo} \quad \text{(Android variant command)}
$$

For Android:
```
adb shell service call iphonesubinfo 1
```

**Step 4: Calculate hash of source device (if physically imaged)**

$$
H_{source} = \text{SHA-256}(M_{device}) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} M_i \cdot 2^{8i} \pmod{2^{256}}
$$

Where $M_i$ is the i-th byte of the device memory image $M_{device}$.

**Step 5: Logical backup via ADB**

The forensic tool requests an application-level backup:

$$
\text{Backup}_{logical} = \bigcup_{j=1}^{k} A_j \cup \text{SDCard}_{files}
$$

Where $A_j$ represents the j-th application package and $k$ is the total number of installed apps.

**Step 6: Hash the resulting backup**

$$
H_{backup} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Backup}_{logical})
$$

**Step 7: Compare hashes to ensure integrity**

$$
\Delta_{hash} = \vert H_{source} - H_{backup} \vert = 0 \quad \text{(only valid for full image match)}
$$

> For logical backups, the comparison is:
$$
\text{Verification} = \begin{cases} \text{VALID} & \text{if } H_{backup} \text{ matches manifest} \\ \text{TAMPERED} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 8: Mount backup into forensic tool**

The tool creates a virtual mount point:

$$
M_{mount} = \{ /mnt/forensic/case_001/backup.ab \}
$$

**Step 9: Parse databases**

For each SQLite database extracted:

$$
T_{parsed} = \text{SQLite}_{engine}(D_{db}) \rightarrow \{ R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_m \}
$$

Where $R_i$ is the i-th recovered record.

**Step 10: Carve unallocated space for deleted records**

$$
R_{recovered} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \text{Carver}(S_{unalloc}) \quad \text{where } S_{unalloc} = M_{device} - \bigcup_{j=1}^{k} A_j
$$

**Step 11: Generate the forensic report**

$$
\text{Report}_{final} = \{ H_{hashes}, C_{chain}, T_{timeline}, E_{artifacts}, S_{signatures} \}
$$

Where:
- $H_{hashes}$ = cryptographic hash values
- $C_{chain}$ = chain of custody log
- $T_{timeline}$ = super-timeline of events
- $E_{artifacts}$ = extracted evidence items
- $S_{signatures}$ = investigator signatures

## 3.2 Acquisition Throughput Calculation (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A forensic examiner needs to image a **128 GB** mobile device. The acquisition tool sustains a **15 MB/s** throughput. The E01 compression ratio is **2.1 : 1**. Calculate the time required and the final image size.

**Given:**
- $D_{size} = 128 \text{ GB} = 128 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 131{,}072 \text{ MB}$
- $T_{rate} = 15 \text{ MB/s}$
- $R_{comp} = 2.1$

**Solution:**

Time required:
$$
t_{acq} = \frac{D_{size}}{T_{rate}} = \frac{131{,}072 \text{ MB}}{15 \text{ MB/s}} = 8{,}738.13 \text{ s}
$$

Converting to hours:
$$
t_{acq} = \frac{8{,}738.13}{3{,}600} \approx 2.427 \text{ hours}
$$

Final compressed image size:
$$
S_{image} = \frac{D_{size}}{R_{comp}} = \frac{131{,}072 \text{ MB}}{2.1} \approx 62{,}415.2 \text{ MB} \approx 60.95 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always budget **1.5× the calculated time** for setup, verification, and unexpected re-acquisition.

## 3.3 Hash Collision Probability — SHA-256 Bound

By the **birthday paradox**, the probability of finding a collision after $q$ hash attempts on an $n$-bit hash space is approximately:

$$
P_{collision}(q) \approx 1 - e^{-q^2 / (2 \cdot 2^n)}
$$

For SHA-256 ($n = 256$) and $q = 2^{64}$ attempts (computationally infeasible today):
$$
P_{collision} \approx 1 - e^{-(2^{64})^2 / (2 \cdot 2^{256})} = 1 - e^{-2^{72}} \approx 0
$$

This confirms why SHA-256 is the **gold standard** in MobileCheck.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams — MobileCheck Architecture

## 4.1 MobileCheck End-to-End Forensic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MobileCheck Initiated] --> B[Legal Authority Verified]
    B --> C[Device Identification]
    C --> C1[Record IMEI IMSI MSISDN]
    C1 --> C2[Photograph Device 6 Angles]
    C2 --> D[RF Isolation Faraday Bag]
    D --> E[Disable Auto-Lock Biometric]
    E --> F{Acquisition Tier}
    F --> F1[Tier 1 Manual Extraction]
    F --> F2[Tier 2 Logical Extraction]
    F --> F3[Tier 3 File-System Extraction]
    F --> F4[Tier 4 Physical Extraction]
    F1 --> G[Examination Phase]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> G1[SQLite Database Parsing]
    G1 --> G2[Plist LevelDB Decoding]
    G2 --> G3[Deleted Data Carving]
    G3 --> H[Analysis Phase]
    H --> H1[Super-Timeline Construction]
    H1 --> H2[CDR Correlation]
    H2 --> H3[Hash Filtering NSRL]
    H3 --> I[Reporting Phase]
    I --> I1[Cryptographic Hash Documentation]
    I1 --> I2[Chain of Custody Form]
    I2 --> I3[Executive Summary Generation]
    I3 --> J[MobileCheck Complete]
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style J fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style F fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style D fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Acquisition Tier Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tier1["Tier 1: Manual Extraction"]
        T1A[Screenshots] --> T1B[Visible Data]
        T1B --> T1C[Low Admissibility]
    end
    subgraph Tier2["Tier 2: Logical Extraction"]
        T2A[API Based Access] --> T2B[Files SMS Contacts]
        T2B --> T2C[Moderate Admissibility]
    end

        T3A[ADB iTunes Backup] --> T3B[App Sandbox System Files]
        T3B --> T3C[High Admissibility]
    end

        T4A[Bit by Bit Image] --> T4B[Deleted Data Unallocated Space]

    end
    Tier1 --> Tier2
    Tier2 --> Tier3
    Tier3 --> Tier4
    style Tier1 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style Tier2 fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style Tier3 fill:#e377c2,color:#fff
    style Tier4 fill:#7f7f7f,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Chain-of-Custody Flow in MobileCheck

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Inv as Investigator
    participant Dev as Mobile Device
    participant Tool as Forensic Tool
    participant Store as Evidence Locker
    Inv->>Dev: Seize and Isolate
    Dev-->>Inv: Return IMEI 15 digits
    Inv->>Tool: Connect via USB in Faraday
    Tool->>Dev: Initiate Logical Acquisition
    Dev-->>Tool: Return Backup File
    Tool->>Tool: Compute SHA-256 Hash
    Tool-->>Inv: Hash 64 hex chars
    Inv->>Store: Lock in evidence bag
    Store-->>Inv: Acknowledgment signed
    Note over Inv,Store: Every transfer is logged with timestamp
```

## 4.4 MobileCheck Evidence Integrity Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Source Device] -->|Bit Copy| I[Forensic Image E01]
    I -->|SHA-256| H1[Hash 1]
    H1 --> V{Hash Match}
    V -->|Yes| A[Admissible Evidence]
    V -->|No| R[Re-Acquisition Required]
    R --> S
    A --> D[Court Presentation]
    style S fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
```
