```mermaid
    style R fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**Define MobileCheck and list its three primary objectives in digital forensics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MobileCheck** is the systematic, forensically-sound methodology used to identify, isolate, acquire, examine, and report on digital evidence from mobile devices while preserving the integrity and chain-of-custody of the evidence.

**Three Primary Objectives:**
1. **Evidence Preservation** — Ensure the original device is never modified (write-blocking).
2. **Data Integrity** — Maintain cryptographic hash verification (MD5/SHA-256) at every stage.
3. **Legal Admissibility** — Document a complete chain-of-custody to satisfy court scrutiny.

*(Mark split: Definition = 1, Three objectives = 2)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**What is a Faraday bag, and why is it mandatory during mobile forensic acquisition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Faraday bag** is a shielded enclosure made of conductive material (typically copper/nickel fabric) that **blocks external radio frequency (RF) signals** such as cellular (GSM/LTE/5G), Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and GPS.

**Why mandatory:**
- Prevents **remote wipe** commands (e.g., *Find My iPhone*, *Find My Device*).
- Blocks incoming **push notifications** that could alter device state.
- Eliminates **location tracking** of the evidence by adversaries.
- Ensures the **device state remains static** during acquisition.

**Standard:** Effective Faraday bags provide **≥ 60 dB attenuation** across the 800 MHz–6 GHz spectrum.

*(Mark split: Definition = 1, Three reasons = 2)*

---

## 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice Pattern

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 marks] Explain the different tiers of mobile data acquisition in MobileCheck. Compare their admissibility strength and data accessibility.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

MobileCheck supports **four hierarchical acquisition tiers**, each with distinct depth and legal weight:

**Tier 1 — Manual Extraction (1 mark)**
- Method: Photograph screen, manual note-taking, screen recording.
- Data: Only user-visible, currently-displayed content.
- Tools: Camera, notepad.
- Admissibility: **Low** (easily challenged as subjective).

**Tier 2 — Logical Extraction (2 marks)**
- Method: Uses device APIs (ADB, iTunes backup protocol) to extract files.
- Data: Contacts, SMS, call logs, photos, app data, calendar, browser history.
- Tools: Cellebrite UFED, MSAB XRY, MOBILedit Forensic, ADB.
- Admissibility: **Moderate** (accepted in most courts, but may miss deleted data).

**Tier 3 — File-System Extraction (2 marks)**
- Method: Direct access to file-system structures (ext4, APFS, F2FS) bypassing app sandbox restrictions.
- Data: System files, app sandboxes, hidden configuration files, deleted metadata.
- Tools: Cellebrite UFED 4PC, iTunes encrypted backup, custom ADB scripts.
- Admissibility: **High** (recoverable from encrypted backups with proper keys).

**Tier 4 — Physical Extraction (2 marks)**
- Method: Bit-by-bit copy of the flash memory (NAND/eMMC/UFS chip), including unallocated space.
- Data: All data including deleted files, deleted database records, slack space, RAM dumps.
- Tools: Cellebrite Premium, JTAG, chip-off, ISP (In-System Programming).
- Admissibility: **Very High** (gold standard; identical to forensic image of a hard disk).

| Tier | Data Depth | Admissibility | Skill Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface | Low | Low |
| 2 | Logical | Moderate | Medium |
| 3 | File-system | High | High |
| 4 | Physical (bit-level) | Very High | Very High |

---

#### **(b) [7 marks] Describe the MobileCheck procedure for preserving chain-of-custody. Include documentation artifacts and hash verification steps.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Chain-of-Custody (CoC)** is the **unbroken, documented trail** of evidence possession from seizure to court presentation. In MobileCheck, it is enforced via the following procedure:

**Step 1 — Seizure Documentation (1 mark)**
- Record: Date, time, location, seizing officer, witness signatures.
- Photograph device in situ before any handling.

**Step 2 — Evidence Bag Labeling (1 mark)**
- Each evidence container is labeled with: Case number, Item number, Date, Officer, Hash of contents.

**Step 3 — Device Identification (1 mark)**
- Capture: **IMEI (15 digits), IMSI (15 digits), MSISDN (phone number), Make, Model, OS version**.
- Formula: $\text{IMEI} = \text{TAC} (8) + \text{SNR} (6) + \text{CD} (1)$

**Step 4 — Initial Hash Computation (1 mark)**
- If physical image is possible: $H_{source} = \text{SHA-256}(M_{device})$
- Stored in hex format: 64 characters.

**Step 5 — Acquisition Log (1 mark)**
- Document: Tool used, tool version, acquisition method, start/end timestamps, operator name.
- All write operations on the original device are **prohibited**.

**Step 6 — Post-Acquisition Hash (1 mark)**
- $H_{image} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Image}_{forensic})$
- $\Delta_{hash} = \vert H_{source} - H_{image} \vert = 0$ must hold.

**Step 7 — Transfer Log (1 mark)**
- Every transfer between custody persons is recorded with dual signatures and timestamps.
- Storage in tamper-evident locker with controlled access.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **omit the hash verification step** at the end of acquisition. This is a **2-mark deduction** in ESE. Always show the *post-acquisition hash comparison equation*, even if symbolic.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [7 marks] Explain the role of cryptographic hashing in MobileCheck. Derive the SHA-256 compression function and explain why SHA-256 is preferred over MD5.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Role of Hashing (2 marks):**
- **Integrity verification** of forensic images.
- **Tamper detection** — any single-bit change in the image alters the hash completely (**avalanche effect**).
- **Deduplication** — identical evidence blocks can be verified across multiple copies.
- **Non-repudiation** — cryptographic proof that the evidence has not been altered.

**MD5 vs SHA-256 (2 marks):**

| Property | MD5 | SHA-256 |
|---|---|---|
| Hash length | 128 bits | 256 bits |
| Collision resistance | **Broken** (2004) | **Secure** (post-2026 still safe) |
| Speed | Faster | Slower but acceptable |
| Court acceptance | Declining | **Gold standard** |
| Birthday bound | $2^{64}$ | $2^{128}$ |

**SHA-256 Compression Function Derivation (3 marks):**

The SHA-256 algorithm processes input in **512-bit blocks** through 64 rounds. The compression function for round $i$ is:

$$
T_1 = H + \Sigma_1(E) + \text{Ch}(E, F, G) + K_i + W_i
$$

$$
T_2 = \Sigma_0(A) + \text{Maj}(A, B, C)
$$

$$
H' = G, \quad G' = F, \quad F' = E, \quad E' = D + T_1
$$

$$
D' = C, \quad C' = B, \quad B' = A, \quad A' = T_1 + T_2
$$

Where:
- $A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H$ = 32-bit working variables
- $\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1$ = rotation functions
- $\text{Ch}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (\neg x \land z)$ — choice function
- $\text{Maj}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$ — majority function
- $K_i$ = round constant (64 values derived from cube roots of primes)
- $W_i$ = message schedule word (expanded from 512-bit block)

**Why SHA-256 is preferred:**
- **256-bit output** makes brute-force and birthday attacks computationally infeasible ($2^{128}$ operations).
- **No known collisions** as of 2026.
- **Court-accepted** under Indian Evidence Act (now **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023**) and international standards.

---

#### **(b) [7 marks] Discuss the challenges of mobile forensics in MobileCheck. Include encryption, locked devices, and cloud-synced data as sub-topics.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Challenge 1 — Device Encryption (2 marks)**
- Modern smartphones use **Full Disk Encryption (FDE)** or **File-Based Encryption (FBE)**.
  - iOS: AES-256 hardware encryption tied to Secure Enclave + passcode.
  - Android: FBE with hardware-backed keystore since Android 7.0 (Nougat).
- **Impact:** Physical extraction returns ciphertext without the user's passcode.
- **Mitigation:** Exploit GPU/SoC vulnerabilities (e.g., Cellebrite Advanced), check-point screen exploitation, or legal compulsion of the passcode.

**Challenge 2 — Locked & Biometric Devices (2 marks)**
- **Pattern lock, PIN, alphanumeric passcode, Face ID, Touch ID, fingerprint.**
- Android: Brute-force protection with **30-second/5-attempt exponential back-off** (up to 17 hours after 16 attempts).
- iOS: After **10 failed attempts**, the device may **auto-erase** (if enabled in Settings).
- **Mitigation:** Use chip-off (Tier 4), JTAG, or hardware-based key extraction in specialized labs.

**Challenge 3 — Cloud-Synced Data (2 marks)**
- Data may reside on remote servers: iCloud, Google Drive, OneDrive, WhatsApp Cloud.
- MobileCheck on-device acquisition may **miss cloud-only artifacts**.
- **Solution:** Issue legal process to the cloud provider (e.g., MLAT for cross-border).
- **Artifacts:** iCloud backups, Google Takeout, WhatsApp Google Drive backup.

**Challenge 4 — Anti-Forensics & Secure Boot (1 mark)**
- Secure Boot (verified boot chain) detects forensic boot images.
- Custom ROMs, bootloader re-locking, factory-reset protection (FRP) hinder acquisition.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is **confusing "deleted" with "encrypted"**. Deleted data on flash memory often remains in the NAND until garbage collection — hence physical extraction (Tier 4) is essential. Markers deduct **1.5 marks** if this distinction is missing in the answer.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — MobileCheck in 60 Seconds**

- ✅ **MobileCheck = 5 phases:** Identification → Isolation → Acquisition → Examination → Reporting
- ✅ **Three key metadata:** **IMEI (15 digits), IMSI, MSISDN**
- ✅ **Four acquisition tiers:** Manual < Logical < File-System < **Physical (best)**
- ✅ **Gold standard hash:** **SHA-256** (64 hex chars) — not MD5
- ✅ **Faraday bag** = RF isolation, prevents remote wipe
- ✅ **Forensic image formats:** **E01, DD, AFF**
- ✅ **Chain-of-custody** = unbroken signed documentation
- ✅ **Write-blocking principle** = NEVER alter source device
- ✅ **Deleted ≠ Gone** — resides in unallocated NAND until garbage collection
- ✅ **SQLite** is the dominant mobile database format — master it
- ✅ **Tier 4 (Physical)** = bit-by-bit copy, **highest admissibility**
- ✅ **Hash equation:** $H = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ where $M$ is the image
- ✅ **Integrity check:** $\Delta_{hash} = \vert H_{source} - H_{image} \vert = 0$
- ✅ **Cloud data** requires **separate legal process** — not part of device acquisition
- ✅ **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023** governs admissibility in India
- ✅ **Acquisition time budget rule:** $t_{actual} = 1.5 \times t_{calculated}$

---

> **End of Module 3 — MobileCheck Notes** | *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Standards*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BlueStacks (Android Emulator)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** BlueStacks (Android Emulator)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **BlueStacks** is a **Layer-2 Android Runtime Emulator (ARE)** that creates a complete virtualized Android Operating System environment on top of a host machine's kernel (typically Windows or macOS). It translates ARM-based Android application bytecode (APK) into x86-64 host instructions through dynamic binary translation, thereby enabling the execution of mobile-only applications on desktop hardware.

From a **Digital Forensics** perspective, BlueStacks is treated as a **virtual mobile device (VMD)** whose entire filesystem, registry, user data, contacts, SMS, call logs, and chat artifacts reside as ordinary files inside a folder on the suspect's host computer. Hence, it is a high-yield source of *non-traditional* digital evidence.

| KTU 2024 Keyword | Meaning in BlueStacks Context |
|---|---|
| **Virtual Mobile Device (VMD)** | The emulated Android instance created by BlueStacks |
| **Hypervisor Layer** | BlueStacks App Player virtual engine |
| **Guest OS** | Stock Android (AOSP-derived) running inside the VM |
| **Host OS** | Windows 10/11 (where BlueStacks is installed) |
| **Bridged Storage** | Shared `C:/Users/<user>/Documents/...` folder |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Pocket Translator"**
> Imagine you are an English-only speaker (your **Windows PC**) trying to talk to a Japanese tourist (an **Android App**). You do not know Japanese, and the tourist does not know English. BlueStacks acts as a **real-time pocket translator** sitting between you and the tourist. It translates every sentence back and forth, so both sides feel like they are communicating naturally — even though they are speaking two completely different languages.
> The translator's **notebook** (BlueStacks data folder) is where every word spoken, every contact introduced, and every photo shared gets recorded. A forensic investigator is someone who, long after the conversation ends, seizes the notebook to reconstruct what was said, by whom, and when.

## 1.3 Why BlueStacks Matters in Digital Forensics

- **Cross-Platform Evidence Bridging**: Suspects frequently use BlueStacks to access WhatsApp, Instagram, Telegram, or Signal on a desktop, generating evidence that does **not** exist on a physical mobile phone.
- **Anti-Forensics Detection Laggard**: Few mobile forensic suites (Cellebrite, Magnet AXIOM) auto-parse BlueStacks artifacts, so examiners must manually investigate.
- **Cloud-Synced Artifacts**: Many users use BlueStacks to access a *second* account of the same app, deliberately segregated from their primary mobile device — a classic **parallel-channel** deception technique.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Default Installation Path (Windows):** `C:\ProgramData\BlueStacks_nxt` (hidden) and `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\BlueStacks_nxt`
> - **Disk Image Format:** `data.vmdk`, `Root.vhd`, `sdcard.vhd`
> - **Typical VMD Size:** **10 GB to 64 GB** (default), expandable up to **512 GB**
> - **Data.mount.db Size:** **~64 MB** to **256 MB** (rotating)
> - **Virtualization Engine:** BlueStacks Hypervisor (proprietary, based on Android-x86 + QEMU derivatives)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BlueStacks Internal Architecture (Layered View)

BlueStacks does not run Android natively. It uses a multi-layered virtualization stack:

1. **Host Hardware (x86-64 CPU + GPU)**
2. **Host Operating System (Windows 10/11 / macOS)**
3. **BlueStacks Hypervisor (BST)** – proprietary type-2 hypervisor managing virtual disks, RAM, and CPU scheduling.
4. **Android-x86 Kernel** – Linux kernel patched for x86 desktop hardware, with ARM-translation libraries (`libhoudini.so` / `libndk_translation.so`).
5. **Android Runtime (ART / Dalvik)** – executes APK DEX bytecode.
6. **User Applications (APKs)** – WhatsApp, Instagram, banking apps, games.
7. **Bridged File System** – shared folder visible to both host and guest.

## 2.2 Forensic Data Storage Layout (High-Yield)

The following table maps the **exact filesystem path** of each BlueStacks forensic artifact to its **investigative value**.

| Artifact Path (Windows Host) | Artifact Type | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|
| `C:\ProgramData\BlueStacks_nxt\Android\Data.vmdk` | Virtual Disk Image | Full Android filesystem |
| `C:\ProgramData\BlueStacks_nxt\Engine\Root.vhd` | Read-only System Image | OS-level artifacts |
| `...\Engine\UserData\SharedFolder\` | Bridged Folder | Files transferred host↔guest |
| `...\Engine\UserData\db\BlueStacksData.db` | SQLite (Key-Value) | App metadata, account mappings |
| `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\BlueStacks_nxt\Engine\UserData\data.sparseimage` | macOS Sparse Bundle | Same as VMDK (macOS) |
| `C:\Users\<user>\Documents\BlueStacks` | User Documents | Shared files, downloads |
| `...\Engine\UserData\fs\Data\userdata\0\` | Mounted Android Data | App private data, databases |
| `Windows Registry: HKLM\SOFTWARE\BlueStacks_nxt` | Registry Hive | Installation timestamp, version, instance ID |
| `Windows Prefetch: BLUESTACKS_*.pf` | Prefetch File | Execution evidence, frequency, last run time |
| `NTUSER.DAT\Software\BlueStacks` | User Registry | Per-user configuration, last-used apps |

## 2.3 Key SQLite Databases Inside the VMD

| Database Path (within VMD) | DB Name | Forensic Yield |
|---|---|---|
| `/data/data/com.whatsapp/databases/msgstore.db` | WhatsApp Message Store | Chats, calls, media metadata |
| `/data/data/com.android.providers.telephony/databases/telephony.db` | Telephony Provider | SMS, MMS, call logs |
| `/data/data/com.android.contacts/databases/contacts2.db` | Contacts | Phonebook |
| `/data/data/com.google.android.gms/databases/google_ads.db` | Google Ads | User profiling data |
| `/data/data/com.android.browser/databases/browser2.db` | Browser | URL history, downloads |
| `/data/data/com.instagram.android/databases/direct.db` | Instagram Direct | Private messages |
| `/sdcard/Android/data/<app>/` | App External Storage | Cached photos/videos |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Forensic Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total VMD Logical Size | $S_{VMD} = N_{blocks} \times B_{size}$ | blocks, bytes | Bytes (B) |
| 2 | Slack Space Recovery | $S_{slack} = S_{cluster} - (S_{file} \bmod S_{cluster})$ | cluster size, file size | Bytes (B) |
| 3 | SHA-256 Hash Verification | $H_{final} = \text{SHA256}(M_{image} \vert \text{nonce})$ | message, nonce | Hex (64 chars) |
| 4 | Timestamp Decoding (UNIX) | $T_{readable} = T_{epoch} + \Delta_{tz}$ | epoch seconds, TZ offset | Local time |
| 5 | Deleted SQLite Record | $R_{deleted} = R_{free\_list} \cup R_{unalloc$ | free pages, unallocated | Records |
| 6 | App UID → Username | $UID \rightarrow \text{/data/user/}\vert UID$ | numeric UID | Path string |
| 7 | WAL File Carving | $W_{size} = \vert W_{header} \vert + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert F_{i} \vert$ | header, frames | Bytes (B) |
| 8 | Time Zone Offset | $\Delta_{tz} = T_{UTC} - T_{local}$ | UTC, Local | Seconds |
| 9 | Mount Point Recovery | $M_{point} = \text{Parse}(\text{fstab}, \text{VMD})$ | file system table | String |
| 10 | Chain of Custody Hash | $C_{i+1} = \text{SHA256}(C_{i} \vert H_{artifact})$ | prior hash, artifact hash | Hex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use the vertical bar `$\vert$` notation inside LaTeX tables**, never the raw pipe character `|`, to prevent breaking KTU's markdown renderer.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Law Enforcement**: Child-exploitation investigations where suspects use WhatsApp-Web-via-BlueStacks to bypass phone seizure.
- **Corporate E-Discovery**: Harvesting BYOD-side chats from company laptops for litigation.
- **Incident Response**: Detecting data exfiltration via emulated banking trojans (e.g., SharkBot disguised as a BlueStacks plugin).
- **Academic Research**: Studying cross-platform app-data leakage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Forensic Methodology & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive 10-Stage BlueStacks Forensic Acquisition Methodology

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **gold-standard** procedure accepted by KTU evaluators. Each step's *why* and *how* is unpacked so the student can answer both theory and lab-viva questions.

### **Stage 1 — Identify BlueStacks Installation**

**Why:** We must first *prove* the suspect's machine contains the emulator before seizure.

**How:** Run the following on a read-only forensic image of the host:

$$ P_{BlueStacks} = \bigcup_{i} \Big\{ \text{File}_{i} : \text{Name}(\text{File}_{i}) \supseteq \text{"BlueStacks"} \Big\} $$

Search paths to enumerate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Paths}_{search} =\ & \text{"C:\textbackslash ProgramData"} \\
& \cup\ \text{"C:\textbackslash Users"} \\
& \cup\ \text{"HKLM\textbackslash SOFTWARE"} \\
& \cup\ \text{"Prefetch"}
\end{aligned}
$$

Expected positive hits: `BlueStacks_nxt`, `BlueStacksInstaller.exe`, `BLUESTACKS*.pf`.

### **Stage 2 — Verify Installation via Registry**

**Why:** Registry holds the *truth* about installed applications, including install date and version.

**How:** Parse the SOFTWARE hive:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Key}_{root} &= \text{HKLM\textbackslash SOFTWARE\textbackslash BlueStacks\_nxt} \\
\text{Value}_{InstallTime} &\rightarrow \text{Epoch seconds} \\
\text{Value}_{Version} &\rightarrow \text{String, e.g., "5.21.0"} \\
\text{Value}_{InstanceID} &\rightarrow \text{GUID (16 bytes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert the epoch to a readable timestamp:

$$
T_{readable} = 1\,700\,000\,000 + 19\,800 = \text{2023-11-14 22:13:20 UTC}
$$

### **Stage 3 — Create Forensic Image of the VMD**

**Why:** We must never work on the original evidence.

**How:** Use FTK Imager / `dd` to acquire the VMDK file:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{SHA256} &= \text{SHA256}\big(\text{Data.vmdk}\big) \\
H_{MD5} &= \text{MD5}\big(\text{Data.vmdk}\big) \\
C_{0} &= \text{InitialChainHash} \\
C_{1} &= \text{SHA256}\big(C_{0} \,\vert\, H_{SHA256}\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

Store the hash values in the chain-of-custody document.

### **Stage 4 — Mount the VMDK Read-Only**

**Why:** The VMDK is a virtual disk; we mount it in read-only mode to preserve integrity.

**How:** Use `FTK Imager` → *Add Evidence Item* → *Image File* → mount with **Read-Only** flag set. The tool auto-detects the EXT4 partition inside the VMDK.

### **Stage 5 — Extract System-Level Artifacts**

**Why:** System artifacts prove the device was *active* and *configured* by the suspect.

**How:** Navigate to:

$$
\text{Path}_{sys} = \text{"/system/etc/hosts"} \cup \text{"/data/system/accounts.db"} \cup \text{"/data/system/usagestats/"}
$$

The `accounts.db` reveals registered Google accounts; `usagestats` shows the last opened apps with timestamps.

### **Stage 6 — Extract User-Level App Databases**

**Why:** The bulk of evidence (messages, calls, photos) resides in `/data/data/<app>/`.

**How:** For each target app, locate its SQLite DB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For } A &\in \{\text{WhatsApp, Instagram, Telegram, Signal}\}: \\
\text{DB}_{A} &= \text{"/data/data/"} + A + \text{"/databases/*.db"} \\
\text{WAL}_{A} &= \text{"/data/data/"} + A + \text{"/databases/*.db-wal"}
\end{aligned}
$$

Export DB and WAL with:

$$
\text{Copy}_{forensic} = \text{ReadOnlyCopy}\big(\text{DB}_{A}\big) \cup \text{ReadOnlyCopy}\big(\text{WAL}_{A}\big)
$$

### **Stage 7 — Carve Deleted SQLite Records**

**Why:** Suspects may attempt to wipe chat logs; the WAL and unallocated pages still contain residual records.

**How:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{recoverable} &= R_{free\_list} \cup R_{unallocated} \\
\text{For each page } p \in \text{DB}_{A}: & \\
& \text{If } \text{PageType}(p) = \text{OVERFLOW} \Rightarrow \text{scan for SQL record} \\
& \text{If } \text{PageType}(p) = \text{FREE\_TRUNK} \Rightarrow \text{append to } R_{recoverable}
\end{aligned}
$$

Recovered records are then parsed for the same columns as live records (`_id`, `timestamp`, `body`, `key_remote_jid`).

### **Stage 8 — Decode Timestamps**

**Why:** Timestamps are stored in **milliseconds since UNIX epoch** in most Android databases.

**How:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ms} &= 1\,700\,000\,000\,000 \quad \text{(stored value)} \\
T_{s} &= T_{ms} \div 1000 = 1\,700\,000\,000 \\
T_{readable} &= T_{s} + \Delta_{tz} \\
T_{UTC} &= \text{2023-11-14 22:13:20} \\
\text{If } \Delta_{tz} &= +5\text{h}30\text{m (IST)}: \\
T_{IST} &= \text{2023-11-15 03:43:20}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Stage 9 — Reconstruct the Timeline**

**Why:** Investigators must show *when* each event happened in human-readable form.

**How:** Sort all artifacts by `timestamp` ascending and merge:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Timeline} =\ & \text{SortAsc}\Big(\big\{(t_{i}, e_{i}) : e_{i} \in \text{AllArtifacts}\big\}\Big) \\
\text{Output} =\ & \text{Log}(\text{Timeline}) \to \text{CSV / XLSX}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Stage 10 — Report & Court Presentation**

**Why:** Evidence must be defensible in court.

**How:** Generate a report containing:

- Hash of original VMDK
- Hash of every extracted database
- Screenshot of SQLite viewer
- Chain of custody hashes $C_{0}, C_{1}, ..., C_{n}$
- Investigator signature

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Evidence Type | Tool / Technique | Difficulty (KTU 1–5) | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Installation proof | Registry + Prefetch parse | 1 | 2 |
| VMD acquisition | FTK Imager / dd | 2 | 3 |
| Mounted browse | FTK / Autopsy | 2 | 2 |
| SQLite extraction | Forensic Browser | 3 | 4 |
| WAL carving | Manual hex / py-utility | 5 | 5 |
| Timeline correlation | Plaso / log2timeline | 4 | 4 |
| Report writing | Manual + templates | 2 | 2 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define BlueStacks. Why is it relevant as a forensic artifact in 2024?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (2 marks):** BlueStacks is a type-2 Android emulator that creates a virtualized Android OS on top of Windows/macOS, allowing mobile applications to run on desktop hardware through ARM-to-x86 binary translation.
> - **Forensic relevance (1 mark):** It enables suspects to operate a parallel, hidden mobile environment on a desktop, generating evidence (chats, calls, photos) that mobile-only seizure would miss.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **List any three default forensic storage paths of BlueStacks on a Windows host.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 per path):**
> 1. `C:\ProgramData\BlueStacks_nxt\Android\Data.vmdk`
> 2. `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\BlueStacks_nxt\Engine\UserData\db\BlueStacksData.db`
> 3. `C:\ProgramData\BlueStacks_nxt\Engine\Root.vhd`

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> **Important (KTU 2024 ESE Rule):** *Module choice question carries 14 marks, split as 7 + 7 sub-parts.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the layered architecture of BlueStacks. How does each layer contribute to forensic evidence recovery?

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe, with diagram, the step-by-step process of mounting a BlueStacks VMDK image and extracting WhatsApp chat artifacts.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

1. **Host Hardware Layer (1 mark):** Provides x86-64 CPU, GPU, RAM. Forensic link: hardware-level prefetch and timeline.
2. **Host OS Layer (1 mark):** Windows 10/11. Forensic link: Registry keys under `HKLM\SOFTWARE\BlueStacks_nxt`, Prefetch files.
3. **BlueStacks Hypervisor (1 mark):** Manages virtual disks, memory, CPU. Forensic link: VMDK, VHD files in `ProgramData`.
4. **Android-x86 Kernel (1 mark):** Linux kernel for desktop hardware. Forensic link: `/proc/`, `/sys/` mounts inside VMD.
5. **Android Runtime (1 mark):** ART executes DEX bytecode. Forensic link: app usage stats.
6. **Applications (1 mark):** APKs such as WhatsApp, Instagram. Forensic link: SQLite databases.
7. **Bridged FS (1 mark):** Shared folder. Forensic link: file transfer logs.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Open FTK Imager → *File* → *Add Evidence Item* → *Image File*.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Browse to `C:\ProgramData\BlueStacks_nxt\Android\Data.vmdk`.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Mount with **Read-Only** flag; verify SHA-256 hash.
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Navigate to `\data\data\com.whatsapp\databases\`.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Export `msgstore.db`, `msgstore.db-wal`, `wa.db` to forensic drive.
- **Step 6 (1 mark):** Open in DB Browser for SQLite; query `SELECT * FROM messages`.
- **Step 7 (1 mark):** Convert epoch milliseconds to IST; produce timeline CSV; attach to case report.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the significance of the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) file in recovering deleted WhatsApp messages from a BlueStacks VMDK. Derive the formula for WAL page size.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Compare the forensic value of the BlueStacks shared folder versus the VMDK-based app private data.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

- **WAL definition (2 marks):** A WAL file is a rollback journal that stores uncommitted and recently committed-but-not-checkpointed pages of a SQLite database. In BlueStacks, the WAL of WhatsApp (`msgstore.db-wal`) often contains messages that the user *thinks* they deleted.
- **Page size derivation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{size} &= \vert \text{WAL Header} \vert + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert F_{i} \vert \\
\text{Where } \vert \text{WAL Header} \vert &= 32\ \text{bytes (fixed)} \\
F_{i} &= \text{Frame}_{i} = P_{db\_size} + 24\ \text{bytes (frame header)} \\
\text{If } n &= 50 \text{ and } P_{db\_size} = 4096: \\
P_{size} &= 32 + 50 \times (4096 + 24) \\
&= 32 + 50 \times 4120 \\
&= 32 + 206\,000 \\
&= 206\,032\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Significance (2 marks):** The WAL survives even after the user issues a `DELETE` SQL command, until a `PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)` is executed. Hence WAL carving is the single most powerful technique to recover deleted evidence.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

| Aspect | Shared Folder (1 mark each) | VMDK App Data (1 mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | `C:\Users\<user>\Documents\BlueStacks` | `\data\data\<app>\` inside VMD |
| **Visibility** | Plain host files (NTFS) | Encrypted/compressed inside EXT4 |
| **Content** | Manually transferred files (images, APKs) | App databases, shared_prefs, cache |
| **Forensic yield** | File transfer timeline, user documents | Chats, call logs, contacts, credentials |
| **Anti-forensic risk** | Easy to delete | Harder; remnants in unallocated space |
| **Recovery method** | Standard file carving | SQLite + WAL carving |
| **Court weight** | Strong circumstantial | Strong direct evidence |

(7 rows × 1 mark = 7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Avoid These Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse VMDK with VHDX.** VMDK is VMware format; BlueStacks sometimes uses both, but `data.vmdk` is the primary user-data virtual disk. Mixing them up costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Always include the SHA-256 hash** of the VMDK before mounting. Examiners specifically check for it; missing it costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Timestamp unit confusion:** Android SQLite stores `INTEGER` as **milliseconds**, not seconds. Forgetting to multiply by 1000 loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting the WAL file:** Many students copy only `msgstore.db` and ignore `msgstore.db-wal`. Examiners deduct **2 marks** as deleted chats would be lost.
> 5. **Do not modify the original VMDK.** Always work on a forensic copy. Mounting read-write is a **chain-of-custody violation** worth losing **2 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam**

- ✅ **BlueStacks** = Type-2 Android Emulator; not a true mobile device.
- ✅ Default paths: `C:\ProgramData\BlueStacks_nxt\Android\Data.vmdk` and `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\BlueStacks_nxt\`.
- ✅ **Virtual disk formats:** VMDK (primary), VHD (system), sparseimage (macOS).
- ✅ **Always hash** the VMDK with **SHA-256** before any operation.
- ✅ Mount the VMDK **read-only**.
- ✅ Target SQLite DBs: `msgstore.db`, `telephony.db`, `contacts2.db`, `browser2.db`.
- ✅ **Always copy the WAL** file alongside the main DB.
- ✅ Timestamps = **milliseconds since UNIX epoch**.
- ✅ Prefetch files = `BLUESTACKS_*.pf`; prove execution history.
- ✅ Registry key = `HKLM\SOFTWARE\BlueStacks_nxt`; contains install time, version, instance ID.
- ✅ **Chain of custody hash** chain: $C_{i+1} = \text{SHA256}(C_i \vert H_{artifact})$.
- ✅ **WAL size formula:** $P_{size} = 32 + n \times (P_{db} + 24)$.
- ✅ Carving order: free-list → unallocated → overflow pages.
- ✅ **Use read-only mounts, never original evidence.**
- ✅ **Do NOT use Python code in viva** unless specifically asked — KTU examiners prefer command-line tools (`dd`, `strings`, `sqlite3`).
- ✅ Common exam trap: confusing **BlueStacks** with **Android-x86** OS. They are related but BlueStacks adds a **proprietary hypervisor layer**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQLite Database Viewer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SQLite Database Viewer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **SQLite Database Viewer** is a specialized forensic software utility designed to open, inspect, query, and export the contents of **SQLite database files** (commonly identified by extensions such as `.db`, `.sqlite`, `.sqlite3`, `.db3`, `.sqlitedb`) during the stages of digital forensic investigation. SQLite is a serverless, self-contained, zero-configuration, transactional SQL database engine embedded directly into applications. In the forensic context, an SQLite viewer is not a database management tool for live systems but a **read-only, evidence-preserving analytical interface** that allows investigators to recover deleted records, examine metadata, decode BLOB (Binary Large Object) data, and interpret schema information without mutating the original artifact.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance for PECST754, the SQLite Database Viewer falls under the **Database Forensics** subdomain of the *Operating System and File System Forensics* module. Forensic examiners deploy it to inspect application-level artifacts stored by mobile operating systems (Android, iOS), web browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge), chat applications (WhatsApp, Signal, Telegram), email clients, and other productivity software that rely on SQLite for local persistent storage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crime scene where detectives find dozens of locked transparent jars (the SQLite `.db` files). Each jar contains neatly stacked index cards representing user actions — text messages sent, web URLs visited, GPS coordinates logged, contacts added, files cached. Normally the original owners of the jars locked them away, but the **SQLite Database Viewer acts as a special forensic magnifying glass with built-in translation tables** that allows the investigator to:

1. See the structural layout of the jar (the database schema — its tables, columns, and data types).
2. Read every visible card inside (live records) using simple or advanced queries.
3. Detect the faint pencil marks of cards that were erased but not shredded (deleted but recoverable data inside SQLite free pages).
4. Translate blurry photographic evidence pasted on some cards (BLOB data such as images, audio clips, thumbnails, and tokens).
5. Seal the jar back exactly as it was found (forensic integrity preservation) and produce a notarized transcript of what was read (an exportable report).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Why SQLite Matters in Forensics**
> Over **1 trillion** SQLite databases are estimated to be in active use worldwide, and the format has been adopted as an application file format by Adobe, Apple, Google, McAfee, and Microsoft. This ubiquity means that in a typical mobile device seizure, an examiner can encounter **hundreds of SQLite artifacts**, making mastery of an SQLite Database Viewer a non-negotiable skill for a digital forensic analyst.

### 1.3 Core Forensic Objectives of an SQLite Database Viewer

1. **Schema Visualization** — Display the full database architecture (master schema, table definitions, indices, triggers, views).
2. **Live Data Browsing** — Tabular rendering of all rows and columns of every table.
3. **SQL Query Execution** — Permit the investigator to run `SELECT`, `PRAGMA`, and forensic-recovery queries.
4. **Deleted Record Recovery** — Identify unallocated pages, free blocks, and orphaned rows.
5. **BLOB Decoding** — Render or export binary attachments (images, timestamps in epoch, geolocation tokens).
6. **WAL/Journal Inspection** — View the Write-Ahead Log and rollback journal to reconstruct unsynced transactions.
7. **Hashing & Integrity Verification** — Compute SHA-1 / SHA-256 of the database file and individual tables for chain-of-custody.
8. **Export & Reporting** — Generate CSV, HTML, PDF, and Excel outputs admissible in court.

### 1.4 Standard Forensic SQLite Viewer Tools (Industry Baseline)

| Tool | License | Primary Use Case |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **DB Browser for SQLite (DB4S)** | Open Source / GPL | General viewing, querying, light forensics |
| **SQLite Forensic Reporter** | Commercial (Sanderson Forensics) | Court-grade forensic examination |
| **Autopsy + Forensic Kit** | Open Source | Integrated platform-level forensics |
| **Magnet AXIOM** | Commercial | Mobile/cloud artifact recovery from SQLite |
| **Oxygen Forensic Detective** | Commercial | Mobile SQLite decryption + viewing |
| **MDF Sonar SQLite Recovery** | Commercial | Deep carving of deleted SQLite records |
| **SQLiteStudio** | Open Source | Multi-database viewing and analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Immutability Principle (Daubert Standard)** — A forensic SQLite viewer must NEVER write to the original evidence file. All interactions should be performed on a **forensically sound working copy** whose hash is verified against the original using tools like `sha256sum`, `FTK Imager`, or `EnCase`. Violating this principle renders the evidence inadmissible in most jurisdictions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The SQLite File Format — A Structural Breakdown

A SQLite database is physically stored as a single, ordinary disk file. Internally, the file is divided into fixed-size **pages** (the default page size is **4096 bytes**, configurable to any power of two between **512 and 65536 bytes**). The logical architecture consists of:

1. **Page 1 — The Database Header Page**
   - Contains the **100-byte database file header** with the magic string `SQLite format 3\000`.
   - Stores the page size, file format versions (legacy, WAL, journal), text encoding, root B-tree page numbers, and schema cookie.

2. **B-Tree Pages** — The actual data structures holding tables and indices.
   - **Table B-Tree** (leaf pages) holds row records.
   - **Index B-Tree** accelerates lookups.
3. **Freelist Pages** — A linked list of pages previously allocated but currently unused. These often contain **recoverable deleted data**.
4. **Write-Ahead Log (WAL) File** (`-wal` suffix) — Contains pages modified but not yet checkpointed to the main file.
5. **Rollback Journal** (`-journal` suffix) — Stores the original page contents prior to uncommitted transactions.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of an SQLite Database Viewer

1. **File Acquisition Phase**
   - The investigator loads the `.db` file (or the parent container) into the viewer.
   - The viewer parses the **100-byte header** and validates the magic string.

2. **Schema Extraction Phase**
   - The viewer reads the `sqlite_master` table, which holds the **DDL (Data Definition Language)** of every table, index, view, and trigger.
   - For example, executing `SELECT name, sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';` returns the complete schema.

3. **Data Browsing Phase**
   - The viewer walks the B-Tree pages and renders row content in tabular form.
   - Each cell is decoded according to the column's declared `type`, `affinity`, and the record format header (`varint` length descriptors).

4. **Forensic Query Phase**
   - The investigator writes custom `SELECT` statements, often joining `sqlite_master` with data tables, using date functions, and searching `LIKE` patterns.

5. **Recovery & Carving Phase**
   - The viewer scans freelist pages, `cell_payload_overflow` regions, and the WAL to recover deleted records.

6. **Export & Hashing Phase**
   - Results are exported, and SHA-256 hashes are appended to a forensic report.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters Forensically |
|------|-----------------------------|
| Header parsing | Confirms the file is genuine SQLite and not a malicious decoy |
| Schema extraction | Reveals what kind of data the application chose to persist |
| Data browsing | Establishes what is currently live and accessible |
| Custom SQL queries | Allows hypothesis-driven investigation (e.g., time-range analysis) |
| Recovery | Recovers evidence the suspect believed to be destroyed |
| Export & hashing | Ensures the result is reproducible, auditable, and court-admissible |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations and metrics are standard forensic quantities evaluated by the SQLite Database Viewer engine.

| Quantity | Symbol / Expression | Description | Unit |
|----------|--------------------|------------|------|
| Database Page Size | $P_s$ | Default $4096$ bytes, range $512$ to $65536$ | bytes |
| Header Length | $L_h$ | $100$ bytes (fixed) | bytes |
| Number of Pages | $N_p = \left\lceil \dfrac{F_s}{P_s} \right\rceil$ | Total logical pages in file | pages |
| File Size | $F_s$ | Physical file size on disk | bytes |
| Reserved Space per Page | $R_{p}$ | Bytes at start of each page reserved (default $0$ to $100$) | bytes |
| Freeblock Threshold | $T_{fb} = 4$ | Min freeblock size to be tracked | bytes |
| Cell Payload Overflow Threshold | $U = \left\lfloor \dfrac{(P_s - R_{p})}{4} \right\rfloor$ | Max in-page payload | bytes |
| SQLite Date JDN Conversion | $JDN = 2440587.5 + \dfrac{epoch}{86400}$ | Julian Day Number from Unix epoch | days |
| SHA-256 Hash Output | $H(d) = \text{SHA-256}(d)$ | Integrity hash of database $d$ | 256 bits |
| VACUUM Recovery Ratio | $R_{vac} = \dfrac{P_{free}}{N_p}$ | Fraction of file in freelist | unitless |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

SQLite Database Viewers are indispensable in:

- **Mobile Forensics** — Recovering WhatsApp `msgstore.db`, call logs, SMS databases, browser history, GPS waypoints.
- **Incident Response** — Detecting malware persistence mechanisms that store C2 (Command & Control) data inside `.db` files in `%APPDATA%`, `/data/data/`, or `Library/Application Support/`.
- **E-Discovery & Litigation** — Producing defensible exports of user-generated content.
- **Cybercrime Investigations** — Decrypting application data vaults and password manager databases (e.g., KeePass, Bitwarden, iOS Keychain).
- **IoT Forensics** — Parsing SQLite files extracted from drones, smart home hubs, automotive infotainment systems, and wearable devices.

### 2.6 Critical Forensic SQL Queries (Cheat Reference)

```sql
-- 1. Enumerate every table and view
SELECT name, type, sql FROM sqlite_master;

-- 2. Inspect table columns and their types
PRAGMA table_info('messages');

-- 3. Find recently deleted records via the freelist
SELECT * FROM messages WHERE _id NOT IN (SELECT _id FROM messages);

-- 4. Convert SQLite epoch timestamps to human-readable
SELECT datetime(timestamp/1000, 'unixepoch') FROM messages;

-- 5. Check database integrity
PRAGMA integrity_check;

-- 6. List indices for performance review
SELECT * FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='index';
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip** — When asked to list "common forensic SQL queries," examiners expect at least three of the six above. Memorize the `PRAGMA` and `sqlite_master` patterns as they are **high-frequency exam topics**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — SQLite Header Field Decoding

The first 100 bytes of a SQLite database contain 24 distinct fields, each critical to forensic parsing. The investigator's task is to decode them byte-by-byte.

| Offset | Field | Size (bytes) | Meaning |
|--------|-------|--------------|---------|
| 0 | Magic Header String | 16 | Must equal `"SQLite format 3\000"` |
| 16 | Page Size | 2 | Big-endian unsigned integer; 1 = 65536 |
| 18 | File Format Write Version | 1 | Legacy 1, WAL 2 |
| 19 | File Format Read Version | 1 | Legacy 1, WAL 2 |
| 20 | Reserved Space per Page | 1 | Bytes at start of each page |
| 21 | Max Embedded Payload Fraction | 1 | Must be 64 |
| 22 | Min Embedded Payload Fraction | 1 | Must be 32 |
| 23 | Leaf Payload Fraction | 1 | Must be 32 |
| 24 | File Change Counter | 4 | Incremented on every commit |
| 28 | Database Size in Pages | 4 | Size of the database file in pages |
| 32 | First Freelist Trunk Page | 4 | Pointer to the head of the freelist |
| 36 | Total Freelist Pages | 4 | Count of freelist pages |
| 40 | Schema Cookie | 4 | Incremented on schema change |
| 44 | Schema Format Number | 4 | 1, 2, 3, or 4 |
| 48 | Default Page Cache Size | 4 | Suggested cache size |
| 52 | Largest Root B-Tree Page Number | 4 | Auto-vacuum/incremental-vacuum |
| 56 | Database Text Encoding | 4 | 1=UTF-8, 2=UTF-16le, 3=UTF-16be |
| 60 | User Version | 4 | User-defined version number |
| 64 | Incremental Vacuum Mode | 4 | Non-zero enables |
| 68 | Application ID | 4 | Set by application for branding |
| 72 | Reserved for Expansion | 20 | Must be zero |
| 92 | Version-Valid-For Number | 4 | Counter of last schema change |
| 96 | SQLite Version Number | 4 | Version of the library that wrote |

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Converting an SQLite Timestamp to a Human-Readable Date

SQLite databases commonly store timestamps in one of three formats: a **Unix epoch** in seconds, a **Unix epoch in milliseconds**, or a **Julian Day Number (JDN)**. A forensic examiner must fluently convert between them.

#### Case 1: Unix Epoch in Seconds → Human Date

Let $T_s$ be the timestamp in seconds. The conversion is:

$$
T_{\text{date}} = \text{date}(T_s, \text{'unixepoch'}, \text{'localtime'})
$$

**Worked Example** — Given $T_s = 1\,713\,456\,000$ (an Android SMS database timestamp from a WhatsApp `messages` table):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= 1{,}713{,}456{,}000 \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{date}} &= \text{date}(1{,}713{,}456{,}000, \text{'unixepoch'}, \text{'localtime'}) \\[4pt]
T_{\text{date}} &= \text{'2024-04-21 18:30:00'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The function `date()` interprets the integer as seconds since **1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC**.
2. The `'localtime'` modifier converts the result from UTC to the system's local time zone (forensically, this is the suspect's device time zone if known).
3. The output format is `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS`.

#### Case 2: Unix Epoch in Milliseconds → Human Date

Mobile platforms (Android, iOS) frequently store millisecond precision. The conversion divides by $1000$ before invoking `unixepoch`:

$$
T_{\text{date}} = \text{date}\!\left( \dfrac{T_{ms}}{1000},\ \text{'unixepoch'}\right)
$$

**Worked Example** — Given $T_{ms} = 1\,713\,456\,000\,000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{T_{ms}}{1000} &= 1{,}713{,}456{,}000 \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{date}} &= \text{'2024-04-21 18:30:00'}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The division by $1000$ is mandatory; otherwise, the `date()` function would interpret the value as seconds elapsed since epoch, producing a date in the year **52,464** — a classic forensic error.

#### Case 3: Julian Day Number → Gregorian Date

Forensic databases such as Mozilla Firefox `places.sqlite` (history) and Safari history use the JDN format. The conversion uses the baseline constant **$2440587.5$** (the JDN corresponding to **1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC**):

$$
T_{\text{date}} = \text{date}(JDN - 2440587.5)
$$

**Worked Example** — Given $JDN = 2\,460\,415.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
JDN - 2440587.5 &= 2{,}460{,}415.5 - 2{,}440{,}587.5 \\[4pt]
&= 19{,}828.0 \text{ days since epoch} \\[4pt]
\dfrac{19{,}828}{365.25} &\approx 54.28 \text{ years} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{date}} &= \text{'2024-04-21 00:00:00 UTC'}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the constant $2440587.5$?** It is the Julian Day Number for **January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 UTC** (the Unix epoch). Subtracting it from any JDN gives the number of days elapsed since the epoch, which SQLite's `date()` function accepts directly without a `'unixepoch'` modifier.

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — SQLite VACUUM and Recovery Ratio

The freelist recovery ratio $R_{vac}$ indicates how much of a SQLite file consists of pages that have been freed and are not currently in use. This is a strong forensic indicator of recently deleted bulk data.

$$
R_{vac} = \dfrac{P_{\text{free}}}{N_p}
$$

Where:

- $P_{\text{free}}$ is the count of pages recorded in the `Total Freelist Pages` field (offset 36, 4 bytes, big-endian).
- $N_p$ is the total page count from the `Database Size in Pages` field (offset 28, 4 bytes, big-endian).

**Worked Example** — Consider a forensic image of a WhatsApp `msgstore.db` with the following header values:

- Database Size in Pages: $N_p = 4096$
- Total Freelist Pages: $P_{\text{free}} = 1024$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{vac} &= \dfrac{1024}{4096} \\[4pt]
&= 0.25 \\[4pt]
&= 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A full $25\%$ of the file is composed of freelist pages. The investigator should immediately run a freelist carving tool (or query with `PRAGMA freelist_count;`) to recover potentially deleted messages, media, and contact records.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Forensic Artifact Reference Table

| Application | SQLite File Path | Forensic Significance |
|-------------|------------------|----------------------|
| WhatsApp | `/data/data/com.whatsapp/databases/msgstore.db` | Messages, contacts, calls |
| Chrome | `/data/data/com.android.chrome/app_chrome/Default/History` | URL visits, search history, downloads |
| Firefox | `places.sqlite`, `formhistory.sqlite` | Bookmarks, history, form fills |
| iOS Safari | `History.db` | Web navigation, top sites |
| iOS Messages | `sms.db` | SMS, iMessage, MMS metadata |
| Skype | `main.db` | Chat logs, file transfers, contacts |
| VLC | `vlc-qt-interface.conf` (SQLite on some builds) | Recently played media |
| Telegram | `cache4.db` | Cached media and messages |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — SQLite Database Viewer Forensic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Evidence Acquisition Bit Stream Image DD E01 AFF4] --> B[Hash Verification SHA 256]
    B --> C[Working Copy Creation Write Blocked]
    C --> D[SQLite File Detection Magic Header Validation]
    D --> E[Header Parsing 100 Byte Field Decode]
    E --> F[Schema Extraction sqlite_master]
    F --> G[Data Browsing B Tree Traversal]
    G --> H[Forensic SQL Query Layer]
    H --> I[Deleted Record Recovery WAL Freelist Carving]
    I --> J[BLOB Decoding Images Audio Tokens]
    J --> K[Report Generation HTML PDF CSV]
    K --> L[Chain of Custody Hash and Signature]
```

### 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Internal Layout of a SQLite Database File

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Page 1 Database Header 100 Bytes] --> P2[Page 2 Schema B Tree sqlite_master]
    P2 --> P3[Page 3 Table B Tree Users]
    P2 --> P4[Page 4 Table B Tree Messages]
    P3 --> P5[Overflow Pages for BLOB Data]
    P4 --> P5
    P1 --> F1[Freelist Linked List Trunk and Leaf Pages]
    P1 --> J1[Rollback Journal -journal Suffix]
    P1 --> W1[Write Ahead Log -wal Suffix]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Deleted Record Recovery via WAL and Freelist

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Open Database in Viewer] --> Q1{Query WAL File Exists}
    Q1 -- Yes --> WAL[Parse WAL Frames for Uncommitted Pages]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Query Freelist}
    WAL --> Rec1[Reconstruct In Flight Transactions]
    Rec1 --> Q2
    Q2 -- Pages Found --> FL[Walk Freelist Trunk Pages]
    FL --> Cv[Carve Cell Payloads]
    Cv --> Out[Append Recovered Records to Report]
    Q2 -- No Pages --> Out2[Database Shows No Deletable Records]
    Out --> End[Export to CSV HTML PDF]
    Out2 --> End
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Role of SQLite Viewer in the Investigative Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ID[Identification of SQLite Artifact] --> CO[Collection and Hashing]
    CO --> EX[Examination Using Viewer]
    EX --> AN[Analysis Querying and Interpretation]
    AN --> PR[Presentation in Court]
    EX --> DR[Deleted Data Recovery]
    DR --> AN
```

### 4.5 Component Relationship — Tools and Outputs of an SQLite Database Viewer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Viewer[SQLite Database Viewer] --> SQL[SQL Query Engine]
    Viewer --> SCH[Schema Browser]
    Viewer --> BLOB[BLOB Renderer]
    Viewer --> REC[Recovery Engine]
    Viewer --> EXP[Export Module]
    SQL --> Out1[Tabular Output]
    SCH --> Out2[DDL and ER Diagram]
    BLOB --> Out3[Decoded Attachments]
    REC --> Out4[Recovered Deleted Records]
    EXP --> Out5[CSV HTML PDF Excel Reports]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Order (for exam):** The examiner must demonstrate that they can (1) acquire, (2) hash, (3) parse, (4) browse, (5) query, (6) recover, and (7) report. Memorize this as the **"A-H-P-B-Q-R-R" pipeline** — it appears verbatim in KTU module question banks.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define an SQLite Database Viewer. List any four forensic objectives it fulfils.*

**Model Answer:**

An **SQLite Database Viewer** is a specialized forensic software utility that allows digital investigators to open, query, recover, and export the contents of SQLite database files in a **read-only, evidence-preserving** manner during a forensic investigation.

**Four forensic objectives:**

1. **Schema Visualization** — Reveals the full database architecture (tables, columns, indices, triggers) via the `sqlite_master` table.
2. **Live Data Browsing** — Tabular rendering of all currently active rows and columns.
3. **Deleted Record Recovery** — Carving of unallocated and freelist pages to recover erased data.
4. **BLOB Decoding** — Interpretation of binary attachments (images, audio, thumbnails) and tokens stored in fields.

*(Self-explanatory marking: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any four objectives.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Explain the role of the `sqlite_master` table in forensic SQLite analysis. Give its column structure.*

**Model Answer:**

The `sqlite_master` table is the **schema catalog** of any SQLite database. It stores the complete Data Definition Language (DDL) of every table, index, view, and trigger created by the application. Forensically, it is the **entry point** for understanding what kind of evidence resides in the database.

**Column Structure (5 columns):**

| Column | Type | Meaning |
|--------|------|---------|
| `type` | TEXT | Object kind: `table`, `index`, `view`, `trigger` |
| `name` | TEXT | Name of the object |
| `tbl_name` | TEXT | Table the object is associated with |
| `rootpage` | INTEGER | Root B-tree page number of the object |
| `sql` | TEXT | Original DDL statement that created the object |

**Forensic Use:** The investigator runs `SELECT name, sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';` to enumerate every user table and its creation statement — a standard step in every examination.

*(Marking: 1.5 marks for explanation, 1.5 marks for the column structure.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** *Explain the architecture of an SQLite database file with a neat block diagram. Discuss the significance of the **100-byte database header** in forensic parsing. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *With a worked example, derive the procedure to convert a Unix epoch timestamp stored in milliseconds inside a WhatsApp `msgstore.db` `messages` table to a human-readable date. State the constant used and explain why the division by 1000 is mandatory. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Architecture of an SQLite File:**

A SQLite database is physically a single disk file logically partitioned into equal-sized **pages** of default size **4096 bytes**. The architecture is layered as follows:

1. **Page 1 — Database Header Page** — Contains the 100-byte header and (in some versions) the root of the schema B-tree.
2. **B-Tree Pages** — Two varieties:
   - **Table B-Tree** leaf pages store the actual row records.
   - **Index B-Tree** pages accelerate query lookups.
3. **Freelist Pages** — A linked list of pages previously allocated but now unallocated; often contain **recoverable deleted data**.
4. **Overflow Pages** — Used to store BLOB or large TEXT fields that exceed the in-page payload limit.
5. **Auxiliary Files** — The **Write-Ahead Log (`.db-wal`)** and **Rollback Journal (`.db-journal`)** may exist alongside the main file.

**Significance of the 100-Byte Header:**

The 100-byte header is the **forensic signature block** of SQLite. Its forensic significance:

1. **Magic String Validation** — The first 16 bytes must equal `"SQLite format 3\000"`. If absent, the file is either corrupted, encrypted, or a decoy.
2. **Page Size Recovery** — Bytes 16–17 reveal $P_s$, allowing the examiner to compute $N_p$ via the file size.
3. **Encoding Detection** — Byte 56 identifies UTF-8 (1), UTF-16le (2), or UTF-16be (3), critical for proper text decoding.
4. **Schema Cookie** — Bytes 40–43 increment on every schema change; mismatches between cookie and `sqlite_master` indicate a corrupted or partially recovered database.
5. **Application ID** — Bytes 68–71 store an Application ID set by the software (e.g., WhatsApp, Firefox), enabling **artifact attribution**.

*(Marking: 3 marks for architecture explanation with diagram, 4 marks for the five header significances.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:** A WhatsApp `msgstore.db` row contains the column `timestamp` with value $T_{ms} = 1\,713\,456\,789\,012$ milliseconds.

**Required:** Convert to human-readable date and explain the role of the **2440587.5** constant.

**Step 1 — Identify the Storage Format**
The column type is `INTEGER`, and the value has 13 digits, indicating **millisecond precision**.

**Step 2 — Convert Milliseconds to Seconds**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= \dfrac{T_{ms}}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1{,}713{,}456{,}789{,}012}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}713{,}456{,}789.012 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the SQLite `datetime()` Function with `'unixepoch'` Modifier**

$$
T_{\text{date}} = \text{datetime}(T_s,\ \text{'unixepoch'},\ \text{'localtime'})
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{date}} &= \text{datetime}(1{,}713{,}456{,}789.012,\ \text{'unixepoch'},\ \text{'localtime'}) \\[4pt]
&= \text{'2024-04-21 18:33:09'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why division by 1000 is mandatory:**
The SQLite `datetime()` function, when used with the `'unixepoch'` modifier, interprets its argument as **seconds elapsed since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC**. If the millisecond value is passed directly, the function will treat it as a count of seconds, producing a date in the year **52,464** — a catastrophic analytical error. Dividing by 1000 normalizes the value to the unit the function expects.

**Role of the constant $2440587.5$:**
This is the **Julian Day Number (JDN)** corresponding to **1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC** — the Unix epoch. Some databases (notably Mozilla Firefox `places.sqlite` and iOS Safari `History.db`) store timestamps in JDN format. The conversion is:

$$
T_{\text{date}} = \text{date}(JDN - 2440587.5)
$$

The subtraction aligns the JDN to the Unix epoch baseline, after which `date()` returns a Gregorian calendar string.

*(Marking: 2 marks each for Step 1, Step 2, and Step 3; 1 mark for explaining the 1000 division; 1 mark for the 2440587.5 constant; 1 mark for forensic interpretation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** *Discuss the forensic procedure to recover deleted records from an SQLite database. Mention the role of the **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** and the **freelist**. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Compare and contrast three popular SQLite Database Viewer tools used in digital forensics. Highlight their licensing, primary use case, and a unique forensic feature. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Forensic Procedure to Recover Deleted Records from SQLite:**

1. **Acquire a Forensic Image** — Use a write-blocker and create a bit-stream copy. Compute SHA-256 of the original.
2. **Create a Working Copy** — All examinations occur on a duplicate; the original is sealed.
3. **Validate the Database** — Open in a forensic viewer; verify the 100-byte header magic string.
4. **Check for the WAL File** — If `<filename>-wal` exists, the database has uncommitted transactions. The WAL stores **modified pages not yet checkpointed** to the main file. Forensic recovery tools parse WAL frames to reconstruct in-flight deletions, edits, and inserts that the user believed were rolled back.
5. **Inspect the Freelist** — The freelist is a linked list of pages previously used but now unallocated. It is anchored by:
   - `First Freelist Trunk Page` (offset 32, 4 bytes).
   - `Total Freelist Pages` (offset 36, 4 bytes).
6. **Compute the Recovery Ratio** — $R_{vac} = P_{\text{free}} / N_p$. A ratio above $0.10$ (10%) warrants deep carving.
7. **Run Carving Queries** — Use the viewer's recovery engine or manual SQL like:

```sql
SELECT * FROM messages WHERE _id IS NULL;
```

8. **Inspect Overflow Pages and Cell Payload** — Even when rows are deleted, their cell payload may linger in the page until the page is reused.
9. **Export and Hash** — Save recovered records to CSV/PDF; compute a fresh hash for chain-of-custody.

**Role of the WAL:**
The WAL is a **forward log** of uncheckpointed page modifications. In the forensic context, it is invaluable because:

- It may contain the **most recent state of a record before deletion**.
- It allows reconstruction of **transactional intent** (e.g., the precise moment a message was deleted).
- It survives even after a `VACUUM` in some cases.

**Role of the Freelist:**
The freelist is a **ledger of abandoned pages**. Forensically:

- Pages on the freelist still contain their **old cell payloads** until reused.
- They are the **primary source of post-deletion recovery**.
- Tools like `MDF Sonar` and `SQLite Forensic Reporter` excel at walking the freelist in order.

*(Marking: 4 marks for procedure, 1.5 marks for WAL role, 1.5 marks for freelist role.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Comparison of Three SQLite Database Viewer Tools:**

| Feature | **DB Browser for SQLite (DB4S)** | **SQLite Forensic Reporter** | **Magnet AXIOM** |
|---------|----------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| **Licensing** | Open Source / GPL v3 | Commercial (Sanderson Forensics) | Commercial (Magnet Forensics) |
| **Primary Use Case** | General browsing, learning, light forensics | Court-grade SQLite forensic examination | Integrated mobile/cloud + SQLite analysis |
| **Unique Forensic Feature** | Manual SQL execution; cross-platform | Automated deleted-record carving; pre-built forensic reports; chain-of-custody hash export | Cross-artifact correlation with call logs, GPS, images, and chat apps in a single case file |
| **Target User** | Students, junior examiners | Professional forensic labs | Law enforcement, federal agencies |
| **Court Admissibility** | Limited (open-source) | High (validated, signed reports) | High (validated against NIST test sets) |

**Analytical Commentary:**

- **DB4S** is the educational workhorse — its GPL license and intuitive GUI make it the most common tool taught in KTU labs. However, it lacks automated deleted-record recovery and is rarely accepted as the sole tool in court.
- **SQLite Forensic Reporter** is the **gold standard** for dedicated SQLite forensics. Its automated carving of freelist pages, WAL parsing, and tamper-evident PDF reports make it the tool of choice when a case hinges on recovered deleted messages.
- **Magnet AXIOM** offers the **broadest investigative context** — it does not stop at SQLite. It correlates SQLite artifacts with image EXIF, mobile call detail records, and cloud downloads, presenting a unified timeline. This is critical in modern investigations where evidence is dispersed across dozens of databases.

**Strategic Recommendation for KTU Examination Answer:**
If asked to recommend a tool, answer:
> *"For academic study and preliminary triage, **DB Browser for SQLite** suffices. For court-grade forensic reporting and deleted-record recovery, **SQLite Forensic Reporter** is the industry benchmark. For multi-artifact correlation in a complex case, **Magnet AXIOM** provides the most comprehensive workflow."*

*(Marking: 2 marks each for the three tool descriptions; 1 mark for the strategic recommendation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing WAL and Journal** — Students frequently interchange the Write-Ahead Log with the Rollback Journal. Remember: **WAL stores the new state; Journal stores the old state**. They are mutually exclusive modes.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1000 division** — When asked to convert a WhatsApp timestamp, students pass the millisecond value directly to `datetime()`. The examiner awards **zero marks** for the conversion step.
> 3. **Ignoring the magic string** — A SQLite viewer must always verify `"SQLite format 3\000"` before parsing. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 4. **Writing to the original** — Suggesting any modification to the original `.db` file during examination is an **automatic 2-mark penalty** under the forensic-immutability principle.
> 5. **Forgetting the `localtime` modifier** — For timestamps, always include `'localtime'` in your query; otherwise, the result is in UTC, which may not match the suspect's device clock.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SQLite Database Viewer (Module 3)**

- [ ] An **SQLite Database Viewer** is a forensic tool for read-only, evidence-preserving examination of `.db`, `.sqlite`, `.sqlite3` files.
- [ ] SQLite is **serverless, zero-configuration, transactional** — and the most widely deployed database engine in the world.
- [ ] Default **page size = 4096 bytes**; range **512–65536**; header length **100 bytes**.
- [ ] The **magic string** at offset 0 must equal `"SQLite format 3\000"` (16 bytes).
- [ ] The **`sqlite_master`** table enumerates all schema objects (tables, indices, views, triggers).
- [ ] The **WAL** (`-wal`) and **Journal** (`-journal`) are auxiliary files storing uncommitted/old state.
- [ ] The **freelist** is the primary source of **deleted-record recovery**.
- [ ] The constant **$2440587.5$** is the JDN for the Unix epoch; used for Firefox and Safari timestamp conversion.
- [ ] **WhatsApp** uses millisecond-precision Unix epochs; **divide by 1000** before calling `datetime()`.
- [ ] Always operate on a **forensically verified working copy**; never write to the original.
- [ ] Common tools: **DB Browser for SQLite (open-source)**, **SQLite Forensic Reporter (commercial)**, **Magnet AXIOM (commercial)**.
- [ ] Forensic SQL queries: `SELECT * FROM sqlite_master;`, `PRAGMA table_info(...);`, `PRAGMA integrity_check;`, `PRAGMA freelist_count;`.
- [ ] The recovery ratio $R_{vac} = P_{\text{free}} / N_p$ indicates the proportion of the file available for carving.
- [ ] Hash the original AND the working copy with **SHA-256**; document both in the chain-of-custody.
- [ ] The five-step forensic pipeline: **Acquire → Hash → Parse → Query → Recover → Report**.
- [ ] Always include the `'localtime'` modifier in timestamp conversions; otherwise the result is in UTC.
- [ ] The **Application ID** (header offset 68) can attribute a database to a specific software (e.g., WhatsApp, Firefox).

> **End of Module 3 — SQLite Database Viewer Notes**
> *(Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme PECST754 — Digital Forensics, Outcome-Based Education Format)*

---
