topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wireshark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔍 Wireshark — The Forensic Microscope of Network Traffic

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Wireshark** is a free, open-source **network packet analyzer** (formerly known as *Ethereal*) that captures, decodes, and displays packet-level data traversing a computer network in real-time. In the context of **Digital Forensics**, Wireshark functions as a non-intrusive, passive *network forensic tool* used to acquire volatile network evidence, reconstruct communication sessions, detect anomalies, and generate legally admissible packet-level artifacts during post-incident investigations.

It operates on the principle of **libpcap** (Linux) / **WinPcap / Npcap** (Windows) — the underlying packet capture library — and supports deep inspection of hundreds of protocols across all seven layers of the **OSI Reference Model**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Network X-Ray Machine"

Imagine you are a **forensic pathologist** performing an autopsy on a human body. You cannot see the bloodstream directly, but you attach monitors and sensors that record every heartbeat, every blood cell, and every chemical reaction in real-time. 

Wireshark does the **exact same thing for a network**:

| Forensics Domain | Network Domain (Wireshark) |
|---|---|
| Human Body | Computer Network |
| Heartbeat Monitor | Packet Capture Engine (libpcap) |
| Blood Sample | Individual Network Packet |
| Patient's Medical Record | PCAP-NG Capture File |
| Microscope Lens | Protocol Decoder / Dissector |
| Diagnosis Report | Filtered Statistics & I/O Graph |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Three Golden Properties of Wireshark:**  
> 1. **Passive** — It only *listens*; it does not generate or modify traffic.  
> 2. **Deep Inspection** — Decodes from Layer 1 (bits) up to Layer 7 (application payload).  
> 3. **Cross-Platform** — Runs natively on Windows, Linux, macOS, BSD, Solaris.

---

## 1.3 Why Wireshark is Indispensable in Digital Forensics

In **PECST754 — Digital Forensics**, Wireshark is positioned as the **primary network forensic acquisition and analysis utility** because it:

- Captures **volatile evidence** (RAM-resident sessions) before it disappears.
- Reveals **encrypted vs. plaintext** traffic patterns.
- Detects **exfiltration**, **C2 beaconing**, **lateral movement**, and **DNS tunneling**.
- Produces **standard PCAP files** that are court-admissible and universally readable.

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU 2024 Pacing Note:** Examiners in this module repeatedly stress the difference between **active sniffing** (ARP poisoning, MITM) and **passive sniffing** (Wireshark default mode). Always mention *passive capture* first unless explicitly asked otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Architecture, Workflow & Filter Logic

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of Wireshark

Wireshark's architecture is modular and follows a strict pipeline. Understanding this is **mandatory** for KTU Module 4 questions.

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow:

1. **Packet Capture Source (libpcap / Npcap)**  
   The capture engine hands raw bytes from the NIC to Wireshark via a standard interface. The NIC must be placed into **promiscuous mode** to capture all frames on the segment (not just those addressed to it).

2. **Capture Filter (BPF Syntax)**  
   *Berkeley Packet Filter* expressions are applied **at the kernel/driver level** to drop unwanted traffic *before* it reaches Wireshark. Example: `tcp port 80`.

3. **Packet Buffering**  
   Surviving frames are buffered in a circular memory buffer. This is **volatile** — if the buffer fills, the oldest packets are overwritten.

4. **Dissection Engine**  
   Each frame is passed through a chain of **dissectors** — modular plugins (one per protocol) — that decode the bytes into the familiar hierarchical tree.

5. **Display Filter Engine**  
   A *post-capture* filter applied to the in-memory copy. Syntax is far richer than BPF. Example: `http.request.method == "POST"`.

6. **Visualization & Export**  
   The dissected packet is rendered in the three-pane GUI (Packet List / Packet Details / Packet Bytes) and can be exported to `.pcap`, `.pcapng`, CSV, or PSML/XML.

---

## 2.2 The KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Below is the **high-yield reference matrix** that examiners expect you to memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE.

| Construct | Syntax Family | Performance Scope | Example | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capture Filter** | BPF (kernel) | Reduces file size, drops early | `host 192.168.1.5` | Save disk on high-volume links |
| **Display Filter** | Wireshark-specific | Operates on loaded capture | `tcp.flags.syn == 1` | Drill into a saved file |
| **Follow Stream** | Action menu | Reconstructs session | `tcp.stream eq 4` | Read entire HTTP/FTP chat |
| **IO Graph** | Statistics menu | Time-series plot | Y-axis = packets/sec | Detect traffic bursts |
| **Protocol Hierarchy** | Statistics menu | % by protocol/bytes | Auto-generated | Spot rogue protocols |

### 2.2.1 The Three-Pane Display Layout

$$
\text{Wireshark UI} = \underbrace{\text{Packet List Pane}}_{\text{flat timeline}} \;\;+\;\; \underbrace{\text{Packet Details Pane}}_{\text{protocol tree}} \;\;+\;\; \underbrace{\text{Packet Bytes Pane}}_{\text{raw hex + ASCII}}
$$

### 2.2.2 Promiscuous Mode Equation (Conceptual)

A NIC in normal mode only accepts frames whose destination MAC matches its own. In promiscuous mode:

$$
\text{Frame Acceptance} = \{ F \mid \text{dst}(F) \in \{ \text{MyMAC},\ \text{FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF},\ \text{Multicast} \} \} \;\cup\; \{ F \mid \text{PromiscuousMode} = \text{TRUE} \}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> On **switched networks**, promiscuous mode alone is insufficient. The investigator must use **port mirroring (SPAN)**, **network taps**, or **ARP spoofing** (active methodology) to redirect traffic to the forensic workstation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Wireshark Application |
|---|---|
| **Incident Response** | Reconstruct malware C2 traffic over HTTPS |
| **Lawful Interception** | Provide PCAP as court evidence |
| **Penetration Testing** | Validate that exfiltration payloads leave the network |
| **Troubleshooting** | Pinpoint TCP retransmissions, DNS latency |
| **Academic Labs (KTU)** | Practicals on packet dissection, MITM detection |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Legal Pitfall (exam-favourite):** Capturing traffic on a network you do not own or administer may violate the **IT Act 2000 (India)** and equivalent global statutes. Always obtain written authorization before live capture.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Packet Analysis Methodology

## 3.1 The KTU Forensic Analysis Pipeline (Exhaustive)

When a KTU question asks *"How do you use Wireshark to investigate a network incident?"*, examiners expect the following **6-step methodology** with no skipped steps.

### Step 1 — Authorize & Scope
Define the investigation scope, time window, and legal authority.

### Step 2 — Position the Capture Point
Decide between (a) **SPAN port**, (b) **network tap**, or (c) **endpoint NIC**.

### Step 3 — Initiate Capture with Filters
Start the Wireshark capture with a **BPF capture filter** to reduce noise:

$$
\texttt{tcp\ or\ udp\ and\ not\ port\ 22}
$$

This excludes SSH noise on a typical investigation box.

### Step 4 — Capture & Save in PCAP-NG Format
Save the live capture as `.pcapng` (preferred over legacy `.pcap` because it supports multiple interfaces, comments, and name-resolution blocks).

### Step 5 — Apply Display Filters Iteratively

Begin broad, then narrow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Layer 1: } & \texttt{tcp} \\
\text{Layer 2: } & \texttt{tcp\ \&\&\ ip.addr == 10.0.0.5} \\
\text{Layer 3: } & \texttt{http.request.uri\ contains\ "login"} \\
\text{Layer 4: } & \texttt{tcp.stream eq 12} \quad \text{(follow full session)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Export & Document Evidence
Export the filtered packets using **File → Export Specified Packets**. Generate a SHA-256 hash for chain-of-custody:

$$
H_{\text{evidence}} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{PCAP-NG file bytes})
$$

Record $H_{\text{evidence}}$ in the case log.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Detecting a Suspected DNS Exfiltration

> [!NOTE]  
> **Scenario (frequently given in KTU 14-mark questions):**  
> An attacker is suspected of exfiltrating data via DNS TXT queries to `evil.example.com`. Wireshark has captured 50,000 packets over 10 minutes.

**Step A — Statistical Triage**

Open *Statistics → Conversations* and *Protocol Hierarchy*. You should observe a disproportionately high **DNS byte count** relative to HTTP/HTTPS.

**Step B — Apply Targeted Display Filter**

$$
\texttt{dns.qry.name\ contains\ "evil.example.com"}
$$

This filters only queries destined for the rogue domain.

**Step C — Examine Packet Details Pane**

Expand the `Domain Name System` tree. Note:
- `Queries` field shows long, randomized subdomains (e.g., `aGVsbG8.d29ybGQ.evil.example.com`).
- `Type: TXT` queries with high entropy payloads.

**Step D — Reconstruct Exfiltrated Data**

Extract each subdomain label (Base32/Base64 encoded):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \text{aGVsbG8} \rightarrow \text{Base64-decode} \rightarrow \texttt{"hello"} \\
D_2 &= \text{d29ybGQ} \rightarrow \text{Base64-decode} \rightarrow \texttt{"world"}
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenate the decoded labels in order of their `Transaction ID`:

$$
\text{Exfiltrated} = \text{Concatenate}(D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n)
$$

**Step E — Follow UDP Stream**

Right-click → *Follow → UDP Stream* to view the entire DNS conversation in linear form.

**Step F — Hash & Export**

Export the filtered set, compute SHA-256, and attach to the forensic case report.

---

## 3.3 Quantitative Throughput Check (Examiner Favourite)

Suppose Wireshark reports a captured file of size $S$ bytes containing $N$ packets, captured over $T$ seconds. The **average bit-rate** is:

$$
R = \frac{S \times 8}{T} \quad [\text{bits per second}]
$$

**Numerical sanity-check** (often asked as a 3-mark sub-part):

Given $S = 250 \,\text{MB}$, $T = 100 \,\text{s}$:

$$
R = \frac{250 \times 10^{6} \times 8}{100} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{9}}{100} = 2.0 \times 10^{7} \,\text{bits/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 20 \,\text{Mbps}}
$$

> [!TIP]  
> Always carry units through every step. Examiners deduct marks if the final number lacks units or dimension.

---

## 3.4 Logical Dissection of a Sample TCP SYN Packet

For each packet, Wireshark dissects the bytes top-down:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ethernet Header} &: 14 \text{ bytes (dst MAC \vert src MAC \vert EtherType)} \\
\text{IP Header} &: 20\text{-}60 \text{ bytes (IHL \vert Total Length \vert TTL \vert Protocol)} \\
\text{TCP Header} &: 20\text{-}60 \text{ bytes (Src Port \vert Dst Port \vert Seq \vert Flags)} \\
\text{Payload} &: \text{variable} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The **TCP Flags byte** at offset 13 inside the TCP header is parsed as:

$$
\text{Flags} = C\ E\ U\ A\ P\ R\ S\ F
$$

where a typical SYN-only packet shows:

$$
\text{Flags} = 0\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 1\ 0 \implies \text{SYN set, ACK clear}
$$

The corresponding Wireshark display filter is therefore:

$$
\texttt{tcp.flags.syn == 1\ \&\&\ tcp.flags.ack == 0}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Wireshark Forensic Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Network Under Investigation] --> B1[Network TAP or SPAN Port]
    A0 --> B2[Endpoint NIC in Promiscuous Mode]
    B1 --> C1[libpcap / Npcap Capture Engine]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[BPF Capture Filter]
    D1 --> E1[In-Memory Packet Buffer]
    E1 --> F1[Dissection Engine - Protocol Decoders]
    F1 --> G1[Display Filter Engine]
    G1 --> H1[Three Pane GUI View]
    H1 --> I1[Statistics - IO Graph - Conversations]
    H1 --> J1[Follow Stream Reconstruction]
    I1 --> K1[PCAP NG Export with SHA 256 Hash]
    J1 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Chain of Custody Case File]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Six-Step Forensic Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Authorize and Scope] --> S2[Step 2 Position Capture Point]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Apply BPF Capture Filter]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Capture to PCAP NG]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Iterative Display Filtering]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Export Hash and Document]
    S6 --> R1[Admissible Forensic Artifact]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Three-Pane GUI Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PCAP[Loaded PCAP File] --> PL[Pane 1 Packet List]
    PCAP --> PD[Pane 2 Packet Details Protocol Tree]
    PCAP --> PB[Pane 3 Packet Bytes Hex and ASCII]
    PD -->|Click on Layer| PB
    PL -->|Click on Row| PD
    PL -->|Right Click| ACT[Follow Stream Apply as Filter Export]
    ACT --> PD
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Capture vs. Display Filter Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need to reduce file size at capture time?} -->|YES| A1[Use BPF Capture Filter in Capture Options]
    Q1 -->|NO| A2[Capture Everything then Filter Later]
    A2 --> Q2{Ad-hoc investigation of saved file?} -->|YES| B1[Use Display Filter in Toolbar]
    Q2 -->|NO| B2[Run Statistics and IO Graph]
    A1 --> C1[Example tcp port 443]
    B1 --> C2[Example http.request.uri contains admin]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
*List any three distinguishing features of Wireshark that make it suitable for digital forensic investigations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Passive deep-packet inspection** — Captures and decodes traffic without injecting packets, preserving evidentiary integrity.
2. **Cross-platform portability** — Runs on Windows, Linux, macOS, allowing acquisition in heterogeneous environments.
3. **Standard PCAP-NG output** — Generates portable capture files readable by *tcpdump*, *NetworkMiner*, and courtroom evidence systems, supporting chain-of-custody requirements.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
*Differentiate between a **Capture Filter** and a **Display Filter** in Wireshark.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Capture Filter | Display Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Syntax family | BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) | Wireshark-proprietary |
| Stage applied | Kernel/driver level | Post-capture on loaded file |
| Effect | Drops packets *before* they enter Wireshark | Hides packets *after* capture |
| Mutability | Set *before* capture starts | Can be changed any time |
| Example | `host 192.168.1.10` | `ip.addr == 192.168.1.10 && http` |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### ❓ Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**  
**(a)** Explain the internal architecture of Wireshark with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **dissection engine** and **promiscuous mode** in forensic acquisition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wireshark capture file of size **400 MB** was recorded over a span of **200 seconds** on a corporate LAN during an exfiltration incident.  
    (i) Compute the average capture bit-rate in **Mbps**.  
    (ii) If 70 % of the bytes belong to HTTPS traffic on TCP port 443, estimate the **average HTTPS throughput** in Mbps.  
    (iii) Briefly justify why this throughput figure is *forensically significant*. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

Wireshark's architecture is a **layered pipeline** (draw the block diagram first; KTU expects this):

**Layer 1 — Capture Source:** *libpcap* (Linux/BSD) or *Npcap/WinPcap* (Windows) interfaces the NIC with Wireshark. The NIC must be set to **promiscuous mode** so that every frame on the collision domain, regardless of destination MAC, is delivered to the kernel buffer.

**Layer 2 — Capture Filter (BPF):** Filters expressed in BPF syntax are compiled into a small in-kernel program that decides whether each arriving frame is kept or discarded at the driver level. This *reduces disk consumption* and *protects investigator privacy* by excluding unrelated traffic.

**Layer 3 — Dissection Engine:** This is the analytical heart of Wireshark. As each frame is read, a **chain of dissectors** (one per protocol) is invoked. The Ethernet dissector hands off the IP payload to the IP dissector, which in turn hands off the TCP segment to the TCP dissector, and so on up to the application layer. The dissectors populate the protocol tree shown in the Packet Details pane.

**Layer 4 — Display Filter Engine:** Applies Wireshark's powerful display-filter language against the already-dissected packets. The engine evaluates expressions such as `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && ip.ttl < 10` against the in-memory packet metadata.

**Layer 5 — Visualization & Export:** The three-pane GUI plus statistics modules (IO graph, conversations, flow graph, protocol hierarchy) provide the investigator with multi-angle views. Files may be exported as `.pcap`, `.pcapng`, CSV, or PSML.

> **Role of Promiscuous Mode:** In a **shared Ethernet hub** or a **SPAN-mirrored switch port**, promiscuous mode is essential; without it the NIC discards frames whose destination MAC does not match its own, blinding the investigator.

> **Role of Dissection Engine:** It transforms raw bytes into **human-readable forensic meaning**, which is the single most important step in converting "data" into "evidence".

*(Award 7 marks for: 1 mark diagram, 2 marks layer-by-layer explanation, 2 marks dissection engine elaboration, 2 marks promiscuous-mode forensic relevance.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Throughput Computation (7 marks)

**(i) Average capture bit-rate:**

$$
R = \frac{S \times 8}{T}
$$

Substitute $S = 400 \times 10^{6}$ bytes and $T = 200$ s:

$$
R = \frac{400 \times 10^{6} \times 8}{200}
$$

$$
R = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{9}}{200}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 1.6 \times 10^{7} \;\text{bits/s} = 16 \,\text{Mbps}}
$$

**(ii) HTTPS throughput (70 % of bytes):**

$$
R_{\text{HTTPS}} = 0.70 \times 16 \,\text{Mbps}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{HTTPS}} = 11.2 \,\text{Mbps}}
$$

**(iii) Forensic significance:**

A sustained HTTPS throughput of 11.2 Mbps from a single endpoint over 200 seconds (≈ **280 MB transferred**) is anomalous for typical user browsing. It indicates **bulk data movement**, a strong indicator of **data exfiltration** to an attacker-controlled cloud endpoint. The investigator should now correlate this finding with endpoint logs and DLP alerts.

---

### ❓ Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**  
**(a)** Discuss any **four commonly used Wireshark display filters** with examples and explain how each contributes to forensic triage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable packet-flow diagram, explain how an investigator would use Wireshark to **reconstruct a full HTTP session** between a victim browser (`192.168.1.10`) and a web server (`203.0.113.45`). Mention the exact menu options, filters, and follow-up actions required. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Four Display Filters (7 marks)

1. **`ip.addr == 192.168.1.10`** — Filters traffic to or from a specific host.  
   *Forensic triage:* Isolates the victim's traffic for host-centric analysis.

2. **`http.request.method == "POST"`** — Locates upload transactions.  
   *Forensic triage:* Highlights form submissions and possible data exfiltration through HTTP POST bodies.

3. **`dns.qry.name contains "evil.com"`** — Filters DNS queries to a suspect domain.  
   *Forensic triage:* Identifies DNS-tunneling or C2 beaconing.

4. **`tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0`** — Selects only TCP SYN packets.  
   *Forensic triage:* Counts **half-open scans** indicating reconnaissance activity from an attacker.

*(Award 7 marks: ~1.5 marks per filter for syntax, example, and forensic rationale, plus 1 mark for triage synthesis.)*

---

#### Part (b) — HTTP Session Reconstruction (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Initial capture:** Start a live capture on the investigator's NIC (SPAN port if possible).

**Step 2 — Apply the host-pair filter:**

$$
\texttt{ip.addr == 192.168.1.10 \;\&\&\; ip.addr == 203.0.113.45 \;\&\&\; http}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the TCP stream:**  
Locate the *first* SYN packet between the two hosts. Note the TCP stream index from the Packet Details pane (e.g., `TCP Stream → Stream: 7`).

**Step 4 — Follow the stream:**  
Right-click any packet → *Follow → HTTP Stream*. Wireshark assembles every segment of that stream into a single reconstructed dialogue, distinguishing client (`red`) and server (`blue`) payloads.

**Step 5 — Inspect reconstructed content:**  
Examine request URIs, headers (`User-Agent`, `Cookie`, `Authorization`), and response bodies. Look for embedded credentials, session tokens, or exfiltrated files.

**Step 6 — Export & preserve:**  
*File → Export Objects → HTTP* saves each transferred object (images, JS, ZIP, PDFs) as a separate file. Compute SHA-256 of the entire capture for chain-of-custody.

**Packet-flow diagram (textual, draw on answer sheet):**

```
Client 192.168.1.10                Server 203.0.113.45
        |-------- SYN seq=x --------->|
        |<------- SYN+ACK seq=y ------>|
        |-------- ACK seq=x+1 ------->|
        |-------- GET /index.html --->|
        |<------- HTTP/1.1 200 OK ----|
        |<------- <html>...</html> ---|
        |-------- GET /logo.png ------>|
        |<------- PNG bytes ----------|
        |-------- FIN --------------->|
        |<------- FIN+ACK ------------|
```

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 4):**
> 1. **Confusing capture and display filters** — they are *not* interchangeable. KTU deducts 1–2 marks if you state that a display filter reduces file size.
> 2. **Forgetting promiscuous-mode limitations on switches** — passive sniffing on a switched port *without* SPAN or tap yields almost no traffic. Always clarify the network topology.
> 3. **Skipping chain-of-custody** — every exported artifact must be SHA-256 hashed; omitting this costs the legal-evidence mark.
> 4. **Unit errors in throughput calculations** — write `Mbps` and `MB` correctly; do not interchange bits and bytes.
> 5. **Ignoring legal authorization** — even a perfectly captured PCAP is inadmissible if obtained without proper written authority.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Wireshark = passive, open-source packet analyzer** built on **libpcap / Npcap**.
- Works across **OSI Layers 1–7** using a chain of modular **dissectors**.
- **Promiscuous mode** is required; on switches, additionally use **SPAN** or **network TAPs**.
- **Capture filters (BPF)** drop traffic *before* Wireshark; **display filters** hide it *after*.
- The **GUI** has three panes: Packet List, Packet Details, Packet Bytes.
- **Follow Stream** reconstructs TCP/UDP/HTTP/TLS sessions in linear form.
- **Statistics menu** offers Protocol Hierarchy, Conversations, IO Graph, Endpoints.
- **Export format** of choice is **PCAP-NG**; always compute **SHA-256 hash**.
- Forensically, use Wireshark to detect **DNS tunneling, C2 beaconing, exfiltration, scanning, and MITM artifacts**.
- **Legal authorization** is a *prerequisite*; unauthorized sniffing violates the IT Act and equivalent statutes.
- Three memorizable filter idioms: `http.request.method`, `dns.qry.name`, `tcp.flags.syn`.
- Throughput formula: $R = \dfrac{S \times 8}{T}$ — always carry units to the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Apache Log Viewer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST754 - DIGITAL FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Apache Log Viewer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Apache Log Viewer** is a specialized, freeware, graphical log analysis utility designed to parse, view, filter, search, and report on **Apache HTTP Server** log files (and other compatible web server log formats such as IIS, Nginx, and W3C). It is widely employed in **digital forensics investigations** to reconstruct user activity, detect web-based attacks (e.g., SQL injection, XSS, path traversal), and establish **evidentiary timelines** from web server access and error logs.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Apache Log Viewer is studied under **Module 4 – Log Analysis and Investigation Tools** as a critical component of **Web Forensics** and **Network Forensics** workflows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Apache Log Viewer enables investigators to convert raw, multi-megabyte ASCII log files into structured, queryable, and reportable datasets without requiring command-line parsing skills (e.g., `awk`, `grep`, `sed`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive, dusty library ledger** that records every single visitor — when they entered, which shelf they browsed, what they borrowed, and whether they caused any disturbance. The ledger is millions of lines long and nearly impossible to read manually. Now imagine a **forensic accountant's magnifying desk** — a tool with high-powered lenses, color-coded filters, sorting trays, and a summary printer. You tell the desk: *"Show me everyone who entered after 11 PM and touched the rare-books section."* Instantly, the desk highlights those entries.

**Apache Log Viewer is that magnifying desk for the Apache web server ledger.**

- The **library** = Apache Web Server
- The **ledger** = `access.log` and `error.log` files
- The **forensic accountant** = Digital Forensic Investigator
- The **magnifying desk** = Apache Log Viewer software

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> **Log File** → A timestamped text record of events generated by a server, application, or device.
> **Access Log** → Records every HTTP request received by the web server.
> **Error Log** → Records diagnostic information, warnings, and errors.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Apache Log Analysis

| Metric | Standard Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Port** | **TCP Port 80 (HTTP)** / **443 (HTTPS)** | Port on which Apache listens by default |
| **Common Log Format (CLF)** | **NCSA Standard** | Log format with remotehost, rfc931, authuser, date, request, status, bytes |
| **Combined Log Format** | CLF + Referer + User-Agent | Extended format used by most modern Apache installations |
| **Default Log Path (Linux)** | `/var/log/apache2/` (Debian) or `/var/log/httpd/` (RHEL) | Standard location of Apache log files |
| **Log Rotation** | **logrotate** utility | Linux tool that rotates, compresses, and mails logs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Apache Log File Formats — Structured Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Common Log Format (CLF)

This is the **original NCSA (National Center for Supercomputing Applications) format** used by early HTTP daemons. Each line represents one HTTP request.

**Structure of a CLF entry:**

```
remotehost rfc931 authuser [date:time timezone] "request" status bytes
```

**Example:**
```
127.0.0.1 - frank [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36 -0700] "GET /apache_pb.gif HTTP/1.0" 200 2326
```

**Field-by-Field Explanation:**

| Field | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `remotehost` | IP address or hostname of the client | `127.0.0.1` |
| `rfc931` | Remote logname of the user (usually `-`) | `-` |
| `authuser` | Authenticated username (if Basic Auth used) | `frank` |
| `[date:time timezone]` | Timestamp of request | `[10/Oct/2024:13:55:36 -0700]` |
| `"request"` | HTTP method, URI, protocol version | `"GET /index.html HTTP/1.1"` |
| `status` | HTTP response code returned | `200` |
| `bytes` | Size of response in bytes | `2326` |

### 2.1.2 Combined Log Format (CLF + 2 fields)

The **de facto modern standard**, formed by appending two additional fields to CLF:

```
remotehost rfc931 authuser [date:time timezone] "request" status bytes "referer" "user-agent"
```

**Example:**
```
192.168.1.50 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:00:12 +0000] "GET /login.php HTTP/1.1" 200 1532 "http://example.com/" "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36"
```

### 2.1.3 Error Log Format

```
[Sun Oct 10 14:00:12 2024] [error] [client 192.168.1.50] File does not exist: /var/www/html/admin
```

**Key components:**
- Timestamp in standard `Day Mon DD HH:MM:SS YYYY` format
- Log level (`debug`, `info`, `notice`, `warn`, `error`, `crit`, `alert`, `emerg`)
- Client IP address
- Descriptive error message

## 2.2 Apache Log Viewer — Key Features

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** These features are the most frequently asked in 3-mark questions.

### 2.2.1 Multi-Format Support
Apache Log Viewer supports parsing of:
- **Apache** Common Log Format
- **Apache** Combined Log Format
- **IIS** (Internet Information Services) W3C format
- **Nginx** default format
- **W3C Extended** format

### 2.2.2 Filtering and Search
The tool provides a **powerful filter engine** that allows investigators to apply Boolean expressions to log fields. Filters can be **stacked** (AND/OR logic) and **saved as reusable templates**.

**Common filter patterns in forensic investigations:**

| Filter Goal | Filter Expression |
|---|---|
| Find all 404 errors | `Status = 404` |
| Suspicious POST requests | `Method = POST` |
| Access from a specific IP | `IP Address = 192.168.1.50` |
| Requests to a sensitive file | `URL CONTAINS "/admin"` |
| Time-window analysis | `Date BETWEEN 2024-10-10 14:00 AND 2024-10-10 15:00` |
| SQL injection attempts | `URL CONTAINS "'" OR URL CONTAINS "union select"` |

### 2.2.3 Statistics and Reports
Generates **summary statistics** including:
- Total requests
- Unique visitors (by IP)
- Bandwidth usage
- Top requested URLs
- HTTP status code distribution
- Referrer statistics
- Browser/OS breakdown (from User-Agent parsing)

### 2.2.4 Export and Conversion
Logs can be exported to:
- **CSV** (Comma-Separated Values) — for spreadsheet analysis
- **TXT** (plain text)
- **HTML** (formatted report)
- **XML** (structured for other forensic tools)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Apache Log Viewer operates on **discrete event data** rather than continuous physical quantities, the "formulas" here are **analytical expressions** for log analysis, not physical equations.

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP Request Rate | $$R = \frac{N_{\text{requests}}}{\Delta t}$$ | Requests per second/minute/hour |
| Error Ratio | $$E_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{N_{\text{4xx}} + N_{\text{5xx}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$$ | Fraction of failed responses |
| Bandwidth per IP | $$B_{\text{ip}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{bytes}_i \text{ where } \text{IP}_i = \text{target}$$ | Total bytes served to a client |
| Average Response Size | $$\bar{S} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{bytes}_i}{n}$$ | Mean bytes per request |
| Status Code Class | $$\text{Class} = \lfloor \text{Status} / 100 \rfloor$$ | 1xx→Info, 2xx→Success, 3xx→Redirect, 4xx→Client Err, 5xx→Server Err |
| Unique Visitor Count | $$U = \vert \{ \text{IP} \mid \text{IP} \in \text{log} \} \vert$$ | Distinct client IPs (using set cardinality) |
| Time Delta Between Events | $$\Delta t_{i,j} = t_j - t_i \quad \text{where } j > i$$ | Gap between two log entries |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **Incident Response (IR)**, the error ratio $E_{\text{ratio}}$ exceeding **5%** is a strong indicator of reconnaissance or attack activity (e.g., vulnerability scanning). In **Capacity Planning**, request rate $R$ drives server scaling decisions.

## 2.4 HTTP Status Code Reference (High-Yield)

| Code | Class | Meaning | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **200** | 2xx | OK | Successful access — establishes baseline |
| **301** | 3xx | Moved Permanently | URL redirection (legitimate or evasion) |
| **302** | 3xx | Found | Temporary redirect (often used in phishing) |
| **400** | 4xx | Bad Request | Malformed payload — possible fuzzing |
| **401** | 4xx | Unauthorized | Brute-force indicator |
| **403** | 4xx | Forbidden | Access denied to restricted resource |
| **404** | 4xx | Not Found | Reconnaissance for hidden files |
| **500** | 5xx | Internal Server Error | Possible exploitation success |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Forensic Analysis of a Suspicious IP Address

> **Scenario (KTU-style problem):** A forensic investigator is given a 1,000-line Apache `access.log`. They are asked to identify all suspicious activity originating from IP address `203.0.113.45` between `2024-10-10 14:00` and `2024-10-10 16:00`, and to calculate the request rate and error ratio.

### Step 1: Load the Log File in Apache Log Viewer

- **Action:** Open Apache Log Viewer → **File → Open Log File** → select `access.log`
- The tool auto-detects the **Combined Log Format** (or CLF) and parses into columns: `IP`, `Date`, `Time`, `Method`, `URL`, `Status`, `Bytes`, `Referer`, `User-Agent`.

### Step 2: Apply IP Filter

In the **Filter** pane, set:
- `IP Address` → **Equals** → `203.0.113.45`

This reduces the dataset from 1,000 entries to a subset $S_{\text{ip}}$.

### Step 3: Apply Time-Window Filter (Stacked with AND)

- `Date/Time` → **Between** → `2024-10-10 14:00:00` AND `2024-10-10 16:00:00`

Resulting subset: $S_{\text{ip,time}}$.

### Step 4: Identify Suspicious Requests

Sort by **Status Code** ascending. Examine entries with status $\ge 400$.

**Sample suspicious entries extracted:**

```
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:05:11 +0000] "GET /admin.php HTTP/1.1" 404 512 "-" "sqlmap/1.5"
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:05:13 +0000] "GET /phpmyadmin/ HTTP/1.1" 404 510 "-" "sqlmap/1.5"
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:05:18 +0000] "GET /wp-login.php HTTP/1.1" 404 511 "-" "sqlmap/1.5"
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:06:02 +0000] "GET /?id=1' OR '1'='1 HTTP/1.1" 500 1024 "-" "Mozilla/5.0"
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:06:45 +0000] "POST /login.php HTTP/1.1" 200 2048 "http://evil.com/" "Mozilla/5.0"
```

**Observations:**
- The `User-Agent` `sqlmap/1.5` is a well-known **SQL injection tool** — high-confidence attack indicator.
- URL `/?id=1' OR '1'='1` is a classic **SQL injection payload**.
- Rapid sequential requests in milliseconds indicate **automated scanning**.

### Step 5: Calculate Analytical Metrics

Given the extracted dataset (assume 5 entries as above, occurring between 14:05:11 and 14:06:45):

**Time window calculation:**

$$\Delta t = t_{\text{end}} - t_{\text{start}} = 14\!:\!06\!:\!45 - 14\!:\!05\!:\!11 = 94 \text{ seconds}$$

**Request Rate:**

$$R = \frac{N_{\text{requests}}}{\Delta t} = \frac{5}{94} \approx 0.0532 \text{ requests/second}$$

Equivalently:

$$R \approx 3.19 \text{ requests/minute}$$

**Error Ratio:**

Of the 5 requests, the status codes are: `404, 404, 404, 500, 200`.

$$N_{\text{4xx}} + N_{\text{5xx}} = 4 + 1 = 5$$

$$E_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{5}{5} = 1.00 = 100\%$$

> **Forensic Conclusion:** A **100% error ratio** combined with **sqlmap User-Agent** and **SQL injection payload** is conclusive evidence of an **active web application attack** from `203.0.113.45`. This IP must be added to blocklists and the incident escalated.

### Step 6: Export the Evidence

- **File → Export → CSV** → save as `evidence_203.0.113.45.csv`
- Generate an **HTML report** for the case file.
- Compute **SHA-256 hash** of the original `access.log` for chain-of-custody integrity.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Apache Log Viewer

| Investigation Scenario | Suitable Tool? | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Apache/Nginx/IIS web server logs | **Yes (Primary)** | Native format support |
| Windows Event Logs (.evtx) | No | Use Event Log Explorer instead |
| Firewall logs (pfsense, iptables) | Limited | Use specialized SIEM (Splunk, Wireshark) |
| Database query logs | No | Use DB-specific tools |
| Email server logs | No | Use MailDetective or similar |
| FTP/SSH logs | No | Use bare log readers + regex |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Chain of Custody Workflow

A KTU 2024 Scheme examiner expects the **forensic process**, not just the tool:

1. **Acquire** — Copy `access.log` and `error.log` to forensic media (write-blocker, bit-stream image if required).
2. **Hash** — Compute `MD5` and `SHA-256` of original.
3. **Verify** — Compare hashes before and after analysis.
4. **Parse** — Open in Apache Log Viewer (read-only mode).
5. **Filter** — Apply investigator-defined filters; document each filter.
6. **Document** — Screenshot every significant finding with timestamps.
7. **Export** — Save filtered subsets as separate CSV files, re-hash.
8. **Report** — Compile findings with IOCs (Indicators of Compromise).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Explain the structure of the Apache Common Log Format (CLF) with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Common Log Format (CLF)** is the original NCSA-standardized format for HTTP server logs. Each log line contains seven fields in the order:

```
remotehost rfc931 authuser [date:time timezone] "request" status bytes
```

**Example (3 marks for full structure):**
```
127.0.0.1 - frank [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36 -0700] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.0" 200 2326
```

- `127.0.0.1` → Client IP address
- `-` → RFC 931 identifier (usually unavailable)
- `frank` → Authenticated user
- `[10/Oct/2024:13:55:36 -0700]` → Timestamp with timezone
- `"GET /index.html HTTP/1.0"` → HTTP request line
- `200` → HTTP status code (OK)
- `2326` → Response size in bytes

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**List and briefly explain any THREE features of Apache Log Viewer useful in digital forensics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Multi-format log parsing (1 mark):** Supports Apache CLF, Combined Log Format, IIS W3C, and Nginx logs, allowing investigators to analyze logs from heterogeneous web servers using a single tool.
2. **Advanced filtering and search (1 mark):** Enables Boolean (AND/OR) filters on any field (IP, URL, status, time), crucial for narrowing millions of log entries to a forensic-relevant subset.
3. **Statistical analysis and reporting (1 mark):** Generates summaries of top IPs, URLs, status codes, and bandwidth — useful for identifying anomalous patterns and producing court-admissible reports.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of Apache Log Viewer. Discuss the role of the **Format Detector** and **Filter Manager** modules. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An investigator obtains a 500 MB Apache `access.log` from a compromised server. Describe **step-by-step** how you would use Apache Log Viewer to extract evidence of an SQL injection attack originating from IP `198.51.100.23`. Include at least **three filter criteria** you would apply. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Differentiate between **Common Log Format (CLF)** and **Combined Log Format** with examples. Why is the Combined Log Format more valuable in forensic investigations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A web server log shows 1,200 requests in 10 minutes from a single IP. Of these, 1,150 returned HTTP 404, 40 returned HTTP 500, and 10 returned HTTP 200. Calculate:
- (i) The request rate $R$ in requests/second.
- (ii) The error ratio $E_{\text{ratio}}$.
- (iii) Interpret these values from a forensic perspective. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Architecture of Apache Log Viewer (4 marks for diagram + explanation):**

Apache Log Viewer follows a **modular, pipeline-based architecture** consisting of four key layers:

1. **Input Layer** — Accepts multiple log file types: `access.log`, `error.log`, IIS W3C, Nginx, and W3C extended logs.
2. **Parser Engine** — Contains the **Format Detector** which auto-identifies the log syntax using header signatures and field patterns, then dispatches to the appropriate parser (CLF, Combined, W3C).
3. **Analysis Core** — Houses the **Filter Manager** (Boolean filter stack), **Search Engine** (full-text and regex), **Statistics Module** (count, sum, average), and **Report Module** (templated outputs).
4. **Output Layer** — Renders the parsed and filtered data into a sortable grid, plus exports to CSV, HTML, XML, and TXT.

**Role of Format Detector (1.5 marks):**
The Format Detector examines the **first few lines** of the log file and matches field patterns (e.g., presence of `Referer` and `User-Agent` indicates Combined Log Format). It enables **plug-and-play analysis** without manual configuration.

**Role of Filter Manager (1.5 marks):**
The Filter Manager allows investigators to construct **stacked, reusable Boolean expressions** across any parsed field. Filters are crucial for **triaging massive logs** down to the small subset of forensically relevant entries (e.g., all requests from a suspect IP, all 5xx errors, all POST requests to `/login`).

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Step-by-step forensic extraction (7 marks — distribute ~1 mark per significant step):**

**Step 1 — Acquisition & Integrity (1 mark):**
Copy `access.log` to forensic media. Compute `SHA-256` hash of original; record in chain-of-custody log.

**Step 2 — Load into Apache Log Viewer (1 mark):**
Open Apache Log Viewer in **read-only mode** (do not modify original). The Format Detector identifies **Combined Log Format**.

**Step 3 — Apply IP Filter (1 mark):**
- `IP Address` → **Equals** → `198.51.100.23`

**Step 4 — Apply SQL Injection Indicators (2 marks — three filters):**
Stack the following filters using AND logic:
- `URL CONTAINS "'"` (single-quote — classic SQL injection marker)
- `URL CONTAINS "union"` OR `URL CONTAINS "select"` OR `URL CONTAINS "or 1=1"` (SQL keywords)
- `User-Agent CONTAINS "sqlmap"` OR `User-Agent CONTAINS "havij"` (automated SQL tools)

**Step 5 — Apply Time Window (1 mark):**
Restrict to the time of suspected compromise using `Date/Time BETWEEN ...`.

**Step 6 — Document and Export (1 mark):**
- Screenshot each significant finding with system clock visible.
- Export filtered subset to `evidence_sqli_198.51.100.23.csv`.
- Re-hash exported file; preserve in case folder.

**Examiner's Note:** A complete answer must show the **filtering cascade** and **chain-of-custody preservation**.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**Difference between CLF and Combined Log Format (5 marks for table + explanation):**

| Aspect | Common Log Format (CLF) | Combined Log Format |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | NCSA, early 1990s | Extended by Apache, late 1990s |
| **Fields** | 7 fields | 9 fields (CLF + 2) |
| **Referer included?** | No | Yes |
| **User-Agent included?** | No | Yes |
| **Browser tracking** | Not possible | Possible via UA parsing |
| **Example** | `127.0.0.1 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36 -0700] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 2326` | `127.0.0.1 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36 -0700] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 2326 "http://google.com" "Mozilla/5.0"` |

**Why Combined Log Format is more valuable in forensics (2 marks):**
- The **Referer** field reveals **how the victim reached the page** — useful in phishing investigations and tracing attack chains.
- The **User-Agent** field identifies the **client software** (browser, OS, attack tool) — critical for **attribution** and detecting automated tools (e.g., `sqlmap`, `Nikto`, `curl`).
- Both fields allow reconstruction of the **complete HTTP request context**, which CLF cannot provide.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Given:** $N_{\text{total}} = 1200$ requests, $N_{\text{404}} = 1150$, $N_{\text{500}} = 40$, $N_{\text{200}} = 10$, $\Delta t = 10$ minutes = 600 seconds.

**(i) Request Rate (2 marks):**

$$R = \frac{N_{\text{requests}}}{\Delta t} = \frac{1200}{600} = 2.0 \text{ requests/second}$$

**(ii) Error Ratio (2 marks):**

$$E_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{N_{\text{4xx}} + N_{\text{5xx}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1150 + 40}{1200} = \frac{1190}{1200} \approx 0.9917 = 99.17\%$$

**(iii) Forensic Interpretation (3 marks):**

- A request rate of **2 req/s** sustained for 10 minutes from a single IP indicates **automated activity** (human browsing does not sustain this rate).
- An **error ratio of 99.17%** is **catastrophically high** and incompatible with legitimate browsing (typical benign error ratios are < 5%).
- The pattern of **dominant 404s** suggests **directory/file enumeration** — the attacker is probing for hidden resources (`/admin`, `/.git`, `/backup.zip`).
- The **40 × 500 errors** indicate attempts to **exploit vulnerabilities** that caused server-side crashes.
- Only **10 successful (200) responses** may indicate the few exploits that succeeded — these are **critical IOCs (Indicators of Compromise)**.
- **Verdict:** This is a **high-confidence active reconnaissance and exploitation attack**; the server should be considered compromised.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse CLF with Combined Log Format** — write out the additional `Referer` and `User-Agent` fields explicitly.
> 2. **Always include units** in formula answers (`requests/second`, `%` for ratios).
> 3. **Forensic interpretation matters** — simply calculating numbers without explaining their significance loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mention chain-of-custody** (hashing, read-only mode, screenshots) wherever logs are handled.
> 5. **Avoid using the term "hacker"** — use precise terms: *threat actor*, *adversary*, *suspect IP*, *attacker*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Apache Log Viewer**

- **Definition (1-liner):** Apache Log Viewer is a freeware GUI tool to parse, filter, search, and report on Apache (and other web server) log files for forensic analysis.
- **Log Location (Linux):** `/var/log/apache2/` (Debian/Ubuntu) or `/var/log/httpd/` (RHEL/CentOS).
- **Two main log files:** `access.log` (every HTTP request) and `error.log` (diagnostics and failures).
- **CLF fields (7):** `IP, ident, user, [date:time tz], "request", status, bytes`.
- **Combined Log Format (9 fields):** CLF + `"Referer"` + `"User-Agent"`.
- **Default Ports:** HTTP = **80**, HTTPS = **443**.
- **HTTP Status Classes:** 1xx Info, 2xx Success, 3xx Redirect, 4xx Client Error, 5xx Server Error.
- **Critical forensic status codes:** 401 (brute force), 404 (reconnaissance), 500 (exploitation success).
- **Key Formulae:**
  - Request rate: $R = N / \Delta t$
  - Error ratio: $E = (N_{4xx} + N_{5xx}) / N_{\text{total}}$
- **Top 3 forensic features:** Multi-format parsing, Boolean filtering, statistical reporting.
- **Forensic attack indicators in logs:**
  - User-Agent = `sqlmap`, `nikto`, `nmap`, `havij`, `w3af` → automated attack tool.
  - URL contains `' OR '1'='1`, `UNION SELECT`, `<script>` → injection attack.
  - URL contains `../`, `..%2f`, encoded traversal → path traversal.
  - Rapid sequential 404s to sensitive paths → reconnaissance.
- **Forensic Workflow:** Acquire → Hash → Parse → Filter → Document → Export → Re-hash → Report.
- **Export formats supported:** CSV, HTML, XML, TXT.
- **Limitation:** Not suitable for Windows Event Logs, firewall logs, or database logs.
- **Examiner's mantra:** *"Always hash before and after, work in read-only mode, and document every filter."*
